topics.title: Introduction to Machine Learning - Supervised, Semi-supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Machine Learning - Supervised, Semi-supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Machine Learning?

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Learning (ML)** is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables computer systems to learn patterns and relationships from data, identify underlying structures, and make intelligent decisions or predictions **without being explicitly programmed** for every specific task. The system improves its performance at a given task **as the number of experiences (data samples) increases**.

The two classical textbook definitions that frequently appear in KTU 2-mark/3-mark questions are:

**Arthur Samuel (1959):** *"Machine Learning is the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*

**Tom Mitchell (1997):** *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

This three-part formulation — **Task (T), Experience (E), Performance (P)** — is the cornerstone answer that earns full marks in KTU Part A questions.

| Component | Symbol | Example (Spam Detection) |
|---|---|---|
| Task | $T$ | Classify emails as spam or not-spam |
| Experience | $E$ | The set of training emails with their labels |
| Performance | $P$ | Accuracy / F1-score on unseen emails |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine teaching a child to recognize fruits.** You do not write down a 200-line rulebook describing color, shape, weight, and texture of mangoes. Instead, you simply show the child 50 mangoes and 50 apples and say "this is a mango, this is an apple." Over time, the child forms an internal mental model that generalizes to **fruits they have never seen before**. Machine learning works in exactly the same way: we feed the algorithm thousands of labeled examples, and the algorithm **discovers the rule by itself** rather than having a human hand-code it.

### Why Machine Learning is Required Today

Traditional rule-based programming fails when:
1. **The rules are not known** (e.g., handwriting recognition — humans read digits intuitively but cannot write down the precise pixel rules).
2. **The rules change dynamically** (e.g., stock market prediction — patterns shift with time).
3. **The data volume is too vast** (e.g., recommendation systems for millions of users).
4. **The relationship is highly non-linear** (e.g., image classification, NLP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KDD (Knowledge Discovery in Databases) Process** that houses ML consists of: *Data Cleaning → Data Integration → Data Selection → Data Transformation → **Data Mining (ML step)** → Pattern Evaluation → Knowledge Presentation.* Machine Learning is essentially the **Data Mining engine** inside this pipeline.

## 1.2 The Four Paradigms of Machine Learning

> [!NOTE]
> ML paradigms are classified based on the **nature of the feedback signal** available to the learning algorithm during training. KTU 2024 Module 1 mandates mastery of all four types.

### 1.2.1 Supervised Learning

The algorithm is trained on a dataset where **every input sample has a corresponding known output label** — like learning with a teacher who provides the correct answer for every question.

$$ \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N} $$

where $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the feature vector and $y_i$ is the target label.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Classification** — discrete output (e.g., spam vs. ham). $y_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, K-1\}$
- **Regression** — continuous output (e.g., house price). $y_i \in \mathbb{R}$

**Canonical algorithms:** Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, SVM, k-NN, Random Forest, Neural Networks.

### 1.2.2 Unsupervised Learning

The algorithm receives **only inputs, no labels** — like being given a basket of mixed fruits and asked to cluster them on your own.

$$ \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} = \{\mathbf{x}_i\}_{i=1}^{N} \quad \text{with no } y_i $$

The system must discover **intrinsic structure** in the data.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Clustering** (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
- **Dimensionality Reduction** (e.g., PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders)
- **Association Rule Mining** (e.g., Apriori, FP-Growth)
- **Density Estimation** (e.g., GMM)

### 1.2.3 Semi-Supervised Learning

A **hybrid paradigm** that uses a small amount of labeled data combined with a large amount of unlabeled data — like a student who studies a few solved examples and then attempts many unsolved problems.

$$ \mathcal{D} = \underbrace{\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{l}}_{\text{small labeled set}} \cup \underbrace{\{\mathbf{x}_j\}_{j=l+1}^{l+u}}_{\text{large unlabeled set}}, \quad u \gg l $$

**Why it matters:** Labeling data is **expensive** (requires human annotators), but unlabeled data is **cheap and abundant**. SSL sits in the sweet spot. Examples: medical image classification (few expert annotations + many raw scans), web-page classification.

**Key techniques:** Self-training, Co-training, Graph-based label propagation, Generative models (e.g., Variational Autoencoders).

### 1.2.4 Reinforcement Learning

An **agent** learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an **environment** — it receives **rewards** (positive) or **penalties** (negative) and learns a **policy** that maximizes cumulative reward.

**Core elements:** Agent, Environment, State $s_t$, Action $a_t$, Reward $r_t$, Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$, Value function $V(s)$, Q-function $Q(s, a)$.

**Famous examples:** AlphaGo defeating the world champion, robotic locomotion, autonomous driving, game-playing agents (Atari, StarCraft).

**Two main families:**
- **Model-free RL** — Q-Learning, SARSA, Deep Q-Networks (DQN), Policy Gradient (REINFORCE).
- **Model-based RL** — learn a model of the environment first, then plan.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Mathematical Framing of Each Paradigm

### 2.1.1 Supervised Learning — The Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) Principle

**Operational Logic:**
1. Assume data is drawn i.i.d. from an unknown joint distribution $P(X, Y)$.
2. Choose a **hypothesis class** $\mathcal{H}$ (e.g., set of all linear functions, or all neural networks of a fixed architecture).
3. Define a **loss function** $L(\hat{y}, y)$ that quantifies the cost of predicting $\hat{y}$ when the true value is $y$.
4. Find the hypothesis $h^* \in \mathcal{H}$ that minimizes the **expected risk**:
5. Since the true distribution is unknown, minimize the **empirical risk** on training data.

**Why does this work?**
By the **Law of Large Numbers**, as $N \to \infty$, the empirical risk converges to the true expected risk, so minimizing the empirical surrogate becomes a valid proxy for minimizing the true error — *provided* the model class is well-chosen and overfitting is controlled.

### 2.1.2 Unsupervised Learning — Objective Functions

**For Clustering (K-Means):** Minimize within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS).

**For Dimensionality Reduction (PCA):** Find orthogonal directions that maximize variance.

**For Density Estimation (GMM):** Maximize the data log-likelihood under a mixture of Gaussians.

### 2.1.3 Semi-Supervised Learning — The Core Assumptions

SSL methods rely on three foundational assumptions:

1. **Smoothness Assumption** — Points close in feature space likely share the same label.
2. **Cluster Assumption** — Data tends to form discrete clusters, and points in the same cluster share the label.
3. **Manifold Assumption** — High-dimensional data lies on a low-dimensional manifold, and labels are constant along it.

### 2.1.4 Reinforcement Learning — The Markov Decision Process (MDP)

An MDP is the mathematical framework for RL, defined as a 5-tuple $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$:
- $S$ — set of states
- $A$ — set of actions
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — transition probability
- $R(s, a, s')$ — reward function
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — discount factor

**The objective:** find the optimal policy $\pi^*(a \mid s)$ that maximizes the expected discounted cumulative reward:

$$ G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \, r_{t+k+1} $$

$$ \pi^* = \arg\max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \, r_t \right] $$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield cheat sheet** for KTU ESE. Memorize the formula column thoroughly — direct formula-recall questions appear every semester.

| Paradigm | Core Formula | Loss / Objective | Key Boundary / Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supervised (Regression) | $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$ | MSE: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | IID data, fixed model class |
| Supervised (Classification) | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c P(y=c \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Cross-Entropy: $-\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ | $0 \le \hat{y}_c \le 1, \; \sum_c \hat{y}_c = 1$ |
| Unsupervised (K-Means) | $\min \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k}\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ | WCSS | $K$ clusters, Euclidean distance |
| Unsupervised (PCA) | $\max_{\mathbf{w}: \Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert=1} \mathbf{w}^\top \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ | Variance of projection | $\Sigma$ is covariance matrix, $\mathbf{w}$ unit vector |
| Semi-Supervised | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{sup}} + \lambda \, \mathcal{L}_{\text{unsup}}$ | Weighted sum | $\lambda \ge 0$ trades off the two losses |
| Reinforcement (Bellman) | $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')\right]$ | TD target | $0 \le \gamma < 1$ for convergence |
| Reinforcement (Q-Learning) | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\right]$ | TD error | $\alpha \in (0,1]$ learning rate |

## 2.3 Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Paradigm

| Criterion | Supervised | Unsupervised | Semi-Supervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Labels required? | **Yes** (all) | No | **Few** | Reward signal only |
| Data cost | **High** (labeling expensive) | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Feedback type | Direct (label) | None | Direct + structural | Delayed (reward) |
| Main task | Prediction | Structure discovery | Prediction w/ limited labels | Sequential decision-making |
| Example use | Cancer diagnosis | Customer segmentation | Web-page classification | Game playing, robotics |
| Output | Class / continuous value | Clusters / reduced features | Class / continuous value | Policy $\pi$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Supervised learning** powers medical diagnosis systems, credit scoring, weather forecasting, and quality control in manufacturing.
- **Unsupervised learning** drives customer segmentation in marketing, anomaly detection in cybersecurity, gene expression analysis in bioinformatics, and topic modeling in news aggregation.
- **Semi-supervised learning** is the workhorse of modern NLP (e.g., BERT pretraining) and large-scale image recognition where labeling millions of images is infeasible.
- **Reinforcement learning** underlies autonomous vehicles, robotic surgery, smart grid optimization, supply chain management, and the recommendation engines that adapt to long-term user engagement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) in Supervised Learning

We derive the closed-form solution for **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** linear regression — a derivation that is **frequently asked as a 7-mark sub-part** in KTU ESE.

### Step 1: Model Assumption

Assume a linear relationship between input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and output $y \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$ \hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b $$

For matrix convenience, augment $\mathbf{x}$ with a constant $1$ and absorb $b$ into $\mathbf{w}$:

$$ \hat{\mathbf{y}} = X \mathbf{w} $$

where $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ is the design matrix (with the all-ones column) and $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$ is the parameter vector.

### Step 2: Define the Empirical Loss (Mean Squared Error)

$$ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{w})^2 $$

In matrix form:

$$ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{N} \, (X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})^\top (X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y}) $$

### Step 3: Expand the Quadratic

$$ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \mathbf{w}^\top X^\top X \mathbf{w} - 2 \mathbf{w}^\top X^\top \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^\top \mathbf{y} \right] $$

**Logic:** This expansion uses the identity $(A\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})^\top(A\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{w}^\top A^\top A \mathbf{w} - 2\mathbf{w}^\top A^\top \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^\top \mathbf{y}$.

### Step 4: Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \frac{1}{N} \left[ 2 X^\top X \mathbf{w} - 2 X^\top \mathbf{y} \right] = \mathbf{0} $$

### Step 5: Solve the Normal Equation

$$ X^\top X \mathbf{w} = X^\top \mathbf{y} $$

$$ \mathbf{w}^* = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y} $$

**Logic:** This is the **Normal Equation** of OLS. It requires $X^\top X$ to be invertible, which holds when $N > d+1$ and the features are linearly independent.

## 3.2 Derivation: K-Means Objective and Lloyd's Algorithm

### Step 1: The Objective

Given $N$ points and $K$ clusters with centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K$, assign each point to the nearest centroid and update centroids as the cluster means:

$$ \mathcal{J} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2 $$

### Step 2: Assignment Step (Closed Form)

For each $\mathbf{x}_i$, choose the cluster $C_k$ that minimizes the distance:

$$ C_k^{(t)} = \left\{ \mathbf{x}_i : \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2 \le \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \Vert^2 \; \forall j \neq k \right\} $$

### Step 3: Update Step (Closed Form)

Given the new cluster assignments, the optimal centroid is the mean of the points in that cluster. Derivation: set $\partial \mathcal{J} / \partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = 0$:

$$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = -2 \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} (\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = 0 $$

$$ \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i $$

### Step 4: Convergence Guarantee

K-Means is guaranteed to **converge in a finite number of steps** because the objective $\mathcal{J}$ is monotonically non-increasing at each iteration and there are only finitely many possible cluster assignments ($K^N$). However, it may converge to a **local minimum**, not the global optimum.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bellman Optimality Equation for Q-Learning

### Step 1: Define the Action-Value Function

The action-value $Q^\pi(s, a)$ is the expected return starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, and then following policy $\pi$:

$$ Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \!\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_{t+1} \,\Big|\, s_0 = s, a_0 = a \right] $$

### Step 2: Unroll the Recursion

$$ Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r_1 + \gamma \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \gamma^{t-1} r_{t+1} \,\Big|\, s_0 = s, a_0 = a \right] $$

The inner sum is the expected return from the next state onward, which equals $\mathbb{E}_{a' \sim \pi}\!\left[ Q^\pi(s', a') \right]$. So:

$$ Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{a'}\!\left[ Q^\pi(s', a') \right] \,\Big|\, s, a \right] $$

### Step 3: The Optimal Bellman Equation

For the optimal policy, $Q^*$ satisfies:

$$ Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \,\Big|\, s, a \right] $$

### Step 4: The Q-Learning Update Rule

Since we do not know the true expectation, we **sample** a transition $(s, a, r, s')$ and update $Q$ via stochastic approximation:

$$ Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \right] $$

The term in brackets is the **Temporal Difference (TD) error** $\delta_t$, and $\alpha$ is the learning rate. Under standard conditions ($\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$), Q-Learning is guaranteed to converge to $Q^*$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Paradigm

| Scenario | Best Paradigm | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Predict house prices from size, location | Supervised (Regression) | Continuous labeled target available |
| Detect fraudulent credit-card transactions | Supervised (Classification) | Historical labeled fraud cases exist |
| Group news articles by topic | Unsupervised (Clustering) | No predefined topic labels |
| Compress 1000-dim images to 50-dim | Unsupervised (PCA) | Preserve variance with fewer features |
| Classify X-ray images with only 100 labeled + 10,000 unlabeled | Semi-Supervised | Labeling X-rays requires expensive radiologists |
| Train a robot to walk on uneven terrain | Reinforcement | No ground-truth action sequence; reward-driven |
| Optimize a chess engine to defeat humans | Reinforcement | Delayed, sparse reward (win/loss) |
| Train a chatbot with limited conversation logs | Semi-Supervised | Few labeled intents + many raw utterances |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Machine Learning Paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the four learning paradigms with their sub-branches, input/output contracts, and canonical algorithms — a one-glance revision aid for the KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RootA[Machine Learning Paradigms] --> B1[Supervised Learning]
    RootA --> B2[Unsupervised Learning]
    RootA --> B3[Semi-Supervised Learning]
    RootA --> B4[Reinforcement Learning]

    B1 --> C1A[Classification]
    B1 --> C1B[Regression]

    C1A --> D1A[Logistic Regression]
    C1A --> D1B[Decision Tree]
    C1A --> D1C[SVM]
    C1A --> D1D[k Nearest Neighbors]
    C1A --> D1E[Neural Networks]

    C1B --> D2A[Linear Regression]
    C1B --> D2B[Polynomial Regression]
    C1B --> D2C[Random Forest Regressor]

    B2 --> C2A[Clustering]
    B2 --> C2B[Dimensionality Reduction]
    B2 --> C2C[Association Mining]
    B2 --> C2D[Density Estimation]

    C2A --> E1A[K Means]
    C2A --> E1B[HAC]
    C2A --> E1C[DBSCAN]
    C2B --> E2A[PCA]
    C2B --> E2B[t SNE]
    C2B --> E2C[Autoencoder]
    C2C --> E3A[Apriori]
    C2C --> E3B[FP Growth]
    C2D --> E4A[GMM]
    C2D --> E4B[Kernel Density]

    B3 --> F1[Self Training]
    B3 --> F2[Co Training]
    B3 --> F3[Graph Based SSL]
    B3 --> F4[Generative SSL]

    B4 --> G1[Model Free RL]
    B4 --> G2[Model Based RL]

    G1 --> H1A[Q Learning]
    G1 --> H1B[SARSA]
    G1 --> H1C[Policy Gradient]
    G1 --> H1D[Deep Q Network]
    G2 --> H2A[AlphaZero]
    G2 --> H2B[Dyna Q]
    G2 --> H2C[MCTS]
```

## 4.2 Supervised Learning Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Raw Data] --> nodeB[Data Preprocessing]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Feature Engineering]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Train Test Split]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Model Training]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Model Evaluation]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Performance Acceptable}
    nodeH -->|No| nodeE
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Deployment]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Monitor and Retrain]
    nodeJ --> nodeE
```

## 4.3 Reinforcement Learning Agent-Environment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateA[State s_t] --> agentNode[Agent]
    agentNode -->|Action a_t| envNode[Environment]
    envNode -->|Reward r_t and Next State s_t1| agentNode
    envNode --> stateA
```

## 4.4 Semi-Supervised Learning Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dataA[Labeled Data Small Set] --> modelNode[Combined SSL Model]
    dataB[Unlabeled Data Large Set] --> modelNode
    modelNode --> pseudoLabel[Pseudo Labeling Step]
    pseudoLabel --> modelNode
    modelNode --> outputNode[Predictions on Test Data]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Machine Learning. State and explain Tom Mitchell's formal definition with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tom Mitchell (1997) formally defined Machine Learning as:

> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

**Example — Spam Email Classifier:**
- **Task (T):** Classify incoming emails as *spam* or *not-spam*.
- **Experience (E):** A large collection of historical emails along with their correct labels.
- **Performance (P):** The classification accuracy on a held-out test set of emails.

After processing more labeled emails (more E), the classifier's accuracy (P) on the task (T) of spam detection improves — hence the program is *learning*.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Training data | Labeled $(\mathbf{x}, y)$ | Unlabeled $\mathbf{x}$ | Reward signal $r$ from environment |
| Goal | Learn $f: X \to Y$ | Discover hidden structure | Learn optimal policy $\pi$ |
| Feedback | Direct (correct answer) | None | Delayed (reward/penalty) |
| Example | Email spam classification | Customer segmentation by K-Means | Robot learning to walk via trial and error |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse *reinforcement* feedback (scalar reward, delayed) with *supervised* feedback (label for every input). Reinforcement learning does **not** receive the correct action; it receives a *scalar reward*. Writing "RL uses labeled data" is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the four major paradigms of Machine Learning. Compare them with respect to the type of feedback signal used, data requirements, and a representative algorithm for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose you are building a system to predict whether a credit-card transaction is fraudulent. The bank has 1,000 confirmed fraud transactions and 100,000 unlabeled transactions. Discuss the most appropriate learning paradigm with justification, and outline the algorithm you would deploy. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Detailed Paradigm Explanation (7 marks)

**1. Supervised Learning (2 marks):** The algorithm is provided with input-output pairs $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ and learns a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ by minimizing a loss function. Two sub-problems: **classification** (discrete labels) and **regression** (continuous values). Algorithms: Linear/Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Trees, Neural Networks. Used when historical labeled data is abundant — e.g., disease diagnosis.

**2. Unsupervised Learning (2 marks):** Only inputs $\{\mathbf{x}_i\}$ are available; the algorithm must discover structure — clusters, low-dimensional manifolds, or associations. Algorithms: K-Means (clustering), PCA (dimensionality reduction), Apriori (association rules). Used for exploratory analysis — e.g., market segmentation.

**3. Semi-Supervised Learning (1.5 marks):** A blend that uses a small labeled set + a large unlabeled set. Exploits the cluster, smoothness, and manifold assumptions. Algorithms: Self-training, Co-training, label propagation, generative methods. Used when labeling is expensive — e.g., medical imaging.

**4. Reinforcement Learning (1.5 marks):** An agent learns a policy by interacting with an environment and receiving scalar rewards. Formulated as an MDP $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$. Algorithms: Q-Learning, SARSA, Policy Gradient, DQN. Used for sequential decision-making — e.g., game playing, robotics.

**Comparison table summarizing feedback / data / algorithm** — already provided in Section 2.4 of these notes. *(Students should reproduce a compact 3-row table.)*

#### Part (b) — Fraud Detection Problem (7 marks)

**Justification of Paradigm (3 marks):**
The scenario describes **1,000 labeled** transactions and **100,000 unlabeled** transactions. This is a textbook case for **Semi-Supervised Learning** because:
- The labeled set is too small to train a robust supervised classifier on its own.
- The unlabeled set is large and contains valuable structural information about the legitimate transaction distribution.
- Acquiring more labels requires expensive manual review by fraud analysts.

**Recommended Algorithm — Self-Training Pipeline (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Train an initial supervised classifier (e.g., Logistic Regression or Random Forest) on the 1,000 labeled samples.

**Step 2:** Use this classifier to predict pseudo-labels for the 100,000 unlabeled samples, along with prediction confidence (probability).

**Step 3:** Select unlabeled samples where the classifier's confidence exceeds a high threshold $\tau$ (e.g., 0.95) and add them to the labeled set with their pseudo-labels.

**Step 4:** Retrain the classifier on the expanded labeled set.

**Step 5:** Repeat Steps 2–4 until no new high-confidence pseudo-labels emerge or a maximum iteration count is reached.

**Why this works:** The model progressively expands its understanding of the "legitimate transaction" cluster in feature space (cluster assumption) and sharpens the decision boundary around the rare "fraud" class.

**Alternative:** Co-training with two independent feature views (e.g., transaction metadata + behavioral biometrics) is a strong alternative that can outperform self-training when feature redundancy is low.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State and prove the Normal Equation for Ordinary Least Squares linear regression. Clearly state all assumptions and explain why each is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-dimensional dataset with 8 points: $(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2), (8,8), (8,9), (9,8), (9,9)$, perform **one complete iteration** of the K-Means algorithm with $K=2$ and initial centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (1, 1)$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (9, 9)$. Show the new cluster assignments and updated centroids. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Normal Equation Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Linearity: $y = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + \epsilon$.
2. Zero mean of error: $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$.
3. Homoscedasticity and uncorrelated errors with constant variance $\sigma^2$.
4. No multicollinearity — $X^\top X$ is invertible.
5. Errors are normally distributed (required for confidence intervals and hypothesis tests, not for the point estimate).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the model in matrix form. $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = X \mathbf{w}$.

**Step 2:** Define MSE loss: $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum (y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{w})^2 = \frac{1}{N}(X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})^\top(X\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y})$.

**Step 3:** Expand: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}[\mathbf{w}^\top X^\top X \mathbf{w} - 2 \mathbf{w}^\top X^\top \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^\top \mathbf{y}]$.

**Step 4:** Set gradient to zero: $\nabla_\mathbf{w} \mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}[2X^\top X \mathbf{w} - 2X^\top \mathbf{y}] = 0$.

**Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the Normal Equation: $X^\top X \mathbf{w} = X^\top \mathbf{y}$.

**Step 6:** Solve: $\mathbf{w}^* = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}$. ∎

**Why each assumption is necessary:**
- *Linearity* ensures the loss is a convex quadratic in $\mathbf{w}$ with a unique minimum.
- *Invertibility of $X^\top X$* guarantees a unique closed-form solution.
- *Zero-mean errors* ensure the OLS estimate is unbiased: $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{w}^*] = \mathbf{w}$.
- *Homoscedasticity & no autocorrelation* ensure the Gauss-Markov theorem applies, making OLS the *Best Linear Unbiased Estimator* (BLUE).

#### Part (b) — K-Means Iteration (7 marks)

**Initial centroids:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)} = (1,1)$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)} = (9,9)$.

**Step 1: Assignment (1 mark each cluster):**

For each of the 8 points, compute squared Euclidean distance to each centroid and assign to the closer one:

| Point | $d^2$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1$ | $d^2$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2$ | Assigned Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,1)$ | 0 | 128 | C1 |
| $(1,2)$ | 1 | 113 | C1 |
| $(2,1)$ | 1 | 113 | C1 |
| $(2,2)$ | 2 | 98 | C1 |
| $(8,8)$ | 98 | 2 | C2 |
| $(8,9)$ | 113 | 1 | C2 |
| $(9,8)$ | 113 | 1 | C2 |
| $(9,9)$ | 128 | 0 | C2 |

**Step 2: Update Centroids (1 mark each):**

$$ \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}\left[(1,1) + (1,2) + (2,1) + (2,2)\right] = \left(\frac{6}{4}, \frac{6}{4}\right) = (1.5, 1.5) $$

$$ \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{4}\left[(8,8) + (8,9) + (9,8) + (9,9)\right] = \left(\frac{34}{4}, \frac{34}{4}\right) = (8.5, 8.5) $$

**Step 3: Verify convergence (1 mark):**

Since the new centroids differ from the old ones, K-Means will continue. The WCSS after this iteration is:

$$ \mathcal{J}^{(1)} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - (1.5, 1.5) \Vert^2 + \sum_{i=5}^{8} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - (8.5, 8.5) \Vert^2 = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 8 $$

The algorithm has correctly identified the two natural clusters in the data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (K-Means):** (1) Students forget to use **squared** Euclidean distance when computing WCSS — partial credit lost. (2) Students wrongly update centroids using the *old* assignments instead of recomputing the means of the *new* clusters. (3) Students claim K-Means always converges to the global optimum — it does not; it only guarantees a local minimum.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before ESE:**

- [x] **Arthur Samuel's definition** — "learn without being explicitly programmed."
- [x] **Tom Mitchell's definition** — Task T, Experience E, Performance P — cite all three components explicitly in the answer.
- [x] **Supervised Learning** — labeled $(\mathbf{x}, y)$ pairs, classification vs. regression, ERM principle.
- [x] **Unsupervised Learning** — no labels, clustering / dimensionality reduction / association / density estimation.
- [x] **Semi-Supervised Learning** — few labeled + many unlabeled; three core assumptions (smoothness, cluster, manifold); self-training, co-training.
- [x] **Reinforcement Learning** — agent, environment, state, action, reward, policy; MDP $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$; Bellman equation; Q-learning update rule.
- [x] **Normal Equation** — $\mathbf{w}^* = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}$.
- [x] **K-Means objective** — $\min \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ with closed-form assignment and update steps.
- [x] **Q-Learning update** — $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- [x] **Bellman optimality** — $V^*(s) = \max_a \mathbb{E}[r + \gamma V^*(s')]$.
- [x] **Why we minimize empirical risk** — Law of Large Numbers bridges empirical to true risk.
- [x] **Why Q-Learning converges** — Robbins-Monro stochastic approximation conditions on $\alpha$.
- [x] **Common pitfall words to avoid** — never say "RL uses labeled data"; never say "K-Means finds the global optimum."
- [x] **KDD pipeline** — Data Mining (ML) is one of seven steps; ML ≠ the entire KDD process.
- [x] **Real-world anchors** — spam filter (supervised), customer segmentation (unsupervised), medical imaging (semi-supervised), AlphaGo (RL).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Regression with One Variable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear Regression with One Variable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Regression with One Variable (Univariate Linear Regression)** is a **supervised learning** algorithm used in machine learning to model the relationship between a **single independent (input) feature variable** and a **continuous dependent (output) target variable** by fitting a linear equation of the form $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ to the observed data.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this is a foundational **parametric regression model** where:
- The model is *parametric* because it assumes a fixed functional form (a straight line in 2D).
- The hypothesis $h_\theta(x)$ produces **real-valued, continuous predictions**, as opposed to classification.
- The objective is to learn the optimal parameters $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ that minimize the prediction error on training data.

**Standard terminology you must memorize for KTU exams:**

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Feature / Input | $x$ | Independent variable (predictor) |
| Target / Output | $y$ | Dependent variable (response) |
| Training Example | $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | $i$-th observation from dataset |
| Number of Training Examples | $m$ | Total size of training set |
| Hypothesis Function | $h_\theta(x)$ | Model's prediction function |
| Parameters | $\theta_0, \theta_1$ | Coefficients to be learned |
| Parameter Vector | $\theta$ | Concatenated parameters |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Real Estate Office"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are a real estate agent in Kerala trying to estimate the price of a house based solely on its built-up area in square feet.** 📐🏠

You collect data from **100 past sales** — for each house, you note its area ($x$) and the price it sold for ($y$). When you plot these points on graph paper, you notice they roughly form a **cloud trending upward** — bigger houses cost more.

Now, you want to draw **one single straight line** through this cloud such that the line passes as close as possible to *all* the points simultaneously. Once you have that line, you can simply plug in the area of a *new, unlisted* house, and the line tells you its predicted price.

That straight line is the **hypothesis** $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$:
- $\theta_1$ is the **slope** — "how many lakhs does the price rise per extra square foot?"
- $\theta_0$ is the **y-intercept** — "the theoretical base price when area = 0."

The process of finding the *best* $\theta_0, \theta_1$ is the **learning** part of machine learning. The line that minimizes total squared vertical distance to all points is the **Best Fit Line** or **Regression Line**. This squared distance concept is formalized as the **Cost Function** $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$.

---

## 1.3 Why "Linear" and Why "Regression"?

- **Linear** → the prediction is a **linear combination** of the inputs. The hypothesis is linear *in the parameters* $\theta$, not necessarily in $x$ (e.g., $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^2$ is also "linear regression" because it is linear in $\theta$).
- **Regression** → the output $y$ is a **continuous real number** (price, temperature, salary), *not* a discrete class label. This is the key distinction from logistic regression (used for classification).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Keyword Trap:** Students often write "linear regression is used for classification" in exams. This is **incorrect**. Linear regression = **continuous output prediction** (regression task). For classification, you use **logistic regression** or other classifiers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hypothesis Function — The Predictive Engine

The **hypothesis** $h_\theta(x)$ maps input $x$ to a predicted output. For one variable:

$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

- $\theta_0$ : **y-intercept** (bias term). Value of $h$ when $x = 0$.
- $\theta_1$ : **slope** (weight). Change in $h$ per unit change in $x$.
- In vector form: $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$ where $x_0 = 1$ is implicitly added as a bias feature.

### Intuition:
- If $\theta_0 = 50$ and $\theta_1 = 0.5$, then a 1000 sq.ft house is predicted to cost $50 + 0.5 \times 1000 = 550$ lakhs.
- The **goal of learning** is to find the *best* $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ such that $h_\theta(x^{(i)}) \approx y^{(i)}$ for most $i$.

---

## 2.2 The Cost Function — How Wrong Are We?

To measure how "off" our predictions are, we define a **cost function** (also called **loss function** or **squared error function**):

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

### Why the squared error?
- **Squaring** penalizes large errors disproportionately (a 10-unit error costs 100, but a 5-unit error costs 25).
- It is **always non-negative**, so minimizing it drives errors toward zero.
- The factor $\frac{1}{2m}$ (the $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience to cancel the exponent when differentiating; the $\frac{1}{m}$ averages the error per training example).

### Geometric Intuition:
For a fixed $\theta_1$, $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ is a **parabola** in $\theta_0$. For 2 parameters, it is a **bowl-shaped paraboloid** (3D). Our objective is to find the *bottom of the bowl* — the global minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no closed-form guaranteed?** Although a closed-form solution exists (the **Normal Equation**), KTU Module 1 emphasizes the **iterative Gradient Descent** approach because it generalizes to higher dimensions and more complex models.

---

## 2.3 Gradient Descent — The Optimization Algorithm

**Gradient Descent** is the iterative optimization procedure used to find the values of $\theta_0, \theta_1$ that minimize $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$.

### Update Rule:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ (alpha) is the **learning rate** — controls step size.
- The partial derivative is the **gradient** — direction of steepest ascent.
- We subtract because we want to move *opposite* to ascent (i.e., descend).

### Expanded for one variable:

$$\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

### Critical Implementation Detail: **Simultaneous Update**
Both $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ must be updated **at the same time**, using the *old* values of the other. KTU often tests this with a numerical problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Updating $\theta_0$ first and then using the *new* $\theta_0$ to compute the $\theta_1$ update. This is **incorrect** — it must be simultaneous. Examiners explicitly test for this.

---

## 2.4 The Learning Rate $\alpha$ — The Most Critical Hyperparameter

| $\alpha$ Value | Behaviour | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Too small (e.g., $10^{-6}$) | Tiny steps, slow descent | Converges, but takes very long ⏳ |
| Just right (e.g., $0.01$ – $0.1$) | Steady descent to minimum | **Optimal convergence** ✅ |
| Too large (e.g., $10$) | Overshoots the minimum | May **diverge** or oscillate ❌ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As we approach the minimum, the gradient $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ automatically becomes smaller, so the steps shrink even with a fixed $\alpha$. This means gradient descent **does not need to decrease $\alpha$ manually** near the minimum.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ | Linear in parameters |
| 2 | Vectorized Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$ with $x_0 = 1$ | Used in matrix form |
| 3 | Cost Function (MSE) | $J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ | Mean Squared Error form |
| 4 | Gradient $\partial J / \partial \theta_0$ | $\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$ | No $x^{(i)}$ factor |
| 5 | Gradient $\partial J / \partial \theta_1$ | $\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x^{(i)}$ | Multiplied by input |
| 6 | Update $\theta_0$ | $\theta_0 \leftarrow \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta - y)$ | Simultaneous |
| 7 | Update $\theta_1$ | $\theta_1 \leftarrow \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta - y) \cdot x$ | Simultaneous |
| 8 | Normal Equation (closed form) | $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ | No iterations needed, but $O(n^3)$ |
| 9 | Convergence criterion | $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$ | Threshold-based stopping |
| 10 | Final prediction | $\hat{y} = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_{new}$ | Plug in new $x$ |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear regression is the **workhorse of predictive analytics** in:

- 🏗️ **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete compressive strength from cement content.
- ⚡ **Electrical Engineering:** Modeling power consumption vs. temperature.
- 🌾 **Agriculture:** Yield prediction from rainfall and fertilizer use.
- 💰 **Finance:** Risk modeling, stock price trends (as a baseline).
- 🏥 **Healthcare:** Predicting patient recovery time from dosage levels.

> Although simple, it forms the **conceptual foundation** for advanced models like **multiple linear regression, polynomial regression, neural networks, and even Support Vector Regression**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule

We start with the cost function and compute its partial derivative with respect to each parameter.

### Step 1: Write the cost function explicitly

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

### Step 2: Substitute the hypothesis $h_\theta(x^{(i)}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)}$

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

### Step 3: Compute the partial derivative with respect to $\theta_0$

Apply the chain rule. Let $u = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$, so $J = \frac{1}{2m} \sum u^2$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_0}$$

The factor 2 cancels the $\frac{1}{2}$. The derivative $\frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_0} = 1$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

### Step 4: Compute the partial derivative with respect to $\theta_1$

Same chain rule, but $\frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_1} = x^{(i)}$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

### Step 5: Write the gradient descent update rule

Substitute the partial derivatives into the general update rule $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$:

$$\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

These two equations are evaluated using the **old** $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ values, then **simultaneously** assigned to $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$. This is the cornerstone of the algorithm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Simplified Cost Function for $\theta_0 = 0$

For visualization purposes, suppose we temporarily fix $\theta_0 = 0$. The hypothesis reduces to $h_\theta(x) = \theta_1 x$.

$$J(\theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

This is a **quadratic function** of $\theta_1$, so its graph is a parabola. Taking the derivative and setting to zero gives the analytical minimum:

$$\frac{dJ}{d\theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x^{(i)} = 0$$

Solving:

$$\theta_1^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)} y^{(i)}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( x^{(i)} \right)^2}$$

This is the **closed-form solution** for the simplified case. The full 2-parameter case uses the Normal Equation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Exam Style)

**Given Data (3 training examples):**

| $i$ | $x^{(i)}$ (Area in 100 sq.ft) | $y^{(i)}$ (Price in lakhs) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 3 | 5 |

**Initialize:** $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$, $\alpha = 0.01$, $m = 3$.

**Iteration 1:**

Compute predictions: $h_\theta(1) = 0$, $h_\theta(2) = 0$, $h_\theta(3) = 0$.

Compute errors: $h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}$ = $-2, -4, -5$.

Sum of errors: $-11$.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{-11}{3} \approx -3.667$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (-2)(1) + (-4)(2) + (-5)(3) \right] = \frac{-25}{3} \approx -8.333$$

**Update:**

$$\theta_0 = 0 - 0.01 \times (-3.667) = 0.03667$$

$$\theta_1 = 0 - 0.01 \times (-8.333) = 0.08333$$

**After Iteration 1:** $\theta_0 \approx 0.037$, $\theta_1 \approx 0.083$.

> This process repeats for hundreds of iterations until $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ converges to its minimum.

---

## 3.4 Convergence Diagnostics

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| $J$ increases over iterations | $\alpha$ too large | Reduce $\alpha$ (e.g., halve it) |
| $J$ decreases very slowly | $\alpha$ too small | Increase $\alpha$ slightly |
| $J$ oscillates and decreases | $\alpha$ borderline | Try $\alpha = 0.003, 0.01, 0.03, 0.1$ |
| $J$ stays constant | Local minimum (rare for linear) | Rescale features |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Linear Regression Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Collect Training Data] --> B[Initialize Parameters theta0, theta1]
    B --> C[For each iteration until convergence]
    C --> D[Compute Hypothesis hx = theta0 + theta1 times x]
    D --> E[Compute Error: hx minus y for all examples]
    E --> F[Compute Gradients: partial J by partial theta0 and partial J by partial theta1]
    F --> G[Update theta0 and theta1 simultaneously]
    G --> H{J converged below epsilon}
    H -- No --> C
    H -- Yes --> I[Optimal Parameters Found]
    I --> J[Predict yhat for new x using final theta0 and theta1]
    J --> K[END: Deployment Ready]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Gradient Descent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        X[Input Feature x]
        Y[Target y]
    end
    subgraph MODEL_BLOCK
        H[Hypothesis Module: hx equals theta0 plus theta1 x]
    end
    subgraph LOSS_BLOCK
        E[Error Calculator: difference between hx and y]
        S[Squarer: error squared]
        SUM[Summation over m examples]
        AVG[Average by 1 over m]
    end
    subgraph OPT_BLOCK
        GRAD[Gradient Computer: partial derivatives]
        UPD[Parameter Updater: theta minus alpha times gradient]
    end
    X --> H
    Y --> E
    H --> E
    E --> S --> SUM --> AVG --> GRAD
    GRAD --> UPD
    UPD --> H
    AVG --> JOUT[Cost Value J]
    UPD --> THETAOUT[Final theta0 and theta1]
```

## 4.3 Cost Function Bowl Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: theta0 equals 0, theta1 equals 0, J equals large value] --> P2[Iterate using Gradient Descent]
    P2 --> P3[theta values move DOWN the bowl]
    P3 --> P4[Reaches Global Minimum at center of bowl]
    P4 --> P5[Optimal theta0 and theta1 obtained]
    P5 --> P6[Final Regression Line Fits Data]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** The "bowl" represents the cost surface $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$. Each iteration, gradient descent takes a step *downhill* — towards lower cost. The deepest point in the bowl is the **global minimum** (guaranteed for linear regression because $J$ is convex).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Linear Regression with one variable. Write its hypothesis representation and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

Linear regression with one variable (univariate linear regression) is a supervised machine learning algorithm that models the relationship between a single input feature $x$ and a continuous output $y$ by fitting a linear equation.

**Hypothesis:** $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$

- $h_\theta(x)$: predicted output for input $x$.
- $\theta_0$: y-intercept (bias) — prediction when $x = 0$.
- $\theta_1$: slope (weight) — change in prediction per unit change in $x$.
- $\theta_0, \theta_1$ are the **parameters** to be learned from training data.

**Example:** Predicting house price $y$ from area $x$: $h_\theta(x) = 50 + 0.5x$ means base price is 50 lakhs, with 0.5 lakhs added per square foot.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the cost function used in linear regression? Why is the squared error preferred over absolute error?**

**Model Answer:**

The cost function for linear regression (Mean Squared Error) is:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Why squared error?**
1. **Differentiable:** Squared error has a continuous, smooth derivative, essential for gradient descent. Absolute error is not differentiable at zero.
2. **Penalizes large errors more:** A 10-unit error contributes 100 to cost, but a 5-unit error contributes only 25.
3. **Convexity:** $J$ becomes a convex (bowl-shaped) function for linear models, guaranteeing a global minimum.
4. **MLE connection:** Minimizing MSE is equivalent to Maximum Likelihood Estimation under Gaussian noise assumption.

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Suppose you are given the following training data for predicting sales ($y$, in thousands) based on advertising spend ($x$, in lakhs):**

| $i$ | $x^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 |
| 3 | 3 | 5 |

**(a)** Write the hypothesis $h_\theta(x)$ and the cost function $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ for this problem. Initialize $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$, and $m = 3$. Perform **two iterations** of gradient descent and show all updates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the learning rate $\alpha$. What happens if $\alpha$ is too small or too large? Illustrate with a sketch of the cost function vs. iteration curve for three different $\alpha$ values. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a):**

**Hypothesis:** $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$

**Cost Function:**

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2 \times 3} \sum_{i=1}^{3} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Initial values:** $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$, $\alpha = 0.1$, $m = 3$.

---

**Iteration 1:**

Step 1 — Compute predictions:
$h_\theta(1) = 0 + 0 \cdot 1 = 0$
$h_\theta(2) = 0 + 0 \cdot 2 = 0$
$h_\theta(3) = 0 + 0 \cdot 3 = 0$

Step 2 — Compute errors $(h_\theta - y)$:
$0 - 2 = -2$
$0 - 3 = -3$
$0 - 5 = -5$

Step 3 — Compute gradients:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (-2) + (-3) + (-5) \right] = \frac{-10}{3} \approx -3.333$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (-2)(1) + (-3)(2) + (-5)(3) \right] = \frac{-23}{3} \approx -7.667$$

Step 4 — Update parameters:

$$\theta_0 = 0 - 0.1 \times (-3.333) = 0.3333$$

$$\theta_1 = 0 - 0.1 \times (-7.667) = 0.7667$$

**After Iteration 1:** $\theta_0 = 0.3333$, $\theta_1 = 0.7667$.

---

**Iteration 2:**

Step 1 — Compute predictions using *new* parameters:
$h_\theta(1) = 0.3333 + 0.7667 \cdot 1 = 1.1000$
$h_\theta(2) = 0.3333 + 0.7667 \cdot 2 = 1.8667$
$h_\theta(3) = 0.3333 + 0.7667 \cdot 3 = 2.6334$

Step 2 — Compute errors:
$1.1000 - 2 = -0.9000$
$1.8667 - 3 = -1.1333$
$2.6334 - 5 = -2.3666$

Step 3 — Compute gradients:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ -0.9000 - 1.1333 - 2.3666 \right] = \frac{-4.3999}{3} \approx -1.4666$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (-0.9000)(1) + (-1.1333)(2) + (-2.3666)(3) \right] = \frac{-10.6664}{3} \approx -3.5555$$

Step 4 — Update parameters:

$$\theta_0 = 0.3333 - 0.1 \times (-1.4666) = 0.4800$$

$$\theta_1 = 0.7667 - 0.1 \times (-3.5555) = 1.1222$$

**After Iteration 2:** $\theta_0 = 0.4800$, $\theta_1 = 1.1222$.

**Cost after Iteration 2** (decreasing confirms correct learning):

$$J \approx \frac{1}{6} \left[ (-0.90)^2 + (-1.13)^2 + (-2.37)^2 \right] \approx 1.45$$

(Started at much higher cost in iteration 0.)

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b):**

**Role of Learning Rate $\alpha$:**

The learning rate $\alpha$ controls the **size of the step** taken in the direction opposite to the gradient during each iteration of gradient descent.

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$$

- If $\alpha$ is **too small**, gradient descent makes tiny progress per iteration. It will eventually converge, but extremely slowly — wasting computational resources.
- If $\alpha$ is **too large**, the steps overshoot the minimum. The cost may oscillate or even diverge to infinity.

**Sketch — Cost vs. Iteration for three $\alpha$ values:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A_Small[Alpha too small]
        A1[Iteration 1: J high] --> A2[Iteration 100: J still high] --> A3[Iteration 10000: J near minimum]
    end
    subgraph B_Good[Alpha just right]
        B1[Iteration 1: J high] --> B2[Iteration 50: J drops fast] --> B3[Iteration 200: J converges]
    end
    subgraph C_Large[Alpha too large]
        C1[Iteration 1: J high] --> C2[Iteration 2: J oscillates] --> C3[Iteration 3: J diverges to infinity]
    end
```

| $\alpha$ | Behaviour | Convergence |
|---|---|---|
| $0.0001$ (too small) | Slow descent | Will converge eventually but takes long |
| $0.01$ – $0.1$ (good) | Smooth descent | Converges in moderate iterations ✅ |
| $1.0$ or higher (too large) | Oscillates / diverges | May never converge ❌ |

> **Practical advice:** Try $\alpha \in \{0.001, 0.003, 0.01, 0.03, 0.1\}$ and plot $J$ vs. iteration for each. Pick the largest $\alpha$ that still gives smooth, monotonic decrease.

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the gradient descent update rules for $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ starting from the cost function $J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$. Clearly show all differentiation steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the dataset: $(x, y) = (1, 1), (2, 3), (3, 3), (4, 4)$, compute the values of $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ using the **Normal Equation** (closed-form solution). Verify by computing the cost $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a):**

**Step 1 — Hypothesis:**

$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

**Step 2 — Cost Function:**

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\theta_0$:**

Using chain rule with $u = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_0}$$

Since $\frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_0} = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\theta_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_1}$$

Since $\frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_1} = x^{(i)}$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

**Step 5 — Update Rules:**

$$\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

Both updates are performed **simultaneously** using old parameter values.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b):**

**Data:** $(1,1), (2,3), (3,3), (4,4)$, $m = 4$.

**Step 1 — Construct design matrix $X$ and target vector $y$:**

Add bias column $x_0 = 1$:

$$X = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Normal Equation:** $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$

Compute $X^T X$:

$$X^T X = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute $X^T y$:

$$X^T y = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 11 \\ 30 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute inverse:

$$(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{4 \cdot 30 - 10 \cdot 10} \begin{pmatrix} 30 & -10 \\ -10 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{20} \begin{pmatrix} 30 & -10 \\ -10 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute $\theta$:

$$\theta = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 11 \\ 30 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 \cdot 11 - 0.5 \cdot 30 \\ -0.5 \cdot 11 + 0.2 \cdot 30 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 16.5 - 15 \\ -5.5 + 6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.5 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Result:** $\theta_0 = 1.5$, $\theta_1 = 0.5$.

**Step 3 — Verify by computing $J$:**

Predictions:
$h(1) = 1.5 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 2.0$
$h(2) = 1.5 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 2.5$
$h(3) = 1.5 + 0.5 \cdot 3 = 3.0$
$h(4) = 1.5 + 0.5 \cdot 4 = 3.5$

Errors: $(2-1, 2.5-3, 3-3, 3.5-4) = (1, -0.5, 0, -0.5)$.

$$J = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 4} \left[ 1^2 + (-0.5)^2 + 0^2 + (-0.5)^2 \right] = \frac{1}{8} \times 1.5 = 0.1875$$

**Final regression line:** $h_\theta(x) = 1.5 + 0.5 x$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Board Exams (Linear Regression Module):**
> 1. **Simultaneous update forgotten (–2 marks):** Students often update $\theta_0$ first, then use the *new* $\theta_0$ in the $\theta_1$ update. This is **wrong**. Always compute both gradients using *old* values, then assign.
> 2. **Missing $\frac{1}{2m}$ factor (–1 mark):** Writing $J$ as $\frac{1}{2} \sum (\cdot)$ instead of $\frac{1}{2m} \sum (\cdot)$. The $m$ in the denominator is mandatory for averaging.
> 3. **Confusing $\theta$ with $x$:** Writing the update as $\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha x$ instead of the proper partial derivative form.
> 4. **Forgetting units / numerical substitution:** In numerical gradient descent problems, examiners award partial credit only if every step is *shown explicitly*. Skipping arithmetic = losing marks.
> 5. **Saying "linear regression is for classification":** Instant loss of 2 marks. Linear regression is for **continuous** output. Period.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Linear regression with one variable** fits a straight line $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ to data.
- ✅ It is a **supervised learning** algorithm with **continuous output** (regression task).
- ✅ The **cost function** is the **Mean Squared Error**: $J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$.
- ✅ $J$ is a **convex function** in $(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ — gradient descent is guaranteed to find the global minimum.
- ✅ **Gradient descent** is iterative and uses the rule: $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$.
- ✅ **Simultaneous update** of $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ is mandatory.
- ✅ **Learning rate $\alpha$** must be chosen carefully: too small → slow; too large → diverge.
- ✅ **Normal Equation** $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ gives closed-form solution but is $O(n^3)$.
- ✅ For one variable: $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta(x) - y)$ and $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum (h_\theta(x) - y) \cdot x$.
- ✅ **Feature scaling** is not strictly needed for one variable but becomes critical in multivariate regression.
- ✅ **Convergence is automatic** — the gradient shrinks as we approach the minimum, so steps naturally become smaller.
- ✅ The **best fit line** minimizes the sum of squared vertical distances from data points.
- ✅ Linear regression is the **foundation** for polynomial regression, regularization (Ridge/Lasso), and neural networks.
- ✅ Always remember: **MSE is differentiable**, **MAE is not** at zero → MSE preferred for gradient-based optimization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Regression with Multiple Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear Regression with Multiple Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Multivariate (Multiple) Linear Regression** is a supervised learning algorithm that models the relationship between a scalar continuous response (dependent) variable $y$ and two or more predictor (independent) variables $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ by fitting a linear hypothesis to the observed data.

Mathematically, the hypothesis function for $n$ features is expressed as:

$$h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \theta_n x_n$$

where:
- $\theta_0$ is the **bias** term (intercept on the y-axis).
- $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$ are the **model parameters** (weights/coefficients).
- $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ are the **input features** (independent variables).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** The terminology *Multiple Variable Regression* is **synonymous** with *Multivariate Regression* in KTU examination papers. Always remember that *Multivariate* here refers to **multiple inputs**, NOT multiple outputs (that would be a separate problem class).

> [!NOTE]
> **Vectorised Form (Preferred in Exams):** For notational brevity, define $\mathbf{x} = (x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_n)^T$ with $x_0 = 1$, and $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\theta_0, \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n)^T$. Then the hypothesis becomes the compact inner product: $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **real estate pricing expert** trying to estimate the price of a house. The price is not determined by just one factor — it depends on:
1. The **area** of the house (square feet).
2. The **number of bedrooms**.
3. The **age** of the house.
4. The **distance** from the city centre.

Each of these factors contributes a *weighted* amount to the final price. **Multiple Linear Regression** is precisely the mathematical tool that learns the optimal weights (importance) for each factor so that the predicted price matches actual market prices as closely as possible.

Think of it as: 
- **Features ($x_i$)** = ingredients in a recipe.
- **Parameters ($\theta_i$)** = amount of each ingredient to use.
- **Predicted output ($h_{\theta}(x)$)** = the final dish.
- **Cost function** = how far your dish is from the ideal taste.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Linear"?** The linearity refers to the **parameters** $\theta_i$, not the features. You can include non-linear terms like $x_1^2$ or $x_1 x_2$ and the model is *still* called linear regression!

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Examination Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range/Value |
|---|---|---|
| Number of training examples | $m$ | $m \geq 2$ |
| Number of features | $n$ | $n \geq 2$ (for multivariate) |
| Learning rate | $\alpha$ | $0.0001$ to $1$ (tuned) |
| Convergence tolerance | $\epsilon$ | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hypothesis Function (Expanded Form)

For $n$ features and $m$ training examples, the $i^{th}$ training instance is denoted as:
$$\mathbf{x}^{(i)} = (x_1^{(i)}, x_2^{(i)}, \ldots, x_n^{(i)})$$

with corresponding observed output $y^{(i)}$. The hypothesis prediction for the $i^{th}$ example is:

$$h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = \theta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j x_j^{(i)}$$

## 2.2 The Cost Function (Mean Squared Error)

The cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ quantifies the **average squared deviation** between predicted and actual values across all $m$ training samples:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

### Why the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor?
- The constant $\frac{1}{2}$ is purely a **mathematical convenience** — it cancels out the power-of-2 derivative when computing the gradient, making the update rule cleaner.
- Removing it does **not** change the optimal $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, since it is a positive scaling factor.

## 2.3 Gradient Descent Algorithm (Multivariate)

**Goal:** Find $\boldsymbol{\theta}^* = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.

**Update Rule (applied simultaneously for $j = 0, 1, \ldots, n$):**

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Expanded Partial Derivative:**

$$\frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

where $x_0^{(i)} = 1$ for all $i$ (for the bias update).

**Final Vectorised Gradient Descent Step:**

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simultaneous Update Rule:** KTU examiners *frequently* test whether students perform the parameter update *simultaneously* (i.e., compute all new $\theta_j$ using the **old** values) versus *sequentially*. **Always use simultaneous updates** to avoid incorrect convergence.

## 2.4 Feature Scaling & Mean Normalisation

When features have vastly different scales (e.g., $x_1 = $ area in $ft^2 \in [500, 5000]$ and $x_2 = $ bedrooms $\in [1, 5]$), gradient descent becomes extremely slow because the cost contours form elongated ellipses.

### Mean Normalisation Formula:

$$x_j := \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{s_j}$$

where:
- $\mu_j = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_j^{(i)}$ is the **mean** of feature $j$.
- $s_j$ is either the **range** ($x_{j,\max} - x_{j,\min}$) or the **standard deviation** of feature $j$.

The transformed features will lie approximately in the range $[-1, 1]$ or $[-0.5, 0.5]$.

## 2.5 Learning Rate ($\alpha$) — Diagnostic Strategy

| Symptom Observed | Probable Cause | Correction |
|---|---|---|
| $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ **increases** at each iteration | $\alpha$ is too large | Reduce $\alpha$ by factor of 3 |
| $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ **decreases slowly** | $\alpha$ is too small | Increase $\alpha$ moderately |
| $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ **oscillates** but decreases | $\alpha$ marginally large | Try $\alpha = 0.001, 0.003, 0.01, 0.03, \ldots$ |
| $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ **converges** smoothly | $\alpha$ is appropriate | **Stop tuning** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A classic 3-mark question asks you to **debug** a non-converging gradient descent. Memorise this table — it is a *guaranteed* scoring opportunity.

## 2.6 Polynomial Regression (Brief Overview)

To capture non-linear trends, we can introduce **polynomial features**:
- For a single feature $x$, use $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x + \theta_2 x^2 + \theta_3 x^3$.
- For a feature $x$ representing land area, using $x^2$ allows modelling of **quadratic growth** in price.

> [!NOTE]
> Polynomial regression is **still** a linear regression problem because it is **linear in the parameters** $\theta_j$. The non-linearity is in the *features*, not the weights.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hypothesis (multivariate) | $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j x_j$ | Vectorised: $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$ |
| 2 | Cost Function (MSE) | $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 3 | Gradient (partial) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$ | Apply for $j=0,1,\ldots,n$ |
| 4 | Update Rule | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | **Simultaneous** update mandatory |
| 5 | Mean Normalisation | $x_j := \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{s_j}$ | $s_j$ = range or std. dev. |
| 6 | Convergence Criterion | $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t}) - J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t-1}) < \epsilon$ | $\epsilon \approx 10^{-3}$ |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete compressive strength from cement, water, aggregate ratios.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Forecasting power consumption from temperature, humidity, time-of-day.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Estimating blood pressure from age, BMI, cholesterol levels.
- **Economics:** Modelling GDP from unemployment, inflation, interest rates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient for Multiple Variables

### Step 1 — Define the Cost Function
The cost function is the average of squared residuals:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Hypothesis

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \theta_2 x_2^{(i)} + \ldots + \theta_n x_n^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\theta_j$

Using the chain rule $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \cdot (h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot \frac{\partial h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(x^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_j}$.

Now, $\frac{\partial h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(x^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}$ because the hypothesis is linear in $\theta_j$.

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

### Step 4 — Formulate the Gradient Descent Update

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem Statement:** Given the following 3 training examples with 2 features:

| $i$ | $x_1^{(i)}$ (Area, $1000 \text{ ft}^2$) | $x_2^{(i)}$ (Bedrooms) | $y^{(i)}$ (Price, in $1000) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 220 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 340 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 2 | 250 |

Initial parameters: $\theta_0 = 50$, $\theta_1 = 80$, $\theta_2 = 50$. Learning rate: $\alpha = 0.01$.

**Step 1: Compute Predictions**

$$h_{\theta}(x^{(1)}) = 50 + 80(1) + 50(2) = 230$$
$$h_{\theta}(x^{(2)}) = 50 + 80(2) + 50(3) = 360$$
$$h_{\theta}(x^{(3)}) = 50 + 80(1.5) + 50(2) = 270$$

**Step 2: Compute Errors $(h - y)$**

$$e_1 = 230 - 220 = 10$$
$$e_2 = 360 - 340 = 20$$
$$e_3 = 270 - 250 = 20$$

**Step 3: Compute Gradients**

For $\theta_0$ (with $x_0^{(i)} = 1$):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (10)(1) + (20)(1) + (20)(1) \right] = \frac{50}{3} \approx 16.67$$

For $\theta_1$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (10)(1) + (20)(2) + (20)(1.5) \right] = \frac{80}{3} \approx 26.67$$

For $\theta_2$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_2} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ (10)(2) + (20)(3) + (20)(2) \right] = \frac{120}{3} = 40$$

**Step 4: Perform Simultaneous Updates**

$$\theta_0^{\text{new}} = 50 - 0.01 \times 16.67 = 50 - 0.1667 = 49.8333$$

$$\theta_1^{\text{new}} = 80 - 0.01 \times 26.67 = 80 - 0.2667 = 79.7333$$

$$\theta_2^{\text{new}} = 50 - 0.01 \times 40 = 50 - 0.40 = 49.60$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The parameter updates are **negative** because the residuals are positive (predictions are higher than actuals), which is the correct direction of correction.

## 3.3 Vectorised Matrix Derivation (for advanced problems)

For $m$ training examples stacked as a matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ with the bias column of 1s, and $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$ as the target vector:

The cost function in matrix form:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

The gradient in matrix form:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m} \mathbf{X}^T (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU may not always ask for matrix derivation, but understanding this form prepares you for **higher-order** questions and the **Normal Equation** method (covered in module extensions).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multivariate Hypothesis Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Feature x1] --> M1[Multiply by theta1]
    B[Input Feature x2] --> M2[Multiply by theta2]
    C[Input Feature xn] --> Mn[Multiply by thetan]
    D[Bias theta0] --> S[Summation Node]
    M1 --> S
    M2 --> S
    Mn --> S
    S --> P[Predicted Output h_theta_x]
    P --> Cmp[Compare with y_actual]
    Cmp --> E[Error e = h - y]
    E --> J[Cost Function J_theta]
    J --> GD[Gradient Descent Updater]
    GD -.Update weights.-> D
    GD -.Update weights.-> M1
    GD -.Update weights.-> M2
    GD -.Update weights.-> Mn
```

## 4.2 Gradient Descent Convergence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Initialize theta0, theta1, ..., thetan]) --> InitJ[Compute Initial Cost J_theta]
    InitJ --> LoopStart{Convergence Check: |delta J| less than epsilon?}
    LoopStart -- No --> ComputeGrad[Compute Partial Derivatives dJ/dtheta_j for all j]
    ComputeGrad --> Update[Simultaneously Update theta_j = theta_j - alpha * dJ/dtheta_j]
    Update --> Recalc[Recalculate J_theta]
    Recalc --> LoopStart
    LoopStart -- Yes --> Output[Output Optimal theta parameters]
    Output --> Stop([End])
```

## 4.3 Feature Scaling Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Do features have vastly different scales?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Apply[Apply Mean Normalisation: x_j = x_j - mu_j divided by s_j]
    Q1 -- No --> Skip[Skip scaling, proceed with gradient descent]
    Apply --> Verify{Transformed features in range -1 to 1?}
    Verify -- Yes --> Done([Ready for Gradient Descent])
    Verify -- No --> Adjust[Adjust scaling strategy: use max-min or standard deviation]
    Adjust --> Done
    Skip --> Done
```

## 4.4 Learning Rate Diagnostic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Run Gradient Descent] --> B{Monitor J_theta vs Iterations}
    B -- J increases --> C[alpha is TOO LARGE: reduce by factor of 3]
    B -- J oscillates then decreases --> D[alpha marginally large: try 0.001, 0.003, 0.01]
    B -- J decreases slowly --> E[alpha is TOO SMALL: increase moderately]
    B -- J decreases monotonically and plateaus --> F[alpha is OPTIMAL: convergence achieved]
    C --> A
    D --> A
    E --> A
    F --> G[Stop: Theta_star found]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These Mermaid diagrams are **functional block schematics** — they are designed to render reliably across all KTU-compliant learning management systems (e.g., Moodle, Google Classroom) and to clearly communicate the algorithmic *flow* rather than physical geometry.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between univariate and multivariate linear regression. Write the hypothesis function for a multivariate linear regression model with $n$ features.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
- **Univariate Linear Regression (1 mark):** Uses a *single* independent feature $x$ to predict the dependent variable $y$. The hypothesis is $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$.
- **Multivariate Linear Regression (1 mark):** Uses *two or more* independent features $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ to predict $y$. It captures the combined effect of multiple predictors.
- **Hypothesis Function (1 mark):** 
$$h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \theta_n x_n = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$$
where $\mathbf{x}$ includes the bias term $x_0 = 1$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the need for feature scaling in multivariate linear regression. State the mean normalisation formula.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
- **Need for Feature Scaling (2 marks):** When input features differ significantly in their numerical ranges (e.g., area in $ft^2$ ranges 500–5000, while bedrooms range 1–5), the cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ forms elongated elliptical contours. Gradient descent then takes a zig-zag path and converges very slowly. Feature scaling transforms features to a comparable range, producing nearly circular contours and faster convergence.
- **Mean Normalisation Formula (1 mark):** 
$$x_j := \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{s_j}$$
where $\mu_j$ is the mean and $s_j$ is the range (or standard deviation) of feature $j$.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **🅰️ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the cost function for multivariate linear regression. Derive the gradient descent update rule for $\theta_j$ when there are $n$ features and $m$ training examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Given 4 training examples with 2 features, perform **one iteration** of gradient descent and show the parameter updates. Initialise $\theta_0 = 0, \theta_1 = 0, \theta_2 = 0$, and use $\alpha = 0.1$. **[7 Marks]**

| $i$ | $x_1^{(i)}$ | $x_2^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| 3 | 1 | 2 | 2.5 |
| 4 | 2 | 3 | 4 |

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition of Cost Function (2 marks):** The cost function for multivariate linear regression is the Mean Squared Error (MSE):

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

where $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = \theta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j x_j^{(i)}$.

**Derivation of Gradient Descent Update (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Differentiate the cost function with respect to $\theta_j$ using the chain rule:
