$$\frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_j}$$

Step 2 — Since $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ is linear in $\theta_j$, $\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}$.

Step 3 — The gradient simplifies to:

$$\frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

Step 4 — The gradient descent update rule is applied **simultaneously** for $j = 0, 1, \ldots, n$:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

where $x_0^{(i)} = 1$ for the bias term.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Initial Predictions (1.5 marks)** — With $\theta_0 = \theta_1 = \theta_2 = 0$:

$$h_{\theta}(x^{(1)}) = h_{\theta}(x^{(2)}) = h_{\theta}(x^{(3)}) = h_{\theta}(x^{(4)}) = 0$$

**Step 2: Compute Errors (1 mark):**
$e_1 = 0 - 2 = -2$, $e_2 = 0 - 3 = -3$, $e_3 = 0 - 2.5 = -2.5$, $e_4 = 0 - 4 = -4$

**Step 3: Compute Gradients (2.5 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{4} \left[ (-2)(1) + (-3)(1) + (-2.5)(1) + (-4)(1) \right] = \frac{-11.5}{4} = -2.875$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{4} \left[ (-2)(1) + (-3)(2) + (-2.5)(1) + (-4)(2) \right] = \frac{-19.5}{4} = -4.875$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_2} = \frac{1}{4} \left[ (-2)(1) + (-3)(1) + (-2.5)(2) + (-4)(3) \right] = \frac{-20}{4} = -5.0$$

**Step 4: Apply Updates Simultaneously (2 marks):**

$$\theta_0^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1 \times (-2.875) = 0.2875$$

$$\theta_1^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1 \times (-4.875) = 0.4875$$

$$\theta_2^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1 \times (-5.0) = 0.5$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Sequential vs Simultaneous Updates:** Many students update $\theta_0$ first and use the *new* value to update $\theta_1$. This is **WRONG**. The correct method is to compute all gradients using the *old* values, then update all parameters *at once*. Loss of 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{m}$ factor:** Some students omit the average or use $\frac{1}{2m}$ incorrectly. Ensure the formula is $\frac{1}{m} \sum e_i x_j^{(i)}$.
> 3. **Sign error in updates:** A common mistake is writing $\theta_j := \theta_j + \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ (should be MINUS). Cost is *minimised*, not maximised.

---

### **🅱️ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **gradient descent algorithm** for multivariate linear regression. List the four important considerations for ensuring **proper convergence**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A dataset has 2 features: $x_1$ ranges from 10 to 100, and $x_2$ ranges from 1 to 5. Apply **mean normalisation** to the following data point: $x_1 = 60$, $x_2 = 3$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Gradient Descent Algorithm (3.5 marks):** 
Gradient descent is an iterative optimisation algorithm used to find the parameter values $\theta_0, \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n$ that minimise the cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. Starting from an initial guess, it updates the parameters in the **direction of steepest descent** (negative gradient) at each step, scaled by a learning rate $\alpha$:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_j} \quad \text{for } j = 0, 1, \ldots, n$$

This process repeats until $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ converges (i.e., change in $J$ per iteration falls below a threshold $\epsilon$).

**Four Important Considerations for Proper Convergence (3.5 marks):**

1. **Feature Scaling (1 mark):** Normalise features to comparable ranges (e.g., $[-1, 1]$) so that the cost function contours are circular, allowing gradient descent to converge in fewer iterations.

2. **Learning Rate Tuning (1 mark):** A small $\alpha$ leads to slow convergence; a large $\alpha$ may cause divergence or oscillation. Use a debug strategy: try $\alpha = 0.001, 0.003, 0.01, 0.03, 0.1, 0.3, 1$ and plot $J$ vs iterations.

3. **Simultaneous Update (0.5 marks):** All parameter updates must be computed using the *old* values and then applied *at the same time*. Sequential updates break convergence guarantees.

4. **Convergence Verification (1 mark):** Declare convergence when $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ decreases by less than $\epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-3}$) per iteration, OR plot $J$ vs iterations and check for a flat plateau. Automatic convergence tests may fail near local minima, so visual inspection is the gold standard.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Compute Mean of Each Feature (2 marks):**

The dataset has $x_1$ ranging from 10 to 100, so assuming uniform distribution or using the midpoint:
$$\mu_1 = \frac{10 + 100}{2} = 55$$

For $x_2$ ranging from 1 to 5:
$$\mu_2 = \frac{1 + 5}{2} = 3$$

(If the data is given as an explicit list, the actual sample mean should be used; here, we use the range midpoint for demonstration.)

**Step 2: Compute the Range $s_j$ of Each Feature (2 marks):**

$$s_1 = x_{1,\max} - x_{1,\min} = 100 - 10 = 90$$

$$s_2 = x_{2,\max} - x_{2,\min} = 5 - 1 = 4$$

**Step 3: Apply Mean Normalisation (3 marks):**

Using the formula $x_j := \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{s_j}$:

For $x_1 = 60$:
$$x_1^{\text{norm}} = \frac{60 - 55}{90} = \frac{5}{90} \approx 0.0556$$

For $x_2 = 3$:
$$x_2^{\text{norm}} = \frac{3 - 3}{4} = 0$$

**Result:** The normalised data point is $(0.0556, 0)$, which lies comfortably in the range $[-1, 1]$ — ideal for gradient descent.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Confusing $s_j$:** Some students confuse $s_j$ as the *standard deviation* in formula form, but the question usually expects either range or standard deviation — always check the wording. If unspecified, mention *both* options.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract $\mu_j$:** A common error is to divide by $s_j$ without centering by the mean, which is *not* mean normalisation (it is *rescaling*).
> 3. **Not specifying the bias term $x_0$:** In some exam questions, students forget that $x_0 = 1$ is *not* normalised.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Multivariate Linear Regression** generalises simple linear regression to $n \geq 2$ features; the hypothesis is $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j x_j$.
- ✅ **Cost Function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$** is the **Mean Squared Error** scaled by $\frac{1}{2m}$ for mathematical convenience.
- ✅ **Gradient Descent** iteratively updates $\theta_j$ in the direction opposite to the gradient, scaled by $\alpha$.
- ✅ **Simultaneous Updates are Mandatory** — compute all new $\theta_j$ values from the *old* values, then apply them together.
- ✅ **Feature Scaling** (mean normalisation or min-max scaling) is critical when features have different units or scales.
- ✅ **Learning Rate Tuning** follows a debug pattern: $\alpha = 0.001, 0.003, 0.01, 0.03, 0.1, \ldots$
- ✅ **Convergence Check** uses a tolerance $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-3}$) on the cost function change per iteration.
- ✅ **Polynomial Features** (e.g., $x^2, x^3, \sqrt{x}$) are allowed; the model remains **linear in the parameters** $\theta_j$.
- ✅ The **bias term $\theta_0$** corresponds to $x_0 = 1$ and is **never** normalised.
- ✅ For numerical problems, KTU expects the **full working** — hypothesis, errors, gradients, then updates — in that exact order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Day Checklist:** Carry the formula sheet from Section 2.6 into your memory — KTU's most common mistake is forgetting the negative sign in the gradient descent update. Practise **at least one** numerical iteration of multivariate gradient descent before the exam!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Methods for Classification - Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear Methods for Classification - Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Logistic Regression** is a **supervised learning** algorithm used for **binary classification** tasks. Despite the name "regression," it is a *classification* method that models the *probability* that a given input belongs to a particular category using a **logistic (sigmoid) function** applied to a linear combination of input features.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, logistic regression belongs to the **Generalized Linear Models (GLM)** family, specifically the case where the dependent variable follows a **Bernoulli distribution** and the link function is the **logit link**.

The model is formally expressed as:

$$
P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x}}}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the feature vector
- $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the parameter (weight) vector
- $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the **sigmoid (logistic) function**
- $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x})$ is the hypothesis function outputting a probability

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Logistic regression is categorized as a **Linear Discriminant** (a *linear method for classification*) because the **decision boundary** $ \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x} = 0 $ is *linear in the input space* $ \mathbb{R}^{n} $.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a doctor deciding whether a patient has a disease (Yes/No). The doctor does not just look at one factor (say, blood sugar level) — she weighs *multiple factors* (age, weight, blood sugar, family history). She then asks: *"What is the **probability** that this patient has the disease?"*
>
> If the weighted sum of risk factors crosses a **threshold** (say, 50%), the doctor classifies the patient as "Diseased" (label = 1), otherwise "Healthy" (label = 0).
>
> **Logistic Regression does exactly this:**
> 1. Takes a **linear combination** of features: $ z = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_n x_n $
> 2. **Squashes** it through the sigmoid to convert it to a probability in [0, 1].
> 3. Applies a **decision rule** (threshold = 0.5 by default).

**Other real-world applications** (frequently asked in KTU exams):
- **Spam email detection** (spam = 1, not spam = 0)
- **Credit card fraud** detection
- **Customer churn** prediction
- **Medical diagnosis** (malignant/benign tumor)
- **Click-through rate (CTR)** prediction in advertising

## 1.3 Why Not Use Linear Regression for Classification?

This is a **classic KTU 2-mark question**. The key reasons are:

| Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|---|---|
| Outputs **continuous** values (unbounded) | Outputs **probability** in $[0, 1]$ |
| Cost function: **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | Cost function: **Log-Loss (Cross-Entropy)** |
| Sensitive to outliers | More robust for classification |
| Predictions can be $>1$ or $<0$ | Predictions always bounded in $[0,1]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Logistic regression is the *natural extension* of linear regression to classification problems by **transforming the output through a non-linear sigmoid** while keeping the *decision boundary linear* in feature space.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sigmoid (Logistic) Function — The Heart of Logistic Regression

The **sigmoid function** maps any real-valued number to the open interval $(0, 1)$, making it ideal for representing probabilities.

### 2.1.1 Mathematical Definition

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

where $ z = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x} $.

### 2.1.2 Key Properties (High-Yield for KTU)

- **Range:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ — strictly bounded
- **Output at 0:** $\sigma(0) = 0.5$ — natural decision threshold
- **Monotonic:** Strictly increasing everywhere
- **Symmetry:** $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$
- **Derivative (the most important property):** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\left[1 - \sigma(z)\right]$
- **Limits:** $\lim_{z \to +\infty} \sigma(z) = 1$ and $\lim_{z \to -\infty} \sigma(z) = 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The clean form of the sigmoid derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ is why logistic regression is computationally elegant — the gradient calculation simplifies dramatically.

## 2.2 The Odds Ratio and Logit Transformation

### 2.2.1 Odds

The **odds** of an event represent the ratio of the probability of the event happening to it not happening:

$$
\text{Odds} = \frac{p}{1 - p}
$$

where $ p = P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) $.

### 2.2.2 Logit (Log-Odds) — The Link Function

Taking the natural logarithm of the odds gives the **logit function**:

$$
\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)
$$

In logistic regression, we model the logit as a **linear function** of the input features:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important?** This is what makes logistic regression a member of the **Generalized Linear Model (GLM)** family. The *logit* is the **canonical link function** for the Bernoulli distribution. The KTU examiner often tests this GLM connection.

## 2.3 The Cost Function (Log-Loss / Cross-Entropy)

If we used the standard **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**, the cost function in logistic regression would become **non-convex** (because sigmoid is non-linear), causing gradient descent to get stuck in local minima.

To fix this, we use the **Log-Loss** (also called **Binary Cross-Entropy**):

### 2.2.3 Cost for a Single Training Example

$$
\text{Cost}(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}), y) = \begin{cases} -\log\!\big(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x})\big) & \text{if } y = 1 \\ -\log\!\big(1 - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x})\big) & \text{if } y = 0 \end{cases}
$$

### 2.2.4 Compact Form (Sigmoid in One Expression)

$$
\text{Cost}(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}), y) = -y \log\!\big(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x})\big) - (1-y)\log\!\big(1 - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x})\big)
$$

### 2.2.5 The Full Cost Function (Average over $m$ examples)

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log\!\big(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\big) + \big(1-y^{(i)}\big)\log\!\big(1 - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\big) \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is convex** in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ for logistic regression — this is the **key reason** for using log-loss. Gradient descent is guaranteed to find the global minimum.

## 2.4 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) Connection

The cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ above is derived directly from the principle of **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**. The likelihood of the dataset under the model is:

$$
L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \big(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\big)^{y^{(i)}} \big(1 - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})\big)^{1-y^{(i)}}
$$

Taking the log-likelihood and negating it recovers the cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$.

## 2.5 Gradient Descent Update Rule

The partial derivative of $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ with respect to $\theta_j$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \big(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big) \, x_j^{(i)}
$$

The **update rule** is:

$$
\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \big(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big) \, x_j^{(i)}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Surprising fact:** This update rule has *exactly the same form* as linear regression! Only $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x})$ differs — it is the sigmoid instead of the identity function. KTU examiners love this comparison question.

## 2.6 The Decision Boundary

The decision boundary is the locus of points where the model is *equally uncertain* between the two classes, i.e., $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = 0.5$. Since $\sigma(z) = 0.5 \iff z = 0$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x} = 0
$$

This is a **hyperplane** in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ — *linear* in the input space.

- If $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x} \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ predict class **1**
- If $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x} < 0 \Rightarrow$ predict class **0**

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hypothesis | $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x})$ | Probability in $(0,1)$ |
| 2 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Range $(0,1)$ |
| 3 | Sigmoid Derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)[1 - \sigma(z)]$ | Critical for gradient |
| 4 | Logit (Log-Odds) | $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right)$ | Unbounded output $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | Odds | $\dfrac{p}{1-p}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Cost (Log-Loss) | $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\!\left[y^{(i)}\log h + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h)\right]$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | Likelihood | $L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod h^{y}(1-h)^{1-y}$ | Probabilistic model |
| 8 | Gradient (component) | $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum(h - y)x_j$ | Vectorizable |
| 9 | GD Update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Iterative |
| 10 | Decision Boundary | $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x} = 0$ | Linear hyperplane |
| 11 | Decision Rule | $y_{pred} = \begin{cases} 1 & h \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & h < 0.5 \end{cases}$ | Default threshold 0.5 |
| 12 | Convergence | $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)}) - J(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)}) \vert < \varepsilon$ | Use $\vert$ not $\mid$ |

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

Logistic regression is the **workhorse** of industry for high-stakes binary classification because:
- It outputs **calibrated probabilities**, not just hard labels.
- Coefficients are **interpretable** as log-odds contributions.
- It is **computationally efficient** (closed-form for small data, scalable to millions of rows).
- It is the **building block** for neural networks (a single neuron with sigmoid activation is logistic regression).
- Used in **medical risk scoring**, **credit underwriting**, and **A/B test analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoid Function from Odds

**Goal:** Start from the requirement that $0 < p < 1$ and derive the sigmoid form.

**Step 1 — Define the odds.** The odds of an event occurring are:

$$
\text{odds} = \frac{p}{1-p}, \quad \text{where } 0 < p < 1
$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm (logit).** We want to model the *log-odds* as a linear function of features:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = z = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$
\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{z}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p$:**

$$
p = (1-p)\,e^{z}
$$

$$
p = e^{z} - p\,e^{z}
$$

$$
p + p\,e^{z} = e^{z}
$$

$$
p(1 + e^{z}) = e^{z}
$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $p$:**

$$
p = \frac{e^{z}}{1 + e^{z}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

This is the **sigmoid function**, derived from first principles. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Sigmoid Derivative

This derivation is **essential** for understanding the gradient update.

**Step 1 — Write the definition of the derivative:**

$$
\sigma'(z) = \frac{d}{dz}\!\left[\frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the quotient rule or rewrite.** Rewriting using $u = 1 + e^{-z}$:

$$
\sigma(z) = u^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Chain rule:**

$$
\sigma'(z) = -u^{-2} \cdot \frac{du}{dz} = -\frac{1}{(1+e^{-z})^2} \cdot (-e^{-z})
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$
\sigma'(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1+e^{-z})^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of $\sigma(z)$ itself.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{z}$:

$$
\sigma'(z) = \frac{1}{e^{z}(1+e^{-z})^2} = \frac{1}{(e^{z}+1)(1+e^{-z})}
$$

Using the identity $(e^z + 1)(1 + e^{-z}) = \dfrac{e^z + 1}{e^z}\cdot e^z\cdot(1+e^{-z})/1$ ... cleaner approach — rewrite as:

$$
\sigma'(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}}
$$

Recognize $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ and $1 - \sigma(z) = \dfrac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}}$, therefore:

$$
\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\left[1 - \sigma(z)\right]
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gradient of $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ for Logistic Regression

This is a **core KTU derivation** that appears almost every exam cycle.

**Step 1 — Recall the cost for a single example.** Define for a single example $(x, y)$:

$$
\text{Cost}(h, y) = -y\log(h) - (1-y)\log(1-h)
$$

where $h = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x})$.

**Step 2 — Take partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_j$:**

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}\text{Cost}(h, y) = -y \cdot \frac{1}{h}\cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j} - (1-y)\cdot \frac{1}{1-h}\cdot \left(-\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\partial h / \partial \theta_j$ using the chain rule and the sigmoid derivative:**

$$
\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial \sigma(z)}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial \theta_j} = \sigma(z)\big[1-\sigma(z)\big] \cdot x_j = h(1-h)x_j
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the cost derivative:**

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}\text{Cost}(h,y) = -y \cdot \frac{h(1-h)x_j}{h} + (1-y)\cdot \frac{h(1-h)x_j}{1-h}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}\text{Cost}(h,y) = -y(1-h)x_j + (1-y)h\,x_j
$$

**Step 6 — Factor:**

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}\text{Cost}(h,y) = \big[-y + yh + h - yh\big]x_j = (h - y)x_j
$$

**Step 7 — Average over the dataset to get the full gradient:**

$$
\frac{\partial J(\boldsymbol{\theta})}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\big(h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\big)x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 8 — Vectorized form:**

$$
\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m}\,\mathbf{X}^{T}\big(\sigma(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta}) - \mathbf{y}\big)
$$

where $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the design matrix. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Given 3 training examples with one feature $x$ and a fixed bias term $x_0 = 1$, fit logistic regression by one step of gradient descent.

| $i$ | $x_1^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 1 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 1 |

Initial parameters: $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Compute $h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$ initially.** With $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{0}$, we have $z = 0$ for all examples, so $h = \sigma(0) = 0.5$ for every example.

**Step 2 — Compute gradient for $\theta_0$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(0.5 - 0)\cdot 1 + (0.5 - 1)\cdot 1 + (0.5 - 1)\cdot 1\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{3}\left[0.5 - 0.5 - 0.5\right] = \frac{-0.5}{3} \approx -0.1667
$$

**Step 3 — Compute gradient for $\theta_1$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(0.5 - 0)(0.5) + (0.5 - 1)(2.0) + (0.5 - 1)(1.5)\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{3}\left[0.25 - 1.0 - 0.75\right] = \frac{-1.5}{3} = -0.5
$$

**Step 4 — Update parameters:**

$$
\theta_0 := 0 - 0.1 \cdot (-0.1667) = 0.0167
$$

$$
\theta_1 := 0 - 0.1 \cdot (-0.5) = 0.05
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.** After one iteration, $\theta_1 = 0.05 > 0$, meaning the model has correctly learned that *higher* $x_1$ values are associated with class 1 — consistent with the data. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Variant | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Two classes, linear boundary | **Binary Logistic Regression** | Default, simplest |
| More than 2 classes, mutually exclusive | **Multinomial (Softmax) Regression** | Generalization of logistic |
| Classes not mutually exclusive | **One-vs-Rest Logistic Regression** | Train $K$ binary classifiers |
| Data is imbalanced | **Class-weighted Logistic Regression** | Penalize majority class errors |
| Features highly correlated | **Regularized Logistic Regression (L1/L2)** | Prevent coefficient explosion |
| Need probabilities + speed | **Logistic Regression with SGD** | Online learning, large data |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logistic Regression — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block captures the **end-to-end pipeline** of logistic regression: from raw data input through parameter learning to classification output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Data X Feature Matrix m x n] --> B[Add Bias Term x0 equals 1]
    B --> C[Linear Combination z equals theta transpose x]
    C --> D[Sigmoid Function sigma of z]
    D --> E[Probability Output p in open interval 0 1]
    E --> F{Decision Threshold 0.5}
    F -- p greater than or equal 0.5 --> G[Predicted Class 1 Positive]
    F -- p less than 0.5 --> H[Predicted Class 0 Negative]
    I[Initial Parameters theta equals 0] --> J[Forward Pass Compute h of x]
    J --> K[Compute Log Loss Cost J of theta]
    K --> L[Compute Gradient via Partial Derivatives]
    L --> M[Update theta using Gradient Descent]
    M --> N{Converged Check J change less than epsilon}
    N -- No --> J
    N -- Yes --> O[Final Trained Model]
    O --> P[Deploy for Prediction]
    P --> E
```

## 4.2 Training Loop — Gradient Descent Cycle

The following diagram isolates the **inner training loop** to highlight the iterative optimization process, which is a frequent KTU 2-mark question topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    init1[Initialize theta 0] --> fwd[Forward Pass h equals sigma of X theta]
    fwd --> loss[Compute Cost J]
    loss --> grad[Compute Gradient]
    grad --> upd[Update theta with learning rate alpha]
    upd --> chk{Converged}
    chk -- No --> fwd
    chk -- Yes --> out[Trained Model]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Logistic Regression as a Generalized Linear Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Input Features x] --> lin[Linear Predictor eta equals theta transpose x]
    lin --> link[Link Function logit inverse]
    link --> prob[Probability p in 0 1]
    prob --> bern[Bernoulli Outcome y in 0 1]
    style lin fill:#E8F0FE
    style link fill:#FFF4E5
    style prob fill:#E6F4EA
    style bern fill:#FCE8E6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *linear predictor* $\eta = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{x}$ lives in $\mathbb{R}$. The *link function* (logit) is applied to $p$ in $(0,1)$ to map it back to $\mathbb{R}$. The *inverse link* (sigmoid) is applied to $\eta$ to map it to a probability. This is the GLM structure the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests.

## 4.4 Conceptual Decision-Boundary Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph feat [Feature Space R2]
        c1[Class 1 Region theta transpose x greater than 0]
        c0[Class 0 Region theta transpose x less than 0]
    end
    c1 --- bound[Linear Decision Boundary theta1 x1 plus theta2 x2 plus theta0 equals 0]
    bound --- c0
    style bound fill:#FFE082
    style c1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style c0 fill:#FFCDD2
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain why the sigmoid function is used in logistic regression. State two of its key properties.**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ is used in logistic regression for the following reasons:

1. **Probability Mapping:** It maps any real-valued input $z = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}$ to a value in the open interval $(0, 1)$, which can be interpreted as a probability $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})$. (1 mark)

2. **Smooth & Differentiable:** It is smooth, continuous, and differentiable everywhere, which makes gradient-based optimization feasible. Its derivative has the elegant form $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)[1 - \sigma(z)]$. (1 mark)

3. **Two Key Properties:**
   - **Symmetry:** $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$
   - **Monotonicity:** Strictly increasing, and $\sigma(0) = 0.5$ (midpoint). (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is Mean Squared Error (MSE) not used as a cost function for logistic regression? What is used instead?**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

MSE is **not used** in logistic regression because the cost function becomes **non-convex** when the sigmoid (a non-linear function) is composed with the squared error. (1 mark)

A non-convex cost function causes **gradient descent to potentially get stuck in local minima**, missing the global optimum. (1 mark)

Instead, logistic regression uses the **Log-Loss / Binary Cross-Entropy** cost function:

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1 - h_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}))\right]
$$

This function is **convex** in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, guaranteeing convergence to the global minimum. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT forget the bias term** $x_0 = 1$ — failing to include it is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Sign of the gradient update:** write the rule explicitly as $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$. Many students drop the minus sign, costing 1 mark.
> 3. **Distinguish between odds and log-odds** — these are *not* the same. Odds are in $[0, \infty)$; log-odds are in $(-\infty, \infty)$.
> 4. **Always state the convexity property** when justifying log-loss over MSE — it is the *key reason* and examiners award 1 mark for it.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the sigmoid function from the log-odds formulation, and hence obtain its derivative. Show that the derivative can be written in the form $\sigma(z)[1 - \sigma(z)]$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary classification problem with $m = 4$ training samples given in the table below, perform **one iteration of gradient descent** with learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$ and initial parameters $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$. State the final parameter values after this single update. **(7 marks)**

| $i$ | $x_1^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | 0 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 0 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 1 |
| 4 | 4.0 | 1 |

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Start with the log-odds (logit) assumption:**

We assume the log-odds of the probability $p = P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x})$ is a *linear* function of features:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = z = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$
\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{z}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Solve for $p$:**

$$
p(1 + e^{z}) = e^{z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \frac{e^{z}}{1 + e^{z}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \sigma(z)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Write the derivative from first principles:**

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\!\left[\frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}\right] = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Rewrite numerator and denominator cleverly.** Multiply top and bottom by $e^{z}$:

$$
\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{1}{e^{z}(1+e^{-z})^2} \cdot e^{z} = \frac{1}{e^{z}+1} \cdot \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Recognize the two factors:**

The first factor is $\dfrac{1}{1+e^z} = 1 - \sigma(z)$, and the second is $\sigma(z)$ itself. Therefore:

$$
\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\big[1 - \sigma(z)\big]
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Concluding remark:** This compact form is what makes gradient computation in logistic regression so elegant and computationally efficient.

#### Part (b) — One-Step Gradient Descent (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — With $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (0, 0)$, compute $h$ for each example.** Since $z = 0$ for all, $h = \sigma(0) = 0.5$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute $\partial J / \partial \theta_0$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{4}(h - y^{(i)})\cdot 1 = \frac{1}{4}\big[(0.5-0)+(0.5-0)+(0.5-1)+(0.5-1)\big]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{4}\big[0.5 + 0.5 - 0.5 - 0.5\big] = \frac{0}{4} = 0
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute $\partial J / \partial \theta_1$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{4}(h - y^{(i)})x_1^{(i)}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{4}\big[(0.5-0)(1.0) + (0.5-0)(2.0) + (0.5-1)(3.0) + (0.5-1)(4.0)\big]
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{4}\big[0.5 + 1.0 - 1.5 - 2.0\big] = \frac{-2.0}{4} = -0.5
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Apply the update rule for $\theta_0$:**

$$
\theta_0^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1 \cdot 0 = 0
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Apply the update rule for $\theta_1$:**

$$
\theta_1^{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1 \cdot (-0.5) = 0.05
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Final answer and interpretation:**

After one iteration, the parameters are $\theta_0 = 0$ and $\theta_1 = 0.05$. Since $\theta_1 > 0$, the model has correctly learned that higher $x_1$ values push predictions towards class 1 — consistent with the data trend.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Verification of one example's new prediction:** For $x_1 = 4$ (class 1), new $z = 0 + 0.05(4) = 0.2$, so $h = \sigma(0.2) \approx 0.55$ — slightly above 0.5, correctly predicting class 1. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define the **logit** function. Starting from the principle of **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**, derive the **log-loss / binary cross-entropy cost function** for logistic regression. Clearly state the final form of $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ and explain why it is **convex**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose you trained a logistic regression model on a dataset with two features and obtained the parameters $\theta_0 = -3$, $\theta_1 = 1$, $\theta_2 = 1$. **(7 marks)**
- (i) Write the equation of the decision boundary and identify the class of any point $(x_1, x_2) = (4, 4)$.
- (ii) Calculate the predicted probability for the point $(2, 1)$. Round to 3 decimal places.
- (iii) If the threshold is raised from 0.5 to 0.7, will the point $(2, 1)$ still be classified as class 1? Justify with a calculation.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Logit & MLE Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Definition of Logit.** The logit function is the natural logarithm of the odds:

$$
\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right), \quad 0 < p < 1
$$

It maps $(0, 1) \to \mathbb{R}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Set up the likelihood.** Assuming the $m$ training examples are i.i.d., the likelihood of the dataset is:

$$
L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \boldsymbol{\theta})
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Express each term using the model.** For a single example:

$$
P(y \mid \mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}) = h^{y}(1-h)^{1-y}, \quad \text{where } h = \sigma(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Write the full likelihood and apply log:**

$$
\log L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h^{(i)})\right]
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Define cost as the negative average log-likelihood (MLE → minimization):**

$$
J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m}\log L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-h^{(i)})\right]
$$

This is the **log-loss / binary cross-entropy**.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Convexity argument.** $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is convex in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ because:
- Each term $-y\log(h) - (1-y)\log(1-h)$ is convex in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (composition of the convex log function with the sigmoid under linear arguments, combined with the fact that the negative log-likelihood of an exponential family is always convex).
- The sum of convex functions is convex.
- The Hessian $\nabla^2 J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{D}\mathbf{X}$ is positive semi-definite (since $\mathbf{D}$ is a positive diagonal matrix of variances).

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Consequence.** Gradient descent on $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is guaranteed to converge to the *global* minimum, not just a local one.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Application (7 marks)

**(i) Decision boundary and class of $(4, 4)$ (2 marks):**

The decision boundary is $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{x} = 0$:

$$
-3 + x_1 + x_2 = 0
$$

For $(4, 4)$: $z = -3 + 4 + 4 = 5 > 0$, so the model predicts **Class 1** with $h = \sigma(5) \approx 0.9933$.

**(ii) Predicted probability at $(2, 1)$ (2 marks):**

$$
z = -3 + 2 + 1 = 0
$$

$$
h = \sigma(0) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0}} = 0.5
$$

So the predicted probability is **0.500**.

**(iii) Effect of raising threshold to 0.7 (3 marks):**

The probability at $(2, 1)$ is still $h = 0.5$ (the model is fixed; only the decision rule changes). Since $0.5 < 0.7$, the point $(2, 1)$ will **no longer** be classified as Class 1 — it will be classified as **Class 0**. The change in threshold reduces the sensitivity (recall) of the classifier because it now requires stronger evidence to predict the positive class.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- Logistic Regression is a **classification** algorithm, not a regression algorithm (despite the name).
- The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ squashes the linear output to a probability in $(0, 1)$.
- **Logit** = log-odds = $\ln(p / (1-p))$; **inverse** of sigmoid.
- Logistic regression is a **GLM** with the **logit link** and **Bernoulli** distribution.
- The **cost function** is **log-loss / binary cross-entropy**, derived from MLE — it is **convex**.
- The **gradient descent update** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum (h - y) x_j$ has the **same form as linear regression** — only $h$ differs.
- The **decision boundary** $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{x} = 0$ is a **linear hyperplane** in the input space.
- The default **classification threshold is 0.5**; changing it trades off precision vs. recall.
- Logistic regression can be **regularized** (L1 → sparsity, L2 → small weights) for high-dimensional data.
- For **multiclass** problems, use **Softmax Regression** (multinomial logistic regression) as a direct generalization.
- **Strengths:** interpretable, fast, probabilistic, no tuning of learning rate is *too* critical.
- **Weaknesses:** cannot capture non-linear boundaries (use feature engineering, kernel tricks, or switch to decision trees/neural networks).
- Key constants: learning rate $\alpha > 0$; convergence tolerance $\varepsilon > 0$; threshold $\tau \in (0, 1)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naive Bayes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Naive Bayes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Naive Bayes** is a family of **probabilistic supervised machine learning classifiers** constructed by directly applying **Bayes' Theorem** with the strong (and deliberately oversimplified) assumption of **conditional independence** between every pair of features given the class label.

Formally, for a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and a class variable $C \in \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$, the Naive Bayes classifier applies the following decision rule:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

This rule is called the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) hypothesis** because it picks the class $c$ that maximizes the posterior probability $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to (i) state Bayes' Theorem, (ii) explain the meaning of *prior*, *likelihood*, *evidence*, and *posterior*, (iii) state and justify the **conditional independence assumption**, and (iv) compute class probabilities for unseen test instances by hand — a frequent Part-B question.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Honest but Naive Doctor":**
> Imagine a doctor who must decide whether a patient has **Flu** or **Allergy**. She sees three symptoms: *fever*, *sneezing*, *body-ache*. A perfectly rigorous doctor would need a **joint probability table** of all 2³ = 8 symptom combinations for each disease. But our **Naive Bayes doctor** says: *"Each symptom independently casts its own vote, and I'll just multiply the votes."* This is mathematically naive (symptoms are usually correlated — fever and body-ache often occur together), but it works surprisingly well in practice because classification only needs the **ratio of votes**, and the correlations largely cancel out.

The term **"Naive"** refers to this simplifying assumption that the features contribute independently to the outcome, even though real-world features are almost never truly independent.

---

## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Bayesian Reasoning

To fully understand Naive Bayes, you must internalize the four key probability components that appear in Bayes' Theorem. KTU examiners frequently test these definitions directly (2-mark or 3-mark Part-A questions).

| Component | Notation | Plain-English Meaning |
|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| **Prior Probability** | $P(C)$ | How likely the class is *before* seeing any feature evidence. Computed as the class frequency in the training set. |
| **Likelihood** | $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C)$ | How likely the observed feature vector is, *given* a particular class. |
| **Evidence (Marginal)** | $P(\mathbf{x})$ | Overall probability of observing that feature vector, irrespective of class. Acts as a normalizer. |
| **Posterior Probability** | $P(C \mid \mathbf{x})$ | How likely the class is *after* seeing the evidence $\mathbf{x}$. This is what we actually want. |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "evidence" $P(\mathbf{x})$ is dropped during classification** because it is constant for every class hypothesis $c$ on a given test instance. Hence, the MAP rule compares only $P(c) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid c)$ across all classes and picks the largest — this is the source of the famous proportionality: **Posterior ∝ Prior × Likelihood**.

---

## 1.4 Why is Naive Bayes Important in Engineering?

* **Speed & Scalability:** Training is a single pass over the data (counting). Prediction involves only a few multiplications. It scales linearly with the number of instances and features.
* **Low Data Requirement:** Performs remarkably well even with modest training sets, making it a baseline for text classification, spam filtering, sentiment analysis, and medical diagnosis.
* **Handles High-Dimensional Data:** Excels in problems like document classification where feature counts (vocabulary size) can run into tens of thousands.
* **Interpretability:** The output is a clean probability distribution over classes — easy to explain to non-technical stakeholders.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation — Bayes' Theorem

Bayes' Theorem is the single most important equation in this module. It relates the conditional probabilities of two random events $A$ and $B$ and is derived directly from the definition of conditional probability.

**Derivation Recap (Read in conjunction with Section 3 for full expansion):**

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)} = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

Equating the two expressions for $P(A \cap B)$ and solving for $P(A \mid B)$ yields Bayes' Theorem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayes' Theorem (Canonical Form):**
> $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$
> In the ML context, $A$ is replaced by class $C$ and $B$ by the feature vector $\mathbf{x}$, giving:
> $$P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

---

## 2.2 The Naive Independence Assumption

Direct computation of the joint likelihood $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C)$ is **intractable** for any non-trivial $n$ because the number of possible feature combinations grows exponentially (the **curse of dimensionality**). Naive Bayes rescues us by assuming:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$$

In words: *given the class label, all features are mutually independent.*

**Why this assumption is "Naive":**
Consider the document classification problem of detecting spam. The words *"free"* and *"offer"* are highly correlated — they tend to appear together. A truly Bayesian classifier would model this correlation. Naive Bayes ignores it. Yet empirically, Naive Bayes often outperforms more sophisticated classifiers on text data because we only care about the *argmax* of the posterior, not its exact value, and miscounted dependencies often affect all classes in roughly equal proportion.

---

## 2.3 The Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Decision Rule

For a test instance $\mathbf{x}^*$, the predicted class is:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i^* \mid c)$$

The $\arg\max$ operator returns the value of $c$ that maximizes the expression. Since the evidence $P(\mathbf{x}^*)$ does not depend on $c$, it is dropped from the maximization (it is a positive constant for all $c$).

**Numerical Stability via Log-Probabilities:**
Multiplying many small probabilities (e.g., $0.001 \times 0.0001 \times \ldots$) causes **arithmetic underflow** in floating-point hardware. The standard fix is to convert products to sums by taking the natural logarithm (a monotonic transformation that preserves the argmax):

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i^* \mid c) \right]$$

---

## 2.4 The Zero-Frequency Problem & Laplace Smoothing

A critical failure mode: if a categorical feature value $x_i = v$ never appears in the training examples for class $c$, then $P(x_i = v \mid c) = 0$. Because the likelihood is a product, this single zero **annihilates the entire posterior** for class $c$, regardless of how strong the other evidence is.

**Solution — Laplace (Additive) Smoothing:**
Add a small pseudo-count $\alpha \geq 0$ (typically $\alpha = 1$, called "plus-one smoothing") to every count:

$$P(x_i = v \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i = v, c) + \alpha}{N_c + \alpha \cdot k}$$

where:
* $N_c$ = total number of training instances belonging to class $c$,
* $k$ = number of distinct possible values that feature $x_i$ can take.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Naive Bayes powers the spam filter in your email inbox (Multinomial NB on bag-of-words), the "Not Spam" probability shown by Gmail, real-time sentiment dashboards (Vader, TextBlob libraries use NB behind the scenes), and even early medical-diagnosis decision support systems. Its combination of O(n) training, O(n) prediction, and probabilistic output makes it the **go-to baseline** that every ML engineer must understand before trying complex models.

---

## 2.5 Variants of Naive Bayes (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Variant | Data Type | Likelihood Assumption | Typical Use Case |
|---------|-----------|------------------------|------------------|
| **Gaussian NB** | Continuous | $P(x_i \mid c) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_c, \sigma_c^2)$ | Iris dataset, sensor data, any real-valued features |
| **Multinomial NB** | Discrete counts (integer) | Multinomial distribution | Text classification, bag-of-words document models |
| **Bernoulli NB** | Binary (0/1) | Bernoulli distribution | Word presence/absence in short documents, bag-of-words with binary features |

For Gaussian NB, the per-feature likelihood is:

$$P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_c^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu_c)^2}{2 \sigma_c^2} \right)$$

where $\mu_c$ and $\sigma_c^2$ are the sample mean and variance of feature $x_i$ computed on training instances belonging to class $c$.

---

## 2.6 Complete KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Memorize This Table**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Meaning / Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Foundation of the algorithm |
| 2 | Posterior Proportionality | $P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(C) P(\mathbf{x} \mid C)$ | Drops the constant $P(\mathbf{x})$ for classification |
| 3 | Naive Independence | $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$ | Core simplifying assumption |
| 4 | MAP Decision Rule | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid c)$ | Predicted class |
| 5 | Log-MAP Rule | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(c) + \sum_{i} \log P(x_i \mid c) \right]$ | Numerically stable prediction |
| 6 | Prior from Data | $P(c) = \dfrac{N_c}{N}$ | Relative frequency of class $c$ |
| 7 | MLE Likelihood | $P(x_i = v \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(v, c)}{N_c}$ | Maximum-likelihood estimate |
| 8 | Laplace Smoothing | $P(x_i = v \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(v, c) + \alpha}{N_c + \alpha k}$ | Avoids zero-frequency problem |
| 9 | Gaussian NB Likelihood | $P(x_i \mid c) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_c^2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x_i-\mu_c)^2}{2\sigma_c^2}\right)$ | Continuous features |
| 10 | Log-Odds Decision | $\log \dfrac{P(c_1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(c_2 \mid \mathbf{x})} = \log \dfrac{P(c_1)}{P(c_2)} + \sum_i \log \dfrac{P(x_i \mid c_1)}{P(x_i \mid c_2)}$ | Binary classification threshold |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides fully expanded, board-style derivations. Every algebraic step is written out, with a one-line textual explanation of the rule applied. The worked numerical example at the end is the **canonical "Play Tennis" dataset** that KTU examiners have used in past internal assessment papers.

---

## 3.1 Full Derivation of Bayes' Theorem

**Step 1: Definition of Conditional Probability**
The probability of event $A$ given that event $B$ has occurred is defined as the joint probability of both events divided by the marginal probability of $B$.

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

*Explanation:* This is the standard definition. It says: among all the times $B$ occurs, how often does $A$ also occur?

**Step 2: Symmetric Form of Conditional Probability**
Swapping the roles of $A$ and $B$ in the same definition gives:

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}$$

*Explanation:* Since the intersection operation is commutative, $A \cap B = B \cap A$. So the numerator of both equations is identical.

**Step 3: Isolate the Joint Probability**
Solving the second equation for the joint probability $P(A \cap B)$:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

*Explanation:* We rearrange the second equation by multiplying both sides by $P(A)$.

**Step 4: Substitute Back into Step 1**
Insert the expression from Step 3 into the numerator of Step 1:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

*Explanation:* This is the celebrated Bayes' Theorem. It allows us to "invert" the conditioning — finding $P(A \mid B)$ from $P(B \mid A)$, which is often easier to estimate from data.

**Step 5: Extend to Multi-Class Classification (ML Form)**
Replace $A$ with class variable $C$ and $B$ with the feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$:

$$P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

*Explanation:* The set of all possible classes $\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$ partitions the sample space, so the law of total probability gives the marginal evidence:

$$P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} P(\mathbf{x} \mid c_j) P(c_j)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Naive Bayes Classifier

**Step 1: Start with the Posterior**
For a class $c$ and feature vector $\mathbf{x}$, we want $P(c \mid \mathbf{x})$. By Bayes' Theorem:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

*Explanation:* Direct application of Bayes' Theorem.

**Step 2: Drop the Denominator**
Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ does not depend on $c$ for a fixed test instance, it is a positive constant across all classes. We can equivalently maximize only the numerator (proportionality):

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) \cdot P(c)$$

*Explanation:* This is the **MAP trick** — we only need relative magnitudes, not absolute probabilities, to pick the winning class.

**Step 3: Apply the Chain Rule of Probability**
Without any assumption, the joint likelihood expands via the chain rule:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = P(x_1 \mid c) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, c) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, c) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, c)$$

*Explanation:* Each successive term conditions on all previous features. This is mathematically exact but requires exponentially growing data to estimate reliably.

**Step 4: Impose Conditional Independence (The "Naive" Step)**
Assert that every feature $x_i$ is independent of every other feature $x_j$ given the class $c$. Mathematically:

$$P(x_i \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, c) = P(x_i \mid c) \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$

Substituting this into Step 3:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$$

*Explanation:* The conditional chain collapses to a product of single-feature likelihoods. This is the defining structural assumption of Naive Bayes.

**Step 5: Form the Final Classifier**

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)}$$

*Explanation:* Predict the class with the largest posterior — equivalently, the largest (prior × likelihood-product) since the evidence is constant.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example — The "Play Tennis" Dataset

This is the most commonly tested numerical problem in KTU Module-1 internal exams. You must be able to reproduce these calculations by hand.

**Training Data (14 days):**

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play Tennis |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|-------------|
| 1   | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak | No          |
| 2   | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No       |
| 3   | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak | Yes         |
| 4   | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak | Yes         |
| 5   | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes         |
| 6   | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No       |
| 7   | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes      |
| 8   | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak | No          |
| 9   | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes         |
| 10  | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak | Yes         |
| 11  | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes      |
| 12  | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes      |
| 13  | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak | Yes         |
| 14  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No       |

**Class Counts:** Yes = 9, No = 5, Total $N = 14$.

**Test Instance to Classify:**
$\mathbf{x}^* = (\text{Outlook} = \text{Sunny}, \text{Temperature} = \text{Cool}, \text{Humidity} = \text{High}, \text{Wind} = \text{Strong})$

---

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Compute the Priors

$$P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{9}{14} \approx 0.6429$$

$$P(\text{No}) = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.3571$$

*Explanation:* Priors are just the relative frequencies of each class in the training set.

---

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Compute Each Likelihood by Counting

**Outlook = Sunny:**
Counted from the table: 2 Yes-days have Sunny, 3 No-days have Sunny.

$$P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2}{9} \approx 0.2222$$

$$P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6000$$

**Temperature = Cool:**
Counted from the table: 3 Yes-days have Cool, 1 No-day has Cool.

$$P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3}{9} \approx 0.3333$$

$$P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2000$$

**Humidity = High:**
Counted from the table: 3 Yes-days have High humidity, 4 No-days have High humidity.

$$P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3}{9} \approx 0.3333$$

$$P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8000$$

**Wind = Strong:**
Counted from the table: 3 Yes-days have Strong wind, 3 No-days have Strong wind.

$$P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{3}{9} \approx 0.3333$$

$$P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6000$$

---

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Compute Unnormalized Posteriors (MAP)

Apply the Naive Bayes formula $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{4} P(x_i \mid c)$.

**For class Yes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}^*) &\propto P(\text{Yes}) \times P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) \times P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{Yes}) \times P(\text{High} \mid \text{Yes}) \times P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) \\
&= \frac{9}{14} \times \frac{2}{9} \times \frac{3}{9} \times \frac{3}{9} \times \frac{3}{9} \\
&= \frac{9 \times 2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3}{14 \times 9 \times 9 \times 9 \times 9} \\
&= \frac{486}{91854} \\
&\approx 0.00529
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Numerator is the product of the prior and all four likelihoods for class Yes. The denominator is the product of all denominators.

**For class No:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}^*) &\propto P(\text{No}) \times P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) \times P(\text{Cool} \mid \text{No}) \times P(\text{High} \mid \text{No}) \times P(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) \\
&= \frac{5}{14} \times \frac{3}{5} \times \frac{1}{5} \times \frac{4}{5} \times \frac{3}{5} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 3 \times 1 \times 4 \times 3}{14 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5} \\
&= \frac{180}{87500} \\
&\approx 0.00206
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Same procedure for the No class.

---

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Normalize to Obtain True Probabilities

$$P(\mathbf{x}^*) = P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}^*) + P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}^*)$$

Using the unnormalized values:

$$P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{0.00529}{0.00529 + 0.00206} = \frac{0.00529}{0.00735} \approx 0.720$$

$$P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{0.00206}{0.00735} \approx 0.280$$

*Explanation:* Division by the sum normalizes the unnormalized scores into a valid probability distribution (sums to 1).

---

### 3.3.5 Step 5 — Make the Final Prediction

Since $P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = 0.720 > P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = 0.280$:

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = \text{Yes} \quad \text{(Play Tennis)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Pitfall:** Many students, on first calculation, get $P(\text{No})$ larger than $P(\text{Yes})$ because the *unnormalized* numbers can be deceptive. **Always normalize** (or at least confirm which is larger after normalization) before announcing the prediction. KTU examiners explicitly check for this.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Laplace Smoothing Demonstration

**Setup:** Suppose in a spam classifier trained on 100 emails, the word *"lottery"* appears in 30 spam emails and 0 ham (legitimate) emails. The vocabulary size for that feature column is $k = 2$ (present / absent).

**Without smoothing:**

$$P(\text{lottery} = \text{present} \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{0}{50} = 0$$

This zero would kill the posterior for Ham whenever a ham email legitimately contains the word *"lottery"*.

**With Laplace smoothing ($\alpha = 1$):**

$$
P(\text{lottery} = \text{present} \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{0 + 1}{50 + 1 \times 2} = \frac{1}{52} \approx 0.0192
$$

*Explanation:* We add 1 to the numerator count and $1 \times k$ to the denominator. This guarantees the estimate is strictly positive and well-defined.

$$P(\text{lottery} = \text{present} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{30 + 1}{50 + 1 \times 2} = \frac{31}{52} \approx 0.5962$$

*Explanation:* Spam likelihood remains overwhelmingly high even after smoothing, demonstrating that the correction only matters when a count was zero or very small.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Naive Bayes Classification Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the end-to-end **inference pipeline** of a Naive Bayes classifier at test time. Each rectangular node represents a discrete computational stage; arrows indicate the direction of data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[INPUT FEATURE VECTOR X STAR] --> PRIOR[STAGE 1 COMPUTE PRIOR P C FROM TRAINING]
    INP --> LIK[STAGE 2 COMPUTE LIKELIHOODS P XI GIVEN C FOR EACH FEATURE]
    PRIOR --> PROD[STAGE 3 MULTIPLY PRIOR AND ALL LIKELIHOODS]
    LIK --> PROD
    PROD --> MAP[STAGE 4 APPLY MAP DECISION RULE ARGMAX]
    MAP --> OUT1[OUTPUT CLASS WITH HIGHEST POSTERIOR Y HAT]
    MAP --> OUT2[OUTPUT POSTERIOR PROBABILITY DISTRIBUTION OVER ALL CLASSES]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The test feature vector $\mathbf{x}^*$ enters from the left. The classifier simultaneously queries two pre-computed lookup tables from the training phase: the **prior table** and the **likelihood table**. These are combined via the Naive Bayes product formula, then the argmax operator selects the winning class. The two parallel output nodes show that Naive Bayes returns not just a label but a full probability distribution — a feature often exploited in downstream decision systems (e.g., setting custom decision thresholds for medical diagnosis).

---

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Relationship of the Four Probabilistic Quantities

This diagram emphasizes the **causal / logical relationship** between prior, likelihood, evidence, and posterior, which is the conceptual heart of Bayes' Theorem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P_C[PRIOR P C] --> POST[POSTERIOR P C GIVEN X]
    P_XC[LIKELIHOOD P X GIVEN C] --> POST
    P_X[EVIDENCE P X] --> POST
    POST --> DEC[MAP DECISION RULE]
    DEC --> CLASS[FINAL CLASS LABEL Y HAT]

    TRAIN[LABELED TRAINING DATA] --> P_C
    TRAIN --> P_XC
    TRAIN --> P_X
```

**Reading the diagram:** Training data feeds all three components (prior, likelihood, evidence). The evidence acts as a *normalizing denominator* that converts the (prior × likelihood) product into a proper probability. The posterior then drives the MAP decision rule, which produces the class label.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Gaussian Naive Bayes

For continuous-valued features, the likelihood lookup table is replaced by a **parametric density function** — the Normal (Gaussian) distribution — whose parameters ($\mu_c, \sigma_c^2$) are estimated per class from training data.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CONTINUOUS FEATURE VECTOR X] --> B[FOR EACH CLASS C]
    B --> C[ESTIMATE MEAN MU C FROM TRAINING SAMPLES OF CLASS C]
    B --> D[ESTIMATE VARIANCE SIGMA SQUARED C FROM TRAINING SAMPLES OF CLASS C]
    C --> E[EVALUATE GAUSSIAN PDF AT X I FOR EACH FEATURE I]
    D --> E
    E --> F[COMPUTE LOG LIKELIHOOD SUM OF LOG P XI GIVEN C]
    F --> G[ADD LOG PRIOR LOG P C]
    G --> H[ARGMAX OVER ALL CLASSES]
    H --> I[PREDICTED CLASS Y HAT]
```

**Reading the diagram:** During the training phase, we store the per-class mean and variance for every feature. At test time, we plug the observed continuous value into the Gaussian PDF, sum the log-likelihoods across features, add the log-prior, and pick the class with the maximum log-score. This is the algorithm used, for example, in the famous **Iris dataset** classification.

---

## 4.4 Independence-Graphical View (Intuition Aid)

Although we cannot draw a true probabilistic graphical model in pure Mermaid syntax, the following schematic conveys the **conditional independence structure** that defines Naive Bayes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CLS[CLASS VARIABLE C] --> F1[FEATURE X 1]
    CLS --> F2[FEATURE X 2]
    CLS --> F3[FEATURE X 3]
    CLS --> F4[FEATURE X 4]
    CLS --> F5[FEATURE X N]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The class $C$ is the **single parent** of all feature nodes. Crucially, there are **no edges between feature nodes** — this is the visual signature of conditional independence. In a more general Bayesian Network, the feature nodes would be connected to each other, leading to a much more complex joint distribution.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** the posterior before announcing the prediction. *Always divide each unnormalized score by the sum of all unnormalized scores and then compare.*
> 2. **Mixing up prior and likelihood** in the formula. *Prior = $P(C)$; Likelihood = $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C)$; Posterior = $P(C \mid \mathbf{x})$.* The arrows in Bayes' Theorem are not symmetric.
> 3. **Ignoring the zero-frequency problem** when a test feature value never appeared in training. *Mention Laplace smoothing explicitly for full credit.*
> 4. **Dropping $P(\mathbf{x})$ silently without justification.** *Always state "since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of $c$, it does not affect the argmax" to earn the conceptual mark.*
> 5. **Confusing the three Naive Bayes variants** (Gaussian / Multinomial / Bernoulli). *Be ready to identify which one applies based on the data type described in the question.*

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Bayes' Theorem. Explain the significance of the "naive" assumption in the Naive Bayes classifier.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Bayes' Theorem expresses the posterior probability of a hypothesis $C$ given observed evidence $\mathbf{x}$ in terms of the prior probability and the likelihood:

$$P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

where:
* $P(C)$ — prior probability of the class,
* $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C)$ — likelihood of observing $\mathbf{x}$ given class $C$,
* $P(\mathbf{x})$ — evidence (marginal probability of $\mathbf{x}$),
* $P(C \mid \mathbf{x})$ — posterior probability we want to compute.

**Naive Assumption (1 mark):** The "naive" assumption is that all features are **conditionally independent of each other given the class label**:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$$

This drastically simplifies the computation of the joint likelihood, making the algorithm tractable and extremely fast, at the cost of a strong simplifying assumption that is rarely strictly true in practice.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the zero-frequency problem in Naive Bayes classification? How is it solved?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Problem statement (1.5 marks):** During training, if a particular categorical feature value $x_i = v$ never appears in any training instance of class $c$, the maximum-likelihood estimate gives $P(x_i = v \mid c) = 0$. Because the Naive Bayes likelihood is a product of per-feature probabilities, this single zero annihilates the entire posterior for class $c$, making it impossible to recover regardless of the strength of other evidence. This is the **zero-frequency problem**.

**Solution (1.5 marks):** It is solved by **Laplace smoothing** (additive smoothing), which adds a small pseudo-count $\alpha$ (typically $\alpha = 1$) to every observed count:

$$P(x_i = v \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i = v, c) + \alpha}{N_c + \alpha \cdot k}$$

where $N_c$ is the total number of training instances of class $c$ and $k$ is the number of distinct values feature $x_i$ can take. This guarantees all conditional probabilities are strictly positive, preventing posterior annihilation.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**A). (a) Derive the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) hypothesis for the Naive Bayes classifier, clearly stating the role of the evidence term $P(\mathbf{x})$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem starting point (1 mark):**
For a class $c$ and feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) P(c)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 2 — Note that the evidence is constant across classes (2 marks):**
For a fixed test instance $\mathbf{x}^*$, the value of $P(\mathbf{x}^*)$ does not depend on $c$. Whether we are evaluating the posterior for $c_1$ or $c_2$, the denominator is the same number. Therefore, when we are only interested in **which class has the largest posterior** (not the exact probability values), we can drop the evidence term because it scales all candidates equally:

$$P(c \mid \mathbf{x}^*) \propto P(\mathbf{x}^* \mid c) P(c)$$

The symbol "$\propto$" means "proportional to".

**Step 3 — Apply the naive independence assumption (2 marks):**
Under the assumption that features are conditionally independent given $c$:

$$P(\mathbf{x}^* \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i^* \mid c)$$

**Step 4 — State the MAP hypothesis (2 marks):**
Combining the previous steps, the MAP hypothesis selects the class that maximizes the unnormalized posterior:

$$\hat{y}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \; P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i^* \mid c)$$

This is the canonical Naive Bayes classifier. The "argmax" notation means: return the value of $c$ that makes the bracketed expression as large as possible.

---

**(b) Consider the following training data for a binary classification task with classes $C_1$ and $C_2$ and two binary features $X_1, X_2 \in \{0, 1\}$. Use Naive Bayes to classify the test instance $\mathbf{x}^* = (X_1 = 1, X_2 = 0)$. (7 marks)**

| Instance | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | Class |
|----------|-------|-------|-------|
| 1        | 1     | 1     | $C_1$ |
| 2        | 1     | 0     | $C_1$ |
| 3        | 0     | 1     | $C_1$ |
| 4        | 1     | 1     | $C_1$ |
| 5        | 0     | 0     | $C_2$ |
| 6        | 1     | 0     | $C_2$ |
| 7        | 0     | 1     | $C_2$ |
| 8        | 0     | 0     | $C_2$ |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute priors from class frequencies (1 mark):**
$C_1$ appears in 4 of 8 instances; $C_2$ appears in 4 of 8 instances.

$$P(C_1) = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5, \quad P(C_2) = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute conditional likelihoods for $X_1$ (2 marks):**
Among the 4 $C_1$ instances: $X_1 = 1$ appears 3 times, $X_1 = 0$ appears 1 time.

$$P(X_1 = 1 \mid C_1) = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75, \quad P(X_1 = 0 \mid C_1) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

Among the 4 $C_2$ instances: $X_1 = 1$ appears 1 time, $X_1 = 0$ appears 3 times.

$$P(X_1 = 1 \mid C_2) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25, \quad P(X_1 = 0 \mid C_2) = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$$

**Step 3 — Compute conditional likelihoods for $X_2$ (2 marks):**
Among $C_1$: $X_2 = 1$ appears 2 times, $X_2 = 0$ appears 2 times.

$$P(X_2 = 1 \mid C_1) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5, \quad P(X_2 = 0 \mid C_1) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

Among $C_2$: $X_2 = 1$ appears 1 time, $X_2 = 0$ appears 3 times.

$$P(X_2 = 1 \mid C_2) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25, \quad P(X_2 = 0 \mid C_2) = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Naive Bayes formula to the test instance (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C_1 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) &\propto P(C_1) \cdot P(X_1 = 1 \mid C_1) \cdot P(X_2 = 0 \mid C_1) \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.75 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.1875
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C_2 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) &\propto P(C_2) \cdot P(X_1 = 1 \mid C_2) \cdot P(X_2 = 0 \mid C_2) \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.25 \times 0.75 \\
&= 0.0938
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize and predict (1 mark):**

$$P(C_1 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{0.1875}{0.1875 + 0.0938} = \frac{0.1875}{0.2813} \approx 0.667$$

$$P(C_2 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{0.0938}{0.2813} \approx 0.333$$

Since $P(C_1 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) > P(C_2 \mid \mathbf{x}^*)$:

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = C_1}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**B). (a) Explain three variants of the Naive Bayes classifier (Gaussian, Multinomial, Bernoulli). For each, state one practical application. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Variant 1 — Gaussian Naive Bayes (2 marks):**
* **Assumption:** Continuous features are assumed to follow a Normal (Gaussian) distribution within each class. The likelihood is parameterized by the per-class mean $\mu_c$ and variance $\sigma_c^2$:

$$P(x_i \mid c) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_c^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu_c)^2}{2\sigma_c^2} \right)$$

* **Parameter estimation:** $\mu_c$ and $\sigma_c^2$ are estimated by sample mean and sample variance over the training instances of class $c$.
* **Application:** Classification of the Iris flower dataset (petal length, sepal width are continuous), medical signal processing, sensor-based activity recognition.

**Variant 2 — Multinomial Naive Bayes (2.5 marks):**
* **Assumption:** Features represent **discrete counts** (typically word frequencies) and follow a Multinomial distribution. The likelihood is:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \frac{(\sum_i x_i)!}{\prod_i x_i!} \prod_{i} P(x_i \mid c)^{x_i}$$

* **Parameter estimation:** $P(x_i \mid c)$ is the relative frequency of feature $i$ in training documents of class $c$, typically with Laplace smoothing.
* **Application:** **Text classification / spam filtering** — features are word counts in an email body. This is the most common real-world deployment of Naive Bayes.

**Variant 3 — Bernoulli Naive Bayes (2.5 marks):**
* **Assumption:** Features are **binary** (0 or 1) and follow a Bernoulli distribution. The likelihood is:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid c) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)^{x_i} \left( 1 - P(x_i \mid c) \right)^{(1 - x_i)}$$

* **Parameter estimation:** $P(x_i \mid c)$ is the fraction of training documents of class $c$ in which feature $i$ is present.
* **Application:** **Sentiment classification of short documents / tweets** where the focus is on word *presence* rather than word *count*.

---

**(b) Using Laplace smoothing with $\alpha = 1$, recompute the predictions for the test instance in Question A(b) above, given that there is a hypothetical third feature $X_3 \in \{A, B\}$ where $X_3 = B$ never appears in any $C_2$ training instance. Assume the original 8 instances with $X_3$ values: $A, A, B, A, A, B, B, A$ for $C_1, C_1, C_1, C_1, C_2, C_2, C_2, C_2$ respectively. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original likelihood without smoothing (1 mark):**
For the test instance, if we were to evaluate $P(X_3 = B \mid C_2)$ without smoothing:

$$P(X_3 = B \mid C_2) = \frac{\text{count}(X_3 = B, C_2)}{N_{C_2}} = \frac{0}{4} = 0$$

This zero would make $P(C_2 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ regardless of the other features' evidence — the zero-frequency problem.

**Step 2 — Apply Laplace smoothing to $P(X_3 = B \mid C_2)$ (2 marks):**
Using $\alpha = 1$ and $k = 2$ (two possible values of $X_3$):

$$P_{\text{smooth}}(X_3 = B \mid C_2) = \frac{0 + 1}{4 + 1 \times 2} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$

*Explanation:* We added 1 to the numerator count (because $X_3 = B$ appeared 0 times in $C_2$) and $1 \times 2 = 2$ to the denominator (to compensate for the smoothed count over $k$ possible values).

**Step 3 — Smooth the remaining $X_3$ likelihoods (2 marks):**
For class $C_1$, $X_3 = B$ appeared once out of 4 instances:

$$P_{\text{smooth}}(X_3 = B \mid C_1) = \frac{1 + 1}{4 + 1 \times 2} = \frac{2}{6} \approx 0.3333$$

$$P_{\text{smooth}}(X_3 = A \mid C_1) = \frac{3 + 1}{4 + 1 \times 2} = \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.6667$$

$$P_{\text{smooth}}(X_3 = A \mid C_2) = \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 1 \times 2} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5000$$

*Explanation:* Same formula applied consistently to all $X_3$ conditional probabilities. The smoothing redistributes a small amount of probability mass from the observed categories to the unseen one.

**Step 4 — Recompute posteriors for the original test instance $\mathbf{x}^* = (X_1 = 1, X_2 = 0, X_3 = A)$ (1.5 marks):**
Reusing the unsmoothed values from Question A(b) for $X_1$ and $X_2$ (since they have no zero counts):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C_1 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) &\propto 0.5 \times 0.75 \times 0.5 \times 0.6667 \\
&= 0.125
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(C_2 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) &\propto 0.5 \times 0.25 \times 0.75 \times 0.5000 \\
&= 0.0469
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize and predict (0.5 marks):**

$$P(C_1 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{0.125}{0.125 + 0.0469} = \frac{0.125}{0.1719} \approx 0.727$$

$$P(C_2 \mid \mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{0.0469}{0.1719} \approx 0.273$$

The predicted class remains $\boxed{C_1}$. Note how the *prediction* did not change, but the **posterior probabilities are now well-defined and strictly positive** for both classes — which is the entire purpose of Laplace smoothing.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Naive Bayes**

* **Bayes' Theorem** is the foundation: $P(C \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C) P(C)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$.
* **Four components:** Prior $P(C)$, Likelihood $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C)$, Evidence $P(\mathbf{x})$, Posterior $P(C \mid \mathbf{x})$.
* **Naive assumption:** Features are conditionally independent given the class — this is the **only** structural assumption that distinguishes Naive Bayes from a full Bayesian classifier.
* **MAP decision rule:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$. The evidence $P(\mathbf{x})$ is dropped because it is constant across all classes for a fixed test instance.
* **Numerical stability:** Use log-probabilities — convert products to sums: $\log P(c) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid c)$.
* **Zero-frequency problem:** If a feature value never appears for a class, the maximum-likelihood estimate becomes zero, annihilating the posterior.
* **Laplace smoothing fix:** $P(x_i = v \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(v, c) + \alpha}{N_c + \alpha k}$ with $\alpha = 1$ as default.
* **Three variants:**
  * **Gaussian NB** → continuous features, uses Normal PDF.
  * **Multinomial NB** → discrete counts (text classification, bag-of-words).
  * **Bernoulli NB** → binary features (word presence/absence).
* **Algorithm complexity:** Training is $O(N \cdot n)$ (single pass of counting); prediction is $O(n \cdot k)$ (one product per class).
* **Strengths:** Fast, interpretable, works with small data, handles high dimensions, gives calibrated probabilities.
* **Weaknesses:** The independence assumption is often violated; hence probability estimates can be poor even when classification accuracy is high. Zero-frequency issue must be addressed.
* **Real-world deployments:** Spam filtering (Gmail, Yahoo Mail), sentiment analysis (Twitter opinion mining), document categorization (news article routing), medical diagnosis support, and recommendation-system pre-filters.
* **Common KTU exam trap:** Always **normalize** the posterior before predicting — examiners explicitly test whether students understand that MAP selects the class with the largest **probability**, not the largest unnormalized score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Tree Algorithm (ID3)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Decision Tree Algorithm (ID3)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Tree (DT):** A non-parametric, **supervised learning** algorithm used for both **classification** (ID3, C4.5, CART) and **regression**. The model is built by recursively partitioning the feature space into homogeneous sub-regions, represented as a rooted, directed tree where internal nodes test a feature, branches hold the test outcome, and leaf nodes emit a class label (or regression value).

> [!NOTE]
