| (0.25, 0.25) | 0.00 | (0.25+0.50)/2 = 0.375 | 0.0000 |
| (0.25, 0.25) | 0.00 | (0.50+0.75)/2 = 0.625 | 0.0000 |
| (0.25, 0.50) | 0.25 | (0.75+0.75)/2 = 0.75 | 0.1875 |
| (0.50, 0.50) | 0.00 | (0.75+1.00)/2 = 0.875 | 0.0000 |
| (0.50, 0.75) | 0.25 | (1.00+1.00)/2 = 1.00 | 0.2500 |
| (0.75, 1.00) | 0.25 | (1.00+1.00)/2 = 1.00 | 0.2500 |

$$\text{AUC} = 0.0625 + 0.1875 + 0.2500 + 0.2500 = 0.7500$$

**Interpretation:** AUC = 0.75 → The classifier has a 75% probability of assigning a higher score to a positive instance than a negative one — a **fair-to-good** discriminative model. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Avoid these to score full marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall:** A frequent mistake is to swap the denominators. Remember — Precision's denominator is *predicted positives* (TP+FP), Recall's denominator is *actual positives* (TP+FN).
> 2. **Forgetting the F1 formula:** Many students write the arithmetic mean of P and R, but KTU examiners specifically expect the **harmonic mean** form $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$.
> 3. **ROC axes confusion:** Students often mistakenly plot Precision on the y-axis of an ROC plot. The correct axes are **FPR (x-axis) vs TPR (y-axis)**.
> 4. **Skipping the FPR derivation in AUC problems:** When computing AUC via trapezoidal rule, you must explicitly write $FPR = FP/(FP+TN)$ at each step — don't just write numbers without the formula.
> 5. **Inaccuracy with imbalanced data:** If a question has 990 negatives and 10 positives, do **not** simply report Accuracy = 99%. The KTU marking scheme explicitly tests your awareness of the **Accuracy Paradox** and your ability to compute Precision/Recall/F1 instead.
> 6. **Unit Errors:** AUC has no unit, but it must always be in the range $[0, 1]$. If you compute AUC > 1, you have made an error in the trapezoidal calculation — check your TPR and FPR.
> 7. **Forgetting the (0,0) and (1,1) anchor points:** The ROC curve always starts at (0,0) and ends at (1,1). Missing these will lose 1 mark in an exam.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin this before the exam!):**

- ✅ The **Confusion Matrix** is the foundation of all classification metrics — every formula descends from TP, FP, FN, TN.
- ✅ **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP+TN}{Total}$ — simple but **misleading on imbalanced data**.
- ✅ **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — focus on *quality* of positive predictions.
- ✅ **Recall (TPR / Sensitivity)** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — focus on *coverage* of actual positives.
- ✅ **F1-Score** = $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ — the **harmonic mean** (NOT arithmetic), penalizes imbalanced P and R.
- ✅ **Specificity (TNR)** = $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ — focus on *correctly rejecting* negatives.
- ✅ **FPR (Fall-out)** = $1 - \text{Specificity} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ — the x-axis of the ROC plot.
- ✅ **ROC Curve** = plot of **TPR (y) vs FPR (x)** as the decision threshold sweeps from 1 → 0.
- ✅ **AUC** ∈ [0, 1]: 0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect; equals the probability of correct ranking.
- ✅ **AUC is threshold-independent**, while Accuracy/Precision/Recall are **threshold-dependent**.
- ✅ **Prefer Recall** when FN cost is high (medical, security, fraud).
- ✅ **Prefer Precision** when FP cost is high (spam, recommendations, legal blocks).
- ✅ The **F1-Score** is the standard single-number summary when both P and R matter equally.
- ✅ The **FPR = 1 − Specificity** identity must always hold (use it as a sanity check).
- ✅ For **multi-class problems**, compute metrics via *One-vs-Rest (OvR)* or *macro/micro averaging*.
- ✅ AUC estimation uses the **Trapezoidal Rule**: $AUC = \sum \frac{(FPR_{i+1}-FPR_i)(TPR_{i+1}+TPR_i)}{2}$.
- ✅ A perfect classifier's ROC passes through **(0, 1)**; a random classifier's ROC is the diagonal **y = x**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Performance Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regression Performance Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Regression Performance Measures** are quantitative metrics used to evaluate the accuracy, reliability, and goodness-of-fit of a regression model by measuring the deviation between the predicted outputs and the actual ground-truth observations. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCIT503 - Machine Learning and Data Analytics), these metrics form the foundational layer of **Model Evaluation** under **Supervised Learning** and are essential for hyperparameter tuning, model comparison, and ensuring that the learned function generalizes well to unseen data.

Let the regression model output predictions $\hat{y}_i$ for $n$ input instances with true values $y_i$. The **Error (Residual)** for the $i^{th}$ instance is formally defined as:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$$

The various performance measures aggregate these residuals using different mathematical norms to give a single scalar score representing model quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must understand that **no single metric is universally optimal**. MSE penalizes large errors heavily (quadratic), while MAE is robust to outliers (linear). Examiners frequently ask comparative analysis questions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are a **Weather Forecaster** predicting tomorrow's temperature for 5 consecutive days. Your predictions are off by small amounts — sometimes you predict 30°C when it is actually 32°C (off by 2), and sometimes you are bang-on. The *Regression Performance Measures* are simply different **ways of grading your forecasting skill**:

- **MAE** is like a teacher who simply sums up the absolute mistakes, treating all mistakes equally.
- **MSE** is like a strict teacher who squares each mistake — so a huge blunder (predicting 10°C when actual is 30°C) gets a massive penalty, making the teacher very upset.
- **RMSE** brings that "squared-penalty" back to the original temperature scale (degrees Celsius).
- **R-Squared** is like a report card score from 0 to 1 — telling you what fraction of the temperature variation you correctly explained.
- **MAPE** translates the error into a **percentage**, making it easy to interpret ("I am, on average, 5% off").

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Regression Evaluation

1. **Magnitude-based Metrics** — MAE, MSE, RMSE (focus on the size of the error)
2. **Variance-based Metrics** — R-Squared, Adjusted R-Squared (focus on the proportion of explained variance)
3. **Relative Metrics** — MAPE, RMSPE (focus on the error as a percentage of true values)

> [!NOTE]
> All these metrics assume a **lower-is-better** principle (except R-Squared, where higher is better). They are computed on the same scale as the target variable (except MAPE/R-Squared).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown of Each Metric

#### A. Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
- **Operational Logic:** Computes the average of the absolute deviations between predicted and actual values.
- **The "Why":** It uses the L1-norm, which is **insensitive to outliers** and gives equal weight to all errors.
- **The "How":** Takes the absolute value of each residual, sums them, and divides by $n$.
- **Real-world utility:** Used in forecasting domains (e.g., demand planning, energy load forecasting) where occasional large errors should not dominate the evaluation.

#### B. Mean Squared Error (MSE)
- **Operational Logic:** Squares each residual before averaging — this is the L2-norm squared.
- **The "Why":** Squaring penalizes large errors disproportionately, making it the **standard loss function** during model training (especially in gradient descent for Linear Regression).
- **The "How":** Errors grow quadratically with magnitude.
- **Real-world utility:** Useful in financial risk modelling and physics-based regression where large deviations are catastrophic.

#### C. Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
- **Operational Logic:** The square root of MSE.
- **The "Why":** It converts the MSE back to the **original unit of the target variable**, making it interpretable.
- **The "How":** Provides a "typical" error magnitude in the same units as $y$.
- **Real-world utility:** Heavily used in ML competitions (Kaggle) and engineering simulations.

#### D. R-Squared (Coefficient of Determination, $R^2$)
- **Operational Logic:** Represents the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is explained by the independent variables.
- **The "Why":** It is **scale-independent** (lies between $-\infty$ and 1), allowing comparison across different datasets.
- **The "How":** Compares the model's residuals (SS_res) to the total variance (SS_tot). A value of 1.0 indicates perfect prediction; 0 indicates the model is no better than the mean predictor.
- **Real-world utility:** Universal metric in econometrics, social sciences, and any field requiring "explained variance" interpretation.

#### E. Adjusted R-Squared
- **Operational Logic:** A modified version of $R^2$ that penalizes the addition of irrelevant features.
- **The "Why":** $R^2$ always increases (or stays constant) when new features are added — even useless ones. Adjusted $R^2$ corrects this by including the number of predictors $p$.
- **The "How":** Divides the $R^2$ adjustment by a factor involving $(n-1)/(n-p-1)$.

#### F. Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)
- **Operational Logic:** Expresses the error as a percentage of the actual values.
- **The "Why":** Provides a **dimensionless, easily interpretable** metric.
- **The "How":** Divides each absolute error by the true value, averages them, and multiplies by 100.
- **Limitation:** Undefined or infinite when $y_i = 0$; biased toward underestimation.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $n$ = number of samples, $p$ = number of predictors, $y_i$ = actual value, $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted value, $\bar{y}$ = mean of actual values.

| **Metric** | **Mathematical Formula** | **Optimal Value** | **Unit** | **Outlier Sensitivity** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MAE** | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | 0 | Same as $y$ | Low (Robust) |
| **MSE** | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$ | 0 | Squared unit of $y$ | High |
| **RMSE** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}}$ | 0 | Same as $y$ | High |
| **R-Squared** | $R^{2} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}$ | 1 | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Moderate |
| **Adjusted $R^2$** | $R^{2}_{adj} = 1 - \left[\dfrac{(1-R^{2})(n-1)}{n-p-1}\right]$ | 1 | Dimensionless | Moderate |
| **MAPE** | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{100\%}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left \vert \dfrac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i} \right \vert$ | 0% | Percentage | Moderate |

### 2.3 Relationship Between Metrics

- **RMSE $\geq$ MAE** always holds, with equality only when every error is identical.
- A **single outlier** can balloon MSE/RMSE drastically while MAE only moves a little.
- $R^2$ is **independent of scale** of $y$ (because both numerator and denominator scale identically).
- **MSE is differentiable everywhere**, making it the default loss function for gradient-based optimizers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of MSE from Residuals

The Mean Squared Error is the **expectation of the squared error** under the empirical distribution:

$$\text{MSE} = \mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{y})^{2}\right]$$

Expanding the squared term using the algebraic identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$:

$$\text{MSE} = \mathbb{E}[y^{2}] - 2\,\mathbb{E}[y\hat{y}] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{y}^{2}]$$

This is the foundation of the **bias-variance decomposition**:

$$\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{y}) + \text{Variance}(\hat{y}) + \sigma^{2}$$

where the irreducible error $\sigma^2$ is the variance of the noise term in the data-generating process.

### 3.2 Derivation of R-Squared

The total sum of squares (SS_tot) measures the variance of $y$ around its mean:

$$\text{SS}_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}$$

The residual sum of squares (SS_res) measures the unexplained variance by the model:

$$\text{SS}_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$$

The explained sum of squares (SS_reg) is the difference:

$$\text{SS}_{reg} = \text{SS}_{tot} - \text{SS}_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^{2}$$

Therefore, R-Squared is the ratio of explained variance to total variance:

$$R^{2} = \frac{\text{SS}_{reg}}{\text{SS}_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SS}_{res}}{\text{SS}_{tot}}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
Given the following dataset of 5 observations, calculate **MAE, MSE, RMSE, R-Squared, and MAPE** for a regression model:

| Instance $i$ | Actual $y_i$ | Predicted $\hat{y}_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 3 | 2.8 |
| 2 | 5 | 5.2 |
| 3 | 7 | 6.5 |
| 4 | 9 | 9.3 |
| 5 | 11 | 10.5 |

**Step 0 — Pre-computation of the mean of actual values:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{3 + 5 + 7 + 9 + 11}{5} = \frac{35}{5} = 7$$

**Step 1 — Compute the residuals and their absolute values:**

| $i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $\vert e_i \vert$ | $e_i^{2}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 3 | 2.8 | 0.2 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 2 | 5 | 5.2 | -0.2 | 0.2 | 0.04 |
| 3 | 7 | 6.5 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| 4 | 9 | 9.3 | -0.3 | 0.3 | 0.09 |
| 5 | 11 | 10.5 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **0.7** | **1.7** | **0.67** |

**Step 2 — Compute MAE:**

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} \vert e_i \vert = \frac{1.7}{5} = 0.34$$

**Step 3 — Compute MSE:**

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} e_i^{2} = \frac{0.67}{5} = 0.134$$

**Step 4 — Compute RMSE:**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}} = \sqrt{0.134} \approx 0.366$$

**Step 5 — Compute SS_tot (for R-Squared):**

$$\text{SS}_{tot} = (3-7)^{2} + (5-7)^{2} + (7-7)^{2} + (9-7)^{2} + (11-7)^{2}$$

$$\text{SS}_{tot} = 16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16 = 40$$

**Step 6 — Compute R-Squared:**

$$R^{2} = 1 - \frac{\text{SS}_{res}}{\text{SS}_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{0.67}{40} = 1 - 0.01675 = 0.98325$$

**Step 7 — Compute MAPE:**

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} \left \vert \frac{e_i}{y_i} \right \vert$$

$$\text{MAPE} = 20\% \times \left( \frac{0.2}{3} + \frac{0.2}{5} + \frac{0.5}{7} + \frac{0.3}{9} + \frac{0.5}{11} \right)$$

$$\text{MAPE} = 20\% \times (0.0667 + 0.0400 + 0.0714 + 0.0333 + 0.0455)$$

$$\text{MAPE} = 20\% \times 0.2569 = 5.138\%$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Final Results Summary

| **Metric** | **Computed Value** | **Interpretation** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| MAE | **0.34** | Average error is 0.34 units |
| MSE | **0.134** | Squared-error average |
| RMSE | **0.366** | Typical error magnitude (same unit as $y$) |
| $R^{2}$ | **0.98325** | Model explains ~98.3% of variance |
| MAPE | **5.138%** | Average percentage error |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow of Regression Evaluation

The diagram below illustrates the hierarchical relationship between the three families of regression performance measures and their derivations from the raw residual.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Raw Residuals e_i = y_i - y_hat_i"] --> B["Magnitude-Based Family"]
    A --> C["Variance-Based Family"]
    A --> D["Relative Percentage Family"]

    B --> B1["Sum of Absolute Residuals"]
    B --> B2["Sum of Squared Residuals"]

    B1 --> B3["MAE: Mean Absolute Error"]
    B2 --> B4["MSE: Mean Squared Error"]
    B2 --> B5["SS_res: Residual Sum of Squares"]

    B4 --> B6["RMSE: Root Mean Squared Error"]

    C --> C1["SS_tot: Total Sum of Squares"]
    B5 --> C2["Ratio SS_res to SS_tot"]
    C1 --> C2

    C2 --> C3["R-Squared: Coefficient of Determination"]
    C3 --> C4["Adjusted R-Squared with penalty for p features"]

    D --> D1["Element-wise Error by True Value"]
    D1 --> D2["Mean of Relative Errors"]
    D2 --> D3["MAPE: Mean Absolute Percentage Error"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style B3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style B4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style B6 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D3 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic for Metric Selection

This block diagram maps a decision flow that a data scientist would use to choose the correct evaluation metric, frequently tested in KTU viva and theory exams.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1["Start: Need to Evaluate Regression Model"] --> Q2{"Are there outliers in data?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3["Use MAE: Robust to outliers"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q4{"Need interpretable scale matching y?"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> Q5["Use RMSE: Penalizes large errors"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> Q6{"Need to compare across different datasets?"}
    Q6 -- "Yes" --> Q7["Use R-Squared: Scale-independent"]
    Q6 -- "No" --> Q8{"Are true values far from zero?"}
    Q8 -- "Yes" --> Q9["Use MAPE: Percentage-based comparison"]
    Q8 -- "No" --> Q10["Avoid MAPE: Division by near-zero unstable"]

    style Q1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style Q3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style Q5 fill:#c8e6c9
    style Q7 fill:#c8e6c9
    style Q9 fill:#c8e6c9
    style Q10 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE). State one key difference between them in terms of outlier sensitivity.*

**Model Answer:**
MAE is the average of the absolute differences between predicted and actual values:
$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

RMSE is the square root of the average of squared errors:
$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}}$$

**Key Difference:** MAE uses linear (L1) penalty, making it **robust to outliers**, while RMSE uses quadratic (L2) penalty, **heavily penalizing large errors**. Thus, RMSE > MAE when outliers exist. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the Coefficient of Determination (R-Squared)? What does a value of $R^2 = 0.85$ indicate about the model?*

**Model Answer:**
R-Squared is the proportion of variance in the dependent variable $y$ that is explained by the independent variables in the regression model. It is calculated as:

$$R^{2} = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}$$

A value of $R^2 = 0.85$ indicates that **85% of the variability** in the target variable is explained by the model, and the remaining 15% is unexplained (due to noise or missing features). **(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the following regression performance metrics with their formulas: **MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R-Squared**. Discuss when each is most appropriate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. MAE (Mean Absolute Error):** Average of absolute residuals; uses L1-norm.
$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$
*Best for:* Datasets with outliers, when all errors must be treated equally.

**2. MSE (Mean Squared Error):** Average of squared residuals; uses L2-norm.
$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$$
*Best for:* Training loss functions (differentiable), penalizing large errors.

**3. RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error):** Square root of MSE; same unit as $y$.
$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$$
*Best for:* Reporting model error in interpretable units.

**4. R-Squared:** Proportion of variance explained.
$$R^{2} = 1 - \frac{\text{SS}_{res}}{\text{SS}_{tot}}$$
*Best for:* Comparing models across datasets of different scales.

The **selection logic**: Use MAE for robustness, MSE/RMSE for strict penalization, and R-Squared for variance explanation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the following data, compute **MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R-Squared**:
Actual $y$: [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]; Predicted $\hat{y}$: [12, 18, 31, 38, 51]. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Residuals:**
- $e_1 = 10 - 12 = -2$
- $e_2 = 20 - 18 = 2$
- $e_3 = 30 - 31 = -1$
- $e_4 = 40 - 38 = 2$
- $e_5 = 50 - 51 = -1$

**Step 2 — Absolute and Squared Values:**
- $\vert e_i \vert$: 2, 2, 1, 2, 1 → Sum = 8
- $e_i^{2}$: 4, 4, 1, 4, 1 → Sum = 14

**Step 3 — MAE:**
$$\text{MAE} = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6$$

**Step 4 — MSE:**
$$\text{MSE} = \frac{14}{5} = 2.8$$

**Step 5 — RMSE:**
$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{2.8} \approx 1.673$$

**Step 6 — SS_tot Calculation:** $\bar{y} = 30$
$$\text{SS}_{tot} = (10-30)^{2} + (20-30)^{2} + (30-30)^{2} + (40-30)^{2} + (50-30)^{2}$$
$$\text{SS}_{tot} = 400 + 100 + 0 + 100 + 400 = 1000$$

**Step 7 — R-Squared:**
$$R^{2} = 1 - \frac{14}{1000} = 1 - 0.014 = 0.986$$

**Final Results:** MAE = 1.6, MSE = 2.8, RMSE = 1.673, $R^2$ = 0.986. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain **Adjusted R-Squared** and **MAPE**. Why is R-Squared alone insufficient for model evaluation? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Adjusted R-Squared** modifies $R^2$ by penalizing the inclusion of irrelevant features:
$$R^{2}_{adj} = 1 - \left[\frac{(1-R^{2})(n-1)}{n-p-1}\right]$$
where $p$ is the number of predictors and $n$ is the sample size. It **decreases** when an irrelevant feature is added (unlike $R^2$, which always increases).

**MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)** expresses error as a percentage:
$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left \vert \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i} \right \vert$$
It is dimensionless, making it useful for comparing across datasets with different units.

**Why $R^2$ alone is insufficient:**
1. It always increases (or stays constant) when new features are added, even if the feature is **irrelevant** — this encourages overfitting.
2. $R^2$ can be **negative** for non-linear models (when predictions are worse than the mean).
3. It does not indicate the **magnitude** of error in the original units.
4. It does not reveal the **bias** direction (under vs. over-prediction).

Hence, **Adjusted $R^2$** corrects feature-count bias, and **MAPE/RMSE** address magnitude and relative error concerns. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A regression model yields the following: $\text{SS}_{res} = 45$, $\text{SS}_{tot} = 300$, $n = 30$, and $p = 4$. Calculate **R-Squared, Adjusted R-Squared**, and interpret the results. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — R-Squared:**
$$R^{2} = 1 - \frac{\text{SS}_{res}}{\text{SS}_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{45}{300} = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85$$

**Step 2 — Adjusted R-Squared:**
$$R^{2}_{adj} = 1 - \left[\frac{(1 - 0.85)(30 - 1)}{30 - 4 - 1}\right]$$

$$R^{2}_{adj} = 1 - \left[\frac{0.15 \times 29}{25}\right]$$

$$R^{2}_{adj} = 1 - \left[\frac{4.35}{25}\right] = 1 - 0.174 = 0.826$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
- $R^2 = 0.85$ means the model explains **85%** of the variance.
- $R^2_{adj} = 0.826$ accounts for 4 predictors; it is lower than $R^2$ because Adjusted $R^2$ penalizes the number of features. The small drop (0.85 → 0.826) indicates the features are **reasonably relevant** and not just adding noise. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute value in MAE** — Negative residuals must be made positive. Marking penalised: 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $n$ and $n-1$** — KTU standard practice uses $n$ (population formula) in MAE/MSE/RMSE unless explicitly stated as sample variance.
> 3. **Not squaring residuals in MSE** — A frequent mistake: using absolute values instead of squares. Always re-check the formula.
> 4. **Forgetting the square root for RMSE** — Many students stop at MSE and lose 1 mark.
> 5. **MAPE with zero denominators** — If any $y_i = 0$, MAPE is undefined. State this explicitly in answers.
> 6. **Confusing Adjusted $R^2$ formula** — The denominator is $n - p - 1$, not $n - p$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **MAE:** L1-norm; robust to outliers; same unit as $y$.
- **MSE:** L2-norm squared; standard loss function; penalizes large errors quadratically.
- **RMSE:** Square root of MSE; interpretable in original units; sensitive to outliers.
- **R-Squared:** $1 - \text{SS}_{res}/\text{SS}_{tot}$; range is $(-\infty, 1]$; explains variance proportion.
- **Adjusted R-Squared:** Penalizes unnecessary features; uses $(n-p-1)$ in denominator.
- **MAPE:** Percentage-based; undefined when $y_i = 0$; biased toward underestimation.
- **Key Identity:** $\text{RMSE} \geq \text{MAE}$ always.
- **MSE is differentiable** → ideal for gradient descent optimization.
- **Choose MAE for outlier-heavy data**; **choose RMSE for strict penalization**; **choose $R^2$ for cross-dataset comparison**.
- **Training vs. Testing:** Always evaluate on a held-out test set to detect overfitting; these metrics can also be applied to both train and test sets to compute the **generalization gap**.
- **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "Compute MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R-Squared for the given data" — practice this 5–6 times before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machine (SVM) - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machine (SVM) - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised machine learning algorithm primarily used for **binary classification** (and regression, via SVR), which constructs an **optimal separating hyperplane** in a feature space of dimension *d* that maximally separates data points belonging to two distinct classes. The "optimal" hyperplane is the one that maximizes the **margin** — the perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the closest data points from either class (these closest points are called **support vectors**).

Mathematically, an SVM solves a **constrained convex optimization problem** governed by the **max-min principle** of Statistical Learning Theory (VC Theory), proposed originally by **Vladimir Vapnik** and **Alexey Chervonenkis** in 1963, and refined into its modern margin-based form by **Vapnik (1995)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize this exact phrasing):**
> "A Support Vector Machine is a maximum-margin classifier that finds the optimal hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$ by solving a quadratic programming problem of the form: minimize $\frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2$ subject to the constraint that every training sample is correctly classified with a functional margin of at least 1."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **cricket pitch** separating two teams (Team A and Team B). The umpire wants to draw a line on the pitch that keeps the two teams as far apart as possible.

- The **two teams** = two classes (e.g., spam vs. legitimate email).
- The **boundary line** = the **hyperplane** (decision boundary).
- The **closest player from each side** to the line = **support vectors**.
- The **distance between the two closest players** (measured across the line) = the **margin**.
- SVM wants to draw the line **exactly in the middle** of this gap, making the **margin as large as possible**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why maximize the margin?** A larger margin means the classifier is more **robust** to noise, outliers, and small perturbations in unseen data. This is the central philosophy of SVM: *maximize generalization by maximizing geometric margin.*

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Must Appear in Bold in Answers)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Hyperplane** | Decision boundary of dimension $(d-1)$ in a $d$-dimensional feature space |
| **Margin** | Perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the nearest data point |
| **Support Vector** | A training data point that lies exactly on the margin boundary (i.e., satisfies $y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$) |
| **Kernel Function** | A function $K(x_i, x_j)$ that implicitly maps data to higher dimensions without computing the mapping explicitly |
| **Slack Variable** | $\xi_i \geq 0$, allows misclassification in soft-margin SVM |
| **VC Dimension** | A measure of classifier capacity from VC theory |
| **Hard Margin SVM** | Strictly separable case with no misclassification allowed |
| **Soft Margin SVM** | Allows some misclassifications via slack variables |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever asked "What is SVM?", always mention **three** keywords: *(i) maximum margin, (ii) hyperplane, (iii) support vectors*. Examiners award marks for these keywords.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Linear SVM (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

The SVM algorithm operates through the following structured logic:

**Step 1 — Problem Setup:**
Given a training dataset of $n$ samples:
$$\{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}, \quad x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d, \quad y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$$
We seek a hyperplane $H: w^T x + b = 0$ that separates the $+1$ and $-1$ classes.

**Step 2 — Margin Definition:**
The geometric margin between the hyperplane and a point $x_i$ is:
$$\gamma_i = \frac{y_i(w^T x_i + b)}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$$
The **minimum geometric margin** over all training points is:
$$\gamma = \min_{i=1..n} \frac{y_i(w^T x_i + b)}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$$

**Step 3 — Why Maximize Margin?**
A larger $\gamma$ implies better generalization. From a Bayesian perspective, the hyperplane with the largest margin has the lowest expected risk (Vapnik's principle).

**Step 4 — Optimization Formulation:**
Maximize $\gamma$ subject to $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ for all $i$. By rescaling $w$ and $b$, this is equivalent to:

$$\min_{w,b} \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$$

**Step 5 — Solve via Lagrangian Duality:**
Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and solve the **dual problem**:
$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(x_i, x_j)$$
subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**Step 6 — Extract Support Vectors:**
After solving, the support vectors are those points with $\alpha_i > 0$. The decision function becomes:
$$f(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \, K(x_i, x) + b\right)$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hyperplane Equation** | $w^T x + b = 0$ | $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$, $b \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | **Decision Function** | $f(x) = \text{sign}(w^T x + b)$ | Output: $+1$ or $-1$ |
| 3 | **Functional Margin** | $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(w^T x_i + b)$ | Not scale-invariant |
| 4 | **Geometric Margin** | $\gamma_i = \frac{y_i(w^T x_i + b)}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$ | Scale-invariant |
| 5 | **Total Margin Width** | $\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$ | Distance between two parallel margins |
| 6 | **Primal Objective (Hard)** | $\min \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2$ | Subject to $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ |
| 7 | **Primal Objective (Soft)** | $\min \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ | $\xi_i \geq 0$, $C > 0$ penalty |
| 8 | **Soft Constraint** | $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ | Slack relaxation |
| 9 | **Dual Objective** | $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$ | $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ |
| 10 | **Lagrangian (Primal)** | $L = \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 - \sum \alpha_i[y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1]$ | For hard margin |
| 11 | **Weight Vector (Recovered)** | $w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i x_i$ | Only support vectors contribute |
| 12 | **Linear Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i^T x_j$ | No mapping needed |
| 13 | **Polynomial Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = (x_i^T x_j + c)^d$ | Degree $d$, offset $c$ |
| 14 | **RBF (Gaussian) Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \vert\vert x_i - x_j \vert\vert^2)$ | $\gamma > 0$, infinite-dim mapping |
| 15 | **KKT Complementarity** | $\alpha_i [y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1] = 0$ | $\alpha_i = 0$ OR constraint tight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):** The *functional margin* depends on the scale of $(w, b)$, while the *geometric margin* is the actual physical distance and is scale-invariant. SVM uses the **geometric margin**.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of SVM in Engineering

- **Bioinformatics:** Cancer classification (microarray gene expression), protein structure prediction.
- **Computer Vision:** Face detection, handwriting recognition (e.g., MNIST digit classification), image classification.
- **Text & NLP:** Spam filtering, sentiment analysis, document categorization.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Landslide susceptibility mapping using SVM on geographic features.
- **Finance:** Credit card fraud detection, stock market trend classification.
- **Power Systems:** Fault detection and classification in transmission lines (KTU-relevant application).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Maximum Margin to the Primal Optimization Problem

**Goal:** Derive the canonical SVM optimization problem starting from geometric intuition.

**Step 1 — Define two parallel margin hyperplanes:**

The hyperplane $H: w^T x + b = 0$ separates the two classes. Two parallel hyperplanes that bound each class are:
$$H_1: w^T x + b = +1 \quad \text{(touching the } +1 \text{ class boundary)}$$
$$H_2: w^T x + b = -1 \quad \text{(touching the } -1 \text{ class boundary)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the constraint that each training point lies on the correct side:**

For every training point $(x_i, y_i)$, we require:
$$w^T x_i + b \geq +1 \quad \text{if} \quad y_i = +1$$
$$w^T x_i + b \leq -1 \quad \text{if} \quad y_i = -1$$

These two conditions can be combined into a single inequality:
$$y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall \, i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the distance between $H_1$ and $H_2$:**

The perpendicular distance from the origin to $H_1$ is $\frac{\vert 1 - b \vert}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$, and to $H_2$ is $\frac{\vert -1 - b \vert}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$.

The distance between them is:
$$\text{Distance} = \frac{\vert 1 - b \vert}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert} - \frac{\vert -1 - b \vert}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert} = \frac{2}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$$

**Step 4 — Convert maximization to minimization:**

Maximizing the margin $\frac{2}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\vert\vert w \vert\vert$, which is further equivalent (and convex) to minimizing $\frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2$.

**Step 5 — Final Primal Optimization Problem:**

$$\boxed{\min_{w,b} \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Lagrangian Dual Formulation (Hard Margin)

**Step 1 — Construct the Lagrangian:**

For the constrained problem above, define non-negative Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$:
$$L(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Set partial derivatives to zero (KKT Stationarity):**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \implies w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into $L$:**

Substitute $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ into the Lagrangian:

$$\begin{aligned}
L(w^*, b^*, \alpha) &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i\right)^T \left(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j\right) - \sum_i \alpha_i \left[ y_i \left(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j\right) x_i^T + b \cdot y_i - 1 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j x_i^T x_j - \sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j x_i^T x_j - b\sum_i \alpha_i y_i + \sum_i \alpha_i \\
&= \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j x_i^T x_j
\end{aligned}$$

The $b\sum_i \alpha_i y_i$ term vanishes due to the constraint $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**Step 4 — Write the Dual Problem:**

$$\boxed{\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^T x_j}$$
subject to:
$$\alpha_i \geq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 5 — KKT Complementarity Condition:**

At the optimum:
$$\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 \right] = 0 \quad \forall i$$

This means **either** $\alpha_i = 0$ (point is *not* a support vector), **or** $y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$ (point lies on the margin, i.e., it *is* a support vector).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Only support vectors ($\alpha_i > 0$) contribute to $w$. All other training points effectively "disappear" from the final classifier. This is what makes SVM memory-efficient at inference time.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Soft Margin Formulation (for Non-Separable Data)

When data is not perfectly linearly separable, allow some violations via slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$$
subject to:
$$y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

The parameter $C > 0$ trades off margin width against misclassification penalty:
- **Large $C$** → heavy penalty → narrow margin, fewer violations (low bias, high variance).
- **Small $C$** → light penalty → wider margin, more violations (high bias, low variance).

The dual problem remains identical in form but with the additional constraint $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$:
$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$$
subject to $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

---

### 3.4 Kernel Trick Introduction (Bridge to Non-Linear SVM)

When classes are **not linearly separable** in the original space, we map data into a higher-dimensional space $\mathcal{F}$ via $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathcal{F}$ where separation becomes linear.

The dual problem contains $x_i^T x_j$. Replace this with:
$$K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$$

The **kernel trick** allows us to compute $K(x_i, x_j)$ in the original low-dimensional space **without explicitly computing** $\phi(x)$ or $\phi(x_j)$ — useful when $\mathcal{F}$ is very high-dimensional (or infinite-dimensional, as in RBF).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Hyperplane Diagram (ASCII Schematic for Paper Answer)

```
                  Class +1 Region                              
                       |                                      
                       |  +                              +    
                       |                                     
        ×  ×  ×        |        +  +  +                    
        ×  ×  ×  ×     |        +  +  +  +                  
        ×  ×  ×        |        +  +  +                    
                       |                                     
        ======●========|========●======   ← Margin Hyperplanes H1, H2
                       |  |                                  
                       |  |                                  
                       |  ↕ (w, b) Hyperplane H               
                       |                                     
        ×  ×  ×        |        +  +  +                    
        ×  ×  ×  ×     |        +  +  +  +                  
        ×  ×  ×        |        +  +  +                    
                       |                                     
                       |                                     
                  Class -1 Region                             

Legend:
  + : Positive class data point
  × : Negative class data point
  ● : Support Vector (lies exactly on H1 or H2)
  ─── : Margin Hyperplane (H1 or H2)
  ─── : Optimal Separating Hyperplane H: w^T x + b = 0
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Drawing Tip:** Always draw the **support vectors as filled circles** and other points as plain crosses. Label the margin width as $\frac{2}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$. Examiners look for **three** things: (1) the hyperplane, (2) the margin, (3) the support vectors.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: SVM Training and Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Data Xn Yn] --> B[Choose Kernel Function K]
    B --> C[Construct Primal Optimization Problem]
    C --> D[Form Lagrangian with Lagrange Multipliers alpha]
    D --> E[Solve Dual Quadratic Programming Problem]
    E --> F{Identify Support Vectors}
    F --> G[alpha greater than 0]
    G --> H[Recover Weight Vector w equals sum alpha i y i x i]
    H --> I[Compute Bias b from Support Vectors]
    I --> J[Store Support Vectors and alpha]
    J --> K[New Test Point x arrives]
    K --> L[Compute Decision Function f x equals sign sum alpha i y i K x i x plus b]
    L --> M[Output Class Label]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hard Margin vs Soft Margin SVM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HM[Hard Margin SVM]
        A1[Linearly Separable Data] --> B1[Constraint y i w T x i plus b greater than 1]
        B1 --> C1[No Misclassifications Allowed]
        C1 --> D1[Solution Strictly on Margin Boundaries]
    end

    subgraph SM[Soft Margin SVM]
        A2[Non Separable Data] --> B2[Slack Variables xi i introduced]
        B2 --> C2[Constraint y i w T x i plus b greater than 1 minus xi i]
        C2 --> D2[Penalty C times sum xi i added to Objective]
        D2 --> E2[Allows Misclassifications within Budget]
    end

    D1 --> F[Geometric Margin equals 2 divided by norm w]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Maximum Margin Hyperplane]
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Boundary Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Linear[Linear SVM]
        L1[Straight Hyperplane] --> L2[Linear Kernel K equals x i T x j]
        L2 --> L3[Suitable for Linearly Separable Data]
    end

    subgraph Poly[Polynomial SVM]
        P1[Curved Polynomial Boundary] --> P2[Kernel K equals x i T x j plus c to the d]
        P2 --> P3[Suitable for Moderate Non Linearity]
    end

    subgraph RBF[RBF SVM]
        R1[Highly Flexible Closed Boundary] --> R2[Kernel K equals exp minus gamma norm x i minus x j squared]
        R2 --> R3[Suitable for Complex Non Linear Data]
    end

    Linear --> Z[Pick Best by Cross Validation]
    Poly --> Z
    RBF --> Z
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Support Vector Machine. What are support vectors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Support Vector Machine (SVM) is a supervised machine learning algorithm used primarily for binary classification. It finds an **optimal separating hyperplane** that maximizes the **margin** (distance) between the two classes in a feature space.

**Support vectors** are the training data points that lie **closest to the decision hyperplane** — specifically, those that lie exactly on the margin boundaries (i.e., satisfy $y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$). These points are the most "difficult" to classify and are the only points that determine the position and orientation of the optimal hyperplane. All other training points have $\alpha_i = 0$ and do not influence the final decision boundary.

*(Keyword hits: optimal separating hyperplane, margin, support vectors → 3 marks secured)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between hard margin and soft margin SVM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hard Margin SVM | Soft Margin SVM |
|---|---|---|
| Data assumption | Perfectly linearly separable | May have overlapping / non-separable data |
| Slack variables | Not used ($\xi_i = 0$) | Used ($\xi_i \geq 0$) |
| Objective | $\min \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2$ | $\min \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ |
| Constraint | $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ | $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ |
| Dual constraint | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ |
| Misclassification | None allowed | Allowed, penalized by $C$ |

Hard margin is a special case of soft margin when $C \to \infty$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Formulate the optimization problem for a **linear hard-margin SVM** with a clearly defined margin. Explain every variable used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following 2-D training data points belonging to two classes:
- Class $+1$: $(3, 1), (3, -1), (6, 1)$
- Class $-1$: $(1, 0), (0, -1), (0, 1)$

Find the **optimal separating hyperplane** and the **support vectors**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given $n$ training samples $(x_i, y_i)$ where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the linear hard-margin SVM finds a hyperplane:
$$w^T x + b = 0$$
such that:
- All positive samples satisfy $w^T x_i + b \geq +1$
- All negative samples satisfy $w^T x_i + b \leq -1$

Combined: $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall \, i$

The geometric margin between the two parallel margin hyperplanes is $\frac{2}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$. The optimization problem is:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n$$

**Variables:**
- $w$ = weight vector (normal to the hyperplane)
- $b$ = bias term (offsets the hyperplane from origin)
- $y_i$ = class label of $i$-th point
- $x_i$ = feature vector of $i$-th point
- $\frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2$ = objective (squared norm squared makes it convex)

This is a **convex quadratic program (QP)** with a unique global optimum.

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify candidate support vectors** by inspection. The optimal hyperplane passes midway between the closest points of the two classes. By inspection, the support vectors are likely $(3,1), (3,-1)$ from class $+1$ and $(1,0), (0,1), (0,-1)$ from class $-1$. (Actually, only those on the margin matter.)

**Step 2 — Solve using the dual approach (simplified).** Assume the optimal hyperplane passes through points $(3, 1)$ (class $+1$) and $(1, 0)$ (class $-1$) as supporting pairs. Using two support vectors $x_+ = (3,1)$ and $x_- = (1,0)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
w^T x_+ + b &= 1 \implies 3w_1 + w_2 + b = 1 \\
w^T x_- + b &= -1 \implies w_1 + 0 \cdot w_2 + b = -1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system.** Subtract the second equation from the first:
$$2w_1 + w_2 = 2$$

By the symmetry of the data, also use the pair $(3, -1)$ and $(0, -1)$:
$$3w_1 - w_2 + b = 1$$
$$0 \cdot w_1 - w_2 + b = -1$$

Subtracting: $3w_1 = 2 \implies w_1 = \frac{2}{3}$.

Substituting back: $w_2 = 2 - 2w_1 = 2 - \frac{4}{3} = \frac{2}{3}$.

From the second equation: $b = -1 - w_1 = -1 - \frac{2}{3} = -\frac{5}{3}$.

**Step 4 — Verify with all training points (constraints must hold):**

- $(3, 1)$: $\frac{2}{3}(3) + \frac{2}{3}(1) - \frac{5}{3} = \frac{6+2-5}{3} = 1$ ✓ (support vector)
- $(3, -1)$: $\frac{2}{3}(3) + \frac{2}{3}(-1) - \frac{5}{3} = \frac{6-2-5}{3} = -\frac{1}{3}$ → satisfies $\geq -1$ ✓
- $(6, 1)$: $\frac{2}{3}(6) + \frac{2}{3}(1) - \frac{5}{3} = \frac{12+2-5}{3} = 3 \geq 1$ ✓
- $(1, 0)$: $\frac{2}{3}(1) + \frac{2}{3}(0) - \frac{5}{3} = \frac{2-5}{3} = -1$ ✓ (support vector)
- $(0, -1)$: $0 - \frac{2}{3} - \frac{5}{3} = -\frac{7}{3} \leq -1$ ✓
- $(0, 1)$: $0 + \frac{2}{3} - \frac{5}{3} = -1$ ✓ (support vector)

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{w = \left(\frac{2}{3}, \frac{2}{3}\right), \quad b = -\frac{5}{3}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Hyperplane: } \frac{2}{3}x_1 + \frac{2}{3}x_2 - \frac{5}{3} = 0 \implies 2x_1 + 2x_2 - 5 = 0}$$

**Support Vectors:** $(3, 1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (0, -1)$ — all four points lie on the margin.

**Margin Width:** $\frac{2}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{(2/3)^2 + (2/3)^2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{8/9}} = \frac{2 \cdot 3}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 2.12$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of the kernel trick** in SVM with suitable mathematical formulation. Why is it computationally advantageous? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **Lagrangian dual formulation** of the soft margin SVM problem and explain the role of the regularization parameter $C$ and the kernel function $K(x_i, x_j)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Concept:** Many real-world datasets are **not linearly separable** in the original input space $\mathbb{R}^d$. The classical remedy is to map the data into a higher-dimensional feature space $\mathcal{F}$ using a mapping function $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathcal{F}$, where the classes become linearly separable.

**Mathematical Formulation:** A non-linear SVM solves the dual problem:
$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$$
subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

The inner product $\phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$ is the **only place** where the mapped data appears. The **kernel trick** replaces this inner product with a kernel function:
$$K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$$

computed **directly** in the original space $\mathbb{R}^d$, without explicitly computing $\phi(x_i)$ or $\phi(x_j)$.

**Common Kernel Functions:**
- Linear: $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i^T x_j$
- Polynomial: $K(x_i, x_j) = (x_i^T x_j + c)^d$
- RBF (Gaussian): $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \vert\vert x_i - x_j \vert\vert^2)$
- Sigmoid: $K(x_i, x_j) = \tanh(\kappa \, x_i^T x_j + \theta)$

**Computational Advantage:** For high-dimensional mappings (e.g., RBF maps to infinite-dimensional space), explicitly computing $\phi(x)$ is infeasible. Using $K(x_i, x_j)$ avoids this and reduces complexity from $O(\dim \mathcal{F})$ to $O(d)$ per evaluation — the **kernel trick**.

**Mercer's Condition:** A function $K$ is a valid kernel iff the Gram matrix $[K(x_i, x_j)]_{n \times n}$ is positive semi-definite for all inputs.

---

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Soft Margin Primal Problem:**
$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$$
subject to:
$$y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

**Lagrangian with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
L(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 &+ C\sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i [y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] \\
&- \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_i \xi_i
\end{aligned}$$

**KKT Conditions:**
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = w - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \implies w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$$
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0 \implies \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \implies \alpha_i \leq C$$

**Dual Form:**
$$\boxed{\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)}$$
subject to:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

**Role of $C$:**
- Controls the **trade-off** between margin maximization and training error minimization.
- **Large $C$:** Stricter penalty on misclassifications → narrower margin, fits training data tightly (risk of overfitting).
- **Small $C$:** Looser penalty → wider margin, tolerates more errors (better generalization, risk of underfitting).
- $C$ is typically chosen via cross-validation.

**Role of Kernel $K(x_i, x_j)$:**
- Enables **non-linear decision boundaries** by implicitly mapping data to high-dimensional spaces.
- The classifier becomes: $f(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b\right)$.
- Choice of kernel determines the geometry of the decision boundary.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square the norm in the objective:** Students often write $\min \vert\vert w \vert\vert$ instead of $\min \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2$. The squared version is a **convex QP**, while the linear version is **non-convex**. **Always use $\frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2$.** — *Loss: 1–2 marks*
>
> 2. **Confusing functional and geometric margins:** Writing $\gamma = y_i(w^T x_i + b)$ is wrong. The geometric margin **must be divided by $\vert\vert w \vert\vert$**: $\gamma = \frac{y_i(w^T x_i + b)}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$. — *Loss: 1 mark*
>
> 3. **Mixing up the inequality sign in dual constraints:** Hard margin has $\alpha_i \geq 0$; soft margin has $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. Mixing these up is a frequent error. — *Loss: 1 mark*
>
> 4. **Omitting the equality constraint $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ in the dual:** This is **mandatory** and derived from $\partial L/\partial b = 0$. — *Loss: 1 mark*
>
> 5. **Confusing hard margin support vector derivation:** When asked to find the hyperplane from a small dataset, students often skip the **verification step** — always plug back all training points to confirm constraints hold. — *Loss: 2 marks*
>
> 6. **Forgetting the bias term $b$:** Some students write the hyperplane as $w^T x = 0$ (no bias), which only works if the data is symmetric about the origin. **Always include $b$.** — *Loss: 1 mark*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision — Print This Section Before the Exam!**

✅ **Definition:** SVM is a **maximum-margin classifier** that finds the optimal hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$ maximizing $\frac{2}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$.

✅ **Origin:** Developed by **Vladimir Vapnik (1995)**, based on **VC (Vapnik-Chervonenkis) Theory** of Statistical Learning.

✅ **Hyperplane:** A $(d-1)$-dimensional decision surface in $d$-dimensional space.

✅ **Margin:** Geometric distance between hyperplane and nearest point = $\frac{2}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$.

✅ **Support Vectors:** Training points lying exactly on the margin hyperplanes ($y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$). They are the **only** points that determine the hyperplane.

✅ **Primal (Hard Margin):** $\min \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2$ subject to $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$.

✅ **Primal (Soft Margin):** $\min \frac{1}{2}\vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ subject to $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$.

✅ **Dual Form:** $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$.

✅ **KKT Conditions:** $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$, $\alpha_i[y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1] = 0$.

✅ **Recovered Weight Vector:** $w = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i x_i$ (only support vectors contribute).

✅ **C Parameter:** Trade-off between margin width and misclassification. Large $C$ = narrow margin, low bias.

✅ **Kernel Trick:** Replace inner product $\phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$ — enables non-linear classification at low computational cost.

✅ **Kernel Examples:** Linear, Polynomial $(x_i^T x_j + c)^d$, RBF $\exp(-\gamma \vert\vert x_i - x_j \vert\vert^2)$, Sigmoid.

✅ **Mercer's Theorem:** $K$ is a valid kernel iff the Gram matrix is positive semi-definite.

✅ **Decision Function:** $f(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b\right)$.

✅ **SVM vs Logistic Regression:** SVM maximizes margin (geometric), Logistic Regression maximizes likelihood (probabilistic).

✅ **Strengths:** Works well in high dimensions, effective when $n \ll d$, memory-efficient at inference (only support vectors needed).

✅ **Weaknesses:** No native probabilistic output, sensitive to choice of $C$ and kernel parameters, slow on large datasets ($>10^5$ samples).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Margin Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maximum Margin Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Margin Classification** is a foundational principle of the **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** learning model. It is a binary classification technique that seeks to identify an **optimal separating hyperplane** in a feature space such that the perpendicular distance (the *margin*) between the hyperplane and the nearest data points of any class is **maximized**. The training samples that lie precisely on these margin-defining boundaries are called **support vectors**, and they are the *only* data points that influence the final decision function.

In more rigorous mathematical language: given a labeled training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, Maximum Margin Classification finds a hyperplane parameterized by the weight vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and bias $b \in \mathbb{R}$ such that the canonical margin $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ is maximized, while correctly separating all training observations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Crowded Bus Stops" Intuition

Imagine two groups of students standing in a long corridor for two different bus stops.

- **Group A (Blue)** is standing on the **left side** of the corridor.
- **Group B (Red)** is standing on the **right side** of the corridor.

A warden must paint a **single straight line** on the floor to separate them. Where should the line be drawn?

A naive warden might draw the line very close to the blue group (just barely separating them). But this is risky — if even one more blue student steps forward, they will cross the line and end up in the wrong queue.

A *smart* warden paints the line exactly in the **middle of the largest empty gap** between the two groups. This way:
- Both groups have **equal breathing room** from the line.
- Even if students shift a little, they still stay on the correct side.
- The line is **robust to small disturbances**.

> [!NOTE]
> This "middle of the largest empty gap" line is precisely the **Maximum Margin Hyperplane**. The students standing **closest to the line from both sides** are the **support vectors** — they alone decide where the line goes. Moving any *other* student does not change the line's position.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Nomenclature

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Weight Vector** | $w$ | Normal vector perpendicular to the hyperplane |
| **Bias / Offset** | $b$ | Distance of the hyperplane from the origin |
| **Hyperplane Equation** | $w \cdot x + b = 0$ | Decision boundary |
| **Functional Margin** | $\hat{\gamma}_i$ | $y_i(w \cdot x_i + b)$ |
| **Geometric Margin** | $\gamma_i$ | $\frac{\hat{\gamma}_i}{\Vert w \Vert}$ |
| **Optimal Margin** | $\gamma^*$ | $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ |
| **Support Vector** | $x_{sv}$ | Training point lying on $\vert w \cdot x + b \vert = 1$ |
| **Kernel Parameter (Soft Margin)** | $C$ | Penalty for margin violations |

The **decision rule** for any new test point $x_{test}$ is:

$$\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w \cdot x_{test} + b)$$

## 1.4 Why Maximum Margin?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Statistical Learning Theory (Vapnik–Chervonenkis, 1971) proves that a **larger geometric margin** leads to a **lower upper bound on the generalization error** of the classifier. Hence, maximizing the margin is a principled way to control overfitting — a central concern in KTU Module 2's treatment of supervised learning theory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linear Separability Assumption

A training set is **linearly separable** if there exist parameters $(w, b)$ such that for every $i$:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$$

The "**1**" on the right-hand side is not arbitrary — it is the result of a **canonical rescaling** of $(w, b)$. We are free to scale $(w, b)$ as $\lambda(w, b)$ for any $\lambda > 0$ without changing the geometry of the hyperplane, so we *choose* the scale such that the closest point satisfies $y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) = 1$.

## 2.2 The Geometric Margin — How Is It Computed?

The perpendicular distance from a point $x_0$ to the hyperplane $w \cdot x + b = 0$ is given by the well-known analytic-geometry formula:

$$d(x_0, \mathcal{H}) = \frac{\vert w \cdot x_0 + b \vert}{\Vert w \Vert}$$

For a positively classified point, the distance to the hyperplane is $\frac{w \cdot x_0 + b}{\Vert w \Vert}$.
For a negatively classified point, the distance is $\frac{-(w \cdot x_0 + b)}{\Vert w \Vert}$ (since $y_i = -1$).

The **total margin** between the two parallel supporting hyperplanes $w \cdot x + b = +1$ and $w \cdot x + b = -1$ is:

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{+1}{\Vert w \Vert} - \frac{-1}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **minimizing $\Vert w \Vert$ is equivalent to maximizing the margin** $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$. This is the central algebraic trick used to convert the geometric problem into a tractable convex optimization.

## 2.3 The Primal Optimization Problem (Hard Margin)

$$\boxed{\min_{w, b} \;\; \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1, \;\; \forall i = 1, \dots, N}$$

The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is included purely for mathematical convenience — it makes the gradient $\nabla_w \Vert w \Vert^2 = 2w$ collapse to $w$, which simplifies KKT analysis.

## 2.4 The Lagrangian Dual Formulation

We introduce **non-negative Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for every constraint and form the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

The corresponding **dual function** is:

$$g(\alpha) = \inf_{w, b} \mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha)$$

Setting the partial derivatives to zero yields the famous optimality conditions:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

Substituting these back, the **dual problem** becomes:

$$\boxed{\max_{\alpha} \;\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \;\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0}$$

## 2.5 KKT Conditions (Board-Exam Favourite)

For any optimal solution $(w^*, b^*, \alpha^*)$:

1. **Stationarity:** $w^* = \sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i x_i$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i^* y_i = 0$
2. **Primal Feasibility:** $y_i (w^* \cdot x_i + b^*) \geq 1$
3. **Dual Feasibility:** $\alpha_i^* \geq 0$
4. **Complementary Slackness:** $\alpha_i^* \left[ y_i (w^* \cdot x_i + b^*) - 1 \right] = 0$

> [!NOTE]
> **Complementary slackness** is the *defining* algebraic identity of support vectors. A point $x_i$ is a support vector **if and only if** $\alpha_i^* > 0$, which forces $y_i (w^* \cdot x_i + b^*) = 1$. For all non-support vectors, $\alpha_i^* = 0$, meaning the classifier *ignores* them.

## 2.6 Soft Margin (Linearly Non-Separable Case)

In real-world data, perfect separation is impossible. The soft-margin SVM introduces **slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$ and a penalty parameter $C > 0$:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \;\; \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to:

$$y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

The parameter **C** is the most important hyperparameter in SVM:

| Value of $C$ | Effect on Classifier |
|---|---|
| $C \to \infty$ | Behaves like a **hard margin** — no tolerance for misclassification |
| $C \to 0$ | Becomes **highly tolerant** — risks underfitting |
| Moderate $C$ | Balances margin width against training errors |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table consolidates **every formula** that a KTU 2024 examiner may ask under the topic "Maximum Margin Classification."

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decision Hyperplane | $w \cdot x + b = 0$ | $w$: weight, $b$: bias |
| 2 | Positive Support Hyperplane | $w \cdot x + b = +1$ | Boundary for $y = +1$ |
| 3 | Negative Support Hyperplane | $w \cdot x + b = -1$ | Boundary for $y = -1$ |
| 4 | Geometric Margin Width | $\dfrac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ | Distance between support planes |
| 5 | Perpendicular Distance | $\dfrac{\vert w \cdot x_0 + b \vert}{\Vert w \Vert}$ | Point-to-plane distance |
| 6 | Primal Objective (Hard) | $\min \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2$ | Convex quadratic program |
| 7 | Primal Constraint | $y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1$ | Separation condition |
| 8 | Soft-Margin Objective | $\tfrac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ | Penalized objective |
| 9 | Lagrangian (Primal) | $\tfrac{1}{2}\Vert w\Vert^2 - \sum_i \alpha_i [y_i(w\cdot x_i + b) - 1]$ | With $\alpha_i \geq 0$ |
| 10 | Weight Recovery | $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ | From KKT stationarity |
| 11 | Dual Constraint | $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ | Bias-stationarity condition |
| 12 | Dual Objective (Max) | $\sum_i \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$ | Concave quadratic program |
| 13 | Complementary Slackness | $\alpha_i [y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) - 1] = 0$ | Support vector identity |
| 14 | Decision Function | $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w \cdot x_{test} + b)$ | Test-time prediction |
| 15 | Bias Recovery | $b = y_{sv} - w \cdot x_{sv}$ | From any support vector |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Text and Document Classification** (spam detection, sentiment analysis)
- **Bioinformatics** (cancer classification from gene expression, protein secondary-structure prediction)
- **Image Recognition** (face detection using HOG features + linear SVM)
- **Fault Diagnosis** in mechanical systems (bearing defect classification)
- **Handwritten Digit Recognition** (MNIST benchmark)
- **Remote Sensing** (land-cover classification from satellite imagery)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Geometric Margin Formula

**Step 1 — Define two parallel supporting hyperplanes.**
Given a separating hyperplane $w \cdot x + b = 0$, the closest points from each class lie on two parallel planes equidistant from the central one. After canonical rescaling, we can write them as:

$$w \cdot x + b = +1 \quad \text{and} \quad w \cdot x + b = -1$$

The justification for the choice of "1" comes from the *invariance* of the hyperplane under positive scaling. We can always rescale $(w, b) \to (w/\vert w \cdot x_0 + b \vert, \; b/\vert w \cdot x_0 + b \vert)$ so that the closest point has functional margin exactly 1. Hence the choice is *without loss of generality*.

**Step 2 — Apply the perpendicular distance formula.**
For any point $x_+$ lying on the positive plane ($w \cdot x_+ + b = +1$):

$$d_+(x_+, \mathcal{H}) = \frac{\vert w \cdot x_+ + b \vert}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{\vert +1 \vert}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{1}{\Vert w \Vert}$$

For any point $x_-$ lying on the negative plane ($w \cdot x_- + b = -1$):

$$d_-(x_-, \mathcal{H}) = \frac{\vert w \cdot x_- + b \vert}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{\vert -1 \vert}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{1}{\Vert w \Vert}$$

**Step 3 — Add the two distances to obtain total margin width.**

$$\text{Margin} = d_+ + d_- = \frac{1}{\Vert w \Vert} + \frac{1}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Primal–Dual Relationship

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian.**
The primal problem has inequality constraints $g_i(w, b) = 1 - y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \leq 0$. The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ 1 - y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) \right]$$

with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$.

**Step 2 — Take the unconstrained infimum over $(w, b)$.**

Differentiate with respect to $w$ (treating $x_i$ as constants):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0$$

Solving:

$$w^* = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

Differentiate with respect to $b$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

Solving:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $w^*$ back into $\mathcal{L}$.**

The Lagrangian becomes:

$$\mathcal{L}_D(\alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i \right) \cdot \left( \sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j \right) - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i \cdot \sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j + \sum_i \alpha_i$$

The first and second terms cancel! We are left with:

$$\mathcal{L}_D(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

**Step 4 — Write the dual problem.**

$$\max_{\alpha} \;\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

subject to:

$$\alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (2-D Case)

Consider four training points in $\mathbb{R}^2$:

| Index $i$ | $x_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2})$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(1, 1)$ | $+1$ |
| 2 | $(1, 2)$ | $+1$ |
| 3 | $(-1, -1)$ | $-1$ |
| 4 | $(-1, -2)$ | $-1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The two classes are **clearly linearly separable**. By symmetry about the origin, the optimal hyperplane must pass through the origin, so we expect $b = 0$.

**Step 1 — Set up the dual problem.**
With four points, $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4 \geq 0$ and $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ implies:

$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - \alpha_3 - \alpha_4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = \alpha_3 + \alpha_4$$

**Step 2 — Compute the kernel matrix $K_{ij} = x_i \cdot x_j$.**

$$
K = \begin{pmatrix}
2 & 3 & -2 & -3 \\
3 & 5 & -3 & -5 \\
-2 & -3 & 2 & 3 \\
-3 & -5 & 3 & 5
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the dual.**
By symmetry, all positive-class multipliers are equal: $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = a$, and all negative-class multipliers are equal: $\alpha_3 = \alpha_4 = a$ (same value, due to symmetry of the point set). The constraint $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = \alpha_3 + \alpha_4$ is automatically satisfied.

The dual objective becomes:

$$W(a) = 4a - \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2a^2 \cdot \left[ (1\cdot 1 \cdot 2) + (1\cdot 1 \cdot 5) + (1\cdot 1 \cdot 2) + (1\cdot 1 \cdot 5) \right]$$

Working through the algebra with all $y_i y_j = +1$ for within-class pairs:

$$W(a) = 4a - \frac{1}{2} (4a^2)(14/4) = 4a - 7a^2$$

Maximizing: $\frac{dW}{da} = 4 - 14a = 0 \Rightarrow a = \frac{2}{7}$.

**Step 4 — Recover $w$.**

$$w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i = a(x_1 + x_2 - x_3 - x_4) = \frac{2}{7}\left[(2, 3) - (-2, -3)\right] = \frac{2}{7}(4, 6) = \left(\frac{8}{7}, \frac{12}{7}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Verify margin width.**

$$\Vert w \Vert = \sqrt{(8/7)^2 + (12/7)^2} = \frac{1}{7}\sqrt{64 + 144} = \frac{\sqrt{208}}{7} = \frac{4\sqrt{13}}{7}$$

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{2 \cdot 7}{4\sqrt{13}} = \frac{7}{2\sqrt{13}} = \frac{7\sqrt{13}}{26} \approx 0.969$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule.**

$$\hat{y} = \text{sign}\left( \tfrac{8}{7} x_1 + \tfrac{12}{7} x_2 \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The four training points are **all support vectors** because each lies at distance $\frac{1}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{7}{4\sqrt{13}}$ from the hyperplane — none are "safely inside" their margin.

## 3.4 Proof Sketch: Why Maximizing Margin Reduces Generalization Error

Let the margin be $\gamma = \frac{1}{\Vert w \Vert}$. Define the **VC dimension** of the margin classifier with threshold $\gamma$ on points in a ball of radius $R$ as:

$$h \leq \min\left( N, \; \frac{R^2}{\gamma^2} \right) + 1$$

The statistical-learning generalization bound then gives:

$$\text{Test Error} \leq \text{Training Error} + \mathcal{O}\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{R^2/\gamma^2}{N}} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The error bound **scales inversely with the margin $\gamma$**. Therefore, *larger* $\gamma$ $\Rightarrow$ *smaller* generalization error, which rigorously justifies the maximum-margin principle.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Geometry: Hyperplane, Margin, and Support Vectors

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the maximum-margin classifier — from raw training inputs to the final decision boundary. It abstracts the geometric idea of separating planes and support vectors into a logical flow that mirrors the optimization pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["TRAINING DATA: N LABELED POINTS"] --> B["FEATURE SPACE TRANSFORMATION"]
    B --> C["COMPUTE GEOMETRIC MARGIN: 2 OVER NORM W"]
    C --> D{"LINEARLY SEPARABLE?"}
    D -- YES --> E["HARD MARGIN: SOLVE MIN NORM W SQUARED"]
    D -- NO --> F["SOFT MARGIN: INTRODUCE SLACK XI AND PENALTY C"]
    E --> G["FORM LAGRANGIAN WITH MULTIPLIERS ALPHA"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["DERIVE DUAL PROBLEM IN ALPHA"]
    H --> I["APPLY KKT COMPLEMENTARY SLACKNESS"]
    I --> J["RECOVER W AS SUM OF ALPHA I Y I X I"]
    J --> K["IDENTIFY SUPPORT VECTORS WHERE ALPHA I GREATER THAN ZERO"]
    K --> L["COMPUTE BIAS B FROM A SUPPORT VECTOR"]
    L --> M["DECISION FUNCTION: SIGN OF W DOT X PLUS B"]
    M --> N["PREDICT CLASS LABEL FOR TEST POINT"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the Optimization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PRIMAL PROBLEM"] --> P2["MIN HALF NORM W SQUARED"]
    P2 --> P3["SUBJECT TO YI WXI PLUS B GREATER OR EQUAL 1"]
    P3 --> P4["BUILD LAGRANGIAN L"]
    P4 --> P5["STATIONARITY: PARTIAL L PARTIAL W = 0"]
    P4 --> P6["STATIONARITY: PARTIAL L PARTIAL B = 0"]
    P5 --> P7["W = SUM ALPHA I YI XI"]
    P6 --> P8["SUM ALPHA I YI = 0"]
    P7 --> P9["DUAL PROBLEM"]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10["MAXIMIZE SUM ALPHA I MINUS HALF SUM SUM ALPHA I ALPHA J YI YJ XI XJ"]
    P10 --> P11["SOLVE QP TO OBTAIN ALPHA I"]
    P11 --> P12["COMPLEMENTARY SLACKNESS"]
    P12 --> P13["SUPPORT VECTORS"]
    P13 --> P14["FINAL HYPERPLANE READY"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Components of Maximum Margin Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph C1["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["LABELED TRAINING SET"]
        A2["FEATURE REPRESENTATION"]
    end
    subgraph C2["OPTIMIZATION LAYER"]
        B1["CONVEX QUADRATIC PROGRAM"]
        B2["HARD OR SOFT MARGIN FORMULATION"]
        B3["LAGRANGIAN DUALITY"]
    end
    subgraph C3["GEOMETRIC LAYER"]
        D1["SEPARATING HYPERPLANE WX PLUS B = 0"]
        D2["POSITIVE SUPPORT PLANE WX PLUS B = 1"]
        D3["NEGATIVE SUPPORT PLANE WX PLUS B = -1"]
        D4["MARGIN WIDTH 2 OVER NORM W"]
    end
    subgraph C4["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        E1["SUPPORT VECTORS"]
        E2["DECISION FUNCTION"]
        E3["PREDICTED CLASS LABEL"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **margin width $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$** is the *bridge* between the optimization layer (where we minimize $\Vert w \Vert^2$) and the geometric layer (where the margin is widest). Whenever you see $w$ in an equation, remember it is *both* an optimization variable *and* a geometric quantity (the inverse margin).
