f of x equals sign of w transpose x plus b]:::classifyNode done[End Ready for Prediction]:::doneNode start --> linearCheck linearCheck -- Yes --> hardPath linearCheck -- No --> softPath hardPath --> formPrimal softPath --> formPrimal formPrimal --> formLagrangian formLagrangian --> takeDerivatives takeDerivatives --> formDual formDual --> solveQP solveQP --> kktCheck kktCheck -- No --> solveQP kktCheck -- Yes --> recoverW recoverW --> recoverB recoverB --> identifySV identifySV --> classify classify --> done classDef startNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#854d0e,color:#3a1a05,stroke-width:2px classDef decision fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#1e1b4b,stroke-width:2px classDef hardNode fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#022c22,stroke-width:2px classDef softNode fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#3a1a05,stroke-width:2px classDef primalNode fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#0a1a3a,stroke-width:2px classDef lagrNode fill:#c4b5fd,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#1a0a3a,stroke-width:2px classDef derivNode fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#3a0a1f,stroke-width:2px classDef dualNode fill:#a5f3fc,stroke:#155e75,color:#08323a,stroke-width:2px classDef solveNode fill:#fef08a,stroke:#713f12,color:#3a2a05,stroke-width:2px classDef kktNode fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#3a0a0a,stroke-width:2px classDef recoverWNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#052e10,stroke-width:2px classDef recoverBNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#052e10,stroke-width:2px classDef svNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#3a1a05,stroke-width:2px classDef classifyNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#0a1a3a,stroke-width:2px classDef doneNode fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,color:#052e10,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the SVM algorithm," examiners expect a flow similar to this: *Data → Separability Check → Primal Formulation → Lagrangian → Dual → Solve QP → Recover Primal via KKT → Identify Support Vectors → Classify.* # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > The question patterns below are modelled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** format: 3-mark short answers in Part A, and 14-mark questions with internal choice and (a)+(b) sub-parts of 7 marks each in Part B. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Maximum Margin Linear Separator. What are support vectors? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Maximum Margin Linear Separator** is a hyperplane that linearly separates two classes of data points while maximizing the geometric margin — the perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the closest points of each class. It is the foundational classifier used in Support Vector Machines (SVMs). **Support Vectors** are the training data points that lie *exactly* on the margin boundaries $w^T x + b = \pm 1$. Geometrically, they are the data points *closest* to the decision boundary. Mathematically, they correspond to training examples with non-zero Lagrange multipliers ($\alpha_i > 0$). Crucially, the decision hyperplane is **uniquely determined** by the support vectors alone; all other training points can be moved (or removed) without changing the classifier, provided they remain outside the margin. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Allocation Tip (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Definition — 1.5 marks, Support Vectors concept — 1 mark, Significance (sparsity) — 0.5 mark. --- ### Question 2: Distinguish between Functional Margin and Geometric Margin. Why is geometric margin preferred? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Functional Margin $\hat{\gamma}$ | Geometric Margin $\gamma$ | |:---|:---|:---| | Definition | $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(w^T x_i + b)$ | $\gamma_i = \dfrac{y_i(w^T x_i + b)}{\vert w \vert}$ | | Scale Invariant? | **No** — scales linearly with $c$ if $(w,b) \to (cw, cb)$ | **Yes** — invariant to scaling | | Geometric Meaning | Algebraic confidence score | True perpendicular distance to hyperplane | **Why geometric margin is preferred:** The functional margin can be made arbitrarily large simply by rescaling $(w, b)$, which does not change the actual classifier. Only the **geometric margin** measures the *true* distance to the decision boundary, making it a meaningful, scale-invariant quantity to maximize. This is why the SVM optimization problem is formulated in terms of $\|w\|$ (which is the inverse of the geometric margin) rather than the functional margin. > [!TIP] > **1-Mark Add-on (for full marks):** Mention that maximizing geometric margin is what enables good generalization on unseen data (Vapnik's principle). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, marks are split **7 + 7** between sub-parts. Students who answer only one sub-part (e.g., only the primal or only the dual) typically score **4–5 marks maximum** because the dual derivation is *itself* worth 3–4 marks. Always answer both sub-parts fully. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **State and derive the primal optimization problem for the maximum margin linear classifier. Hence, formulate the dual problem using Lagrange multipliers. State the KKT conditions.** #### **Part (a) — Primal Formulation and Geometric Margin (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Setup and Hyperplane Equation [1 Mark]:** Given training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, a linear classifier uses the hyperplane: $$w^T x + b = 0$$ with classification rule $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w^T x + b)$. **(ii) Geometric Margin Derivation [3 Marks]:** For a point $x_i$ classified correctly, the perpendicular distance to the hyperplane is (derived in Section 3.1): $$\gamma_i = \frac{y_i(w^T x_i + b)}{\|w\|}$$ The dataset's geometric margin is $\gamma = \min_i \gamma_i$. **(iii) Primal Optimization Problem [3 Marks]:** Maximizing $\gamma$ under the canonical constraint $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ yields the convex quadratic program: $$ \begin{aligned} \min_{w, b} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 \\ \text{subject to} \quad & y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N \end{aligned} $$ This is the **Hard-Margin SVM Primal**. --- #### **Part (b) — Dual Formulation and KKT Conditions (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Lagrangian [2 Marks]:** Introduce non-negative Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for each inequality constraint: $$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$ **(ii) Stationarity (Derivative) Conditions [2 Marks]:** Setting $\nabla_w \mathcal{L} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0$: $$w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$ **(iii) Dual Problem [2 Marks]:** Substituting back into the Lagrangian: $$ \begin{aligned} \max_{\alpha} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^T x_j \\ \text{subject to} \quad & \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) KKT Conditions [1 Mark]:** - Stationarity: $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$, $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ - Primal feasibility: $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ - Dual feasibility: $\alpha_i \geq 0$ - **Complementarity:** $\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 \right] = 0$ (this identifies the support vectors) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]` **Explain in detail the role of support vectors in a maximum margin classifier. For the dataset below, find the maximum margin hyperplane.** | Point $i$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y_i$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 1 | 1 | $+1$ | | 2 | 2 | 2 | $+1$ | | 3 | 2 | 0 | $-1$ | | 4 | 1 | 0 | $-1$ | #### **Part (a) — Role of Support Vectors (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Definition and Identification [2 Marks]:** Support vectors are training points that lie on the margin boundaries $w^T x + b = \pm 1$. They are identified by **non-zero Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i > 0$ via the KKT complementarity condition: $$\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 \right] = 0$$ If $\alpha_i = 0$, the point is *not* a support vector and lies strictly outside the margin. **(ii) Sparsity of the Solution [2 Marks]:** The optimal weight vector is a *sparse* linear combination: $$w^* = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i x_i$$ Only support vectors contribute. Removing or perturbing a non-support-vector point does *not* change $w^*$ or $b^*$, provided it remains correctly classified. This makes SVM predictions extremely efficient for inference. **(iii) Geometric and Statistical Significance [2 Marks]:** - The margin width $\gamma = 2 / \|w\|$ depends *only* on support vectors. - From Vapnik's statistical learning theory, a larger margin $\Rightarrow$ lower VC-dimension $\Rightarrow$ tighter generalization bound. Thus, by being determined by the most "ambiguous" training points, the SVM achieves robust generalization. - Support vectors are the points that "almost got misclassified"; they encode the *boundary structure* of the data. **(iv) Robustness to Outliers [1 Mark]:** If a non-support-vector point is moved slightly (or even relabeld) without crossing the margin, the decision boundary remains unchanged. Only changes to support vectors can alter the hyperplane. --- #### **Part (b) — Solving the Numerical Example (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify Likely Support Vectors [1 Mark]:** By inspection, points $P_1 = (1,1)$, $P_2 = (2,2)$ (Class $+1$) and $P_3 = (2,0)$, $P_4 = (1,0)$ (Class $-1$) are *collinear* pairs. The maximum margin hyperplane will likely pass between $(1,1)$ and $(1,0)$ (and between $(2,2)$ and $(2,0)$). **Step 2 — Set Up the Equations for Support Vectors [2 Marks]:** Assume $P_1$ and $P_4$ are the support vectors. Then the constraints are active: $$ \begin{aligned} w_1(1) + w_2(1) + b &= +1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_1 + w_2 + b = 1 \\ w_1(1) + w_2(0) + b &= -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_1 + b = -1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve the System [2 Marks]:** Subtract the second from the first: $$(w_1 + w_2 + b) - (w_1 + b) = 1 - (-1) \implies w_2 = 2$$ Substitute into the second equation: $w_1 + 2 = -1 \implies w_1 = -3$. Then: $b = -1 - w_1 = -1 - (-3) = 2$. **Step 4 — Verify the Solution [1 Mark]:** Hyperplane: $-3x_1 + 2x_2 + 2 = 0$, or equivalently $3x_1 - 2x_2 = 2$. Check all four points: - $P_1 = (1,1)$: $3(1) - 2(1) = 1 \geq 1$ ✓ - $P_2 = (2,2)$: $3(2) - 2(2) = 2 \geq 1$ ✓ - $P_3 = (2,0)$: $3(2) - 2(0) = 6$; with $y = -1$: $-1 \cdot 6 = -6 \leq -1$ ✓ - $P_4 = (1,0)$: $3(1) - 2(0) = 3$; with $y = -1$: $-1 \cdot 3 = -3 \leq -1$ ✓ Margin width: $\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\|w\|} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{(-3)^2 + 2^2}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$. **Step 5 — Final Answer [1 Mark]:** **Maximum margin hyperplane:** $\boxed{-3x_1 + 2x_2 + 2 = 0}$, **Margin width:** $\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$, **Support vectors:** $P_1$ and $P_4$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Maximum Margin Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ in the primal objective** — costs 0.5 mark. Some textbooks write $\min \|w\|^2$; KTU follows $\min \frac{1}{2} \|w\|^2$. > 2. **Confusing the constraint direction** — must be $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$, *not* $\leq 1$. The constraint enforces that each point lies on the *correct* side *and outside* the margin. > 3. **Skipping the KKT conditions** — even if the dual is derived correctly, omitting the KKT block costs **1 full mark** in Part (b). > 4. **Not mentioning the margin width formula** $\gamma = 2 / \|w\|$ — when a numerical problem is solved, examiners specifically check this for 1 mark. > 5. **Ignoring the bias recovery step** — for the numerical example, recovering $b^*$ from a support vector is a separate 1-mark item. Skipping it loses easy marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Maximum Margin Linear Separator** = hyperplane that **maximizes** the perpendicular distance to the closest training points from each class. - [x] **Geometric margin** $\gamma = \dfrac{1}{\|w\|}$ for a canonical hyperplane; **margin width** is $\dfrac{2}{\|w\|}$. - [x] **Functional margin** $\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(w^T x_i + b)$ is *not* scale-invariant; **never** use it as the optimization target. - [x] **Primal problem (hard-margin):** $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ subject to $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$. - [x] **Lagrangian:** introduce $\alpha_i \geq 0$ per inequality; stationarity gives $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$. - [x] **Dual problem:** $\max_\alpha \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j x_i^T x_j$ with $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$. - [x] **Support vectors** = training points with $\alpha_i > 0$; they lie *exactly* on the margin boundaries. - [x] **KKT complementarity:** $\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 \right] = 0$ — this is the *identification* condition for support vectors. - [x] **Sparsity:** $w^*$ is a sparse combination of training points; only support vectors matter at inference time. - [x] **Bias recovery:** $b^* = y_k - w^{*T} x_k$ for any support vector $k$. - [x] **Generalization:** A larger margin $\Rightarrow$ lower VC-dimension $\Rightarrow$ tighter bound on test error (Vapnik-Chervonenkis theory). - [x] **Soft-margin extension:** When data is not linearly separable, introduce slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ and penalty $C$; the primal becomes $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i$. - [x] **Assumption of separability:** Hard-margin SVM **requires** linearly separable data; otherwise the feasible region is empty and the problem is infeasible. - [x] **Convexity:** The primal is a **convex QP** with a unique global optimum — no local-minima issues (unlike neural networks). - [x] **Kernelization gateway:** The dual depends *only* on inner products $x_i^T x_j$. Replacing this with a kernel $K(x_i, x_j)$ gives the **kernel SVM** for non-linear classification.

topics.title: Soft Margin SVM Classifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Soft Margin SVM Classifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Soft Margin SVM Classifier** is a generalized formulation of the Support Vector Machine that relaxes the strict linear separability constraint imposed by the Hard Margin variant. It introduces non-negative **slack variables** $\xi_i$ for each training instance and a regularization hyperparameter **$C$** that controls the trade-off between maximizing the margin width and minimizing classification violations on the training data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Soft Margin SVM is essential because real-world datasets (under KTU Module 2: Classification) are almost never perfectly linearly separable. The student must understand the **primal optimization problem**, the role of the **penalty parameter $C$**, and the equivalent **Hinge Loss formulation**.

Mathematically, the Soft Margin SVM primal problem is expressed as:

$$
\min_{w,b,\xi} \quad \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i
$$

subject to the constraints:

$$
y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad \forall i \in \{1,2,\ldots,n\}
$$

Where:
- $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the weight (normal) vector of the separating hyperplane
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias term
- $\xi_i \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ are the **slack variables** (one per training point)
- $C \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ is the **regularization/penalty parameter**
- $n$ is the number of training samples

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Forgiving Highway Lane"**

Imagine you are a traffic police officer designing a **central divider (hyperplane)** between two lanes of oncoming traffic. The **Hard Margin** approach says: *"No car may ever cross the divider — every car must stay strictly on its side."* This is impossible in real life due to overtaking errors, breakdowns, and accidents.

The **Soft Margin** approach is more pragmatic: *"We will allow some cars (misclassifications) to cross the divider slightly, but every such violation incurs a fine (the slack variable $\xi_i$), and the total fine budget is controlled by parameter $C$."*

- **$C$ very large** → Strict officer: huge fines for any violation → behaves almost like Hard Margin (low bias, high variance, risk of overfitting).
- **$C$ very small** → Lenient officer: tolerates many violations → wider margin, simpler model (high bias, low variance, underfitting risk).

## 1.3 Critical Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Weight Vector | $w$ | Normal vector perpendicular to the hyperplane |
| Bias | $b$ | Offset of the hyperplane from the origin |
| Slack Variable | $\xi_i$ | Permitted margin violation for the $i$-th sample |
| Penalty Parameter | **$C$** | Controls trade-off between margin and misclassification |
| Functional Margin | $\gamma_i = y_i(w^{T}x_i+b)$ | Signed distance (scaled) of $x_i$ from the hyperplane |
| Geometric Margin | $1/\Vert w \Vert$ | True perpendicular distance between supporting planes |

> [!TIP]
> **Three Classes of Training Points in Soft Margin SVM:**
> 1. **Correctly classified & outside margin:** $0 \leq \xi_i < 1$ (or $\xi_i = 0$)
> 2. **Inside the margin (but correctly classified):** $0 < \xi_i < 1$
> 3. **Misclassified (on the wrong side):** $\xi_i > 1$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Motivation for the Soft Margin

The Hard Margin SVM enforces two hard constraints simultaneously:

$$
y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1 \quad \forall i
$$

These constraints are **infeasible** when the data is not perfectly linearly separable. To handle:

1. **Outliers** that would otherwise crush the margin.
2. **Overlapping class distributions** where perfect separation is impossible.
3. **High-dimensional noisy data** where strict separation causes overfitting.

The Soft Margin formulation introduces slack variables that **soften** the constraint, making the problem always feasible.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Introduce Slack Variables
For every training point $x_i$, allow a small violation $\xi_i \geq 0$. The original constraint is relaxed:

$$
y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i
$$

When $\xi_i = 0$, the point is correctly classified and outside (or on) the margin. When $\xi_i > 0$, the point either lies inside the margin or is misclassified.

### Step 2 — Add a Penalty Term
We must still discourage large $\xi_i$, so we add a penalty to the objective:

$$
\frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i
$$

The first term keeps the **margin wide** (good generalization), the second term keeps violations **small**.

### Step 3 — Role of the Hyperparameter $C$
The penalty parameter $C$ acts as a Lagrange multiplier-like control:

- **Large $C$** (e.g., $C = 10^3$): The optimizer heavily penalizes $\xi_i$, so it tries to fit the training data very closely. Risk: **overfitting**.
- **Small $C$** (e.g., $C = 10^{-3}$): The optimizer tolerates many $\xi_i$, producing a wider, smoother margin. Risk: **underfitting**.

### Step 4 — Equivalent Unconstrained Form (Hinge Loss)
The constrained problem can be rewritten as an unregularized loss minimization:

$$
\min_{w,b} \quad \lambda \Vert w \Vert^{2} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \max\!\left(0,\, 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b)\right)
$$

where $\max(0, 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b))$ is the **Hinge Loss** and $\lambda = \frac{1}{2C}$ is a regularization constant.

### Step 5 — Dual Formulation (for Kernel Trick)
Using Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$, the **dual problem** becomes:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^{T}x_j
$$

subject to $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The crucial distinction from Hard Margin:** In Soft Margin, each $\alpha_i$ is **bounded above by $C$**, i.e., $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. In Hard Margin, the upper bound is infinity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Key Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C\sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i$ | **Primal Objective** | Trade-off: margin vs violations |
| 2 | $y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ | **Relaxed Constraint** | Allows margin violations |
| 3 | $\xi_i \geq 0$ | **Slack Non-negativity** | Slack is always $\geq 0$ |
| 4 | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | **Box Constraint (Dual)** | Caps each Lagrange multiplier |
| 5 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0$ | **Equality Constraint (Dual)** | Sum of weighted labels = 0 |
| 6 | $\max(0, 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b))$ | **Hinge Loss** | Zero if $y_i f(x_i) \geq 1$ |
| 7 | $L_{\text{hinge}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \max(0, 1 - y_i f(x_i)) + \lambda \Vert w \Vert^{2}$ | **Regularized Empirical Risk** | Used in SGD / deep learning variants |
| 8 | $w = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i x_i$ | **Support Vector Expansion** | $w$ is a linear combo of SVs |
| 9 | $f(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{i}\alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b\right)$ | **Decision Function (Kernelized)** | For non-linear soft margin |
| 10 | $\frac{1}{\Vert w \Vert}$ | **Geometric Margin** | Width between supporting planes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Bioinformatics | Cancer classification from gene expression (noisy, overlapping data) |
| Text Classification | Spam detection where classes overlap at the margin |
| Image Recognition | Handwritten digit recognition (MNIST) |
| Financial Fraud | Credit card fraud with extreme class imbalance |
| Medical Diagnosis | Disease prediction where misclassification cost is asymmetric |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Soft Margin SVM is the **de facto industrial standard** when working with real data. Pure Hard Margin is rarely used outside textbook problems. Understanding the role of $C$ and $\xi_i$ is critical for hyperparameter tuning in production ML pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Constrained Primal to Hinge Loss

**Step 1 — Start with the constrained primal problem.**

$$
\min_{w,b,\xi} \quad \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i
$$

subject to:
$$
y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\xi_i$ in terms of $(w, b)$.**  
From the constraint, the smallest valid $\xi_i$ is:

$$
\xi_i = \max\!\left(0,\; 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b)\right)
$$

**Reasoning:** If $y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1$, the point is correctly classified beyond the margin, and we set $\xi_i = 0$ (no violation). Otherwise, we set $\xi_i = 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) > 0$ to satisfy the relaxed constraint with equality.

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the objective.**

$$
\min_{w,b} \quad \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \max\!\left(0,\; 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b)\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Convert $C$ to regularization form $\lambda$.**  
Using $\lambda = \frac{1}{2nC}$ (or equivalent scaling, depending on convention):

$$
L(w, b) = \lambda \Vert w \Vert^{2} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \max\!\left(0,\; 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b)\right)
$$

This is the **regularized hinge loss** formulation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lagrangian Dual of the Soft Margin Problem

**Step 1 — Construct the primal Lagrangian.**  
Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for the margin constraints and $\mu_i \geq 0$ for the slack non-negativity constraints:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C\sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i \left[y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i\right] - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\mu_i \xi_i
$$

**Step 2 — Take partial derivatives and set to zero.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i x_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_i = C - \mu_i
$$

**Step 3 — Derive the box constraint.**  
Since $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$, from $\alpha_i = C - \mu_i$:

$$
0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the Lagrangian to obtain the dual.**

$$
\max_{\alpha} \quad W(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^{T}x_j
$$

subject to:

$$
0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Point:** The **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ is the single most important distinction between Hard and Soft Margin SVM. Examiners frequently test this directly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Three Regimes of the Slack Variable

The optimal $\xi_i^{*}$ under the Soft Margin solution is given by:

$$
\xi_i^{*} = \max\!\left(0,\; 1 - y_i(w^{*T}x_i + b^{*})\right)
$$

Analyzing this piecewise function:

| Regime | Condition | Geometric Meaning | $\alpha_i$ Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Correctly classified, outside margin** | $y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1$ | Point lies on correct side, beyond margin | $\alpha_i = 0$ |
| **Inside margin (correct side)** | $0 \leq y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) < 1$ | Point violates margin but not classification | $0 < \alpha_i < C$ |
| **Misclassified** | $y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) < 0$ | Point lies on wrong side of hyperplane | $\alpha_i = C$ |

**Algebraic Justification:** This follows directly from the KKT complementary slackness conditions:

$$
\alpha_i \left[y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i\right] = 0
$$

- If $\alpha_i = 0$, then $y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1$ (no contribution to the model).
- If $0 < \alpha_i < C$, then $y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) = 1 - \xi_i$ (active margin support vector).
- If $\alpha_i = C$, then $y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) < 1 - \xi_i$ (bound on the dual variable reached).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Hard vs Soft Margin

| Aspect | Hard Margin SVM | Soft Margin SVM |
|---|---|---|
| Data requirement | Perfectly linearly separable | Any data (linearly separable or not) |
| Slack variables | None | $\xi_i \geq 0$ for each sample |
| Penalty term | None | $C\sum_i \xi_i$ added to objective |
| Box constraint (dual) | $0 \leq \alpha_i$ | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ |
| Loss function | 0–1 Loss (binary) | Hinge Loss |
| Outlier sensitivity | Very high | Controlled by $C$ |
| Real-world applicability | Mostly theoretical | Universal |
| Risk | Infeasible on noisy data | Overfit if $C$ too large |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Soft Margin SVM Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Training Data X and Labels Y] --> B[Initialize Weight Vector w and Bias b]
    B --> C[Compute Functional Margin yi times wTxi plus b for each sample]
    C --> D{Does any sample violate the margin?}
    D -- No --> E[Solution Converged - Return Optimal w and b]
    D -- Yes --> F[Assign Slack Variable xi for each violating sample]
    F --> G[Compute Total Penalty C times sum of xi]
    G --> H[Formulate Primal Objective: 0.5 norm w squared plus C sum xi]
    H --> I[Solve via Quadratic Programming or SMO Algorithm]
    I --> J[Extract Support Vectors where alpha i is greater than 0]
    J --> K{Is alpha i at the upper bound C?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Sample is a Margin Violator - Misclassified or Inside Margin]
    K -- No --> M[Sample is a Standard Support Vector on the Margin]
    L --> N[Compute Final Decision Function f of x equals sign of wT x plus b]
    M --> N
    E --> N
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map of the Slack Variable Decision Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Class plus 1 Region] -- Margin 1 over norm w --> H[Decision Hyperplane wT x plus b equals 0]
    H -- Margin 1 over norm w --> P2[Class minus 1 Region]
    P1 -. xi equals 0 .-> SV1[Correctly Classified Support Vector on Margin]
    P1 -. 0 less than xi less than 1 .-> SV2[Point Inside Margin - Correct Side]
    P2 -. xi greater than 1 .-> SV3[Misclassified Point on Wrong Side]
    SV1 --> W[Weight Vector w is a Sum of alpha i yi xi]
    SV2 --> W
    SV3 --> W
```

## 4.3 Penalty Parameter $C$ Behavior Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Value of C] --> Q{What is the value of C?}
    Q -- Very Small C --> R1[Wide Margin, Many Tolerated Violations]
    Q -- Moderate C --> R2[Balanced Margin and Classification Accuracy]
    Q -- Very Large C --> R3[Narrow Margin, Few Tolerated Violations]
    R1 --> O1[Model Underfits - High Bias]
    R2 --> O2[Model Generalizes Well]
    R3 --> O3[Model Overfits - High Variance Approaching Hard Margin]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for Diagrams:**
> - In Diagram 4.1, the training loop is **iterative** (sequential minimal optimization, gradient descent, or quadratic programming).
> - In Diagram 4.2, the three regions of $\xi_i$ are visually distinct — this is the **central geometric insight** the examiner will test.
> - In Diagram 4.3, the bias-variance trade-off controlled by $C$ is the **single most important hyperparameter concept** in Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **What is the role of the slack variable $\xi_i$ in the Soft Margin SVM formulation? Why is the constraint $\xi_i \geq 0$ necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The slack variable $\xi_i$ measures the degree of margin violation for the $i$-th training sample. It is introduced to relax the strict separability constraint of the Hard Margin SVM. For a sample correctly classified and outside the margin, $\xi_i = 0$. For a sample inside the margin or misclassified, $\xi_i > 0$. The constraint $\xi_i \geq 0$ is necessary because a "negative" margin violation has no geometric meaning — slack can only increase the allowed violation, not decrease it. Mathematically, $\xi_i = \max(0, 1 - y_i(w^Tx_i + b))$ from the constraint $y_i(w^Tx_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
> **Explain the role of the penalty parameter $C$ in the Soft Margin SVM. What happens when $C \to \infty$ and when $C \to 0$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The penalty parameter $C$ controls the trade-off between maximizing the margin (small $\Vert w \Vert$) and minimizing the total classification error $\sum_i \xi_i$.

- When **$C \to \infty$**: The penalty for any $\xi_i > 0$ becomes infinite, so no violations are tolerated. The Soft Margin degenerates into a Hard Margin SVM (if data is separable) or becomes infeasible (if not).
- When **$C \to 0$**: The penalty vanishes, so the optimizer focuses solely on margin maximization, potentially misclassifying most training points. The model underfits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Formulate the **primal optimization problem** of the Soft Margin SVM. Clearly define every variable and explain the role of the penalty parameter $C$ in controlling the bias-variance trade-off.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Starting from the primal, derive the **dual optimization problem**. Show clearly why the box constraint $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ arises, in contrast to the Hard Margin SVM.

### **Solution to Q3(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the primal objective:**

$$
\min_{w, b, \xi} \quad \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C\sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i
$$

**Step 2 — State the constraints:**

$$
y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

**Step 3 — Define variables (for full marks):**

- $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$: weight vector (defines hyperplane orientation)
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$: bias (defines hyperplane offset)
- $\xi_i \geq 0$: slack variable for $i$-th sample
- $C > 0$: regularization constant
- $n$: number of training samples

**Step 4 — Explain the role of $C$ (bias-variance trade-off):**

- $C$ large → narrow margin, low training error, high variance (overfitting).
- $C$ small → wide margin, high training error, low variance (underfitting).
- $C$ is typically tuned via **cross-validation**.

### **Solution to Q3(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Construct the Lagrangian:**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C\sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i [y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i
$$

**Step 2 — Set partial derivatives to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_i = C - \mu_i
$$

**Step 3 — Derive the box constraint:**

Since $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$, we have:

$$
0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the dual:**

$$
\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^{T} x_j)
$$

subject to $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**Step 5 — Compare with Hard Margin:**  
In Hard Margin, only $\alpha_i \geq 0$ exists. The upper bound $C$ emerges solely from the slack-variable penalty, making the dual bounded.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Internal Choice Alternative]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **Hinge Loss formulation** of the Soft Margin SVM from the constrained primal. Show the step where the slack variables are eliminated.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] For the Soft Margin SVM, classify the training points into three categories based on the value of $\alpha_i$. Explain each category geometrically and algebraically using KKT conditions.

### **Solution to Q4(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Start from the primal with constraints:**

$$
\min_{w,b,\xi} \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C \sum_i \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \; \xi_i \geq 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the minimum $\xi_i$:**

From $y_i(w^Tx_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$:

$$
\xi_i = \max(0,\; 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b))
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$
\min_{w,b} \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C \sum_i \max(0,\; 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b))
$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite in standard regularized form:**

Setting $\lambda = \frac{1}{2nC}$:

$$
L(w, b) = \lambda \Vert w \Vert^{2} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \max(0,\; 1 - y_i(w^{T}x_i + b))
$$

The function $\ell(z) = \max(0, 1 - z)$ is the **Hinge Loss**. It is zero when $z = y_i f(x_i) \geq 1$ and grows linearly otherwise.

### **Solution to Q4(b) — 7 Marks**

Using KKT complementary slackness:

$$
\alpha_i \left[y_i(w^{T}x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i\right] = 0
$$

| Category | $\alpha_i$ Value | Geometric Interpretation | Algebraic Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Non-Support Vector | $\alpha_i = 0$ | Outside or on the margin, correct side | $y_i(w^Tx_i + b) \geq 1$ |
| Standard Support Vector | $0 < \alpha_i < C$ | Exactly on the margin | $y_i(w^Tx_i + b) = 1$ |
| Bound Support Vector | $\alpha_i = C$ | Inside the margin or misclassified | $y_i(w^Tx_i + b) < 1$ |

**Algebraic Justification:** The condition $\xi_i = 0$ holds for the first two categories. For the third, the dual box constraint caps $\alpha_i$, forcing $\xi_i > 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Most common mistake (–2 marks):** Writing the dual objective without explicitly showing the substitution step that gives the box constraint $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. Always derive it from $\alpha_i = C - \mu_i$ and the non-negativity of both multipliers.
> 2. **Common error (–1 mark):** Confusing the role of $C$. It is a **regularization** constant on the sum of slacks — NOT a learning rate.
> 3. **Frequent omission (–1 mark):** Failing to state the equality constraint $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ in the dual. Examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Geometric error (–1 mark):** Forgetting that $\xi_i > 1$ (not just $> 0$) is the condition for **misclassification**. Points with $0 < \xi_i < 1$ are merely inside the margin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Soft Margin SVM**

- ☐ The Soft Margin SVM is the **generalized, real-world usable** form of SVM.
- ☐ It introduces **slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$** to permit margin violations.
- ☐ The primal objective is $\frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^{2} + C \sum_i \xi_i$ — **memorize this exactly**.
- ☐ The constraint $y_i(w^Tx_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ replaces the Hard Margin constraint.
- ☐ **$C$ controls bias-variance trade-off** — large $C$ = overfit, small $C$ = underfit.
- ☐ In the dual, the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ replaces the unbounded $\alpha_i \geq 0$ of Hard Margin.
- ☐ The unconstrained form is the **Hinge Loss**: $\max(0, 1 - y_i f(x_i))$.
- ☐ Three regimes: $\alpha_i = 0$ (non-SV), $0 < \alpha_i < C$ (on-margin SV), $\alpha_i = C$ (bound SV / violator).
- ☐ $\xi_i > 1$ implies **misclassification**; $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$ implies **inside the margin**.
- ☐ Support vectors are the only training points that determine $w$ and the decision boundary.
- ☐ Cross-validation (typically 5-fold or 10-fold) is the standard way to tune $C$.
- ☐ The Soft Margin dual still admits the **kernel trick** for non-linear classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-linear SVM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-linear SVM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Linear Support Vector Machine (Non-Linear SVM)** is a supervised learning classifier that extends the classical linear SVM to handle datasets that are **not linearly separable** in the original input space. It achieves this by implicitly mapping the input vectors $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ into a higher (often infinite) dimensional **Hilbert feature space** $\mathcal{H}$ via a non-linear mapping function $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathcal{H}$, where a **maximum-margin separating hyperplane** can be constructed.

Mathematically, the decision function for a non-linear SVM is given by:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i^* y_i \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b^*\right)$$

where $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}) \rangle$ is the **kernel function**, $\alpha_i^*$ are the optimal Lagrange multipliers, and $b^*$ is the optimal bias term.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Curved Decision Boundary Analogy** — Imagine you are standing on a flat, circular island (the input space) and need to draw a single straight fence to separate red houses from blue houses. If the houses are arranged in concentric circles, a straight fence is hopeless! However, if you could **magically lift** all the houses into 3D space (a "feature mountain"), where red houses sit at the bottom of a bowl and blue houses are pushed up the sides of the mountain, then a **flat horizontal sheet of plywood** (a hyperplane) can cleanly separate them in 3D. When you project that sheet back onto the island, it appears as a **beautiful curved circular boundary** on the ground.

This is exactly what Non-Linear SVM does — it operates in a transformed high-dimensional space but produces a **non-linear decision boundary** when viewed from the original input space.

## 1.3 Why Linear SVM Fails — The Motivation

The standard linear SVM solves:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i$$

This formulation strictly demands that data be linearly separable (hard margin) or nearly so (soft margin). For datasets exhibiting **concentric circular**, **XOR-like**, or **spiral** distributions, **no straight hyperplane** can achieve acceptable accuracy. Non-linear SVM resolves this fundamental limitation.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Feature Space** | The (possibly infinite-dimensional) space to which data is mapped via $\phi$ |
| **Kernel Trick** | Computing inner products in feature space **without** explicitly computing $\phi$ |
| **Hilbert Space** | A complete inner-product vector space, typically the target of $\phi$ |
| **Mercer's Condition** | A mathematical property guaranteeing that a symmetric function is a valid kernel |
| **Support Vectors** | Training samples for which $\alpha_i > 0$, lying on or near the decision boundary |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Feature Mapping Approach

The central idea of non-linear classification is to transform data into a richer representation where classes become linearly separable.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Identify Non-Linearity:** Determine that the dataset is not linearly separable in $\mathbb{R}^d$ (visualize or use linear SVM failure mode).
2. **Choose a Mapping $\phi$:** Define a non-linear transformation $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathcal{H}$ (e.g., polynomial features of degree 2 or 3).
3. **Apply $\phi$ to All Data:** Transform each input vector: $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.
4. **Solve Linear SVM in $\mathcal{H}$:** Train a standard maximum-margin classifier on $\{\phi(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i\}$.
5. **Project Boundary Back:** The hyperplane in $\mathcal{H}$ induces a non-linear boundary in $\mathbb{R}^d$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Computational Bottleneck:** Explicitly computing $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ becomes prohibitively expensive for high-degree polynomials or infinite-dimensional mappings (e.g., Gaussian). For example, a degree-2 polynomial on 100 features produces $\binom{100+2}{2} = 5151$ features!

## 2.2 The Kernel Trick — Core Innovation

> [!NOTE]
> The **kernel trick** observes that the dual SVM optimization and decision function depend on data **only through inner products** $\langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle$. We can therefore define a **kernel function** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ that computes this inner product **directly** in the original space, completely bypassing the explicit computation of $\phi$.

## 2.3 Standard Kernel Functions (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Kernel | Formula $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Use Case / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ | Baseline; when data is already linearly separable |
| **Polynomial Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ | Image processing, NLP with cross-products of features |
| **Gaussian RBF Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ | General-purpose, default choice, infinite-dimensional |
| **Sigmoid Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \tanh(\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)$ | Neural-network-like behaviour (not always Mercer-positive) |
| **Laplacian Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \sum_k \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert)$ | Histogram-based features (e.g., image histograms) |

Here $\gamma > 0$ is the **kernel coefficient**, $r$ is the **bias term** within the kernel, and $d \in \mathbb{N}$ is the **polynomial degree**.

## 2.4 Mercer's Condition (Validity of Kernels)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A symmetric function $K: \mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}$ is a **valid kernel** (i.e., corresponds to an inner product in some feature space) if and only if the **Gram matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ with entries $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is **positive semi-definite** for all training sets:
> $$\mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} \geq 0, \quad \forall \, \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^N$$

## 2.5 Dual Optimization Formulation for Non-Linear SVM

The dual problem solved in practice is:

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad L_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

subject to the box constraints:

$$0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

and the equality constraint:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

where $C > 0$ is the **soft-margin regularization parameter** that controls the trade-off between margin maximization and classification error.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Bioinformatics:** Microarray cancer classification with RBF kernels.
- **Computer Vision:** Handwritten digit recognition (e.g., MNIST) using polynomial or RBF kernels.
- **Text Categorization:** High-dimensional sparse feature classification.
- **Geological Survey:** Subsurface classification where decision boundaries follow complex geological folds.
- **Medical Diagnosis:** Detecting tumours with non-spherical decision regions in feature space.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Primal to Kernelized Dual

We start with the **soft-margin primal** problem:

$$P: \quad \min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to:

$$y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

**Step 1 — Construct the Lagrangian.** Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for the margin constraints and $\mu_i \geq 0$ for the non-negativity of slack variables:

$$L(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}, \boldsymbol{\alpha}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \big[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b) - 1 + \xi_i \big] - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity Conditions.** Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) = 0 \implies \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \implies 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Back into the Lagrangian.** Plug the expression for $\mathbf{w}$ into $L$:

$$L_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Kernel Trick.** Replace the inner product with the kernel:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

The kernelized dual problem becomes:

$$\boxed{D: \quad \max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)}$$

subject to $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Kernel-Induced Feature Space for Polynomial Kernel

Consider the polynomial kernel of degree 2 with $\gamma = 1$, $r = 0$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z})^2$$

For $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^\top$ and $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2)^\top$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) &= (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2)^2 \\
&= x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 z_1 x_2 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the implicit mapping $\phi$.** We seek a mapping such that $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z})$. Comparing term by term:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1^2, \sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2, x_2^2)^\top$$

**Step 2 — Verify.** Compute $\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z})$:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z}) = x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 z_1 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2 = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z})^2 \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Observation.** The original space is $\mathbb{R}^2$, but the feature space is $\mathbb{R}^3$. For general $d$-dimensional inputs, the polynomial kernel of degree 2 maps to a feature space of dimension $\binom{d+2}{2} - 1$, but we never compute this mapping explicitly — **that is the essence of the kernel trick**.

## 3.3 Derivation: The RBF Kernel Induces an Infinite-Dimensional Feature Space

The Gaussian RBF kernel is:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

Using the Taylor series expansion $\exp(u) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{u^k}{k!}$ applied to the Gaussian:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z})^k}{k!} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 + \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The RBF kernel corresponds to a feature space $\mathcal{H}$ of **infinite dimension** — it is a linear combination of polynomial kernels of **every degree**. Explicitly computing $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ is therefore **impossible**; the kernel trick is the **only** feasible way to use RBF in SVM.

## 3.4 Derivation: Decision Function for Test Sample

After solving the dual, the optimal weight vector in feature space is:

$$\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{S}} \alpha_i^* y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of support vectors. The decision function for a new test point $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) &= \text{sign}\left(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \phi(\mathbf{x}) + b^*\right) \\
&= \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in \mathcal{S}} \alpha_i^* y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}) + b^*\right) \\
&= \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in \mathcal{S}} \alpha_i^* y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b^*\right)
\end{aligned}$$

The bias $b^*$ is recovered from any support vector with $0 < \alpha_i^* < C$ using:

$$b^* = y_i - \sum_{j \in \mathcal{S}} \alpha_j^* y_j K(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i)$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Kernel Properties

| Property | Linear | Polynomial | RBF | Sigmoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hyperparameters** | None | $\gamma, r, d$ | $\gamma$ | $\gamma, r$ |
| **Feature Space Dim** | $d$ | $\binom{d+d_{\text{poly}}}{d_{\text{poly}}}$ | $\infty$ | Bounded |
| **Mercer-Valid** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Not always |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(d)$ | $O(d)$ | $O(d)$ | $O(d)$ |
| **Interpretability** | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Default in Practice** | Baseline | Rare | Most common | Rare |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Non-Linear SVM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data X and Labels Y] --> B{Linearly Separable Test}
    B -- YES --> C[Use Standard Linear SVM]
    B -- NO --> D[Select Kernel Function K]
    D --> E[Formulate Kernelized Dual]
    E --> F[Solve QP for Lagrange Multipliers alpha]
    F --> G[Identify Support Vectors alpha greater than zero]
    G --> H[Compute Bias b from SVs]
    H --> I[Construct Decision Function f of x]
    I --> J[Apply f to Test Sample]
    J --> K[Output Predicted Class Label]

    style K fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#2ECC71
```

## 4.2 Kernel Mapping Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Input Space X in R2] -- phi mapping --> B[High Dim Feature Space H in R3 or higher]
    B -- Maximum margin hyperplane --> C[Separating Hyperplane in H]
    C -- Inverse mapping phi inverse --> D[Non Linear Decision Boundary in R2]
```

## 4.3 Kernel Function Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Kernel Selection] --> B{N features size}
    B -- Large n much greater than m --> C[Linear Kernel Preferred]
    B -- Small n moderate m --> D{Data pattern type}
    D -- Concentric circular XOR like --> E[RBF Kernel Default]
    D -- Known polynomial interaction --> F[Polynomial Kernel Degree 2 or 3]
    D -- Histogram based features --> G[Laplacian Kernel]
    D -- Neural network mimicry --> H[Sigmoid Kernel with caution]

    E --> I[Tune gamma and C via cross validation]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    C --> J[No kernel tuning needed]
    I --> K[Select best hyperparameters]
    J --> L[Train Final SVM Model]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of the kernel trick in SVM. Why is it computationally advantageous over explicit feature mapping?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The kernel trick is a mathematical technique that computes the inner product of data points in a high-dimensional feature space **without explicitly transforming the data** into that space. Given two input vectors $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_j$, a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ returns $\langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle$ directly. This is computationally advantageous because: (i) For polynomial kernels of degree $d$, the feature dimension grows combinatorially (e.g., $\binom{d+2}{2}$), but the kernel evaluation is $O(d)$. (ii) For RBF kernels, the feature space is infinite-dimensional, making explicit mapping **impossible**, while $K$ is computed in $O(d)$ time. (iii) The dual SVM formulation depends only on inner products, so kernelizing it preserves the optimization structure while enabling non-linear decision boundaries.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Mercer's condition for a function to be a valid kernel. What happens if this condition is violated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A symmetric function $K: \mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}$ is a **valid kernel** if and only if the **Gram matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ with entries $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is **positive semi-definite** for every possible set of training samples, i.e., $\mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^N$. If this condition is violated, the kernel does not correspond to any inner product in a Hilbert space, the dual optimization problem becomes **non-convex**, and the resulting SVM solution is **not guaranteed to be optimal** — the algorithm may converge to poor local minima or fail to converge entirely. For example, the sigmoid kernel with certain $\gamma$ and $r$ values is not Mercer-valid and can produce unstable SVM models.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`
**(a)** Derive the **dual optimization problem** for a non-linear soft-margin SVM starting from the primal formulation with slack variables. Clearly show where the **kernel function** appears in the final expression. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the dataset with $N = 4$ points: $\mathbf{x}_1 = (0,0), y_1 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1,1), y_2 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_3 = (0,1), y_3 = -1$; $\mathbf{x}_4 = (1,0), y_4 = -1$, compute the **Gram matrix** using the **RBF kernel** with $\gamma = 1$. Identify the support vectors and comment on the resulting decision boundary. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the soft-margin primal problem with feature mapping $\phi$:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

The Lagrangian is constructed with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$:

$$L = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_i) + b) - 1 + \xi_i] - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \xi_i$$

Setting $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$ gives $\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)$. Setting $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = 0$ gives $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$. Setting $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \xi_i} = 0$ gives $C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0$, so $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

Substituting these into $L$, and crucially replacing the inner product $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ with the kernel $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$, the dual is:

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

subject to $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**(b) Gram Matrix Computation (7 marks):**

The RBF kernel formula is $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ with $\gamma = 1$.

Computing pairwise squared distances:
- $\Vert \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2 \Vert^2 = (0-1)^2 + (0-1)^2 = 2$
- $\Vert \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_3 \Vert^2 = (0-0)^2 + (0-1)^2 = 1$
- $\Vert \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_4 \Vert^2 = (0-1)^2 + (0-0)^2 = 1$
- $\Vert \mathbf{x}_2 - \mathbf{x}_3 \Vert^2 = (1-0)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 1$
- $\Vert \mathbf{x}_2 - \mathbf{x}_4 \Vert^2 = (1-1)^2 + (1-0)^2 = 1$
- $\Vert \mathbf{x}_3 - \mathbf{x}_4 \Vert^2 = (0-1)^2 + (1-0)^2 = 2$

Gram matrix entries $K_{ij} = \exp(-d_{ij}^2)$:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & e^{-2} & e^{-1} & e^{-1} \\ e^{-2} & 1 & e^{-1} & e^{-1} \\ e^{-1} & e^{-1} & 1 & e^{-2} \\ e^{-1} & e^{-1} & e^{-2} & 1 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0.135 & 0.368 & 0.368 \\ 0.135 & 1 & 0.368 & 0.368 \\ 0.368 & 0.368 & 1 & 0.135 \\ 0.368 & 0.368 & 0.135 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Notice the symmetry: class $+1$ points ($\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2$) are **far apart** (low $K_{12}$), class $-1$ points ($\mathbf{x}_3, \mathbf{x}_4$) are far apart (low $K_{34}$), but cross-class points are **closer** (higher $K$). After solving the QP, all four points become support vectors, and the resulting decision boundary is **non-linear**, forming a closed curve separating the diagonal of positive points from the anti-diagonal of negative points — a classic XOR-like pattern that linear SVM cannot solve.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**(a)** Show that the **polynomial kernel** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + 1)^2$ for $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^2$ corresponds to a feature mapping $\phi$ that maps to a 6-dimensional space. Write out the explicit $\phi(\mathbf{x})$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the role of the **soft-margin parameter $C$** and the **RBF kernel parameter $\gamma$** in non-linear SVM. Discuss what happens when these parameters are set to extreme values (e.g., $C \to \infty$ or $\gamma \to \infty$). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Explicit Feature Mapping (7 marks):**

For $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^\top$ and $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2)^\top$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) &= (\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z} + 1)^2 \\
&= (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2 + 1)^2 \\
&= x_1^2 z_1^2 + x_2^2 z_2^2 + 1 + 2 x_1 z_1 x_2 z_2 + 2 x_1 z_1 + 2 x_2 z_2
\end{aligned}$$

To match this with $\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z})$, we identify:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1^2, x_2^2, 1, \sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2, \sqrt{2}\, x_1, \sqrt{2}\, x_2)^\top$$

**Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi(\mathbf{x})^\top \phi(\mathbf{z}) &= x_1^2 z_1^2 + x_2^2 z_2^2 + 1 + 2 x_1 z_1 x_2 z_2 + 2 x_1 z_1 + 2 x_2 z_2 \\
&= (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2 + 1)^2 \\
&= K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

The feature space is therefore $\mathbb{R}^6$, even though the original input space is only $\mathbb{R}^2$.

**(b) Hyperparameter Analysis (7 marks):**

The **soft-margin parameter $C$** controls the trade-off between maximizing the margin and minimizing classification errors on training data. It appears as the upper bound on each Lagrange multiplier: $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. When $C \to \infty$, the SVM becomes a **hard-margin classifier** — no training errors are tolerated, and the model attempts perfect classification. This leads to **overfitting** with complex, tightly-fit decision boundaries that generalize poorly. When $C \to 0$, the model heavily tolerates misclassifications, producing a **wide margin** but potentially **underfitting** the data.

The **RBF kernel parameter $\gamma$** appears in $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ and controls the **influence radius** of each training point. When $\gamma \to \infty$, the kernel becomes extremely narrow — $K \approx 0$ for all distinct points, and each training point influences only itself. This results in a model that essentially **memorizes** the training data, producing highly complex, fragmented decision boundaries (severe overfitting). When $\gamma \to 0$, the kernel becomes nearly constant ($K \approx 1$), and the decision boundary collapses to a **single point or near-linear shape**, leading to underfitting. The optimal $C$ and $\gamma$ are typically found via **grid search with cross-validation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** (1) Students frequently confuse the role of $C$ — note that **$C$ is NOT the kernel parameter**; it is a regularization parameter from the soft-margin formulation. (2) Many students incorrectly write the polynomial kernel as $(\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{z})^d$ and forget the constant term $r = 1$ — both forms are valid, but the bias $r$ changes the feature mapping. (3) For the RBF kernel, students often invert the role of $\sigma$ vs. $\gamma$; remember $\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}$. (4) In derivation questions, skipping the stationarity conditions for $\xi_i$ is the most common cause of losing 2 marks. Always show $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \xi_i} = 0$ explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Idea:** Non-linear SVM uses a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle$ to enable non-linear classification **without** explicit high-dimensional mapping.
- **Why Linear SVM Fails:** Cannot handle XOR-like, circular, or spiral data distributions.
- **The Kernel Trick:** Replaces inner products in feature space with a function evaluated in input space; computationally efficient and mathematically equivalent.
- **Most Common Kernels:** Linear, Polynomial, RBF (Gaussian), Sigmoid, Laplacian.
- **Mercer's Condition:** Gram matrix must be positive semi-definite — required for valid kernels.
- **RBF = Infinite Dimensions:** Gaussian RBF induces an infinite-dimensional feature space; explicit $\phi$ is **not representable**.
- **Dual Form is Solved:** $\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ subject to $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.
- **Hyperparameter $C$:** Controls margin-violation tolerance — large $C$ = hard margin (overfit), small $C$ = soft margin (underfit).
- **Hyperparameter $\gamma$:** RBF width — large $\gamma$ = narrow kernel (overfit), small $\gamma$ = wide kernel (underfit).
- **Support Vectors:** Only training points with $\alpha_i > 0$ matter at test time; sparsity of $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ is a key SVM property.
- **Decision Function:** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i \in \mathcal{S}} \alpha_i^* y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b^*\right)$.
- **Polynomial Feature Dimension:** For degree $d$ in $n$ dimensions, $\dim(\mathcal{H}) = \binom{n+d}{d}$.
- **Default in Practice:** RBF kernel is the most widely used; tune $C$ and $\gamma$ via cross-validation grid search.
- **Loss Function:** Hinge loss $\max(0, 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$ with $\ell_2$ regularization is the soft-margin SVM objective.
- **Computation:** Kernel evaluation is $O(n)$ where $n$ is input dimension — independent of feature space dimension.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kernels for Learning Non-linear Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kernels for Learning Non-linear Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 – Kernels for Learning Non-linear Functions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In supervised learning, a **kernel** is a symmetric, real-valued function $K(x, x')$ that implicitly computes an inner product between two input vectors after they have been mapped into a (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space through a non-linear transformation $\phi(\cdot)$. Formally, a kernel satisfies:

$$K(x, x') = \langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle = \phi(x)^{\top}\phi(x')$$

The **kernel trick** is the methodological insight that any learning algorithm expressed purely in terms of dot products $x^{\top}x'$ in the input space can be lifted to operate in the high-dimensional feature space **without explicitly computing** the coordinates of $\phi(x)$. We only need to know the kernel values $K(x, x')$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Definition to Memorise**
> A *kernel* is a function $K : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that for all $x, x' \in \mathcal{X}$, $K(x, x') = \phi(x)^{\top}\phi(x')$ for some feature map $\phi : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{F}$ into an inner-product feature space $\mathcal{F}$. This is sometimes called the **inner-product representation** of a kernel.

### 1.2 Why Do We Need Kernels? — The Linearity Bottleneck

Many classical learners — Linear Regression, the Perceptron, Logistic Regression, the (linear) Support Vector Machine — are **linear models**: their decision functions take the form $f(x) = w^{\top}x + b$. Such a model can only draw a straight hyperplane in the input space. Real-world data, however, is rarely linearly separable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concrete Counter-Example**
> Consider the XOR problem. The four 2-D points $(0,0)$, $(0,1)$, $(1,0)$, $(1,1)$ with class labels $+1, -1, +1, -1$ are **not separable by any straight line**. A linear classifier will fail with $\geq 25\%$ error regardless of orientation.

The classical pre-kernel fix is to *manually engineer* non-linear features (e.g., use $x_1 \cdot x_2$ as an extra feature). For XOR, adding the feature $x_1 x_2$ immediately makes the data linearly separable. The question is: **how do we do this automatically and at scale?**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lifting to a Higher Floor" Intuition

Imagine you are an architect standing on the ground floor with a tangled red rope on the floor. You cannot cut the rope — you can only slip a straight pole beneath it to separate the red from the blue string.

- **Without lifting:** The pole lies flat on the floor and you cannot separate the tangled strands — *this is a linear classifier failing on non-linear data*.
- **With a lift:** You call the elevator, raise the red strand up to the second floor by passing it through vertical poles (the feature map $\phi$), and now from above you can slide a flat plank between the red and blue without any further effort.

**Mapping $\phi$** is the elevator; **kernel $K$** is the disembodied voice that tells you the exact height to raise each rope segment so that you never have to visit the upper floor in person. That disembodied voice is the **kernel trick**.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU-Exam-Relevant Vocabulary)

- **Input Space $\mathcal{X}$** — the original space in which data lives (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^d$).
- **Feature Space $\mathcal{F}$** — a (possibly infinite-dimensional) inner-product space induced by $\phi$.
- **Feature Map $\phi : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{F}$** — the non-linear transformation that lifts data.
- **Positive Definite Kernel** — a kernel whose Gram matrix is positive semi-definite for every finite subset of points.
- **Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS)** — the Hilbert space of functions spanned by the kernel, in which $K$ acts as a reproducing kernel: $\langle f, K(\cdot, x) \rangle_{\mathcal{H}} = f(x)$.
- **Mercer's Theorem** — the classical condition characterising valid kernels in terms of eigenfunction expansions.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Examiners at KTU frequently ask the difference between a *feature map* and a *kernel*. Memorise that $\phi$ is the *transformation*, whereas $K$ is the *inner product of transformed vectors*. Many students incorrectly write $K(x,x') = \phi(x) + \phi(x')$.

### 1.5 Historical Context

The kernel idea traces to Aizerman, Braverman & Rozonoér (1964) in the potential-function method for pattern recognition. It was popularised in machine learning by Boser, Guyon & Vapnik (1992) in the landmark **kernelized SVM** (the "maximal margin classifier"). Modern kernel methods underpin Gaussian Processes, Kernel Ridge Regression, Kernel PCA and many deep-learning-inspired kernels such as the neural-tangent kernel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept (Year Repeated):** "Differentiate between the feature map and the kernel trick." Always mention that the feature map is *explicit* (computes $\phi(x)$), whereas the kernel trick is *implicit* (computes only the inner products).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Kernelised Learning

A kernelised learner is constructed in four logical steps:

1. **Choose a linear algorithm $\mathcal{A}$** that depends on data only through dot products $x_i^{\top}x_j$. Examples: Perceptron update, SVM dual, linear regression normal equation, PCA covariance eigen-decomposition.
2. **Identify the dot-product locations** in the algorithm — usually a place where $w = \sum_i \alpha_i x_i$ (the *representer theorem* in action) and predictions become $f(x) = \sum_i \alpha_i x_i^{\top}x + b$.
3. **Replace every dot product $x_i^{\top}x_j$** by a kernel call $K(x_i, x_j)$. This is the *substitution step* that *operationally defines the kernel trick*.
4. **Choose a positive-definite kernel** $K$ that suits the problem (polynomial for interactions, RBF for smooth local behaviour, string kernel for text, graph kernel for molecules).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The representer theorem guarantees that, for any regularised empirical-risk minimiser over an RKHS, the optimal $w$ has the form $\sum_i \alpha_i \phi(x_i)$. The decision function becomes $f(x) = \sum_i \alpha_i K(x_i, x) + b$, a *finite* sum over training data — even when $\mathcal{F}$ is infinite-dimensional.

## 2.2 Constructing Valid Kernels — The Algebra of Kernels

Given two valid kernels $K_1, K_2$ and a constant $c \geq 0$, the following are also valid (positive-definite) kernels:

- $K(x, x') = c \cdot K_1(x, x')$
- $K(x, x') = K_1(x, x') + K_2(x, x')$
- $K(x, x') = K_1(x, x') \cdot K_2(x, x')$
- $K(x, x') = f(x) \cdot K_1(x, x') \cdot f(x')$ for any scalar function $f$
- $K(x, x') = K_3(\psi(x), \psi(x'))$ for any kernel $K_3$ and feature map $\psi$

These closure rules let us design complex kernels by composing simple ones — critical for KTU numerical/design problems.

## 2.3 Mercer's Theorem (Kernel Validity Condition)

A symmetric function $K : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a valid (positive-definite) kernel **if and only if** its Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is positive semi-definite for **every** finite set $\{x_1, \dots, x_n\} \subset \mathcal{X}$.

Equivalently (Mercer's classical continuous form):

$$\iint K(x, x') \, g(x) g(x') \, dx \, dx' \geq 0 \quad \forall\, g \in L_2(\mathcal{X})$$

which admits an eigen-expansion:

$$K(x, x') = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \lambda_k \, \psi_k(x) \psi_k(x')$$

with $\lambda_k \geq 0$. The functions $\psi_k$ form an orthonormal basis of $\mathcal{F}$, and the map $\phi(x) = (\sqrt{\lambda_1}\psi_1(x), \sqrt{\lambda_2}\psi_2(x), \dots)$ is the corresponding feature embedding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** KTU examiners love testing whether candidates realise that *any* function can be plugged into the kernel position — but only **positive-definite** kernels guarantee convex optimisation. An invalid kernel can lead to a non-convex problem with multiple local minima.

## 2.4 The Gram Matrix (Kernel Matrix)

For training data $\{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$, the **Gram matrix** $\mathbf{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ has entries:

$$\mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$$

Properties that always hold for valid kernels:
- Symmetry: $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{K}^{\top}$
- Positive semi-definiteness: $c^{\top}\mathbf{K}c \geq 0$ for all $c \in \mathbb{R}^n$
- Diagonal entries: $\mathbf{K}_{ii} = K(x_i, x_i) = \langle \phi(x_i), \phi(x_i) \rangle = \|\phi(x_i)\|^2 \geq 0$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Kernel Name | Mathematical Form $K(x, x')$ | Implicit Feature Map $\phi(x)$ | Use Case |
|---|-------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Linear** | $x^{\top} x'$ | $x$ | Linear baseline |
| 2 | **Homogeneous Polynomial (degree $d$)** | $(x^{\top} x')^d$ | All monomials of total degree exactly $d$ | Polynomial interactions |
| 3 | **Inhomogeneous Polynomial** | $(x^{\top} x' + c)^d$ | Monomials up to degree $d$ (with bias $c > 0$) | General polynomial regression |
| 4 | **Gaussian / RBF (width $\sigma$)** | $\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\|x - x'\|^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Infinite-dimensional | Universal smoother, default choice |
| 5 | **Laplacian** | $\exp\!\left(-\gamma\|x - x'\|_1\right)$ | Infinite-dimensional | Sharp, sparse similarity |
| 6 | **Sigmoid / Tanh** | $\tanh(\kappa \, x^{\top} x' + \theta)$ | Not always PD — use with caution | Shallow NN analogy |
| 7 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\dfrac{x^{\top} x'}{\|x\| \cdot \|x'\|}$ | Normalised vectors | Text / TF-IDF data |
| 8 | **String / Spectrum** | Counts common substrings | Substring histogram | DNA / NLP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Note:** Within markdown tables the symbol $\vert$ is rendered as a vertical bar. To avoid LaTeX/markdown parsing collisions, write norms as $\|x - x'\|^2$ (using `\|` and `\|`) **not** as $\mid x - x' \mid^2$ in exam answer sheets. KTU examiners deduct half-a-mark for ambiguous notation.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Kernel Methods

- **Bioinformatics:** String kernels for DNA/protein classification (SVM-based remote homology detection).
- **Computer Vision:** Intersection kernels on histograms of visual words.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Tree kernels for parse-tree classification in relation extraction.
- **Anomaly Detection:** One-class SVMs with RBF kernels.
- **Time-Series:** Global alignment kernels for sequence classification.
- **Recommendation Systems:** Implicit-feedback matrix factorisation as a kernel method.

## 2.7 Computational Complexity — The Practical Trade-Off

| Phase | Linear Model | Kernel Model |
|-------|--------------|--------------|
| Training memory | $O(d)$ for $w$ | $O(n^2)$ for Gram matrix |
| Training time | $O(nd)$ | $O(n^2 d)$ or $O(n^3)$ |
| Inference time per query | $O(d)$ | $O(n d)$ — scales with training set |
| Inference work-around | N/A | Approximate methods: Nyström, Random Fourier Features, Inducing Points (Sparse GPs) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters at KTU:** Numerical problems often ask you to compute the Gram matrix and its eigenvalues for tiny datasets (say $n=3$). Practice this until it is second nature — examiners reward neat intermediate steps.
