- **Use case:** Set-based data such as market-basket analysis ("customers who bought A also bought B"). Defined for **binary** or **set-valued** features.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table mentally before every KTU exam.** It is the single most-tested sheet in Module 2.

| # | Measure Name | Mathematical Definition (Vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$) | Output Range | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Euclidean** | $d_2 = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2 \right)^{1/2}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Continuous, scaled numeric data |
| 2 | **Squared Euclidean** | $d_2^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Faster computation (no square root) |
| 3 | **Manhattan** | $d_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | High-dimensional sparse data |
| 4 | **Minkowski (General)** | $d_p = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Tuning distance geometry via $p$ |
| 5 | **Chebyshev** | $d_\infty = \max_{i} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Chessboard, warehouse logistics |
| 6 | **Cosine Similarity** | $s_c = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \, \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert} = \dfrac{\sum x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum x_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum y_i^2}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Document / text similarity |
| 7 | **Cosine Distance** | $d_c = 1 - s_c$ | $[0, 2]$ | Convert cosine to a metric |
| 8 | **Jaccard Similarity** | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Binary / set data |
| 9 | **Pearson Correlation** | $\rho = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} \sqrt{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Trend similarity (ignores mean & scale) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Grouping patients by symptom vectors using Mahalanobis/Cosine to detect disease subtypes.
- **Anomaly Detection in IoT:** Chebyshev distance flags any sensor whose **single** reading deviates wildly, even if the average is normal.
- **Customer Segmentation in Marketing:** Jaccard identifies customers with overlapping purchase baskets, enabling collaborative filtering.
- **Image Compression:** K-Means clustering with Euclidean distance groups visually similar pixels into a reduced palette.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently-Asked Pitfall:** Cosine similarity is *not* a true metric (it violates triangle inequality in some spaces). Always phrase it as a "**similarity function**", not a distance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — The Master "Calculate-All" Numerical

This is the **single most important numerical** in KTU Module 2. It typically appears as a 7-mark sub-question. Memorize the structure.

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Pattern):**
> Given two 4-dimensional data points:
> $A = (1, 2, 3, 4)$ and $B = (4, 3, 2, 1)$,
> compute the **(i)** Euclidean, **(ii)** Manhattan, **(iii)** Chebyshev, and **(iv)** Cosine similarity between them.

### 3.1.1 Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute the coordinate-wise differences.**

$$\Delta_i = A_i - B_i = (1-4,\; 2-3,\; 3-2,\; 4-1) = (-3, -1, +1, +3)$$

**Step 2: Compute absolute differences (needed for $L_1$ and $L_\infty$).**

$$\vert \Delta_i \vert = (3, 1, 1, 3)$$

**Step 3: Compute squared differences (needed for $L_2$).**

$$\Delta_i^2 = (9, 1, 1, 9)$$

**Step 4: Apply the four distance formulas in parallel.**

**(i) Euclidean Distance:**

$$d_2(A, B) = \sqrt{9 + 1 + 1 + 9} = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.472$$

**(ii) Manhattan Distance:**

$$d_1(A, B) = 3 + 1 + 1 + 3 = 8$$

**(iii) Chebyshev Distance:**

$$d_\infty(A, B) = \max\{3, 1, 1, 3\} = 3$$

**(iv) Cosine Similarity:**

$$s_c(A, B) = \frac{(1)(4) + (2)(3) + (3)(2) + (4)(1)}{\sqrt{1+4+9+16} \cdot \sqrt{16+9+4+1}}$$

$$= \frac{4 + 6 + 6 + 4}{\sqrt{30} \cdot \sqrt{30}} = \frac{20}{30} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$$

**Cosine Distance:** $d_c = 1 - 2/3 = 1/3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice that the *same pair of points* yields wildly different similarity scores across measures. This is the **central pedagogical point** the KTU examiner wants to test: *the choice of measure changes the cluster assignment.*

## 3.2 Derivation — Minkowski Generalizes Euclidean & Manhattan

We now show **analytically** why Minkowski distance is the "parent" formula.

**Starting Point — Minkowski distance definition:**

$$d_p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$$

**Substitute $p = 1$:**

$$d_1 = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{1} \right)^{1/1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

This is precisely the **Manhattan distance** definition. (Verified.)

**Substitute $p = 2$:**

$$d_2 = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{2} \right)^{1/2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2}$$

This is precisely the **Euclidean distance** definition. (Verified.)

**Take the limit as $p \to \infty$:**

The dominant term inside the sum is the **largest** $\vert x_i - y_i \vert$. Let $M = \max_i \vert x_i - y_i \vert$. Then:

$$d_p = M \cdot \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{\vert x_i - y_i \vert}{M} \right)^{p} \right)^{1/p}$$

As $p \to \infty$, the term with $\vert x_i - y_i \vert = M$ contributes $1^p = 1$, and all other terms (ratios $<1$) vanish to $0$. So:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} d_p = M = \max_i \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

This is precisely the **Chebyshev distance**. (Verified.)

## 3.3 Proof Sketch — Triangle Inequality for Euclidean Distance

A frequent 3-mark question. The Minkowski family satisfies the triangle inequality in general; we illustrate for $p=2$.

**Claim:** $d_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \leq d_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) + d_2(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z})$ for all $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

**Proof sketch (using Cauchy–Schwarz):**

For each coordinate $i$, we have the scalar triangle inequality $\vert x_i - z_i \vert \leq \vert x_i - y_i \vert + \vert y_i - z_i \vert$. Summing the squares:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - z_i)^2 = \sum_{i} \big[ (x_i - y_i) + (y_i - z_i) \big]^2$$

Expanding the square inside the sum:

$$= \sum_{i} (x_i - y_i)^2 + 2 \sum_{i} (x_i - y_i)(y_i - z_i) + \sum_{i} (y_i - z_i)^2$$

By the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**, the middle term is bounded:

$$2 \sum_{i} (x_i - y_i)(y_i - z_i) \leq 2 \sqrt{\sum_{i} (x_i - y_i)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i} (y_i - z_i)^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\sum_{i} (x_i - z_i)^2 \leq \left( \sqrt{\sum_{i} (x_i - y_i)^2} + \sqrt{\sum_{i} (y_i - z_i)^2} \right)^2$$

Taking the square root of both sides gives the desired triangle inequality. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What

| Property / Use Case | Euclidean | Manhattan | Minkowski | Cosine | Jaccard |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Satisfies all 4 metric axioms | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ |
| Scale-invariant | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Handles categorical data | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ |
| Robust to outliers | ✘ | ✔ | Partial | ✔ | ✔ |
| Suitable for sparse high-D data | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ (low $p$) | ✔ | ✔ |
| Computationally cheapest | Medium | High | Medium-High | High | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Similarity-Based Clustering

The following Mermaid diagram traces the **decision pipeline** a clustering algorithm follows when given a similarity measure. It shows how a raw data matrix flows through normalization, distance computation, and finally to cluster assignment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData[Raw Data Matrix Xn x d] --> norm[Normalization Step]
    norm --> choice{Measure Selection}
    choice -- Continuous Numeric --> mink[Minkowski Lp Norm]
    choice -- Text or Sparse --> cosine[Cosine Similarity]
    choice -- Binary or Set --> jaccard[Jaccard Index]
    choice -- Trends or Ratings --> pearson[Pearson Correlation]
    mink --> pTune{Choose p}
    pTune -- p = 1 --> manhattan[Manhattan L1]
    pTune -- p = 2 --> euclid[Euclidean L2]
    pTune -- p infinity --> cheby[Chebyshev L Infinity]
    manhattan --> matrix[Pairwise Distance Matrix D]
    euclid --> matrix
    cheby --> matrix
    cosine --> matrix
    jaccard --> matrix
    pearson --> matrix
    matrix --> algo{Clustering Algorithm}
    algo -- k value --> kmeans[K Means or K Medoids]
    algo -- hierarchical --> hier[Agglomerative Hierarchical]
    algo -- density --> dbscan[DBSCAN or OPTICS]
    kmeans --> out1[Cluster Labels C1 to Ck]
    hier --> out1
    dbscan --> out1
    out1 --> eval[Validation Silhouette Davies Bouldin]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Intuition of the Minkowski Unit Ball

The following Mermaid diagram is a *functional map* explaining how the **shape** of the "equidistant region" changes with $p$. (Mermaid cannot literally draw a contour plot, so we map each $p$ to its resulting geometry.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Parameter p]
    p1 -- p equals 1 --> shape1[Unit Ball Shape: Diamond]
    p1 -- p equals 2 --> shape2[Unit Ball Shape: Circle]
    p1 -- p greater than 2 --> shape3[Unit Ball Shape: Rounded Square]
    p1 -- p approaches infinity --> shape4[Unit Ball Shape: Axis Aligned Square]
    shape1 --> prop1[Allows Diagonal Shortcuts]
    shape2 --> prop2[Standard Cartesian Geometry]
    shape3 --> prop3[Heavier Penalty on Outliers]
    shape4 --> prop4[Only the Largest Coordinate Difference Matters]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — "Which Measure Should I Use?"

This is the **exam-ready** decision tree students should reproduce from memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Choose a Similarity Measure]
    start --> q1{Is your data Continuous Numeric?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Are features on same scale?}
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Need outlier robustness?}
    q3 -- Yes --> man[Use Manhattan L1]
    q3 -- No --> eu[Use Euclidean L2]
    q2 -- No --> norm[Normalize then use Euclidean or Cosine]
    q1 -- No --> q4{Is data Categorical or Set?}
    q4 -- Yes --> jc[Use Jaccard Coefficient]
    q4 -- No --> q5{Is data Text or Sparse Vector?}
    q5 -- Yes --> cs[Use Cosine Similarity]
    q5 -- No --> q6{Trend Similarity Required?}
    q6 -- Yes --> pc[Use Pearson Correlation]
    q6 -- No --> fb[Default to Euclidean after Scaling]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked to "explain similarity measures with a diagram", drawing the **Minkowski unit-ball shapes** (diamond → circle → square) instantly earns you full marks even if the rest of the answer is short.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]`
**Define a similarity measure. List and briefly explain the four properties that a function must satisfy to be a valid distance metric. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (to be written in ~3 minutes):**

A **similarity measure** is a scalar-valued function $s(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ that quantifies the resemblance between two data points, where a larger value indicates greater similarity. Its dual, the **distance (dissimilarity) measure** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$, returns a larger value for less similar points.

For $d$ to be a true **metric**, it must satisfy:
1. **Non-negativity:** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \geq 0$.
2. **Identity of indiscernibles:** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = 0 \iff \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$.
3. **Symmetry:** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = d(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x})$.
4. **Triangle inequality:** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \leq d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) + d(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z})$.

Functions that violate any one of these (e.g., Cosine similarity) are called **dissimilarity measures** but are not strict metrics.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam Pattern]`
**Differentiate between Euclidean distance and Cosine similarity. In which scenario is each most appropriate? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Euclidean Distance | Cosine Similarity |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | $\dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert}$ |
| Output range | $[0, \infty)$ | $[-1, +1]$ |
| Measures | Magnitude of difference | Orientation (angle) between vectors |
| Scale-sensitive? | **Yes** | **No** (scale-invariant) |
| Ideal scenario | Comparing physical measurements (e.g., height, weight) on the same scale | Comparing text documents (TF-IDF) where vector *direction* (topic) matters more than length |

**Key idea:** Use Euclidean when magnitude carries meaning; use Cosine when only the *pattern* (direction) matters.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]`

> **Q A (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the Minkowski distance metric. Show mathematically how Euclidean ($L_2$) and Manhattan ($L_1$) distances are special cases of the Minkowski family. Compute the Minkowski distance of order $p=3$ between points $P = (2, 5, 1)$ and $Q = (1, 3, 4)$.

> **Q A (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of using Euclidean distance in high-dimensional clustering. Briefly explain how Manhattan and Cosine measures overcome these limitations.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Step 1 — Define Minkowski distance:**

$$d_p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}, \quad p \geq 1$$

**Step 2 — Special case $p=1$:** Substituting $p=1$ in the formula:

$$d_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert \quad \text{(Manhattan / City-Block distance)}$$

**Step 3 — Special case $p=2$:** Substituting $p=2$:

$$d_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2} \quad \text{(Euclidean distance)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $d_3$ for the given points:**

Coordinate-wise differences: $\Delta = (2-1,\; 5-3,\; 1-4) = (1, 2, -3)$.
Absolute differences cubed: $\vert \Delta \vert^3 = (1^3, 2^3, 3^3) = (1, 8, 27)$.

$$d_3 = (1 + 8 + 27)^{1/3} = 36^{1/3} \approx 3.302$$

**Answer:** $d_3(P, Q) \approx 3.30$.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q A (b)

**Disadvantages of Euclidean distance in high dimensions:**

1. **Curse of Dimensionality:** As $d \to \infty$, the ratio of the distance to the nearest neighbor and the farthest neighbor approaches $1$, making the metric **uninformative**.
2. **Sensitivity to Scale:** Features with large numerical ranges dominate, distorting clusters unless **standardization (z-score)** is applied first.
3. **Outlier Sensitivity:** Squaring amplifies large errors, pulling centroids toward noise points in K-Means.
4. **Sparsity Issue:** In high-D sparse data (e.g., text), Euclidean distance between almost-all-zeros vectors is nearly constant and uninformative.

**How alternatives overcome these:**

- **Manhattan ($L_1$):** Uses absolute differences, so outliers contribute *linearly* instead of quadratically → more robust. In sparse high-D spaces, $L_1$ retains discriminative power better than $L_2$.
- **Cosine Similarity:** Ignores magnitude entirely and focuses on angular separation, making it ideal for sparse TF-IDF document vectors where the *topic* (direction) is what matters.

---

### ⭐ Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

> **Q B (a) [7 Marks]:** Define Cosine similarity and Jaccard similarity. Compute both for the following two sets of binary vectors representing customer purchases:
> $A = (1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1)$ and $B = (1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0)$.

> **Q B (b) [7 Marks]:** What is the role of normalization before applying a distance metric? Explain Min-Max scaling and Z-score standardization with formulas. Why is normalization mandatory for Euclidean but not for Cosine similarity?

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Step 1 — Cosine similarity formula:**

$$s_c(A, B) = \frac{A \cdot B}{\Vert A \Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert}$$

**Step 2 — Compute dot product:** $A \cdot B = (1)(1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(0) + (1)(0) = 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 2$.

**Step 3 — Compute norms:**

$$\Vert A \Vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$

$$\Vert B \Vert = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{3}$$

**Step 4 — Final cosine value:**

$$s_c(A, B) = \frac{2}{2 \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774$$

**Step 5 — Jaccard similarity:** Treat $1$s as elements in a set:
$A_{set} = \{1, 3, 4, 6\}$, $B_{set} = \{1, 2, 4\}$.
$A \cap B = \{1, 4\} \Rightarrow \vert A \cap B \vert = 2$.
$A \cup B = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 6\} \Rightarrow \vert A \cup B \vert = 5$.

$$J(A, B) = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

**Final Answers:** Cosine $\approx 0.577$, Jaccard $= 0.40$.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Role of Normalization:**
To bring all features to a **common scale**, preventing features with intrinsically larger numerical ranges from dominating the distance calculation.

**Min-Max Scaling (range $[0, 1]$):**

$$x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

**Z-score Standardization (zero mean, unit variance):**

$$x' = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

where $\mu$ is the feature mean and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation.

**Why mandatory for Euclidean, not for Cosine:**

- **Euclidean** uses **raw magnitudes** of coordinate differences. If one feature is in $[0, 1000]$ and another in $[0, 1]$, the first will overwhelm the sum of squares. Hence scaling is **mandatory**.
- **Cosine** uses the **ratio** of dot product to product of norms. The norms in the denominator automatically rescale both vectors to unit length, neutralizing magnitude differences. Hence Cosine is **inherently scale-invariant**, and pre-normalization is unnecessary (though still often applied for numerical stability).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Similarity Measure questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Cosine Similarity with Cosine Distance.** They are *not* the same; Cosine Distance $= 1 - $ Cosine Similarity. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the $p$-th root** in Minkowski calculations. Writing $d_p = \sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p$ without the outer $1/p$ exponent is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Saying Cosine is a "distance metric".** It is a *similarity* function; it does **not** satisfy the triangle inequality in general. The correct phrase is "**angular similarity measure**".
> 4. **Skipping the absolute value** in $L_1$ distance — a negative sign shows incomplete understanding. Always write $\vert x_i - y_i \vert$.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** when applying a measure (e.g., "assuming features are on the same scale and independent"). Examiners reward explicit assumptions with 1 extra mark.
> 6. **Jaccard on continuous data** — Jaccard is defined for **sets/binary vectors**. Using it on continuous floats is conceptually wrong and loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Minkowski distance** is the umbrella formula; $p=1 \Rightarrow$ Manhattan, $p=2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean, $p \to \infty \Rightarrow$ Chebyshev.
- A true **metric** must satisfy **all four axioms**: non-negativity, identity, symmetry, triangle inequality.
- **Euclidean** is the default for continuous data of the same scale; **Manhattan** is more robust to outliers; **Chebyshev** captures the worst-case single feature.
- **Cosine similarity** measures *angular orientation* between vectors, ignoring magnitude; ideal for text/TF-IDF. Range: $[-1, +1]$.
- **Jaccard similarity** is for binary/set data: $J = \vert A \cap B \vert / \vert A \cup B \vert$. Range: $[0, 1]$.
- **Pearson correlation** captures *trend similarity* by removing the mean; insensitive to linear transformations of inputs.
- **Normalization** is mandatory for Euclidean/Manhattan; redundant (but numerically safe) for Cosine.
- The choice of similarity measure **directly determines** cluster boundaries in K-Means, hierarchical clustering, and DBSCAN.
- Cosine similarity is **not a true metric** — phrase it as a "similarity function".
- The most frequently tested numerical in KTU is the **"compute all four"** type (Euclidean + Manhattan + Chebyshev + Cosine) on a 2- or 4-dimensional vector pair.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC)** is a **bottom-up unsupervised learning algorithm** that builds a hierarchy of clusters by iteratively merging the two **most similar** (least distant) clusters at each step, starting from singleton clusters (each data point is its own cluster) and ending with a single cluster containing all observations. The output is typically represented as a **dendrogram** — a tree-like diagram that records the sequence of merges and the inter-cluster distance (or dissimilarity) at which each merge occurred.

Formally, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$, HAC produces a sequence of partitions $\mathcal{P}_0, \mathcal{P}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{P}_{n-1}$ where:
- $\mathcal{P}_0 = \{\{x_1\}, \{x_2\}, \ldots, \{x_n\}\}$ (n singleton clusters)
- $\mathcal{P}_{n-1} = \{\mathcal{D}\}$ (one cluster)
- $\mathcal{P}_{k+1}$ is obtained from $\mathcal{P}_k$ by merging the two clusters in $\mathcal{P}_k$ that minimize a chosen **linkage criterion** $L(\cdot, \cdot)$.

The algorithm belongs to the **deterministic**, **greedy**, and **non-iterative-refinement** family of clustering methods — once two points/clusters are merged, they are **never split** in subsequent steps.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Reunion"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine 5 strangers standing in a room. The two people who are *most similar* (e.g., same height, same outfit color) shake hands and form a small pair. Next, the pair finds the next-most-similar person (or another pair) and merges into a larger group. This process continues until the entire room is one big group. Now imagine a tape recorder logs *at what moment* each merge happened and *how similar* the merged members were. Playing back the recording from the end to the beginning gives you a **family tree (dendrogram)** of the clustering process.

This "tape recorder" is exactly the dendrogram — its **vertical axis** represents dissimilarity, and the **height at which two branches join** is the distance at which those clusters merged.

## 1.3 Why Hierarchical? Why Agglomerative?

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Hierarchical** | The output is a multi-level tree of clusters, not a single flat partition. |
| **Agglomerative** | Uses a *bottom-up* merging strategy (Latin *agglo-merare* = "to form into a ball"). |
| **Divisive** (contrast) | The *opposite* strategy — starts with one cluster and recursively splits. |

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary

> [!NOTE]
> - **Linkage Criterion $L$** — the rule that defines the distance between two clusters (single, complete, average, Ward, centroid).
> - **Proximity / Distance Matrix $D$** — an $n \times n$ symmetric matrix holding pairwise distances between points or clusters.
> - **Dendrogram** — the binary tree that visually encodes the merging history.
> - **Cophenetic Distance** — the dissimilarity level at which two points are first joined in the dendrogram; used to assess clustering quality.
> - **Cutting the Dendrogram** — choosing a horizontal threshold that defines the final flat partition (e.g., $k$ clusters = number of vertical lines crossed by the cut).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic (Step-by-Step)

The agglomerative procedure is a strict pipeline. Each step depends on the previous one.

1. **Initialization:** Start with $n$ singleton clusters, one per data point: $C_i^{(0)} = \{x_i\}$.
2. **Compute Proximity Matrix:** Build the symmetric matrix $D^{(0)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ with entries $D_{ij}^{(0)} = \delta(x_i, x_j)$, where $\delta$ is the chosen pointwise distance.
3. **Iterative Merging Loop (Repeat Steps 3a–3c until only 1 cluster remains):**
   - **3a.** Search the current proximity matrix for the minimum off-diagonal entry: $(p, q) = \arg\min_{i \neq j} D_{ij}^{(k)}$.
   - **3b.** Merge clusters $p$ and $q$ into a new cluster $r = p \cup q$, and record the merge height $h_k = D_{pq}^{(k)}$ in the dendrogram.
   - **3c.** Update the proximity matrix by computing the distance from $r$ to every remaining cluster $s$ using the chosen **linkage formula** $L(C_r, C_s)$.
4. **Termination:** Stop when $k = n-1$ merges have been recorded and a single cluster $\mathcal{D}$ remains.

## 2.2 Linkage Criteria — The Four Pillars

For two clusters $A$ and $B$ containing points $\{a_1, \ldots, a_m\}$ and $\{b_1, \ldots, b_p\}$:

### 2.2.1 Single Linkage (MIN)
$$
L_{\text{single}}(A, B) \;=\; \min_{a \in A,\, b \in B} \delta(a, b)
$$

- Connects clusters by their **closest pair of points**. 
- Tends to produce **chaining** — long, elongated, snake-like clusters.
- Computationally cheap; equivalent to computing the **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** of the data.

### 2.2.2 Complete Linkage (MAX)
$$
L_{\text{complete}}(A, B) \;=\; \max_{a \in A,\, b \in B} \delta(a, b)
$$

- Connects clusters by their **farthest pair of points**.
- Tends to produce **compact, tight, spherical** clusters of similar diameter.
- Sensitive to outliers because the *maximum* is dominated by the worst pair.

### 2.2.3 Average Linkage (UPGMA)
$$
L_{\text{average}}(A, B) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert} \sum_{a \in A}\sum_{b \in B} \delta(a, b)
$$

- Averages all inter-point distances between the two clusters.
- A balanced compromise between single and complete linkage.
- **UPGMA** = Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic mean.

### 2.2.4 Ward's Linkage
$$
L_{\text{Ward}}(A, B) \;=\; \sqrt{\, \frac{2 \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}\,} \cdot \bigl\| \mu_A - \mu_B \bigr\|
$$

- Minimizes the **increase in total within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS / inertia)** upon merging.
- Equivalent to:
$$
\Delta \text{WCSS} \;=\; \frac{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \cdot \bigl\| \mu_A - \mu_B \bigr\|^2
$$
- Tends to produce clusters of similar size; the default in many statistical packages.

### 2.2.5 Centroid Linkage (for completeness)
$$
L_{\text{centroid}}(A, B) \;=\; \bigl\| \mu_A - \mu_B \bigr\|
$$

- Uses Euclidean distance between the two cluster centroids $\mu_A, \mu_B$. Can suffer from **inversions** in the dendrogram.

## 2.3 Pointwise Distance Metrics $\delta(\cdot, \cdot)$

| Metric | Formula | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean ($\ell_2$)** | $\delta(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Straight-line (L²) distance. Default in K-means, HAC. |
| **Manhattan / City-block ($\ell_1$)** | $\delta(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Sum of axis-aligned distances. Robust to outliers. |
| **Minkowski ($\ell_p$)** | $\delta(x, y) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p \right)^{1/p}$ | Generalization: $p=1 \to$ Manhattan, $p=2 \to$ Euclidean. |
| **Cosine Distance** | $\delta(x, y) = 1 - \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \, \|y\|}$ | Angular distance on the unit hypersphere. Used for text. |
| **Mahalanobis** | $\delta(x, y) = \sqrt{(x-y)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (x-y)}$ | Accounts for feature covariance $\Sigma$. Scale-invariant. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units:** All distances are dimensionless (raw units of the input features) unless data are standardized. For Mahalanobis, the result is dimensionless because $\Sigma^{-1}$ has units of $1/(\text{unit}^2)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| $D^{(0)}_{ij} = \delta(x_i, x_j)$ | Initial $n \times n$ distance matrix | Algorithm start |
| $L_{\text{single}}(A,B) = \min_{a\in A, b\in B} \delta(a,b)$ | Nearest-neighbour merge rule | Chaining-prone data |
| $L_{\text{complete}}(A,B) = \max_{a\in A, b\in B} \delta(a,b)$ | Farthest-neighbour merge rule | Compact clusters |
| $L_{\text{average}}(A,B) = \frac{1}{\vert A\vert \cdot \vert B\vert}\sum \sum \delta$ | Mean inter-point distance | Balanced default |
| $L_{\text{Ward}} \propto \sqrt{\frac{2 \vert A\vert \vert B\vert}{\vert A\vert + \vert B\vert}} \, \|\mu_A - \mu_B\|$ | Variance-minimizing merge | Equal-size clusters |
| $\mu_C = \frac{1}{\vert C \vert}\sum_{x \in C} x$ | Centroid of cluster $C$ | Required by centroid/Ward |
| Time Complexity $\;=\; \mathcal{O}(n^3)$ | Naive implementation | Infeasible for $n > 10^4$ |
| Space Complexity $\;=\; \mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Distance matrix storage | Memory-bound |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics / Genomics** — building phylogenetic trees of species or gene-expression dendrograms.
- **Document clustering / NLP** — hierarchical topic trees, news aggregation.
- **Image segmentation** — region-merging at multiple granularities.
- **Social network analysis** — community detection at multiple resolutions.
- **Anomaly detection** — points that join the dendrogram at very large heights are outliers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Single Linkage, Euclidean Distance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dataset:** Five 2-D points. We will perform HAC with **single linkage** and **Euclidean distance**, then draw the resulting dendrogram.

$$
A = (2, 10), \quad B = (2, 5), \quad C = (8, 4), \quad D = (5, 8), \quad E = (7, 5)
$$

### 3.1.1 Step 0 — Compute the Initial $5 \times 5$ Proximity Matrix

We compute every pairwise Euclidean distance $\delta(x, y) = \sqrt{(x_1-y_1)^2 + (x_2-y_2)^2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(A,B) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (10-5)^2} = \sqrt{0+25} = 5.00 \\
\delta(A,C) &= \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (10-4)^2} = \sqrt{36+36} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49 \\
\delta(A,D) &= \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (10-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61 \\
\delta(A,E) &= \sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (10-5)^2} = \sqrt{25+25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07 \\
\delta(B,C) &= \sqrt{(2-8)^2 + (5-4)^2} = \sqrt{36+1} = \sqrt{37} \approx 6.08 \\
\delta(B,D) &= \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (5-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24 \\
\delta(B,E) &= \sqrt{(2-7)^2 + (5-5)^2} = \sqrt{25+0} = 5.00 \\
\delta(C,D) &= \sqrt{(8-5)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00 \\
\delta(C,E) &= \sqrt{(8-7)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \\
\delta(D,E) &= \sqrt{(5-7)^2 + (8-5)^2} = \sqrt{4+9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61
\end{aligned}
$$

The symmetric proximity matrix is:

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C & D & E \\
A & 0 & 5.00 & 8.49 & 3.61 & 7.07 \\
B & 5.00 & 0 & 6.08 & 4.24 & 5.00 \\
C & 8.49 & 6.08 & 0 & 5.00 & \mathbf{1.41} \\
D & 3.61 & 4.24 & 5.00 & 0 & 3.61 \\
E & 7.07 & 5.00 & \mathbf{1.41} & 3.61 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.2 Iteration 1 — Find the Minimum Distance

$$
\min_{i \neq j} D^{(0)}_{ij} \;=\; 1.41 \;=\; \delta(C, E)
$$

**Merge-1:** Combine $C$ and $E$ into a new cluster $CE$. Record **merge height** $h_1 = 1.41$.

### 3.1.3 Update the Matrix Using Single Linkage

For every remaining singleton cluster $s \in \{A, B, D\}$, the new distance is:

$$
L_{\text{single}}(CE, s) \;=\; \min\{\delta(C, s),\; \delta(E, s)\}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(CE, A) &= \min\{\delta(C,A),\, \delta(E,A)\} = \min\{8.49,\, 7.07\} = 7.07 \\
\delta(CE, B) &= \min\{\delta(C,B),\, \delta(E,B)\} = \min\{6.08,\, 5.00\} = 5.00 \\
\delta(CE, D) &= \min\{\delta(C,D),\, \delta(E,D)\} = \min\{5.00,\, 3.61\} = 3.61
\end{aligned}
$$

Other distances are unchanged. The new $4 \times 4$ matrix is:

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & D & CE \\
A & 0 & 5.00 & 3.61 & 7.07 \\
B & 5.00 & 0 & 4.24 & 5.00 \\
D & 3.61 & 4.24 & 0 & 3.61 \\
CE & 7.07 & 5.00 & 3.61 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.4 Iteration 2 — Find the Minimum Distance

$$
\min_{i \neq j} D^{(1)}_{ij} \;=\; 3.61 \quad \text{(ties at } A \text{–} D \text{ and } D \text{–} CE\text{)}
$$

**Tie-breaking rule:** Choose alphabetically first pair $(A, D)$.

**Merge-2:** Combine $A$ and $D$ into $AD$. Record $h_2 = 3.61$.

### 3.1.5 Update the Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(AD, B) &= \min\{\delta(A,B),\, \delta(D,B)\} = \min\{5.00,\, 4.24\} = 4.24 \\
\delta(AD, CE) &= \min\{\delta(A,CE),\, \delta(D,CE)\} = \min\{7.07,\, 3.61\} = 3.61
\end{aligned}
$$

The new $3 \times 3$ matrix is:

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & B & AD & CE \\
B & 0 & 4.24 & 5.00 \\
AD & 4.24 & 0 & 3.61 \\
CE & 5.00 & 3.61 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.6 Iteration 3 — Find the Minimum Distance

$$
\min_{i \neq j} D^{(2)}_{ij} \;=\; 3.61 \;=\; \delta(AD, CE)
$$

**Merge-3:** Combine $AD$ and $CE$ into a super-cluster $ADCE$. Record $h_3 = 3.61$.

### 3.1.7 Update the Matrix

$$
\delta(ADCE, B) \;=\; \min\{\delta(AD, B),\, \delta(CE, B)\} \;=\; \min\{4.24,\, 5.00\} \;=\; 4.24
$$

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & B & ADCE \\
B & 0 & 4.24 \\
ADCE & 4.24 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.1.8 Iteration 4 — Final Merge

$$
\min_{i \neq j} D^{(3)}_{ij} \;=\; 4.24
$$

**Merge-4:** Combine $B$ and $ADCE$ into the universal cluster $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$. Record $h_4 = 4.24$.

**Algorithm terminates** — $n-1 = 4$ merges completed.

## 3.2 Merge History Summary

| Step $k$ | Merged Pair | Merge Height $h_k$ | Resulting Clusters |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $(C, E)$ | **1.41** | $\{A\},\{B\},\{D\},\{CE\}$ |
| 2 | $(A, D)$ | **3.61** | $\{B\},\{AD\},\{CE\}$ |
| 3 | $(AD, CE)$ | **3.61** | $\{B\},\{ADCE\}$ |
| 4 | $(B, ADCE)$ | **4.24** | $\{ABCDE\}$ |

## 3.3 Derivation of Ward's Distance from First Principles

For two disjoint clusters $A$ and $B$, Ward defines the merge cost as the increase in total within-cluster sum of squares:

$$
\Delta(A, B) \;=\; \sum_{x \in A \cup B} \|x - \mu_{A \cup B}\|^2 \;-\; \sum_{x \in A} \|x - \mu_A\|^2 \;-\; \sum_{x \in B} \|x - \mu_B\|^2
$$

Using the identity $\sum_{x \in C} \|x - \mu\|^2 = \frac{1}{2 \vert C \vert} \sum_{x,y \in C} \|x - y\|^2$ and the parallelism theorem, this reduces to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta(A, B) &= \frac{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \, \bigl\| \mu_A - \mu_B \bigr\|^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding **distance** is the square root:

$$
L_{\text{Ward}}(A, B) \;=\; \sqrt{ \frac{2 \, \vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} } \; \bigl\| \mu_A - \mu_B \bigr\|
$$

The factor $\sqrt{2}$ is a convention so that $L_{\text{Ward}}$ is comparable in scale to the other linkages.

## 3.4 Time Complexity Derivation

- Outer loop runs $n-1$ times (we do $n-1$ merges).
- Each merge requires scanning an $\mathcal{O}(m^2)$ proximity matrix where $m$ is the current number of clusters, and updating $m$ entries.
- Total cost is dominated by the matrix scan at every step:

$$
T(n) \;=\; \sum_{m=2}^{n} \mathcal{O}(m^2) \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left( \sum_{m=1}^{n} m^2 \right) \;=\; \mathcal{O}\!\left( \frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6} \right) \;=\; \mathcal{O}(n^3)
$$

- With a priority queue (e.g., nearest-neighbour chain or SLINK), this reduces to $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$.

## 3.5 How to Choose $k$ from the Dendrogram

The dendrogram is cut at a chosen dissimilarity threshold $\tau$. The number of vertical lines crossed by a horizontal line at height $\tau$ equals the number of clusters $k$. A common heuristic: cut at the **largest vertical gap** between successive merge heights (the "elbow" of the dendrogram, analogous to the elbow method for K-means).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — HAC Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: n data points]) --> initA[Initialize n singleton clusters]
    initA --> compMatrix[Compute n x n proximity matrix D]
    compMatrix --> checkClusters{Number of clusters = 1?}
    checkClusters -- No --> findMin[Find min off-diagonal entry in D]
    findMin --> merge[Merge the two closest clusters]
    merge --> record[Record merge height in dendrogram]
    record --> updateLinkage[Update D using linkage rule L]
    updateLinkage --> checkClusters
    checkClusters -- Yes --> outputDendro[Output dendrogram]
    outputDendro --> cutStep[Cut dendrogram at threshold tau]
    cutStep --> finalK[Final partition with k clusters]
    finalK --> stopA([Stop])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Merge History of the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    leafA[A = 2,10]
    leafB[B = 2,5]
    leafC[C = 8,4]
    leafD[D = 5,8]
    leafE[E = 7,5]
    cluCE[Cluster CE]
    cluAD[Cluster AD]
    cluADCE[Cluster ADCE]
    rootABCDE[All Points ABCDE]

    leafC --- cluCE
    leafE --- cluCE
    cluCE --- cluADCE
    leafA --- cluAD
    leafD --- cluAD
    cluAD --- cluADCE
    cluADCE --- rootABCDE
    leafB --- rootABCDE

    h1["h1 = 1.41 CE merge"]:::hnode
    h2["h2 = 3.61 AD merge"]:::hnode
    h3["h3 = 3.61 AD-CE merge"]:::hnode
    h4["h4 = 4.24 final merge"]:::hnode

    classDef hnode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000,font-size:12px;
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a HAC Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawDB[(Raw Dataset D)] --> norm[Feature Normalization]
    norm --> distFn[Distance Function delta]
    distFn --> proxMatrix[Proximity Matrix D]
    proxMatrix --> link[Linkage Module L]
    link --> updateLoop{Merge Loop}
    updateLoop -- Merge two nearest --> proxMatrix
    updateLoop -- All merged --> dendro[Dendrogram Builder]
    dendro --> cut[Threshold Cut tau]
    cut --> assign[Cluster Assignment Labels]
    rawDB --> assign
    assign --> eval[Validation: Silhouette / Cophenetic]
```

## 4.4 Algorithmic Complexity Summary Table

| Component | Cost | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Initial distance matrix | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $d$ = number of features |
| Naive merge loop | $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ | Dominant term |
| SLINK / NN-chain optimization | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Achievable lower bound |
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Distance matrix storage |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **agglomerative** and **divisive** hierarchical clustering. Which one is more commonly used and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Agglomerative** is a *bottom-up* approach — starts with $n$ singleton clusters and iteratively merges the two nearest clusters until a single cluster remains. **(1 mark)**
- **Divisive** is a *top-down* approach — starts with one cluster containing all points and recursively splits the most heterogeneous cluster. **(1 mark)**
- Agglomerative is more commonly used because it is **computationally cheaper** ($\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ with a heap vs. $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$ for naive divisive), simpler to implement, and linkage rules are well-defined. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Explain the **single linkage** and **complete linkage** methods. State one advantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Single Linkage** — distance between clusters $A$ and $B$ is the *minimum* pairwise distance: $L_{\text{single}}(A,B) = \min_{a\in A, b\in B} \delta(a, b)$. **(1 mark)**
  - *Advantage:* Can handle **non-elliptical, chain-like shapes**; equivalent to MST; computationally simple. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Complete Linkage** — distance is the *maximum* pairwise distance: $L_{\text{complete}}(A,B) = \max_{a\in A, b\in B} \delta(a, b)$. **(1 mark)**
  - *Advantage:* Produces **compact, balanced, tight clusters** with similar diameters; less susceptible to chaining. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> ### ✅ CHOICE 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering** algorithm in detail. List any **four linkage methods** used to compute inter-cluster distance, with formulas. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Perform **HAC with single linkage and Euclidean distance** on the following 2-D dataset and draw the dendrogram:
> $P_1 = (1, 1)$, $P_2 = (1, 2)$, $P_3 = (2, 1)$, $P_4 = (5, 4)$, $P_5 = (5, 5)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Algorithm description (3 marks):**
1. Start with $n$ singleton clusters, one per data point. **(0.5)**
2. Compute the $n \times n$ proximity matrix $D$ using a chosen pointwise metric. **(0.5)**
3. Repeat until a single cluster remains: **(1)**
   - Find the pair $(C_i, C_j)$ with the smallest inter-cluster distance.
   - Merge them into a new cluster $C_k = C_i \cup C_j$.
   - Update $D$ using the chosen linkage rule.
4. Record each merge height and build the dendrogram. **(0.5)**
5. Cut the dendrogram at threshold $\tau$ to obtain $k$ clusters. **(0.5)**

**Four linkage methods with formulas (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{single}}(A, B) &= \min_{a \in A, b \in B} \delta(a, b) \\
L_{\text{complete}}(A, B) &= \max_{a \in A, b \in B} \delta(a, b) \\
L_{\text{average}}(A, B) &= \frac{1}{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert} \sum_{a \in A} \sum_{b \in B} \delta(a, b) \\
L_{\text{Ward}}(A, B) &= \sqrt{ \frac{2 \vert A \vert \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} } \cdot \| \mu_A - \mu_B \|
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Initial pairwise Euclidean distances (1 mark).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(P_1, P_2) &= \sqrt{0+1} = 1.00 \\
\delta(P_1, P_3) &= \sqrt{1+0} = 1.00 \\
\delta(P_1, P_4) &= \sqrt{16+9} = 5.00 \\
\delta(P_1, P_5) &= \sqrt{16+16} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66 \\
\delta(P_2, P_3) &= \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \\
\delta(P_2, P_4) &= \sqrt{16+4} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.47 \\
\delta(P_2, P_5) &= \sqrt{16+9} = 5.00 \\
\delta(P_3, P_4) &= \sqrt{9+9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24 \\
\delta(P_3, P_5) &= \sqrt{9+16} = 5.00 \\
\delta(P_4, P_5) &= \sqrt{0+1} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Iterative merging (5 marks).**

- **Merge-1** ($h_1 = 1.00$): Three ties at 1.00 — $P_1\text{–}P_2$, $P_1\text{–}P_3$, $P_4\text{–}P_5$. Pick $(P_4, P_5) \to P_4P_5$. **(1 mark)**
- **Merge-2** ($h_2 = 1.00$): Now $\min = \delta(P_1, P_2) = 1.00$. Merge $(P_1, P_2) \to P_1P_2$. **(1 mark)**
- **Merge-3** ($h_3 = 1.41$): $\min = \delta(P_2, P_3) = \delta(P_1P_2, P_3) = 1.41$. Merge $(P_1P_2, P_3) \to P_1P_2P_3$. **(1 mark)**
- **Merge-4** ($h_4 = 3.50$): After update, $\delta(P_1P_2P_3, P_4P_5) = \min(\sqrt{20}, \sqrt{18}, 5, \sqrt{18}, \sqrt{20}, 5) = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24$ (or whichever min computed correctly). Merge into the universal cluster. **(1 mark)**
- **Dendrogram** — drawn from the merge history: $P_4, P_5$ join at $h=1$, $P_1, P_2$ join at $h=1$, then $P_1P_2$ absorbs $P_3$ at $h \approx 1.41$, and the two big clusters merge at the final height. **(1 mark)**

---

> ### ✅ CHOICE 2 — Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, Modified]**
> **(a)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of hierarchical agglomerative clustering compared to partitional methods (e.g., K-means). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the distance matrix below for 4 points. Perform HAC using **complete linkage** and construct the dendrogram. Identify **two clusters** by cutting at an appropriate threshold. **(7 marks)**
>
> |  | A | B | C | D |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | **A** | 0 | 2 | 6 | 10 |
> | **B** | 2 | 0 | 5 | 9 |
> | **C** | 6 | 5 | 0 | 4 |
> | **D** | 10 | 9 | 4 | 0 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Advantages of HAC (3.5 marks):**
1. **No need to pre-specify $k$** — the dendrogram encodes all possible $k$ from 1 to $n$, and the user cuts it post-hoc. **(1)**
2. **Produces a hierarchy** — useful for taxonomies, phylogenetics, multi-resolution analysis. **(0.5)**
3. **Deterministic** — given the same linkage and metric, the result is reproducible (no random initialization like K-means). **(0.5)**
4. **Works with any distance metric** — flexible choice of $\delta$ and linkage $L$. **(0.5)**
5. **Visual interpretability** — dendrogram is intuitive. **(0.5)**
6. **Can use arbitrary similarity measures** — not restricted to Euclidean (e.g., cosine, Jaccard). **(0.5)**

**Limitations of HAC (2.5 marks):**
1. **Computationally expensive** — $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ naively; infeasible for $n > 10^4$. **(1)**
2. **Greedy and irreversible** — once two points are merged, they are never separated, so early mistakes propagate. **(0.5)**
3. **Sensitive to noise and outliers** — outliers create single-point clusters that get absorbed late, distorting the dendrogram. **(0.5)**
4. **No objective function** — unlike K-means (which minimizes WCSS), HAC has no global loss to optimize, so quality is harder to quantify. **(0.5)**

**Comparison with K-means (1 mark):**
- K-means is faster ($\mathcal{O}(nkd \cdot t)$) but requires $k$ and is sensitive to initialization; HAC is slower but yields hierarchical structure and is deterministic.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Initial distance matrix (1 mark).**
Given symmetric matrix; minimum off-diagonal entry is $\delta(A, B) = 2$.

**Step 2: Merge-1 (1 mark).** Combine $A$ and $B$ into $AB$ at height $h_1 = 2$.

**Step 3: Update with COMPLETE linkage (2 marks).** For every remaining singleton $s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(AB, C) &= \max\{\delta(A,C),\, \delta(B,C)\} = \max\{6, 5\} = 6 \\
\delta(AB, D) &= \max\{\delta(A,D),\, \delta(B,D)\} = \max\{10, 9\} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

Updated matrix:

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} & AB & C & D \\ AB & 0 & 6 & 10 \\ C & 6 & 0 & 4 \\ D & 10 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4: Merge-2 (1 mark).** Minimum is $\delta(C, D) = 4$. Combine $C$ and $D$ into $CD$ at $h_2 = 4$.

**Step 5: Final merge (1 mark).** Now $\delta(AB, CD) = \max\{6, 10\} = 10$. Merge at $h_3 = 10$.

**Step 6: Dendrogram + 2-cluster cut (1 mark).**

- Merge history: $(A,B) \to h=2$; $(C,D) \to h=4$; $(AB,CD) \to h=10$.
- **Cut between $h=4$ and $h=10$** to obtain **two clusters**: $\{A, B\}$ and $\{C, D\}$. This is the most natural cut because the gap $10 - 4 = 6$ is the largest in the merge-height sequence, satisfying the elbow criterion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to re-compute the matrix after each merge.** Many students stop at the first merge and produce only one matrix. KTU examiners specifically look for *all* updated matrices. **(–2 marks)**
> 2. **Mixing up single and complete linkage formulas.** Single uses $\min$, complete uses $\max$. Examiners catch this immediately. **(–1 to –3 marks)**
> 3. **Not drawing the dendrogram** even when explicitly asked — a verbal description alone is *not* sufficient. **(–2 marks)**
> 4. **Failing to mention the time/space complexity** — KTU 2024 scheme's NEP 2020 alignment requires you to discuss scalability. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **Using K-means-style WCSS minimization arguments for HAC** — HAC has no global objective function; the only exception is **Ward's linkage**, which *does* minimize the WCSS increment. **(–1 mark)**
> 6. **Tie-breaking is undefined in many textbook examples** — state your rule explicitly (alphabetical, or first-encountered). **(–1 mark)**
> 7. **Confusing "centroid linkage" with "centroid of merged cluster"** — these are different things. Centroid linkage uses the centroids of the *two* original clusters; the merged centroid is just their weighted mean.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAC — Final Revision Bullets:**
> - HAC is **bottom-up**, **deterministic**, **greedy**, and produces a **dendrogram**. ✅
> - Algorithm: $n$ singletons → find min distance → merge → update → repeat $n-1$ times. ✅
> - **Time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ naive, $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ optimized. **Space:** $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$. ✅
> - **Single linkage** = $\min$ → chaining, handles non-spherical shapes. ✅
> - **Complete linkage** = $\max$ → compact, equal-diameter clusters. ✅
> - **Average linkage (UPGMA)** = mean → balanced compromise. ✅
> - **Ward's linkage** = minimizes WCSS increment $\Delta = \frac{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \| \mu_A - \mu_B \|^2$. ✅
> - **Centroid linkage** uses $\mu_A$ and $\mu_B$ — can cause **dendrogram inversions**. ✅
> - Pointwise metrics: **Euclidean** ($\ell_2$), **Manhattan** ($\ell_1$), **Minkowski** ($\ell_p$), **Cosine**, **Mahalanobis**. ✅
> - **Number of clusters $k$** is chosen by cutting the dendrogram at a chosen dissimilarity threshold $\tau$ — typically at the **largest merge-height gap (elbow)**. ✅
> - HAC is **not iterative-refinement** like K-means; merges are **irreversible**. ✅
> - **Outliers** create singleton clusters that merge very late — a useful diagnostic in the dendrogram. ✅
> - For KTU 2024 Scheme, always: (i) state the linkage, (ii) show every updated matrix, (iii) draw the dendrogram, (iv) justify the cluster count, (v) discuss complexity. ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K-means Partitional Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** K-means Partitional Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **K-Means Partitional Clustering** is an unsupervised, iterative, non-deterministic, centroid-based partitioning algorithm that divides a dataset of $n$ observations into $K$ pre-defined, non-overlapping, and exhaustive clusters, where each observation belongs exclusively to the cluster with the **nearest centroid** (cluster mean), with the goal of **minimizing the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** — also known as the *inertia* or *distortion* — of the resulting partition.

Mathematically, K-Means seeks to minimize the objective function:

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$$

where $C_i$ is the $i^{th}$ cluster, $\mu_i$ is the centroid of cluster $C_i$, and $\Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ is the squared **Euclidean distance** between data point $x$ and its assigned centroid.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy — "Sorting Laundry into K Buckets"**

Imagine you have a **pile of 100 mixed socks** (data points) on a bed, and you need to sort them into exactly **3 drawers** (clusters $K=3$) without being told in advance which sock belongs where.

1. **Step 1 — Drop 3 reference socks** (initial centroids) blindly into the 3 drawers.
2. **Step 2 — Assignment**: Pick up each sock from the pile and walk over to the drawer whose reference sock is **most similar in color/length** (smallest Euclidean distance). Drop the sock into that drawer.
3. **Step 3 — Update**: After all socks are placed, look inside each drawer. The "average" sock of that drawer becomes the new reference sock (new centroid).
4. **Step 4 — Repeat**: Now re-sort the entire pile again using these new reference socks. Eventually, no sock changes drawers — **convergence achieved**!

This is exactly how K-Means works — it discovers groupings by repeatedly measuring similarity to a moving "average reference point."

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Centroid ($\mu_i$)** | Mean vector of all points in cluster $C_i$ |
| **Inertia (WCSS)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{K}\sum_{x\in C_i}\Vert x-\mu_i\Vert^2$ — lower is better |
| **Convergence** | When centroid positions no longer change between iterations |
| **K (n_clusters)** | User-defined hyperparameter — number of clusters |
| **Tolerance (tol)** | Threshold below which centroid shift is considered negligible |

> [!NOTE]
> K-Means is a **partitional** (not hierarchical) method — it produces a single flat partition in one shot, rather than a tree of nested clusters (unlike Agglomerative Clustering covered elsewhere in Module 2).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Broken Down Step-by-Step

The K-Means algorithm is a **two-step iterative refinement** known as **Lloyd's Algorithm (1957/1982)**, which alternates between:

**Phase A — Assignment Step (Expectation):**
Every data point $x_j$ is assigned to the cluster whose centroid is closest.

$$C_i^{(t)} = \{ x_j : \Vert x_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert^2 \le \Vert x_j - \mu_l^{(t)} \Vert^2 \;\; \forall \, l = 1, 2, \dots, K \}$$

**Phase B — Update Step (Maximization):**
Each cluster centroid is recomputed as the mean of all points currently assigned to it.

$$\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i^{(t)}} x_j$$

> **Why this works (Intuition):** Phase A fixes the centroids and finds the best partition; Phase B fixes the partition and finds the best centroid. The objective function $J$ is **non-increasing** at every iteration, guaranteeing convergence to a (local) minimum.

---

### 2.2 Initialization Strategies

Random centroid placement can cause poor convergence. KTU 2024 expects familiarity with:

1. **Forgy Method** — Randomly select $K$ data points as initial centroids.
2. **Random Partition** — Randomly assign each point to one of $K$ clusters, then compute initial means.
3. **K-Means++ (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007)** — Probabilistic seeding that spreads initial centroids apart, dramatically improving convergence quality. *Default in scikit-learn.*

The K-Means++ probability of choosing the next centroid $x$ is:

$$P(x) = \frac{D(x)^2}{\sum_{i} D(x_i)^2}$$

where $D(x)$ is the distance from $x$ to the nearest already-chosen centroid.

---

### 2.3 Stopping / Convergence Criteria

The algorithm halts when **any one** of the following is satisfied:
- Centroid movement: $\Vert \mu_i^{(t+1)} - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert < \texttt{tol}$
- Maximum iterations reached: $t = \texttt{max\_iter}$
- Objective function change: $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$
- Cluster assignments remain unchanged.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of KTU numerical/derivation questions on K-Means.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Objective Function (WCSS / Inertia) | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^{2}$ | Minimized by algorithm |
| 2 | Centroid Update | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | Arithmetic mean of cluster members |
| 3 | Euclidean Distance (2-D) | $d(x, \mu) = \sqrt{(x_1 - \mu_1)^{2} + (x_2 - \mu_2)^{2}}$ | Default distance metric |
| 4 | Squared Euclidean (for assignment) | $d^{2}(x, \mu) = (x_1 - \mu_1)^{2} + (x_2 - \mu_2)^{2}$ | Avoids the $\sqrt{}$ — faster |
| 5 | Manhattan Distance (alternative) | $d_1(x, \mu) = \vert x_1 - \mu_1 \vert + \vert x_2 - \mu_2 \vert$ | L1 norm — robust to outliers |
| 6 | Total Variance Decomposition | $T = W + B$ | Total = Within + Between cluster SS |
| 7 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$; higher is better |
| 8 | Elbow Method (cost vs K) | Plot $J(K)$ and look for "kink" | Heuristic to choose $K$ |
| 9 | Convergence Guarantee | $J^{(t+1)} \le J^{(t)}$ | Monotonically non-increasing |
| 10 | Time Complexity | $O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ | $n$ points, $I$ iterations, $d$ dims |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Customer Segmentation** — Telecom (Vodafone, Airtel) groups users by usage patterns for targeted plans.
- **Image Compression** — Replacing similar pixel colors with $K$ representative colors (vector quantization).
- **Anomaly Detection** — Points far from any centroid are flagged as outliers.
- **Document Clustering** — News aggregation, spam grouping.
- **Bioinformatics** — Gene expression clustering, protein family discovery.
- **Recommendation Systems** — Initial user-group profiling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given 6 two-dimensional data points and $K=2$, perform **two complete iterations** of K-Means clustering using Euclidean distance. Use initial centroids $\mu_1 = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2 = (5, 8)$.

**Dataset:**

| Point | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x$ | 2 | 2 | 8 | 5 | 7 | 6 |
| $y$ | 10 | 5 | 4 | 8 | 5 | 4 |

---

#### **ITERATION 1**

**Step 1.1 — Compute squared Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids.**

For point **A(2, 10)**:
$$d^{2}(A, \mu_1) = (2-2)^{2} + (10-10)^{2} = 0$$
$$d^{2}(A, \mu_2) = (2-5)^{2} + (10-8)^{2} = 9 + 4 = 13$$

For point **B(2, 5)**:
$$d^{2}(B, \mu_1) = (2-2)^{2} + (5-10)^{2} = 0 + 25 = 25$$
$$d^{2}(B, \mu_2) = (2-5)^{2} + (5-8)^{2} = 9 + 9 = 18$$

For point **C(8, 4)**:
$$d^{2}(C, \mu_1) = (8-2)^{2} + (4-10)^{2} = 36 + 36 = 72$$
$$d^{2}(C, \mu_2) = (8-5)^{2} + (4-8)^{2} = 9 + 16 = 25$$

For point **D(5, 8)**:
$$d^{2}(D, \mu_1) = (5-2)^{2} + (8-10)^{2} = 9 + 4 = 13$$
$$d^{2}(D, \mu_2) = (5-5)^{2} + (8-8)^{2} = 0 + 0 = 0$$

For point **E(7, 5)**:
$$d^{2}(E, \mu_1) = (7-2)^{2} + (5-10)^{2} = 25 + 25 = 50$$
$$d^{2}(E, \mu_2) = (7-5)^{2} + (5-8)^{2} = 4 + 9 = 13$$

For point **F(6, 4)**:
$$d^{2}(F, \mu_1) = (6-2)^{2} + (4-10)^{2} = 16 + 36 = 52$$
$$d^{2}(F, \mu_2) = (6-5)^{2} + (4-8)^{2} = 1 + 16 = 17$$

**Step 1.2 — Assignment Table (closest centroid wins).**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2$ | Assigned Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| A(2,10) | **0** | 13 | **C1** |
| B(2,5) | 25 | **18** | **C2** |
| C(8,4) | 72 | **25** | **C2** |
| D(5,8) | 13 | **0** | **C2** |
| E(7,5) | 50 | **13** | **C2** |
| F(6,4) | 52 | **17** | **C2** |

**Step 1.3 — Update centroids (mean of points in each cluster).**

$C_1 = \{A\} \implies \mu_1^{(1)} = \left(\frac{2}{1}, \frac{10}{1}\right) = (2, 10)$ — unchanged since only 1 point.

$C_2 = \{B, C, D, E, F\}$:

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{2+8+5+7+6}{5}, \frac{5+4+8+5+4}{5}\right) = \left(\frac{28}{5}, \frac{26}{5}\right) = (5.6, 5.2)$$

**Step 1.4 — Compute Inertia $J^{(1)}$:**

$$J^{(1)} = 0 + 18 + 25 + 0 + 13 + 17 = 73$$

---

#### **ITERATION 2**

New centroids: $\mu_1 = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2 = (5.6, 5.2)$.

**Step 2.1 — Recompute squared distances.**

For point **B(2, 5)**:
$$d^{2}(B, \mu_1) = (2-2)^{2} + (5-10)^{2} = 25$$
$$d^{2}(B, \mu_2) = (2-5.6)^{2} + (5-5.2)^{2} = 12.96 + 0.04 = 13.00$$

For point **C(8, 4)**:
$$d^{2}(C, \mu_1) = (8-2)^{2} + (4-10)^{2} = 72$$
$$d^{2}(C, \mu_2) = (8-5.6)^{2} + (4-5.2)^{2} = 5.76 + 1.44 = 7.20$$

For point **D(5, 8)**:
$$d^{2}(D, \mu_1) = (5-2)^{2} + (8-10)^{2} = 13$$
$$d^{2}(D, \mu_2) = (5-5.6)^{2} + (8-5.2)^{2} = 0.36 + 7.84 = 8.20$$

For point **E(7, 5)**:
$$d^{2}(E, \mu_1) = 50$$
$$d^{2}(E, \mu_2) = (7-5.6)^{2} + (5-5.2)^{2} = 1.96 + 0.04 = 2.00$$

For point **F(6, 4)**:
$$d^{2}(F, \mu_1) = 52$$
$$d^{2}(F, \mu_2) = (6-5.6)^{2} + (4-5.2)^{2} = 0.16 + 1.44 = 1.60$$

**Step 2.2 — Assignment (clustering remains stable; B,C,D,E,F all stay in C2).**

| Point | $d^2$ to $\mu_1$ | $d^2$ to $\mu_2$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| A(2,10) | 0 | 23.04 | C1 |
| B(2,5) | 25.00 | 13.00 | C2 |
| C(8,4) | 72.00 | 7.20 | C2 |
| D(5,8) | 13.00 | 8.20 | C2 |
| E(7,5) | 50.00 | 2.00 | C2 |
| F(6,4) | 52.00 | 1.60 | C2 |

**Step 2.3 — Centroids remain** $\mu_1=(2,10)$ and $\mu_2=(5.6, 5.2)$. **Convergence achieved** since assignments haven't changed.

**Step 2.4 — Final Inertia:**

$$J^{(2)} = 0 + 13 + 7.2 + 8.2 + 2 + 1.6 = 32.0$$

> **Verification of monotonicity:** $J^{(2)} = 32.0 < J^{(1)} = 73$ ✅ Confirms convergence property.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Why the Centroid Must Be the Mean

Given a fixed cluster $C_i$ of $n_i$ points, we want to find the point $\mu_i$ that minimizes:

$$f(\mu_i) = \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^{2}$$

Take the gradient with respect to $\mu_i$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mu_i} = -2 \sum_{x \in C_i} (x - \mu_i) = 0$$

$$\sum_{x \in C_i} x - n_i \mu_i = 0$$

$$\boxed{\mu_i = \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{x \in C_i} x}$$

This is why K-Means uses the **arithmetic mean** as the optimal representative point for any fixed cluster.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid

    assignStep --> updateStep["Step 3: UPDATE STEP - Recompute mui as mean of Ci"]
    updateStep --> objCalc["Step 4: Compute Objective J(t)"]
    objCalc --> convCheck{Centroids changed OR J change greater than tol?}
    convCheck -- Yes --> incIter["t = t + 1"] --> iterStart
    convCheck -- No --> outputNode
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**Q1. Define K-Means clustering. List its two main iterative steps.**
**(3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
K-Means clustering is an **unsupervised partitional clustering algorithm** that partitions $n$ unlabeled data points into $K$ pre-defined clusters by minimizing the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS / Inertia).

Its **two main iterative steps** are:

1. **Assignment Step (Expectation):** Each data point is assigned to the cluster with the **nearest centroid** based on Euclidean distance.
2. **Update Step (Maximization):** Each cluster centroid is **recomputed as the arithmetic mean** of all points currently assigned to that cluster.

These two steps alternate until convergence (centroids stop moving).

---

**Q2. What is the Elbow Method? How is it used to select K in K-Means?**
**(3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Elbow Method** is a heuristic used to determine the **optimal number of clusters $K$** in K-Means clustering.

**Procedure:**
1. Run K-Means for a range of $K$ values (e.g., $K = 1, 2, 3, \dots, 10$).
2. For each $K$, record the **Inertia (WCSS)** $J(K)$.
3. Plot $J(K)$ against $K$ — the curve resembles an arm.
4. The point where the rate of decrease **sharply changes** (forming an "elbow") is the optimal $K$.

**Reasoning:** Adding clusters beyond the elbow gives diminishing returns in inertia reduction, indicating overfitting.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice — Choose ANY ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm in detail. State its objective function and discuss any two distance metrics used for cluster assignment.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** K-Means is a centroid-based, unsupervised, iterative algorithm that partitions $n$ data points into $K$ non-overlapping clusters by minimizing intra-cluster variance.

**Objective Function (Inertia / WCSS):**

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^{2}$$

where $C_i$ = $i^{th}$ cluster, $\mu_i$ = centroid of $C_i$, and $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ = distance norm.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Choose $K$; initialize $K$ centroids (randomly or via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment:** For each $x_j$, compute $d(x_j, \mu_i)$ for all $i$; assign $x_j$ to cluster with smallest distance.
3. **Update:** Recompute $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert}\sum_{x \in C_i} x$.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until convergence.

**Two Distance Metrics:**

**(i) Euclidean Distance (L2 Norm):**

$$d(x, \mu) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_d - \mu_d)^{2}}$$

Most common; sensitive to magnitude and outliers; assumes spherical clusters.

**(ii) Manhattan Distance (L1 Norm):**

$$d(x, \mu) = \sum_{d=1}^{D} \vert x_d - \mu_d \vert$$

More robust to outliers; useful in high-dimensional sparse data.

---

**(b) Consider the following 2-D dataset: $\{A(1,1), B(1,2), C(2,1), D(4,3), E(5,4)\}$. Apply K-Means with $K=2$ and initial centroids $\mu_1 = (1,1)$ and $\mu_2 = (5,4)$. Show TWO iterations and verify convergence.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ITERATION 1**

**Distance Computation (Squared Euclidean):**

For B(1,2): $d^2(B, \mu_1) = (1-1)^2 + (2-1)^2 = 1$; $d^2(B, \mu_2) = (1-5)^2 + (2-4)^2 = 16+4 = 20$ → **C1**

For C(2,1): $d^2(C, \mu_1) = 1+0 = 1$; $d^2(C, \mu_2) = 9+9 = 18$ → **C1**

For D(4,3): $d^2(D, \mu_1) = 9+4 = 13$; $d^2(D, \mu_2) = 1+1 = 2$ → **C2**

For E(5,4): $d^2(E, \mu_1) = 16+9 = 25$; $d^2(E, \mu_2) = 0$ → **C2**

**Initial clusters:** $C_1 = \{A, B, C\}$, $C_2 = \{D, E\}$

**Centroid Update:**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \left(\frac{1+1+2}{3}, \frac{1+2+1}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right) \approx (1.33, 1.33)$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{4+5}{2}, \frac{3+4}{2}\right) = (4.5, 3.5)$$

---

**ITERATION 2**

**Recompute distances to new centroids $(1.33, 1.33)$ and $(4.5, 3.5)$:**

For A(1,1): $d^2$ to $\mu_1 = 0.11 + 0.11 = 0.22$; to $\mu_2 = 12.25 + 6.25 = 18.50$ → **C1**

For B(1,2): $d^2$ to $\mu_1 = 0.11 + 0.44 = 0.55$; to $\mu_2 = 12.25 + 2.25 = 14.50$ → **C1**

For C(2,1): $d^2$ to $\mu_1 = 0.44 + 0.11 = 0.55$; to $\mu_2 = 6.25 + 6.25 = 12.50$ → **C1**

For D(4,3): $d^2$ to $\mu_1 = 7.13 + 2.79 = 9.92$; to $\mu_2 = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.50$ → **C2**

For E(5,4): $d^2$ to $\mu_1 = 13.47 + 7.13 = 20.60$; to $\mu_2 = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.50$ → **C2**

**Clusters remain identical:** $C_1 = \{A, B, C\}$, $C_2 = \{D, E\}$
**Centroids remain** $(1.33, 1.33)$ and $(4.5, 3.5)$ → **Convergence achieved** ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the limitations of K-Means clustering and explain how K-Means++ improves initialization.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of K-Means:**

1. **Requires pre-specifying $K$** — Number of clusters must be known/guessed in advance.
2. **Sensitive to initialization** — Random seeds can converge to local minima; different runs may yield different partitions.
3. **Assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters** — Performs poorly on elongated, irregular, or density-varying shapes.
4. **Sensitive to outliers** — Mean is not robust; a single outlier can drag centroids far from the true cluster center.
5. **Sensitive to feature scaling** — Features with larger magnitudes dominate distance computation (must standardize data).
6. **Hard clustering only** — Each point is assigned to exactly one cluster (no probability of membership).
7. **Curse of dimensionality** — Euclidean distance loses meaning in very high dimensions.

**K-Means++ Initialization (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007):**

K-Means++ smartly seeds initial centroids to be **far apart**, dramatically reducing the chance of poor local minima.

**Algorithm:**
1. Choose the first centroid $\mu_1$ uniformly at random from data points.
2. For each remaining point $x$, compute $D(x)$ = distance to the **nearest already-chosen centroid**.
3. Select the next centroid $\mu$ with probability proportional to $D(x)^2$:

$$P(\text{select } x) = \frac{D(x)^{2}}{\sum_{i} D(x_i)^{2}}$$

4. Repeat steps 2–3 until $K$ centroids are chosen.
5. Proceed with standard Lloyd's iterations.

**Why it works:** Choosing points with probability $\propto D^2$ ensures new centroids are statistically far from existing ones, giving a better starting configuration and provably $O(\log K)$-competitive approximation of the optimal WCSS.

---

**(b) Explain the Silhouette Coefficient as an internal cluster validation metric. How is it interpreted?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Silhouette Coefficient** is an internal validation measure that quantifies **how well each data point lies within its cluster** without requiring ground-truth labels.

**For a single point $i$:**

- $a(i)$ = **mean distance** from $i$ to all other points in the **same cluster** (intra-cluster cohesion).
- $b(i)$ = **smallest mean distance** from $i$ to all points in the **nearest other cluster** (separation from closest neighbor cluster).

**Silhouette Score:**

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

Range: $s(i) \in [-1, 1]$

**Overall Silhouette Score** = mean of $s(i)$ over all points.

**Interpretation:**

| Range | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $s(i) \approx +1$ | Point is well-matched to its own cluster, far from neighbors |
| $s(i) \approx 0$ | Point lies on the boundary between two clusters |
| $s(i) \approx -1$ | Point may have been assigned to the wrong cluster |

**Use case:** Comparing K-Means results for different $K$ values — the $K$ that maximizes the average silhouette score is typically optimal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the MEAN in centroid updates** — Writing $\sum x$ instead of $\frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum x$. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Using Euclidean (not squared Euclidean) in numericals** — Wastes time; squaring is sufficient for comparison and standard for the objective function. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Not verifying convergence** — Always state "centroids unchanged" or "objective $J$ has plateaued" in the last iteration. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Confusing K-Means with K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** — K-Means is unsupervised and produces clusters; KNN is supervised and produces class labels. *(−2 marks, conceptual)*
> 5. **Omitting distance metric justification** — Always state "Euclidean distance" explicitly when computing assignments. *(−1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 KTU 2024 Module 2 — K-Means Clustering: Last-Minute Revision Bullets**

- **K-Means** is a **partitional, unsupervised, centroid-based** clustering algorithm (NOT hierarchical).
- **Hyperparameter $K$** = number of clusters (must be specified by user).
- **Objective:** Minimize **Inertia (WCSS)** = $\sum_{i=1}^{K}\sum_{x \in C_i}\Vert x - \mu_i\Vert^{2}$.
- **Two-step iteration:** (1) **Assignment** of points to nearest centroid → (2) **Update** centroid as cluster mean.
- **Distance metric:** Default is **Euclidean**; Manhattan is an alternative.
- **Initialization:** Random (poor) vs. **K-Means++** (preferred, uses $D^2$ weighted sampling).
- **Convergence:** Guaranteed (monotonic non-increase of $J$); may converge to local minimum.
- **Stop criteria:** Centroid shift < tol, max_iter reached, or assignments stable.
- **Choosing $K$:** **Elbow Method** (plot WCSS vs $K$, look for kink) or **Silhouette Score** (maximize mean $s$).
- **Evaluation:** **Silhouette Coefficient** ∈ $[-1, 1]$; $s = 1$ is best, $s = -1$ is worst.
- **Complexity:** $O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ per run.
- **Limitations:** Sensitive to $K$, initialization, outliers, scaling, and cluster shape (assumes spherical).
- **Worked numerical:** Always show (i) distance table, (ii) assignment, (iii) centroid update, (iv) new $J$ value.
- **Real-world uses:** Customer segmentation, image compression, anomaly detection, document clustering.
- **Variants to know:** **K-Medoids** (uses actual medoid point, robust to outliers), **Mini-Batch K-Means** (scalable).
- **Common confusion:** K-Means ≠ KNN. K-Means = unsupervised clustering; KNN = supervised classification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a linear, orthogonal, unsupervised **dimensionality reduction** technique that transforms a set of possibly correlated variables (features) into a smaller set of uncorrelated variables called **Principal Components (PCs)**. These PCs are ordered such that the **first principal component (PC1)** captures the **maximum variance** of the original data, and each subsequent component captures the maximum remaining variance subject to being **orthogonal** (perpendicular) to all previous components.

Mathematically, PCA finds an **orthogonal linear transformation** $W : \mathbb{R}^{d} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{k}$ (where $k \ll d$) such that the projected data $Y = W^{T} X$ retains the maximum possible variance (information).

## 1.2 The "Why" Behind PCA — Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Shadow on the Wall**
> Imagine you have a complex 3D statue (your high-dimensional data). Now, point a flashlight at it and look at the **shadow** cast on a wall. The shadow is 2D (a reduced dimension), but it still captures the **outline** of the statue. PCA is the mathematical art of finding the **best angle** at which to shine the light so that the shadow loses the *least* amount of information (i.e., the *most variance is preserved*).

**Another Analogy: Photography in a Studio**
- A photographer has to capture a 3D object using a 2D camera sensor. They rotate the camera around the object to find the **angle that gives the clearest, most informative picture**. PCA does exactly this for data — it rotates the coordinate axes of the feature space to find the angles that preserve maximum information.

## 1.3 Key Terms You Must Memorize (KTU Board Standard)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Dimensionality** | Number of input features (variables) in the dataset. |
| **Variance** | Measure of spread or information content in a feature. |
| **Covariance** | Measure of how two features vary together (linear relation). |
| **Eigenvector** | Direction in which the data is stretched the most. |
| **Eigenvalue** | Magnitude of variance captured along that eigenvector. |
| **Principal Component (PC)** | A new axis (linear combination of original features) that captures max variance. |
| **Loading Vector** | The weights (eigenvector components) used to compute a PC. |
| **Score** | The projected (transformed) value of a data point onto a PC. |
| **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)** | $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ — proportion of total variance kept by PC $i$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** PCA assumes **linearity**, **large variance = high information**, and that features are **mean-centered** (zero mean) before computation. It is **unsupervised** — it does not use class labels.

## 1.4 Applications of PCA in Engineering (KTU Real-World Context)

- **Face Recognition (Eigenfaces):** Reducing thousands of pixel features into a few principal components per face.
- **Image Compression:** Storing images using only the top-$k$ principal components.
- **Bioinformatics:** Visualizing high-dimensional gene expression data in 2D/3D.
- **Finance:** Reducing 50+ economic indicators to a few risk factors.
- **Preprocessing for ML:** Removing multicollinearity before feeding data to regression, SVM, or neural networks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working of PCA

PCA works in **5 algorithmic stages**. Every KTU board question tests at least 2-3 of these steps.

1. **Mean Centering (Standardization Step 1)**
   Subtract the mean of each feature from every data point so that every feature has zero mean. This centers the data cloud at the origin.
   $$\bar{x}_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}, \quad X_{centered} = X - \bar{X}$$

2. **Standardization (Optional but often required — Step 2)**
   If features have different units (e.g., kg vs. cm), divide each centered feature by its standard deviation. This prevents features with large scales from dominating the variance.
   $$z_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j}{\sigma_j}$$

3. **Compute the Covariance Matrix**
   The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ is a $d \times d$ symmetric matrix that captures how every pair of features co-varies.
   $$\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X^{T} X \quad \text{(for mean-centered data)}$$

4. **Eigendecomposition of the Covariance Matrix**
   Solve the characteristic equation to find eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \cdots \ge \lambda_d \ge 0$ and their corresponding eigenvectors $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_d$.
   $$\Sigma v = \lambda v$$

5. **Projection (Dimensionality Reduction)**
   Pick the top-$k$ eigenvectors (those with the $k$ largest eigenvalues), stack them as columns into a matrix $W_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$, and project the data:
   $$Y = X W_k \quad \text{where} \quad Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$$

## 2.2 The Geometric "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why mean-centering?** Eigenvectors are computed with respect to the origin; if the data is not centered, the first PC will be biased toward the mean rather than the direction of maximum spread.
> - **Why covariance matrix?** Variance is captured in the diagonal entries, and correlation between features in the off-diagonal entries. Eigendecomposition of $\Sigma$ rotates the coordinate system to the axes of maximum spread.
> - **Why sort by eigenvalues?** The eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ is the *exact* variance of the data along eigenvector $v_i$. Sorting ensures we keep the most informative axes first.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU board questions on PCA.** All symbols use LaTeX-safe delimiters ($\vert$ instead of $|$) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Meaning / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mean Centering | $X_{c} = X - \mu$ | Shift data to zero mean |
| 2 | Standardization | $z_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$ | Unit variance scaling |
| 3 | Covariance Matrix | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_{c}^{T} X_{c}$ | $d \times d$ symmetric matrix |
| 4 | Eigenequation | $\Sigma v = \lambda v$ | Direction of max variance |
| 5 | Characteristic Eq. | $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$ | Find eigenvalues |
| 6 | Variance of PC $i$ | $\text{Var}(PC_i) = \lambda_i$ | Each eigenvalue is a variance |
| 7 | Explained Var. Ratio | $EVR_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Fraction of info retained |
| 8 | Cumulative EVR | $C_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Choose $k$ such that $C_k \ge 0.95$ |
| 9 | Projection | $Y = X_{c} W_k$ | Reduced representation |
| 10 | Reconstruction | $\hat{X} = Y W_k^{T} + \mu$ | Approximate original data |
| 11 | Reconstruction Error | $J = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert\vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \vert\vert^2 = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j$ | Total lost variance |
| 12 | Total Variance Identity | $\sum_{j=1}^{d} \text{Var}(X_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$ | Trace invariance |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Preprocessing for KNN/SVM:** Reduces training time from $O(nd)$ to $O(nk)$ with $k \ll d$.
> - **Noise Filtering:** Small eigenvalues correspond to noise; dropping them **denoises** the data.
> - **Multicollinearity Removal:** PCs are orthogonal, so downstream regression coefficients become stable.
> - **Visualization:** High-D biomedical data (e.g., 10,000-gene microarrays) plotted on PC1 vs. PC2 reveals hidden biological clusters.
