```mermaid
        F5C[Train Fold 3]
        F5D[Train Fold 4]
        F5E[Validation Fold 5]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each iteration's "Validation Fold" (highlighted as the last box in each subgraph) is the held-out test set, while all other folds serve as training data. The validation fold rotates across iterations so that every fold is used as the test set exactly once.

---

### 4.4 Bias-Variance Tradeoff Across CV Variants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LOOCV K equals N] -->|Low Bias| B[High Variance]
    C[K equals 10] -->|Balanced Bias| D[Balanced Variance]
    E[K equals 2] -->|High Bias| F[Low Variance]
    B --> G[Not preferred for noisy data]
    D --> H[Industry standard recommendation]
    F --> I[Too biased for most use cases]
```

---

### 4.5 Stratified vs Standard K-Fold: Class Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Raw[Raw Dataset 60 percent Class 0 and 40 percent Class 1]
    SK[Stratified K Fold K equals 5]
    VK[Vanilla K Fold K equals 5]

    Raw --> SK
    Raw --> VK

    SK --> SK1[Fold 1: 12 Class 0 and 8 Class 1]
    SK --> SK2[Fold 2: 12 Class 0 and 8 Class 1]
    SK --> SK3[Fold 3: 12 Class 0 and 8 Class 1]
    SK --> SK4[Fold 4: 12 Class 0 and 8 Class 1]
    SK --> SK5[Fold 5: 12 Class 0 and 8 Class 1]

    VK --> VK1[Fold 1: random split class ratio may vary]
    VK --> VK2[Fold 2: random split class ratio may vary]
    VK --> VK3[Fold 3: random split class ratio may vary]
    VK --> VK4[Fold 4: random split class ratio may vary]
    VK --> VK5[Fold 5: random split class ratio may vary]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Cross-Validation. Mention any two advantages of using cross-validation over a simple hold-out method.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Cross-Validation is a resampling-based statistical technique used to evaluate the generalization performance of a machine learning model. The dataset is partitioned into K complementary subsets (folds). The model is trained on K−1 folds and tested on the held-out fold, repeating this process K times so that every observation is used exactly once for testing. The final performance metric is the mean of the K evaluation scores.

**Two Advantages (1 Mark):**
1. **Reduced Variance:** Averaging across K folds produces a more stable and reliable performance estimate than a single train/test split, which is highly sensitive to the random partition.
2. **Efficient Use of Data:** Every sample participates in both training and testing, which is especially valuable for small datasets where a large hold-out test set would waste valuable training data.

---

#### **Question 2: What is the difference between K-Fold Cross-Validation and Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)?** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | K-Fold CV | LOOCV |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of folds (K)** | Typically K = 5 or 10 | K = N (number of samples) |
| **Training set size** | $N(K-1)/K$ | $N-1$ |
| **Test set size** | $N/K$ | 1 |
| **Iterations** | K | N |
| **Bias** | Slightly higher bias | Approximately unbiased |
| **Variance** | Lower variance | Higher variance (correlated training sets) |
| **Computation** | Moderate (K trainings) | Expensive (N trainings) |

**Key Takeaway (1 Mark):** K-Fold CV is computationally cheaper and has lower variance, making it the practical default. LOOCV is preferred only when the dataset is very small and maximum bias reduction is critical.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A: K-Fold Cross-Validation in Detail** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the procedure of K-Fold Cross-Validation. How is the final model performance estimated? Discuss the bias-variance tradeoff in choosing K. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure (3 Marks):**

1. **Shuffle:** Randomly shuffle the entire dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ to eliminate any ordering bias.
2. **Partition:** Divide the shuffled data into K mutually exclusive folds $\mathcal{F}_1, \mathcal{F}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{F}_K$, each containing approximately $N/K$ samples.
3. **Iterate:** For each $k = 1, 2, \ldots, K$:
   - **Train** the model on $\mathcal{D}_{train}^{(k)} = \mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{F}_k$.
   - **Validate** the trained model on $\mathcal{F}_k$ and compute the error $E_k$.
4. **Aggregate:** The final CV error is:

$$E_{CV}^{(K)} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} E_k$$

**Bias-Variance Tradeoff (4 Marks):**

| Choice of K | Bias | Variance | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| K = 2 (extreme) | High (training set only half the data) | Low (training sets are very different) | Very low |
| K = 5 or K = 10 (standard) | Moderate | Moderate | Manageable |
| K = N (LOOCV) | Lowest (training set size $N-1$) | Highest (training sets are nearly identical) | Extremely high |

- **Small K (e.g., 2):** Each training set is much smaller than the full dataset, leading to a model that is more biased (underfitting tendency). However, the training sets across folds differ substantially, leading to low-variance estimates.
- **Large K (e.g., N):** Each training set has size $N-1$, very close to $N$, producing models that are almost unbiased. However, the training sets are nearly identical across iterations, meaning the predictions are highly correlated, inflating the variance of the final estimate.
- **K = 10 (Empirical Sweet Spot):** Kohavi's study demonstrated that $K = 10$ optimally balances bias and variance for most practical applications. It is computationally feasible and provides a reliable performance estimate.

---

**(b) Suppose a dataset of 1500 samples is evaluated using 10-Fold Cross-Validation. Each fold yields the following accuracy scores: {0.91, 0.88, 0.93, 0.90, 0.87, 0.92, 0.89, 0.91, 0.88, 0.91}. Compute the mean cross-validated accuracy and the standard deviation. Interpret the result. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: List the fold accuracies.**

$$A_k = \{0.91, 0.88, 0.93, 0.90, 0.87, 0.92, 0.89, 0.91, 0.88, 0.91\}$$

**Step 2: Compute the mean accuracy.**

$$\bar{A} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{k=1}^{10} A_k = \frac{0.91 + 0.88 + 0.93 + 0.90 + 0.87 + 0.92 + 0.89 + 0.91 + 0.88 + 0.91}{10}$$

Numerator sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma &= 0.91 + 0.88 = 1.79 \\
&+ 0.93 = 2.72 \\
&+ 0.90 = 3.62 \\
&+ 0.87 = 4.49 \\
&+ 0.92 = 5.41 \\
&+ 0.89 = 6.30 \\
&+ 0.91 = 7.21 \\
&+ 0.88 = 8.09 \\
&+ 0.91 = 9.00
\end{aligned}$$

$$\bar{A} = \frac{9.00}{10} = 0.90$$

**Step 3: Compute the deviations from the mean and their squares.**

| $A_k$ | $A_k - \bar{A}$ | $(A_k - \bar{A})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.91 | +0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 0.88 | −0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 0.93 | +0.03 | 0.0009 |
| 0.90 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| 0.87 | −0.03 | 0.0009 |
| 0.92 | +0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 0.89 | −0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 0.91 | +0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 0.88 | −0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 0.91 | +0.01 | 0.0001 |

**Step 4: Sum the squared deviations.**

$$\sum (A_k - \bar{A})^2 = 0.0001 + 0.0004 + 0.0009 + 0.0000 + 0.0009 + 0.0004 + 0.0001 + 0.0001 + 0.0004 + 0.0001 = 0.0034$$

**Step 5: Compute the standard deviation (using sample standard deviation, $N-1$ denominator).**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{0.0034}{10-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0034}{9}} = \sqrt{0.000378} \approx 0.01944$$

**Step 6: Interpretation (2 Marks).**

The model achieves a **mean 10-Fold CV accuracy of 90%** with a standard deviation of approximately **1.94%**. The low standard deviation indicates that the model performs **consistently across all 10 folds**, suggesting that the model is stable and not overly sensitive to the particular training data it receives. We can report the model's expected accuracy on unseen data as approximately $90\% \pm 2\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using the population standard deviation formula ($N$ denominator) instead of the sample formula ($N-1$). KTU examiners specifically look for $N-1$ in cross-validation standard deviation calculations.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Reporting only the mean accuracy without the standard deviation. The std deviation quantifies the **reliability** of the estimate — always report both.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Conflating cross-validation accuracy (estimated on training data via CV) with test-set accuracy. They are *estimates* of the test accuracy, not the test accuracy itself.

---

#### **Question B: LOOCV and Stratified CV** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) Explain Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV) with its mathematical formulation. Discuss two advantages and two disadvantages of LOOCV compared to 10-Fold CV. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mathematical Formulation (3 Marks):**

In LOOCV, the dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ is split such that each fold contains exactly one sample. The LOOCV error is:

$$E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\big(y_i, \hat{f}^{-i}(\mathbf{x}_i)\big)$$

where $\hat{f}^{-i}$ denotes the model trained on all samples except $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$, and $L$ is the loss function.

**Procedure (2 Marks):**

1. For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$:
   - Remove the $i^{th}$ sample from the dataset.
   - Train the model on the remaining $N-1$ samples.
   - Predict the held-out sample $\mathbf{x}_i$ and compute the loss $L(y_i, \hat{y}_i)$.
2. Average the losses across all $N$ iterations.

**Advantages (1 Mark — two advantages):**
1. **Low Bias:** The training set in each iteration has $N-1$ samples, nearly identical to the full dataset, producing an approximately unbiased estimate of the generalization error.
2. **No Randomness in Partitioning:** Unlike K-Fold, LOOCV produces a *deterministic* result (no random shuffling affects the outcome), making it fully reproducible.

**Disadvantages (1 Mark — two disadvantages):**
1. **High Computational Cost:** The model must be retrained $N$ times. For $N = 1{,}000{,}000$, this is prohibitively expensive.
2. **High Variance of the Estimate:** Because the training sets across iterations are nearly identical (they differ by only one sample), the model predictions are highly correlated, leading to high variance in the final LOOCV error estimate.

---

**(b) What is Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation? Why is it preferred for classification problems with imbalanced datasets? Illustrate with a numerical example where the original dataset has 100 samples with class distribution 80% Class 0 and 20% Class 1, and K = 5. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**

Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation is a variant of K-Fold CV where each fold preserves the class distribution (class proportion) of the original dataset. If the original dataset has class proportions $(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_C)$, then every fold $\mathcal{F}_k$ contains approximately $(p_1 \cdot \vert \mathcal{F}_k \vert, p_2 \cdot \vert \mathcal{F}_k \vert, \ldots, p_C \cdot \vert \mathcal{F}_k \vert)$ samples per class.

**Why Preferred for Imbalanced Data (2 Marks):**

In a vanilla K-Fold split on an imbalanced dataset, a fold may end up with **zero samples of the minority class** (especially when the minority class is very small, e.g., 2% of the data). This makes the evaluation metric unreliable — the model is tested on a class distribution that does not resemble reality. Stratified K-Fold **guarantees** that every fold has at least a few representatives of each class, ensuring:

1. **Reliable evaluation metrics** (precision, recall, F1-score) for the minority class.
2. **Consistent class exposure** to the model during training, reducing estimation bias.
3. **Realistic simulation** of the true deployment-time class distribution.

**Numerical Illustration (3 Marks):**

Original dataset: $N = 100$ samples, $N_0 = 80$ (Class 0), $N_1 = 20$ (Class 1), $K = 5$.

Each fold should contain $100/5 = 20$ samples total.

**Expected stratified distribution per fold:**

$$\text{Class 0 per fold} = \frac{80}{5} = 16 \text{ samples}$$

$$\text{Class 1 per fold} = \frac{20}{5} = 4 \text{ samples}$$

| Fold | Class 0 Samples | Class 1 Samples | Total | Class 1 Proportion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{F}_1$ | 16 | 4 | 20 | 20% |
| $\mathcal{F}_2$ | 16 | 4 | 20 | 20% |
| $\mathcal{F}_3$ | 16 | 4 | 20 | 20% |
| $\mathcal{F}_4$ | 16 | 4 | 20 | 20% |
| $\mathcal{F}_5$ | 16 | 4 | 20 | 20% |
| **Total** | **80** | **20** | **100** | **20%** |

**Comparison with Vanilla K-Fold:** In a vanilla K-Fold split, a fold might randomly contain $\{18, 2\}$ or $\{20, 0\}$ samples per class — the latter being catastrophic for evaluation since the model would never see a Class 1 example in its validation set. Stratified K-Fold prevents this by enforcing $\{16, 4\}$ in every fold.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to state that Stratified K-Fold is *only* for classification. It has no meaningful analogue for regression (where "Stratified" could be on binned target values, but it is not standard).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not mentioning that scikit-learn's `StratifiedKFold` is the standard implementation, and that it should be imported and used instead of `KFold` for imbalanced classification.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing "Stratified" with "Random" sampling. Stratified is *not* random per class — it is deterministic given the class assignment.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Cross-Validation is a resampling technique for estimating the generalization performance of a model by training and testing on different partitions of the data.
- **Hold-Out:** Single train/test split; **not true CV** but a useful baseline.
- **K-Fold CV:** Partition data into K folds; train on K−1, test on 1, rotate K times; report mean ± std.
- **K = 10:** Empirically the most common choice offering the best bias-variance tradeoff.
- **LOOCV:** $K = N$ special case; **low bias, high variance, computationally expensive**.
- **LPOCV:** Hold out P samples; $\binom{N}{P}$ iterations; rarely used in practice.
- **Stratified K-Fold:** Preserves class proportions in every fold; **mandatory for imbalanced classification**.
- **Repeated K-Fold:** Repeat K-Fold R times with different shuffles; **lower variance** estimate.
- **Time Series CV (Walk-Forward):** Trains on past, tests on future, expanding window; **respects temporal order**.
- **Key Formula:** $E_{CV} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} E_k$ and $\sigma_{CV} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{K-1} \sum (E_k - E_{CV})^2}$
- **Bias-Variance Rule:** Small K → high bias, low variance. Large K → low bias, high variance.
- **Always report** both the mean CV score **and** the standard deviation across folds.
- **Imbalanced data:** Use Stratified K-Fold, not vanilla K-Fold.
- **Time series data:** Use Walk-Forward Validation, never standard K-Fold (to avoid look-ahead bias).
- **Practical implementation:** In scikit-learn, use `cross_val_score`, `KFold`, `StratifiedKFold`, or `LeaveOneOut` from `sklearn.model_selection`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensemble Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ensemble Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Ensemble Methods** are supervised learning meta-algorithms that combine the predictions of multiple individual base learners (also called *weak learners* or *base models*) to produce a single aggregated prediction that is demonstrably more accurate and robust than any of the constituent learners taken in isolation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, an ensemble is constructed under the principle that a *diverse set of moderately accurate models, when strategically combined, can correct each other's individual errors*, thereby minimizing the overall generalization error on unseen data.

Mathematically, given a training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and an ensemble of $M$ base learners $\{h_1, h_2, \dots, h_M\}$, the aggregated hypothesis $H(x)$ is defined as:

$$H(x) = \mathbb{F}\big(h_1(x), h_2(x), \dots, h_M(x)\big)$$

where $\mathbb{F}$ denotes the **combination function** (averaging, voting, or weighted combination).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wisdom of the Crowd"

Imagine you are seriously ill and need a medical diagnosis. Would you trust:
- (a) A single doctor's opinion, or
- (b) The consensus diagnosis from 50 independent doctors, each looking at the same reports from different angles?

The intuition behind **Ensemble Methods** is precisely option (b). A single decision tree might misclassify because it overfits to a particular noise pattern. However, if we train **100 different decision trees** on slightly varied data subsets and let them *vote*, the collective judgment smooths out individual mistakes. This is the celebrated *Condorcet's Jury Theorem* in action — a crowd of moderately competent, *independent* voters almost always outperforms the best single voter.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Definitions for Exam:**
> - **Base Learner / Weak Learner ($h_t$):** A model that performs *slightly better than random guessing*. Example: a shallow decision stump (depth = 1).
> - **Strong Learner ($H$):** A model with arbitrarily high accuracy, built by combining weak learners.
> - **Diversity:** The degree to which base learners make *different* errors on the same inputs. Ensembles *fail* when learners are highly correlated.
> - **Margin:** The confidence difference between the correct class and the nearest competing class in the ensemble's prediction.
> - **Bootstrap Sample:** A sample drawn *with replacement* from the original dataset, of size $N$ (same as original).
> - **Out-Of-Bag (OOB) Error:** Validation error computed on samples *not* included in a particular bootstrap sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The two fundamental conditions for an ensemble to outperform a single model are **(i) Accuracy** (each base learner must beat random chance) and **(ii) Diversity** (base learners must make uncorrelated errors). If both are absent, the ensemble collapses to a single-model performance.

---

## 1.4 The Bias-Variance Trade-off — Why Ensembles Work

The expected prediction error of any model can be decomposed into three irreducible components:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Variance}}_{\text{sensitivity to data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

- **Bagging** primarily *reduces Variance* without inflating Bias — ideal for high-variance models like unpruned decision trees.
- **Boosting** primarily *reduces Bias* by sequentially correcting residual errors — ideal for high-bias underfit models.
- **Stacking** learns an optimal weighted combination to *minimize total error*.

This is the **foundational reason** why KTU examiners test ensemble methods — they are the only systematic, mathematically justified cure for the bias-variance dilemma.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Ensemble Methods

Ensemble methods are classified into four major families, each governed by a distinct combination strategy:

| # | Family | Combination Strategy | Primary Error Targeted | Iconic Algorithm |
|---|--------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Bagging** (Bootstrap Aggregating) | Averaging / Plurality Voting | Variance | Random Forest |
| 2 | **Boosting** | Weighted Majority Voting | Bias | AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting |
| 3 | **Stacking** (Stacked Generalization) | Meta-learner ($H_{meta}$) | Both Bias & Variance | Blended Ensembles |
| 4 | **Voting** | Hard / Soft Voting | Stable Aggregation | Hard & Soft Voting Classifiers |

---

## 2.2 Bagging — Bootstrap Aggregating (Breiman, 1996)

### 2.2.1 Operational Steps

1. From the original training set $\mathcal{D}$ of size $N$, draw $M$ **bootstrap samples** $\{\mathcal{D}_1, \mathcal{D}_2, \dots, \mathcal{D}_M\}$ each of size $N$ using sampling *with replacement*.
2. Train a base learner $h_t$ *independently* on each bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}_t$.
3. For **regression**, aggregate via simple averaging:

$$\hat{y}_{bag} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{t=1}^{M} h_t(x)$$

4. For **classification**, aggregate via majority (plurality) voting:

$$\hat{y}_{bag} = \arg\max_{c \in \mathcal{C}} \sum_{t=1}^{M} \mathbb{I}\big(h_t(x) = c\big)$$

### 2.2.2 Why Bagging Reduces Variance

If the base learners have **identically distributed** predictions with pairwise correlation $\rho$ and individual variance $\sigma^2$, then the variance of the averaged ensemble is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{y}_{bag}) = \rho \, \sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{M} \, \sigma^2$$

> [!NOTE]  
> As $M \to \infty$, the second term vanishes, leaving the residual variance equal to $\rho \sigma^2$. Therefore, bagging's success hinges on making $\rho$ as small as possible (i.e., maximizing diversity).

### 2.2.3 Out-Of-Bag (OOB) Estimation

Each bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}_t$ leaves out, on average, **36.8%** of the original instances (those not selected). These OOB samples form a free validation set:

$$\text{OOB Error} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{I}\big(y_i \neq \hat{y}_{OOB,i}\big)$$

where $\hat{y}_{OOB,i}$ is the aggregated prediction from only those learners that *did not* see sample $i$ during training.

---

## 2.3 Boosting — Sequential Error Correction

### 2.3.1 AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting) — Freund & Schapire, 1997

AdaBoost maintains a **distribution of weights** $w_i^{(t)}$ over training instances, forcing subsequent weak learners to focus on the *hardest* previously misclassified examples.

**Algorithm Outline:**

1. Initialize uniform weights: $w_i^{(1)} = \frac{1}{N}, \quad \forall i \in \{1, \dots, N\}$.
2. For each round $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$:
   - Train weak learner $h_t$ using the current weights.
   - Compute the **weighted error**:

$$\epsilon_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)} \cdot \mathbb{I}(h_t(x_i) \neq y_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)}}$$

   - Compute the **learner confidence coefficient**:

$$\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$$

   - Update instance weights:

$$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\!\big(\alpha_t \cdot \mathbb{I}(h_t(x_i) \neq y_i)\big)$$

   - Renormalize so that $\sum_i w_i^{(t+1)} = 1$.
3. Final prediction:

$$H_{final}(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \, h_t(x)\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Insight:** $\alpha_t$ is *large* when $\epsilon_t$ is small (good learner gets high voting power), and *negative* when $\epsilon_t > 0.5$ (worse than random — AdaBoost aborts).

### 2.3.2 Gradient Boosting — A Generalized View

Gradient Boosting (Friedman, 2001) generalizes AdaBoost by interpreting boosting as **gradient descent in function space**. Instead of reweighting instances, it fits each new learner to the **negative gradient** (residuals) of a chosen loss function $\mathcal{L}$.

For squared-error loss $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - F(x))^2$:

$$r_i^{(t)} = -\left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right]_{F = F_{t-1}} = y_i - F_{t-1}(x_i)$$

The new ensemble becomes:

$$F_t(x) = F_{t-1}(x) + \eta \cdot h_t(x)$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate (shrinkage factor) and $h_t$ is fit to the residuals $r_i^{(t)}$.

---

## 2.4 Random Forest — Bagging + Random Subspace

Random Forest (Breiman, 2001) extends bagging by injecting **randomness at the feature level** as well:

- At each split of each tree, consider only a **random subset** of $m_{try}$ features (typically $m_{try} = \sqrt{p}$ for classification, $p/3$ for regression).
- This decorrelates the trees, driving $\rho$ close to zero and maximizing variance reduction.

**Final prediction:**

$$\hat{y}_{RF}(x) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{t=1}^{M} h_t(x) \quad \text{(regression)}$$

$$\hat{y}_{RF}(x) = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{t=1}^{M} \mathbb{I}(h_t(x) = c) \quad \text{(classification)}$$

---

## 2.5 Stacking (Stacked Generalization)

Stacking (Wolpert, 1992) uses a **meta-learner** $H_{meta}$ to optimally combine the outputs $o_t(x)$ of base learners:

$$H_{final}(x) = H_{meta}\big(o_1(x), o_2(x), \dots, o_M(x)\big)$$

The meta-learner is trained on *out-of-fold predictions* of the base learners to prevent information leakage.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Purpose / Used In |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Ensemble Output (Avg.) | $H(x) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{t=1}^{M} h_t(x)$ | Bagging / RF regression |
| 2 | Ensemble Output (Vote) | $H(x) = \arg\max_c \sum_t \mathbb{I}(h_t(x)=c)$ | Bagging / RF classification |
| 3 | Bagged Variance | $\text{Var} = \rho\sigma^2 + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{M}$ | Quantifies variance reduction |
| 4 | AdaBoost Error | $\epsilon_t = \frac{\sum_i w_i \mathbb{I}(h_t \neq y_i)}{\sum_i w_i}$ | Weighted misclassification |
| 5 | AdaBoost Confidence | $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$ | Voting weight of $h_t$ |
| 6 | AdaBoost Weight Update | $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(\alpha_t \mathbb{I}(h_t \neq y_i))$ | Forces focus on hard cases |
| 7 | Gradient Boost Update | $F_t(x) = F_{t-1}(x) + \eta h_t(x)$ | Sequential residual fitting |
| 8 | OOB Fraction | $\left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N \approx \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | Free validation in RF |
| 9 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Justifies ensemble design |
| 10 | RF Feature Subset | $m_{try} = \sqrt{p}$ (classification) | Decorrelates trees |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Variance Reduction via Bagging

**Goal:** Prove that averaging $M$ independent identically distributed (i.i.d.) base learners reduces variance by a factor of $M$.

**Step 1: Set up the variance of the average.**

Let $h_1(x), h_2(x), \dots, h_M(x)$ be the predictions of $M$ base learners on a fixed input $x$. Define the ensemble prediction:

$$\hat{y}_{bag}(x) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{t=1}^{M} h_t(x)$$

**Step 2: Apply the variance operator to both sides.**

$$\text{Var}\big(\hat{y}_{bag}(x)\big) = \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{M} \sum_{t=1}^{M} h_t(x)\right)$$

**Step 3: Pull the constant $\frac{1}{M}$ out and apply the sum-of-variances rule.**

Using $\text{Var}(aX) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$ and the bilinearity of covariance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\big(\hat{y}_{bag}(x)\big) &= \frac{1}{M^2} \, \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{M} h_t(x)\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{M^2} \left[ \sum_{t=1}^{M} \text{Var}\big(h_t(x)\big) + 2 \sum_{1 \le i < j \le M} \text{Cov}\big(h_i(x), h_j(x)\big) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Assume i.i.d. base learners.**

If each $h_t$ has identical variance $\sigma^2$ and pairwise covariance $\text{Cov}(h_i, h_j) = \rho \sigma^2$ for $i \neq j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}\big(\hat{y}_{bag}(x)\big) &= \frac{1}{M^2}\left[ M\sigma^2 + 2 \binom{M}{2} \rho \sigma^2 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{M^2}\left[ M\sigma^2 + M(M-1)\rho \sigma^2 \right] \\
&= \frac{\sigma^2}{M}\big[1 + (M-1)\rho\big] \\
&= \rho \sigma^2 + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result.**

- If $\rho = 0$ (fully independent learners): $\text{Var} = \sigma^2 / M$ — variance shrinks linearly with $M$.
- If $\rho = 1$ (perfectly correlated learners): $\text{Var} = \sigma^2$ — no benefit at all.
- Realistic case ($0 < \rho < 1$): variance reduces monotonically with $M$, but asymptotes at $\rho \sigma^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conclusion:** Bagging *cannot* eliminate variance completely because real-world models always share some correlation $\rho$ from being trained on similar data. This is why Random Forest adds feature randomness — to drive $\rho \to 0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — AdaBoost Weight Update from Exponential Loss Minimization

**Goal:** Derive the AdaBoost update rule by minimizing the exponential loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_{exp}(H) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\big(-y_i H(x_i)\big)$$

where $H(x) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x)$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$.

**Step 1: Expand the loss at iteration $T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{exp}(H_T) &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\big(-y_i H_{T-1}(x_i) - y_i \alpha_T h_T(x_i)\big) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\big(-y_i H_{T-1}(x_i)\big) \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_T y_i h_T(x_i)\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Let $w_i^{(T)} = \exp(-y_i H_{T-1}(x_i))$ denote the cumulative weight of sample $i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{exp}(H_T) &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(T)} \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_T y_i h_T(x_i)\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Split the sum into correctly and incorrectly classified samples.**

For $h_T(x_i) = y_i$: $y_i h_T(x_i) = +1$, so the inner term is $e^{-\alpha_T}$.
For $h_T(x_i) \neq y_i$: $y_i h_T(x_i) = -1$, so the inner term is $e^{+\alpha_T}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{exp}(H_T) &= e^{-\alpha_T} \sum_{i: \text{correct}} w_i^{(T)} \;+\; e^{\alpha_T} \sum_{i: \text{wrong}} w_i^{(T)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Define weighted accuracy and weighted error.**

Let $W_{correct} = \sum_{i: \text{correct}} w_i^{(T)}$ and $W_{wrong} = \sum_{i: \text{wrong}} w_i^{(T)}$.

Note that $W_{correct} + W_{wrong} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(T)} = 1$ (after normalization), and the weighted error is $\epsilon_T = W_{wrong}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{exp}(H_T) &= e^{-\alpha_T}(1 - \epsilon_T) + e^{\alpha_T} \epsilon_T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Minimize with respect to $\alpha_T$ by setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \alpha_T} = 0$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \alpha_T} &= -e^{-\alpha_T}(1 - \epsilon_T) + e^{\alpha_T} \epsilon_T = 0 \\
e^{\alpha_T} \epsilon_T &= e^{-\alpha_T}(1 - \epsilon_T) \\
e^{2\alpha_T} &= \frac{1 - \epsilon_T}{\epsilon_T} \\
\alpha_T^* &= \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_T}{\epsilon_T}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **exact formula for $\alpha_T$** in AdaBoost.

**Step 6: Substitute back to find the weight update rule.**

The new cumulative weight of sample $i$ after iteration $T$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_i^{(T+1)} &= w_i^{(T)} \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_T y_i h_T(x_i)\big) \\
&= w_i^{(T)} \cdot \exp\!\big(\alpha_T \cdot \mathbb{I}(h_T(x_i) \neq y_i)\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

(since $y_i h_T(x_i) = -1$ on misclassification and $+1$ otherwise)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Final Insight:** The exponential loss is *upper-bounded* by the misclassification loss $\mathbb{I}(h(x) \neq y)$, so minimizing the exponential is a *surrogate* that is differentiable and convex-friendly — this is what makes boosting tractable.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Ensemble

| Method | Best For | Risk of Overfitting | Handles Class Imbalance? | Interpretability | Training Time |
|--------|----------|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| **Bagging** | High-variance base learners (deep trees) | Low | Moderate | Medium (via OOB) | Parallelizable |
| **Random Forest** | Tabular data, mixed feature types | Low | Good (with class weights) | Medium (feature importance) | Parallelizable |
| **AdaBoost** | Clean data, weak learners | High (sensitive to noise) | Moderate | Low (sequential weights) | Sequential |
| **Gradient Boosting** | State-of-the-art accuracy on tabular | Medium (use shrinkage) | Good (custom loss) | Low | Sequential |
| **XGBoost** | Large-scale competitions | Low (regularized) | Excellent | Medium | Parallelizable |
| **Stacking** | Heterogeneous model pools | Medium-High | Depends on meta-learner | Low | Two-stage |
