# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define Apache Hadoop. List any four of its key characteristics.*

**Model Answer:**

Apache Hadoop is an open-source, Java-based framework for **distributed storage and parallel processing** of large datasets across clusters of commodity computers. It implements **HDFS** for storage and **MapReduce** (managed by **YARN**) for processing.

Four key characteristics:
1. **Scalability** — scales horizontally by adding more nodes
2. **Fault Tolerance** — automatic recovery from hardware failure via replication
3. **Cost-Effective** — uses commodity hardware instead of expensive servers
4. **Flexibility** — handles structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for characteristics)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Style]**
*What is HDFS? Why is the default block size 128 MB and not 1 KB or 1 GB?*

**Model Answer:**

**HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files across a Hadoop cluster.

Block size = **128 MB** is chosen because:
- If blocks were **1 KB** → billions of blocks → NameNode memory would crash
- If blocks were **1 GB** → too few blocks per file → no parallelism; one file would use just 1–2 nodes
- **128 MB** strikes a balance: enough blocks for parallelism, few enough for NameNode efficiency

*(3 marks: 1 for HDFS definition + 2 for block-size justification)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the **HDFS architecture** with a neat diagram. Describe the role of **NameNode**, **DataNode**, and **Secondary NameNode** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Hadoop cluster stores a file of size **2 GB** with the default block size and default replication factor. Calculate:
- (i) Number of blocks
- (ii) Number of physical blocks across DataNodes
- (iii) Total physical storage consumed
- (iv) If the replication factor is reduced to 2, what is the new physical storage? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a): HDFS Architecture (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** HDFS is a master-slave, distributed file system following a **write-once, read-many** access model. It is designed to store very large files reliably across commodity hardware.

**Architecture (3 marks):**

HDFS uses a **NameNode (master)** and multiple **DataNodes (slaves)**. The NameNode runs on a master server and manages the file system namespace, storing **metadata** in RAM. The DataNodes run on every other node in the cluster and store the actual **data blocks**. Clients interact with the NameNode for metadata, but data transfer happens **directly** between Client and DataNode — the NameNode is not in the data path.

**Roles (3 marks):**

- **NameNode**: Stores the **filesystem metadata** — file names, directory structure, block → DataNode mappings, permissions, replication factor. On startup, it loads metadata into RAM from the **FSImage** file and replays the **EditLog**. It receives **heartbeats** every 3 seconds from each DataNode and uses them to track cluster health. It also handles block re-replication when a DataNode fails.

- **DataNode**: Stores the actual **data blocks** (default 128 MB each). On startup, it registers with the NameNode. It serves read/write requests from clients and periodically sends block reports and heartbeats to the NameNode. Each block is replicated across multiple DataNodes (default RF = 3).

- **Secondary NameNode**: Despite its name, it is **not a hot standby** for the NameNode. Its job is to **periodically merge the EditLog with the FSImage** to prevent the EditLog from growing too large. This process is called **checkpointing**. In Hadoop 2.x, this role is replaced by a **Standby NameNode** for true High Availability.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes).**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b): Numerical Block Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- FileSize = 2 GB = $2 \times 1024\,\text{MB} = 2048\,\text{MB}$
- Default Block Size = 128 MB
- Default Replication Factor = 3

**(i) Number of blocks (1 mark):**

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{FileSize}}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2048}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 16 \rceil = 16 \text{ blocks}
$$

**(ii) Number of physical blocks (2 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{physical}} = N_{\text{blocks}} \times \text{RF} = 16 \times 3 = 48 \text{ physical blocks}
$$

**(iii) Total physical storage (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Storage} = N_{\text{physical}} \times B = 48 \times 128 = 6144\,\text{MB} = 6\,\text{GB}
$$

(Or shortcut: $2048 \times 3 = 6144\,\text{MB} = 6\,\text{GB}$)

**(iv) Storage with RF = 2 (2 marks):**

$$
\text{New Storage} = 2048 \times 2 = 4096\,\text{MB} = 4\,\text{GB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to **convert GB → MB** before dividing. Using $2 / 128$ would give a wrong result of 0.016. Always express file size in the **same unit** as block size first. Also, do not confuse *logical blocks* (16) with *physical blocks* (48) — they are different.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Choice B]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MapReduce programming model**. Describe the **Map phase**, **Shuffle and Sort phase**, and **Reduce phase** in detail. Write the pseudocode for a Word Count program. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Hadoop 1.x** and **Hadoop 2.x** architectures. Explain the role of **YARN** with a diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a): MapReduce (7 marks)

**Overview (1 mark):** MapReduce is a programming model for **parallel, distributed processing** of large datasets. It works in two main phases — Map and Reduce — separated by an automatic Shuffle & Sort.

**Map Phase (1 mark):** The input is divided into **input splits** (typically of HDFS block size). Each split is processed by a **Mapper** function that produces intermediate (key, value) pairs.

**Shuffle and Sort Phase (1 mark):** The framework automatically groups all intermediate pairs with the same key. Values for the same key are gathered into a list. This phase is network-intensive and is often the bottleneck.

**Reduce Phase (1 mark):** The **Reducer** processes each (key, list-of-values) group and emits final (key, value) pairs that are written to HDFS.

**Word Count Pseudocode (3 marks):**

```
map(key, value):
    for each word w in value:
        emit(w, 1)

reduce(key, list_of_values):
    sum = 0
    for each v in list_of_values:
        sum = sum + v
    emit(key, sum)
```

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.3 of these notes).**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b): Hadoop 1.x vs 2.x + YARN (7 marks)

**Hadoop 1.x Architecture (2 marks):**
- Components: **HDFS** (NameNode + DataNodes) and **MapReduce** (JobTracker + TaskTrackers)
- The **JobTracker** handled both resource management AND job scheduling/monitoring — a heavy responsibility
- Could only run **MapReduce** jobs; no support for other processing engines
- **Single point of failure**: If JobTracker or NameNode crashed, the entire cluster failed

**Hadoop 2.x Architecture (2 marks):**
- Introduced **YARN** as a separate resource-management layer
- Resource management and job scheduling are **decoupled**
- Supports **multiple processing engines** — MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Flink, Storm
- High Availability (HA) NameNode via Standby NameNode
- Scalable up to **10,000+ nodes**

**YARN Components (3 marks):**
- **ResourceManager (RM)** — Master; allocates cluster resources using a Scheduler (Capacity or Fair)
- **NodeManager (NM)** — Per-node; manages containers and reports to RM
- **ApplicationMaster (AM)** — Per-application; negotiates resources with RM and coordinates task execution
- **Container** — Abstract resource bundle (CPU + RAM) where tasks run

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common student error is calling **YARN "Yet Another Resource Manager"**. The correct full form is **"Yet Another Resource Negotiator"**. Examiners deduct marks for this. Also, do not confuse the **Secondary NameNode** with a hot standby — it is only a checkpointing helper in Hadoop 1.x. In Hadoop 2.x+ HA, the **Standby NameNode** plays the hot-standby role.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Hadoop = HDFS (Storage) + MapReduce (Processing) + YARN (Resource Mgmt) + Hadoop Common**
- ⭐ **HDFS** is a **write-once, read-many** distributed file system
- ⭐ **Default block size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x/3.x); **Default replication factor = 3**
- ⭐ **NameNode** stores **metadata only**; **DataNodes** store **actual blocks**
- ⭐ **Heartbeat interval = 3 seconds**; **DataNode timeout = 10 minutes**
- ⭐ **MapReduce** has two main phases: **Map** and **Reduce**, with **Shuffle & Sort** in between
- ⭐ **YARN = "Yet Another Resource Negotiator"** (NOT "Manager")
- ⭐ YARN decouples **resource management** from **data processing**
- ⭐ **YARN components**: ResourceManager, NodeManager, ApplicationMaster, Container
- ⭐ **Hadoop 1.x**: JobTracker/TaskTracker; **Hadoop 2.x+**: ResourceManager/NodeManager
- ⭐ **Schema-on-Read** makes Hadoop flexible for unstructured data
- ⭐ **Data Locality** = computation moves to data, not vice-versa
- ⭐ Hadoop's **fault tolerance** comes from **block replication**
- ⭐ **Block count formula**: $\lceil \text{FileSize} / B \rceil$
- ⭐ **Total physical storage** = $\text{FileSize} \times \text{Replication Factor}$
- ⭐ **Combiner** is an optional Map-side mini-reducer to reduce network traffic
- ⭐ **Partitioner** decides which Reducer gets which key (default = hash mod R)
- ⭐ Hadoop is **NOT** a replacement for RDBMS — it complements it for batch big-data workloads
- ⭐ Hadoop is designed for **few large files**, not millions of small files
- ⭐ YARN supports **multiple processing engines**: MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Flink, Storm
- ⭐ **Rack-aware replication** keeps 2 replicas on one rack, 1 on another
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysing Data with Hadoop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysing Data with Hadoop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Apache Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based, distributed computing framework that enables the **storage, processing, and analysis of massive datasets** (Big Data) across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models. It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, Hadoop is positioned as the foundational **distributed data analytics engine** that allows engineers to perform parallel batch processing on petabytes of unstructured and semi-structured data — a workload that traditional single-node systems cannot handle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition:** Hadoop = **HDFS (Storage Layer)** + **MapReduce (Processing Layer)** + **YARN (Resource Management Layer)**. Every exam answer on this topic must reference this three-pillar architecture.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Hadoop (KTU High-Yield)

| Pillar | Full Form | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **HDFS** | Hadoop Distributed File System | Distributed, fault-tolerant storage |
| **MapReduce** | Map + Reduce programming model | Parallel data processing paradigm |
| **YARN** | Yet Another Resource Negotiator | Cluster resource management & job scheduling |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Mega Library Warehouse:**
>
> Imagine a library so large that no single building can hold all its books. The librarian's solution:
>
> 1. **Shred the catalogue** (data) into small, identical **boxes** (blocks of **128 MB** by default).
> 2. **Distribute the boxes** across **50 warehouse branches** (DataNodes) located in different cities.
> 3. **Hire a head librarian** (NameNode) in the central office who maintains a **master index** of which box is in which branch.
> 4. When a researcher asks, *"How many books mention Hadoop?"* — the head librarian **dispatches junior librarians** (Mappers) to **count words in parallel** at each branch.
> 5. After all branches finish, a **senior librarian** (Reducer) **combines the partial counts** into one final report.
> 6. If one warehouse burns down, the boxes are already **photocopied** (replicated, default **3 copies**) in other branches — no data is lost.
>
> This is exactly how Hadoop works — *distribute, parallel-process, aggregate, and tolerate failure*.

### 1.4 Why Hadoop Exists — The Big Data Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 V's of Big Data** (KTU Frequently Asked):
> - **Volume** — Terabytes to Petabytes of data
> - **Velocity** — Data generated at high speed (streaming, IoT)
> - **Variety** — Structured, semi-structured, unstructured
> - **Veracity** — Data trustworthiness and quality
> - **Value** — Extracting meaningful insights

Traditional RDBMS fails when **Volume** exceeds a single machine's storage or when **Velocity** demands real-time parallel ingestion. Hadoop solves this by **scaling out (horizontal scaling)** — adding more cheap commodity nodes — rather than scaling up (vertical scaling) with expensive mainframes.

### 1.5 Key Terminology Students Must Memorize

- **Cluster**: A collection of commodity machines (nodes) working together.
- **Node**: A single machine in the cluster.
- **Master Node**: Runs the **NameNode** and **ResourceManager** (in YARN).
- **Slave/Worker Node**: Runs **DataNode** and **NodeManager**.
- **Job**: A complete unit of work submitted to Hadoop (e.g., a full MapReduce program).
- **Task**: A sub-unit of a job — either a *Map task* or a *Reduce task*.
- **Block**: The smallest unit of data HDFS can read/write (default **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x+).
- **Rack**: A physical grouping of nodes; Hadoop uses rack-awareness for fault tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HDFS — The Storage Backbone

HDFS follows the **Write-Once, Read-Many (WORM)** model. It is optimized for streaming reads of large files, not for random writes.

#### 2.1.1 HDFS Architecture Components

1. **NameNode (Master)**:
   - Stores **metadata** (file names, permissions, block locations, replication factor).
   - Maintains the filesystem in RAM using two files: `fsimage` (checkpoint) and `edit log` (recent changes).
   - **Single point of failure** (mitigated by Secondary NameNode / Checkpoint Node in Hadoop 2.x, and by HA NameNodes in Hadoop 3.x).

2. **DataNode (Slave)**:
   - Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disk.
   - Sends **heartbeat signals** to NameNode every **3 seconds** by default.
   - If heartbeat is missed for **10 minutes (600 seconds)**, NameNode declares the DataNode dead and triggers re-replication.

3. **Secondary NameNode**:
   - **Misnomer** — it is NOT a backup NameNode.
   - Periodically merges `fsimage` + `edit log` to prevent the edit log from growing unbounded. (Acts as a *Checkpoint Node*.)

4. **Client**:
   - The user application interacting with HDFS.
   - Reads metadata from NameNode, then reads actual blocks directly from DataNodes (the data path bypasses the NameNode for performance).

#### 2.1.2 HDFS Data Write Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

1. Client splits file into **blocks of 128 MB**.
2. Client requests NameNode for block placement.
3. NameNode returns a **replication pipeline** of DataNode locations (rack-aware).
4. Client pushes block to first DataNode → second → third (in pipeline).
5. Each DataNode confirms receipt via **ACK packet** sent backward.
6. After replication, client is notified; the same repeats for next block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rack Awareness:** Hadoop prefers placing **1 replica on a node in the local rack**, **1 replica on a different node in the same rack**, and **1 replica on a different rack entirely**. This balances latency, bandwidth, and fault tolerance.

#### 2.1.3 HDFS Fault Tolerance

- **Replication Factor (default = 3)**: Each block is stored in 3 locations.
- If a DataNode fails, NameNode triggers **re-replication** of its blocks on healthy nodes.
- If NameNode fails (non-HA setup), the entire cluster is unavailable — this is the **biggest weakness** of classic Hadoop.

### 2.2 MapReduce — The Processing Backbone

MapReduce is a **divide-and-conquer parallel programming model** inspired by Google's 2004 paper. It has two main user-defined functions:

| Function | Input | Output | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mapper** | `<K1, V1>` pairs | List of `<K2, V2>` pairs (intermediate) | Transforms/extracts data in parallel per split |
| **Reducer** | `<K2, List<V2>>` | List of `<K3, V3>` pairs | Aggregates intermediate values per key |

Between Mapper and Reducer, the framework automatically performs:

1. **Shuffle**: Intermediate key-value pairs are partitioned, sorted, and transferred to reducers.
2. **Sort**: Pairs are grouped by key (a total ordering is guaranteed).
3. **Partition**: Determines which reducer receives which key. **Default Partitioner** uses `hash(key) mod R`, where $R$ is the number of reducers.

#### 2.2.1 Optional Optimization Components

- **Combiner**: A *local reducer* that runs on the mapper node to pre-aggregate data and reduce network traffic. Must be **idempotent and associative** (e.g., sum, max). The output of a combiner is fed back into the same mapper's output collector.
- **Partitioner**: Custom logic to control data distribution across reducers (useful for skewed keys).
- **Counter**: A global counter incremented in mappers/reducers for monitoring.

### 2.3 YARN — The Resource Manager

YARN decouples resource management from data processing (a key evolution from Hadoop 1.x).

| YARN Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **ResourceManager (RM)** | Global arbiter of cluster resources; runs on master node |
| **NodeManager (NM)** | Per-node agent; manages containers and monitors resource usage |
| **ApplicationMaster (AM)** | Per-application; negotiates resources with RM and works with NMs |
| **Container** | An allocation of CPU + RAM on a node where a task runs |

**YARN Job Flow:**
Client → ResourceManager → ApplicationMaster → NodeManagers → Containers (which run Map/Reduce tasks)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Formulas for Hadoop (Kerala University Exam Focus)**

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Default Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block size | $B$ | **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+) | Configurable via `dfs.blocksize` |
| Replication factor | $r$ | **3** | Configurable via `dfs.replication` |
| Number of blocks for file of size $S$ | $\left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil$ | — | Always rounded **UP** |
| Total storage used by file | $S \times r$ | — | HDFS stores $r$ copies |
| Number of DataNodes needed | $\ge r$ | 3 | Minimum for default replication |
| Number of mappers | $\approx \frac{S}{B}$ | — | One map task per block (approximately) |
| Number of reducers | $R$ (configurable) | **1** (default) | Tuned via `mapreduce.job.reduces` |
| Partitioner function | $\text{part}(k) = \text{hash}(k) \bmod R$ | — | Default partitioner |
| Heartbeat interval | — | **3 seconds** | DataNode → NameNode |
| Heartbeat timeout | — | **10 minutes** (600 s) | Time before declaring a DataNode dead |
| HDFS write packet size | — | **64 KB** | Used in pipeline replication |
| YARN container memory | $M_{\text{cont}}$ | **1 GB** default | Configurable |

> [!NOTE]
> In the LaTeX row above, $\left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil$ represents the **ceiling function** — even a partially-filled last block counts as a full block. This is the most common 3-mark calculation in KTU exams.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance**: Fraud detection across billions of transactions.
- **Healthcare**: Genomic data analysis (a single human genome ≈ 200 GB).
- **Social Media Analytics**: Sentiment analysis of Twitter/Reddit streams.
- **IoT & Smart Cities**: Real-time ingestion and analytics from millions of sensors.
- **ETL Pipelines**: Pre-processing massive logs before feeding into ML models (which is precisely why Hadoop sits in **Module 3 of PCCIT503** — as the data-prep layer for downstream ML).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: HDFS Block Calculation

**Problem (Typical KTU 3-Mark Question):**
> A log file of size **540 MB** is uploaded to HDFS with a block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. Calculate:
> (a) Number of blocks created.
> (b) Total physical storage consumed.
> (c) Number of DataNodes required (minimum).

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- File size $S = 540 \text{ MB}$
- Block size $B = 128 \text{ MB}$
- Replication factor $r = 3$

**(a) Number of blocks $N_{\text{blocks}}$:**

$$N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil$$

$$N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{540}{128} \right\rceil$$

$$\frac{540}{128} = 4.21875$$

Since the result is not a whole number, we apply the ceiling function:

$$N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil 4.21875 \rceil = 5$$

**Logical Explanation:** Dividing 540 by 128 gives 4 full blocks (4 × 128 = 512 MB) with **28 MB leftover**. HDFS cannot store partial blocks; hence, a 5th block is created holding the remaining 28 MB (with the rest of the block's 100 MB being effectively unused space — this is called **internal fragmentation** in HDFS).

**(b) Total physical storage $S_{\text{total}}$:**

$$S_{\text{total}} = S \times r$$

$$S_{\text{total}} = 540 \text{ MB} \times 3$$

$$S_{\text{total}} = 1620 \text{ MB}$$

**Logical Explanation:** Each of the 5 logical blocks is replicated 3 times across the cluster. So 540 MB of logical data occupies 3× the physical disk space.

**(c) Minimum number of DataNodes $N_{\text{DN}}$:**

$$N_{\text{DN}} \geq r = 3$$

$$N_{\text{DN}} = 3$$

**Logical Explanation:** To satisfy the replication factor of 3, each block must reside on at least 3 distinct DataNodes. A cluster with fewer than 3 DataNodes cannot honor the default replication policy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If the question adds *"with rack-awareness"*, the **minimum rack count becomes 2** (since 1 replica is local rack, 1 is different node in same rack, 1 is different rack). Many students miss this nuance.

### 3.2 Exhaustive MapReduce Walkthrough — Word Count Algorithm

**Problem:** Given the input text:
```
"hadoop is big data. hadoop is distributed. big data is valuable."
```
Trace the MapReduce execution step-by-step assuming **2 Reducers** and the default partitioner.

#### Step 1: Input Split
The framework reads the input into splits (typically one per HDFS block). The input to the Mapper is **line-by-line**.

#### Step 2: Map Phase
For each line, the Mapper tokenizes and emits `<word, 1>` pairs.

| Input Line | Mapper Output |
|---|---|
| `hadoop is big data.` | `(hadoop,1), (is,1), (big,1), (data,1)` |
| `hadoop is distributed.` | `(hadoop,1), (is,1), (distributed,1)` |
| `big data is valuable.` | `(big,1), (data,1), (is,1), (valuable,1)` |

**Combined Intermediate Output (after all Mappers):**
`(hadoop,1), (is,1), (big,1), (data,1), (hadoop,1), (is,1), (distributed,1), (big,1), (data,1), (is,1), (valuable,1)`

#### Step 3: Combiner (Optional, but typically applied for Word Count)
The Combiner runs locally on each Mapper node and pre-aggregates by key.

If Mapper 1 produced: `(hadoop,1), (is,1), (big,1), (data,1), (hadoop,1), (is,1)`
After Combiner on Mapper 1: `(hadoop,2), (is,2), (big,1), (data,1)`

If Mapper 2 produced: `(distributed,1), (big,1), (data,1), (is,1), (valuable,1)`
After Combiner on Mapper 2: `(distributed,1), (big,1), (data,1), (is,1), (valuable,1)`

**Network traffic reduced from 11 pairs to 9 pairs.**

#### Step 4: Shuffle & Sort (Framework-managed)
The framework groups all values by key and sorts the keys.

| Key | Values (List of 1s) |
|---|---|
| `big` | `[1, 1]` |
| `data` | `[1, 1]` |
| `distributed` | `[1]` |
| `hadoop` | `[2]` |
| `is` | `[2, 1]` |
| `valuable` | `[1]` |

#### Step 5: Partitioning
With $R = 2$ reducers, default partitioner is:

$$\text{part}(k) = \text{hash}(k) \bmod 2$$

| Key | hash (assumed) | hash mod 2 | Assigned Reducer |
|---|---|---|---|
| `big` | (e.g.) 7 | 1 | Reducer 2 |
| `data` | (e.g.) 9 | 1 | Reducer 2 |
| `distributed` | (e.g.) 14 | 0 | Reducer 1 |
| `hadoop` | (e.g.) 5 | 1 | Reducer 2 |
| `is` | (e.g.) 2 | 0 | Reducer 1 |
| `valuable` | (e.g.) 8 | 0 | Reducer 1 |

#### Step 6: Reduce Phase

**Reducer 1 receives:** `(distributed,[1]), (is,[2,1]), (valuable,[1])`
After summation: `(distributed,1), (is,3), (valuable,1)`

**Reducer 2 receives:** `(big,[1,1]), (data,[1,1]), (hadoop,[2])`
After summation: `(big,2), (data,2), (hadoop,2)`

#### Step 7: Final Output (written back to HDFS)
```
distributed    1
is             3
valuable       1
big            2
data           2
hadoop         2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this example matters for KTU:** It tests the student's understanding of (a) Mapper/Reducer symmetry, (b) Combiner optimization, (c) the `hash mod R` partitioner math, and (d) the distinction between *intermediate* and *final* key-value pairs.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hadoop vs Traditional RDBMS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU commonly asks "Differentiate between Hadoop and RDBMS" for 5–7 marks.** Memorize this comparison matrix.

| Parameter | Hadoop (HDFS + MapReduce) | Traditional RDBMS |
|---|---|---|
| Data scale | **Petabytes / Exabytes** | **Gigabytes / Terabytes** (typically) |
| Data type | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured | Structured (tables) |
| Schema | **Schema-on-read** (flexible) | **Schema-on-write** (rigid) |
| Processing | Batch-oriented (MapReduce) | OLTP / interactive queries |
| Transaction support | Limited (no full ACID in classic Hadoop; Hive added ACID later) | Full **ACID** compliance |
| Cost | **Low** (commodity hardware) | **High** (specialized servers) |
| Scaling model | **Horizontal** (add more nodes) | **Vertical** (bigger machine) |
| Fault tolerance | High (replication) | Lower (RAID-based) |
| Latency | **High** (batch minutes-to-hours) | **Low** (milliseconds) |
| Data locality | Yes (compute moves to data) | No (data moves to compute) |
| Best for | Log analytics, ETL, ML data prep, archival | Transactional systems, reporting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HDFS Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

<!-- Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied: All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; no markdown formatting inside labels. -->

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Client Application]
    nodeB[NameNode Master]
    nodeC[Secondary NameNode Checkpoint]
    nodeD1[DataNode 1 Rack R1]
    nodeD2[DataNode 2 Rack R1]
    nodeD3[DataNode 3 Rack R2]
    nodeD4[DataNode 4 Rack R2]
    nodeE1[Block A1 128 MB]
    nodeE2[Block A2 128 MB]
    nodeE3[Block A3 128 MB]
    nodeF1[Replicated Block A1]
    nodeF2[Replicated Block A2]
    nodeF3[Replicated Block A3]

    nodeA -- "1 Request Metadata" --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "2 Return Block Locations" --> nodeA
    nodeB -- "fsimage + edit log sync" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "3 Send Replication Pipeline" --> nodeD1
    nodeA -- "4 Write Block" --> nodeD1
    nodeD1 -- "5 Forward Block" --> nodeD2
    nodeD2 -- "6 Forward Block" --> nodeD3
    nodeD1 -- "ACK" --> nodeA
    nodeD2 -- "ACK" --> nodeA
    nodeD3 -- "ACK" --> nodeA
    nodeD1 --> nodeE1
    nodeD2 --> nodeE2
    nodeD3 --> nodeE3
    nodeD4 --> nodeF1
    nodeD1 --> nodeF2
    nodeD2 --> nodeF3
    nodeD1 -- "Heartbeat every 3 sec" --> nodeB
    nodeD2 -- "Heartbeat every 3 sec" --> nodeB
    nodeD3 -- "Heartbeat every 3 sec" --> nodeB
    nodeD4 -- "Heartbeat every 3 sec" --> nodeB
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The **Client** first consults the **NameNode** for metadata.
- The **NameNode** replies with the **replication pipeline** (rack-aware ordering).
- The **Client** streams the block to **DataNode 1 → 2 → 3** in sequence.
- Each DataNode sends back an **ACK** (acknowledgment packet).
- The **Secondary NameNode** does NOT serve read/write requests; it periodically merges the NameNode's logs.
- **Heartbeat** arrows show liveness detection — if a heartbeat is missed for 10 minutes, the NameNode marks that DataNode as dead and re-replicates its blocks.

### 4.2 MapReduce Data Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput[HDFS Input Splits]
    nodeM1[Mapper 1]
    nodeM2[Mapper 2]
    nodeM3[Mapper 3]
    nodeC1[Combiner 1]
    nodeC2[Combiner 2]
    nodeC3[Combiner 3]
    nodeShuffle[Shuffle and Sort Framework]
    nodeP[Partitioner hash mod R]
    nodeR1[Reducer 1]
    nodeR2[Reducer 2]
    nodeOut1[HDFS Output Part 1]
    nodeOut2[HDFS Output Part 2]

    nodeInput --> nodeM1
    nodeInput --> nodeM2
    nodeInput --> nodeM3
    nodeM1 --> nodeC1
    nodeM2 --> nodeC2
    nodeM3 --> nodeC3
    nodeC1 --> nodeShuffle
    nodeC2 --> nodeShuffle
    nodeC3 --> nodeShuffle
    nodeShuffle --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeR1
    nodeP --> nodeR2
    nodeR1 --> nodeOut1
    nodeR2 --> nodeOut2
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Input Splits** feed Mappers in parallel.
- **Combiners** (optional) compress data locally to reduce network overhead.
- **Shuffle & Sort** is the framework's responsibility — not user code.
- **Partitioner** routes keys to the appropriate Reducer.
- **Reducers** write final output to HDFS as separate `part-r-00000`, `part-r-00001` files.

### 4.3 YARN Job Submission Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeClient[Client Application]
    nodeRM[ResourceManager Master]
    nodeScheduler[Scheduler FIFO or Capacity or Fair]
    nodeAM[ApplicationMaster Per Job]
    nodeNM1[NodeManager Node 1]
    nodeNM2[NodeManager Node 2]
    nodeCont1[Container 1 Map Task]
    nodeCont2[Container 2 Reduce Task]

    nodeClient -- "1 Submit Job" --> nodeRM
    nodeRM --> nodeScheduler
    nodeRM -- "2 Allocate Container for AM" --> nodeNM1
    nodeNM1 -- "3 Launch AM" --> nodeAM
    nodeAM -- "4 Request Resources from RM" --> nodeRM
    nodeRM -- "5 Grant Containers" --> nodeAM
    nodeAM -- "6 Launch Map Task" --> nodeNM1
    nodeAM -- "7 Launch Reduce Task" --> nodeNM2
    nodeNM1 --> nodeCont1
    nodeNM2 --> nodeCont2
```

**Reading Guide:** YARN's strength is that **multiple applications** (MapReduce, Spark, Tez, etc.) can share the same cluster simultaneously — each gets its own ApplicationMaster but shares the global ResourceManager.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Hadoop. List and briefly explain its two core components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Apache Hadoop is an open-source framework that allows for the **distributed storage and processing of large datasets** across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models.

**Core Components (2 marks):**
1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — Provides distributed, fault-tolerant storage by splitting files into blocks (default 128 MB) and replicating them (default 3 copies) across multiple DataNodes.
2. **MapReduce** — A parallel programming model with `map()` and `reduce()` functions that processes the stored data in parallel across the cluster.

> [!NOTE]
> For a more complete answer, students should also mention **YARN** (resource management), as it is the third pillar in modern Hadoop 2.x/3.x architecture. This may fetch the extra mark for thoroughness.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of the NameNode and DataNode in HDFS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**NameNode (1.5 marks):**
- The **master daemon** that manages the filesystem namespace.
- Stores **metadata** (file names, directory structure, block-to-DataNode mapping, permissions, replication factor) in memory.
- Does NOT store actual file data.
- Acts as the **single point of contact** for all client metadata requests.

**DataNode (1.5 marks):**
- The **slave daemon** that runs on every worker node.
- Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disk.
- Sends **heartbeat signals** to the NameNode every **3 seconds** to report liveness.
- Performs read/write operations on behalf of clients as directed by the NameNode.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write that the NameNode stores the actual data. This is **incorrect** — NameNode stores only **metadata**. Data lives on DataNodes. The examiner will deduct a mark for this confusion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions in this module carry **14 marks** and offer an internal choice. The model below presents two full alternative questions, each with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the HDFS architecture in detail. With a neat diagram, explain the data write process including the role of NameNode, DataNode, replication pipeline, and ACK packets.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**HDFS Architecture (3 marks):**
HDFS follows a **master-slave architecture**:
- **NameNode (Master)**: Maintains filesystem metadata in RAM. Stores information about file-to-block mapping, block locations, and replication factor. Acts as the coordinator.
- **DataNodes (Slaves)**: Run on every worker node. Store actual data blocks. Send heartbeats every 3 seconds to NameNode.
- **Secondary NameNode**: A helper node that periodically merges `fsimage` and `edit logs` to prevent log overflow. **It is NOT a hot standby** for the NameNode.
- **Client**: The user application. Reads metadata from NameNode but bypasses it to read/write actual block data directly from/to DataNodes.

(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for visual representation.)

**Data Write Process (4 marks) — Step-by-step:**

1. **Client Initiates Write**: The client calls `create()` on `DistributedFileSystem`, which contacts the NameNode via RPC.
2. **Metadata Response**: NameNode checks file validity and returns a list of DataNodes for the **replication pipeline** (chosen using rack-awareness policy).
3. **Block Splitting**: The client splits the file into 128 MB blocks.
4. **Pipeline Transfer**: The client pushes the first packet (64 KB) to the first DataNode, which forwards to the second, which forwards to the third — forming a **chain pipeline**.
5. **ACK Reverse Path**: After each DataNode writes the packet to its local disk, it sends an **ACK packet** back up the chain to the client.
6. **Block Completion**: Once the full block is written and replicated, the client is notified. The NameNode updates its metadata.
7. **Repeat**: Steps 4–6 are repeated for each subsequent block.
8. **Failure Handling**: If any DataNode in the pipeline fails, the NameNode reassigns a new DataNode, and the client resumes writing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — "ACK packet confusion":** Students frequently write that ACKs go from DataNodes to the NameNode. **No** — ACKs go back to the **Client** so the Client knows the data is safely persisted. The NameNode gets only the heartbeats and block reports.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the MapReduce programming model. With a suitable example, illustrate the role of Mapper, Combiner, Partitioner, and Reducer. Mention the default partitioner formula.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**MapReduce Overview (1 mark):**
MapReduce is a **parallel, distributed data processing model** based on the `map()` and `reduce()` functions. It processes data in two main phases, with an automatic shuffle/sort phase in between.

**Phases and Components (4 marks):**

| Phase / Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Input Split** | Splits input into logical chunks; one Mapper per split. |
| **Mapper** | Transforms input `<K1, V1>` into intermediate `<K2, V2>` pairs. Runs in parallel on worker nodes. |
| **Combiner** | Local aggregation on Mapper node to reduce network traffic. Must be associative and commutative (e.g., sum, max). |
| **Partitioner** | Routes intermediate keys to specific Reducers. Default formula: `hash(key) mod R` where R = number of reducers. |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | Framework-managed transfer and grouping of data by key. |
| **Reducer** | Aggregates values for each key. Outputs final `<K3, V3>` pairs to HDFS. |

**Worked Example (2 marks) — Word Count:**
For input: `"hadoop is fast. hadoop is distributed."`
- **Mapper output**: `(hadoop,1), (is,1), (fast,1), (hadoop,1), (is,1), (distributed,1)`
- **Combiner output (per mapper)**: `(hadoop,2), (is,2), (fast,1), (distributed,1)`
- **Partitioner** with R=2: Routes keys to Reducer 1 or 2 based on `hash(key) mod 2`.
- **Reducer output**: `(hadoop,2), (is,2), (fast,1), (distributed,1)` written to HDFS as `part-r-0000*` files.

**Default Partitioner Formula:**

$$\text{part}(k) = \text{hash}(k) \bmod R$$

where $R$ is the number of Reducers and `hash` is Java's `Object.hashCode()` (consistent across executions).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks] Differentiate between Hadoop and traditional RDBMS. Mention any 7 points with a clear comparison table. Why is Hadoop preferred for Big Data analytics?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (5 marks — 7 points):**

| Parameter | Hadoop (HDFS + MapReduce) | Traditional RDBMS |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Data scale | Petabytes to Exabytes | Gigabytes to Terabytes |
| 2. Data variety | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured | Primarily structured (tables) |
| 3. Schema | Schema-on-read (flexible) | Schema-on-write (rigid DDL) |
| 4. Processing mode | Batch-oriented (high latency) | Interactive OLTP (low latency) |
| 5. Cost | Low — commodity hardware | High — proprietary enterprise servers |
| 6. Scaling model | Horizontal (scale out) | Vertical (scale up) |
| 7. Fault tolerance | Built-in via replication (default 3 copies) | Relies on hardware RAID |

**Why Hadoop for Big Data (2 marks):**
- **Horizontal scalability**: Add more cheap nodes rather than buying bigger machines.
- **Fault tolerance**: Replicated data survives node failures — ideal for commodity hardware that fails often.
- **Data locality**: Computation is moved to where data resides, reducing network congestion.
- **Schema flexibility**: Ideal for raw, messy, evolving data formats (logs, JSON, images, video).
- **Ecosystem maturity**: Tools like Hive, Pig, Spark, HBase, Sqoop extend Hadoop into a complete analytics platform.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] What is YARN? Explain its architecture and how it improves upon Hadoop 1.x. List the responsibilities of ResourceManager, NodeManager, and ApplicationMaster.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**YARN Definition (1 mark):**
YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) is the **cluster resource management layer** introduced in Hadoop 2.x. It decouples resource management from data processing, enabling multiple applications (MapReduce, Spark, Tez, etc.) to share a single cluster.

**Hadoop 1.x Limitations (2 marks):**
- In Hadoop 1.x, the **JobTracker** handled both resource management and job scheduling/monitoring — a single point of contention and failure.
- The cluster could only run **MapReduce jobs**; other processing models were unsupported.
- **Scalability ceiling** at ~4,000 nodes due to the JobTracker's overhead.

**YARN Architecture (3 marks):**

| Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **ResourceManager (RM)** | Global resource arbitrator; runs on master node. Has two sub-components: **Scheduler** (allocates resources via FIFO/Capacity/Fair policies) and **ApplicationsManager** (manages ApplicationMasters). |
| **NodeManager (NM)** | Per-node agent. Manages **containers** (CPU + RAM allocations), monitors resource usage, and reports back to RM via heartbeats. |
| **ApplicationMaster (AM)** | One per application. Negotiates resources with RM, works with NMs to launch tasks, monitors task progress, and handles task failures. |
| **Container** | A logical bundle of resources (RAM + CPU cores) on a node where a task executes. |

**Improvement Summary (1 mark):**
YARN enables **multi-tenancy** (multiple frameworks on one cluster), **better scalability** (tested at 10,000+ nodes), and **higher cluster utilization** through pluggable schedulers.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 KTU Mark-Deduction Traps in Hadoop Questions:**
>
> 1. **Calling Secondary NameNode a "backup"** — It is a *checkpoint* node, not a hot standby. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Combiner and Partitioner** — Combiner *aggregates values*; Partitioner *routes keys*. Mixing these up is a common error.
> 3. **Wrong block-count formula** — Forgetting the **ceiling function** and writing $\frac{S}{B}$ instead of $\left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing NameNode stores data** — It stores only **metadata**. Data lives on DataNodes.
> 5. **Forgetting replication factor in storage calculation** — Total HDFS storage = file size × replication factor, not just file size.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Analysing Data with Hadoop**

- **Hadoop = HDFS + MapReduce + YARN** (memorize this triad).
- **HDFS** is for storage; **MapReduce** is for processing; **YARN** is for resource management.
- **Block size = 128 MB** (default in Hadoop 2.x+).
- **Replication factor = 3** (default).
- **Number of blocks** for a file of size $S$ = $\left\lceil S / B \right\rceil$ — always round up.
- **Total HDFS storage** = $S \times r$ (logical size × replication factor).
- **NameNode** stores metadata only; **DataNodes** store actual blocks.
- **Heartbeat interval = 3 sec**; **timeout = 10 minutes**.
- **MapReduce flow**: Input Split → Mapper → (Combiner) → Shuffle & Sort → Partitioner → Reducer → Output.
- **Default Partitioner formula**: $\text{hash}(k) \bmod R$.
- **Combiner** is optional; runs locally on Mapper to reduce network traffic. Must be **associative + commutative**.
- **Hadoop is Write-Once, Read-Many (WORM)** — no in-place updates to files.
- **Schema-on-read** vs RDBMS **schema-on-write** — a key differentiator.
- **Hadoop advantages**: Horizontal scaling, fault tolerance via replication, data locality, low cost.
- **Hadoop weaknesses**: High latency, not suitable for real-time OLTP, single NameNode is a failure point (mitigated in Hadoop 3.x with HA).
- **YARN enables multi-tenancy** — MapReduce, Spark, Tez can coexist on the same cluster.
- **MapReduce is batch**; for real-time analytics, use Spark/Flink on top of HDFS.
- **Rack-awareness policy**: 1 replica local rack, 1 replica different node same rack, 1 replica different rack.
- **Word Count** is the canonical example — know it cold (Mapper emits `<word, 1>`, Reducer sums).
- **For ML analytics pipelines**: Hadoop/HDFS is the data-prep layer; Spark MLlib or TensorFlow consume the preprocessed data.
- **Secondary NameNode is NOT a backup** — it merges `fsimage` and `edit log` periodically to prevent log bloat.
- **Hadoop is Java-based**; APIs available in Java, Python (Pydoop, mrjob), Scala, R.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hadoop Ecosystem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hadoop Ecosystem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based, distributed storage and parallel processing framework governed by the Apache Software Foundation. It is designed to reliably and scalably store and process petabytes of data across clusters of commodity computers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hadoop Ecosystem** refers to the **collective suite of open-source projects, libraries, and tools** that surround the core Hadoop framework (HDFS + MapReduce + YARN). These auxiliary components extend Hadoop's capabilities to support **data ingestion, warehousing, querying, scheduling, coordination, and advanced analytics** in distributed environments.

The Hadoop core stack (sometimes called the "Kernel" of Hadoop) is built on two foundational pillars:

| Pillar | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** | Distributed, fault-tolerant storage layer |
| **MapReduce** | Distributed, parallel data processing programming model |
| **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** | Cluster resource management and job scheduling layer |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart City"

> [!TIP]
> Imagine Hadoop as a **massive smart city infrastructure**:
> - **HDFS** is the **road and warehouse network** — vast, distributed, and built so that no single failure can collapse the system.
> - **MapReduce** is the **logistics fleet** — pickup trucks (Mappers) collect raw material from warehouses and deliver sorted bundles to processing plants (Reducers).
> - **YARN** is the **traffic control authority and city administrator** — assigning roads, parking, and routes to every fleet operation.
> - **Hive** is a **centralized reporting office** that lets citizens query warehouse inventory using familiar SQL-like language.
> - **Pig** is a **flexible supply-chain scripting language** for analysts who want ETL-style operations.
> - **Spark** is the **lightning-fast express delivery service** — runs atop HDFS but uses in-memory processing for real-time analytics.
> - **HBase** is a **real-time inventory database** sitting beside the warehouses for point lookups.
> - **Sqoop & Flume** are the **inbound/outbound highways** connecting this city to the outside world (RDBMS, logs, social media).

## 1.3 The 5 V's of Big Data — Why Hadoop Exists

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hadoop was born to address the **Five V's of Big Data**:
> - **Volume** — Terabytes to Petabytes of data
> - **Velocity** — High-speed data generation (sensors, clicks, logs)
> - **Variety** — Structured, semi-structured, unstructured
> - **Veracity** — Handling data uncertainty and noise
> - **Value** — Extracting actionable insights

The standard unit of measurement for storage is **1 Block = 64 MB (default in Hadoop 1.x) or 128 MB (default in Hadoop 2.x+)**. The default **replication factor = 3** (one original + two replicas on different racks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) — The Storage Backbone

HDFS follows the **Write Once, Read Many (WORM)** model. It splits large files into fixed-size **blocks** and distributes them across cluster nodes.

### 2.1.1 Core HDFS Architecture

| Component | Role | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NameNode (Master)** | Stores metadata (file → block mapping, namespace image, edit logs) | Single Master |
| **DataNode (Worker/Slave)** | Stores actual data blocks; sends heartbeats to NameNode every 3 sec | Multiple Workers |
| **Secondary NameNode** | Periodically merges EditLog with FsImage to prevent NameNode memory overflow (NOT a failover node) | Checkpoint Helper |
| **Client** | Application that reads/writes via HDFS API | End User |

### 2.1.2 HDFS Write Protocol (Step-by-Step)

1. Client requests block allocation from NameNode.
2. NameNode returns a list of DataNode pipeline targets.
3. Client writes block to the first DataNode.
4. First DataNode forwards to the second; second to the third.
5. Acknowledgement (ACK) propagates backward up the pipeline.
6. Replication factor of **3** is maintained automatically.

## 2.2 MapReduce — The Processing Engine

MapReduce is a **functional programming model** that processes data in two phases:

| Phase | Function | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Map Phase** | Filters and transforms input into intermediate $\langle key, value \rangle$ pairs | $(k_1, v_1)$ pairs |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | Framework groups all values for the same key together | Grouped $(k_1, [v_1, v_2, ...])$ |
| **Reduce Phase** | Aggregates, summarizes, or filters the grouped values | Final $(k_2, v_2)$ output |

### 2.2.1 The JobTracker/TaskTracker Model (Hadoop 1.x) vs YARN (Hadoop 2.x+)

- **Hadoop 1.x**: Single **JobTracker** orchestrates all jobs → single point of failure, scalability bottleneck (max ~4,000 nodes).
- **Hadoop 2.x+**: **YARN** splits responsibilities:
  - **ResourceManager (RM)** — global resource allocation
  - **NodeManager (NM)** — per-node resource monitoring
  - **ApplicationMaster (AM)** — per-application lifecycle management
  - **Container** — abstract resource bundle (CPU + RAM)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following formulas and metrics are **frequently asked** in KTU ESE numerical and short-answer questions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Number of Blocks | $B = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size (MB)}}{\text{Block Size (MB)}} \right\rceil$ | blocks | Always round up |
| 2 | Total Storage Used | $S_{total} = B \times \text{Block Size} \times R$ | MB | $R$ = replication factor (default 3) |
| 3 | Effective Data Size | $S_{eff} = B \times \text{Block Size}$ | MB | Excludes replicas |
| 4 | Wasted Storage | $S_{waste} = S_{total} - S_{eff}$ | MB | Overhead due to replication |
| 5 | Replication Overhead % | $O\% = \left(\dfrac{R-1}{R}\right) \times 100$ | percent | For $R=3$: $66.67\%$ |
| 6 | Number of Mappers | $M = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{Input Size (MB)}}{\text{Block Size (MB)}} \right\rceil$ | tasks | One per HDFS block |
| 7 | Number of Reducers | $0 \le r \le r_{max}$ | tasks | $r_{max} = 0.95 \times N \times R_{per}$ where $N$ = nodes, $R_{per}$ = max reducers per node |
| 8 | Heartbeat Interval | $T_{hb} = 3$ seconds | sec | DataNode → NameNode |
| 9 | Block Re-replication Trigger | $R_{current} < R_{target}$ | — | Auto-runs when a DataNode fails |
| 10 | HDFS Throughput (read) | $T_r = \dfrac{B \times s}{t}$ | MB/s | $s$ = block size, $t$ = time |

## 2.4 Auxiliary Ecosystem Tools — Roles in Analytics

| Tool | Layer | Function | Language |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hive** | Data Warehousing | SQL-like queries (HQL) on HDFS | HQL → MapReduce/Tez/Spark |
| **Pig** | ETL Scripting | Data flow scripting (Pig Latin) | Pig Latin |
| **HBase** | NoSQL DB | Real-time random read/write on top of HDFS | Java API, REST, Thrift |
| **Spark** | In-memory Processing | Fast iterative ML & stream processing | Scala/Python/Java/R |
| **Sqoop** | Data Transfer | Bulk transfer between RDBMS ↔ HDFS | CLI |
| **Flume** | Log Aggregation | Streaming log data into HDFS/HBase | Agent-based config |
| **Oozie** | Workflow Scheduling | Coordinates Hadoop job DAGs | XML-based |
| **ZooKeeper** | Coordination | Distributed configuration & leader election | Java API |
| **Mahout** | Machine Learning | Scalable ML algorithms on Hadoop | Java/Scala |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility**: Hadoop powers analytics at **Facebook (300+ PB), Yahoo, LinkedIn, Twitter, and Netflix**. In the engineering domain, Hadoop is used in **predictive maintenance of industrial IoT sensors, genomic sequencing, financial fraud detection, and smart grid analytics**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — HDFS Block Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A file of size **1.7 GB** is stored in HDFS with a **block size of 128 MB** and **replication factor of 3**. Calculate:
> 1. The number of blocks.
> 2. The effective storage used.
> 3. The total actual storage used (with replicas).
> 4. The replication overhead percentage.

### Step 1: Convert file size to MB for consistency

$$1.7 \text{ GB} = 1.7 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1740.8 \text{ MB}$$

> **Logic:** HDFS arithmetic always operates in the **block size unit** (MB). GB→MB conversion uses $1\text{ GB} = 1024\text{ MB}$ (binary, not SI).

### Step 2: Compute number of blocks

$$B = \left\lceil \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1740.8}{128} \right\rceil$$

$$B = \lceil 13.6 \rceil = 14 \text{ blocks}$$

> **Logic:** HDFS **never** partially fills the last block. Even if the last block uses only 0.6 MB of its 128 MB capacity, a full 128 MB is allocated. The **ceiling function** is mandatory.

### Step 3: Effective (logical) storage

$$S_{eff} = B \times \text{Block Size} = 14 \times 128 = 1792 \text{ MB}$$

> **Logic:** Effective storage = what the user *believes* they are storing. It counts each block only once.

### Step 4: Total physical storage with replication

$$S_{total} = B \times \text{Block Size} \times R = 14 \times 128 \times 3 = 5376 \text{ MB}$$

> **Logic:** Every block is replicated to $R$ different DataNodes for fault tolerance. Hence the physical disk usage is $R$ times the logical size.

### Step 5: Replication overhead

$$S_{waste} = S_{total} - S_{eff} = 5376 - 1792 = 3584 \text{ MB}$$

$$O\% = \frac{R - 1}{R} \times 100 = \frac{3 - 1}{3} \times 100 = 66.67\%$$

> **Logic:** 2 out of every 3 stored copies are replicas. Hence $2/3$ of the total storage is "overhead."

### Final Tabular Result

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| File Size | $F$ | **1740.8 MB** |
| Block Size | $B_s$ | **128 MB** |
| Number of Blocks | $B$ | **14** |
| Replication Factor | $R$ | **3** |
| Effective Storage | $S_{eff}$ | **1792 MB** |
| Total Storage | $S_{total}$ | **5376 MB** |
| Overhead | $O\%$ | **66.67 %** |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — MapReduce Word Count Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Input Text File (split into 2 input splits):**
> `Split 1 → "hadoop is big data" / Split 2 → "hadoop is scalable"`

### Step 1: Input Splits (HDFS blocks)
Two DataNodes each hold one split of the file.

### Step 2: Map Phase (run on each DataNode)

For Split 1, the Mapper emits:
$$(hadoop, 1), \ (is, 1), \ (big, 1), \ (data, 1)$$

For Split 2, the Mapper emits:
$$(hadoop, 1), \ (is, 1), \ (scalable, 1)$$

> **Logic:** The Mapper tokenizes each line, lowercases every word, and emits a $(key, value)$ pair where $key$ = word and $value$ = 1.

### Step 3: Shuffle & Sort (Framework)

The framework groups all values by key:

$$hadoop \rightarrow [1, 1]$$
$$is \rightarrow [1, 1]$$
$$big \rightarrow [1]$$
$$data \rightarrow [1]$$
$$scalable \rightarrow [1]$$

> **Logic:** The Shuffle phase is a **network-heavy operation**. All $(hadoop, 1)$ pairs are redirected to the *same* Reducer (the one responsible for keys starting with 'h', 'i', etc., based on the Partitioner).

### Step 4: Reduce Phase

For each unique key, sum the values:

$$(hadoop, 2), \ (is, 2), \ (big, 1), \ (data, 1), \ (scalable, 1)$$

> **Logic:** The Reducer iterates through the list $[1, 1]$ for `hadoop`, accumulates the sum (2), and emits the final aggregated record.

### Step 5: Final Output (written back to HDFS)

The final output file is stored in HDFS as a new file:

```
hadoop    2
is        2
big       1
data      1
scalable  1
```

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — HDFS Read Path Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A client wants to read a 256 MB file. The block size is 64 MB. How many blocks? How many requests does the client make to the NameNode?

### Step 1: Number of blocks

$$B = \left\lceil \frac{256}{64} \right\rceil = 4 \text{ blocks}$$

### Step 2: NameNode interaction

In HDFS v1 and v2, the client makes **exactly ONE metadata request** to the NameNode per read operation. The NameNode returns the **DataNode list for every block**. The client then reads blocks **directly from the DataNodes** in parallel.

> **Logic:** This is called the **"Metadata-only lookup"** pattern. The NameNode never transfers actual data — it only hands out the "phonebook" of where blocks live. This is what makes HDFS scale to billions of files.

### Result Summary

| Item | Count |
| :--- | :--- |
| Blocks in file | **4** |
| NameNode metadata requests | **1** (per read operation) |
| DataNode connections (parallel) | **4** (one per block) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Master-Slave Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    clientA[Client Application]
    nameNode[NameNode Master - Metadata Only]
    secNN[Secondary NameNode - Checkpoint]
    dnOne[DataNode 1 - Block Storage]
    dnTwo[DataNode 2 - Block Storage]
    dnThree[DataNode 3 - Block Storage]
    dnFour[DataNode 4 - Block Storage]
    blkA[Block A - 128 MB]
    blkB[Block B - 128 MB]
    blkC[Block C - 128 MB]
    blkD[Block D - 128 MB]
    blkE[Block E - 128 MB]
    
    clientA -->|1. Metadata Request| nameNode
    nameNode -->|2. Block Locations| clientA
    clientA -->|3. Direct Data Transfer| dnOne
    clientA -->|3. Direct Data Transfer| dnTwo
    clientA -->|3. Direct Data Transfer| dnThree
    nameNode -.->|Heartbeat 3 sec| dnOne
    nameNode -.->|Heartbeat 3 sec| dnTwo
    nameNode -.->|Heartbeat 3 sec| dnThree
    nameNode -.->|Heartbeat 3 sec| dnFour
    secNode -.->|EditLog Merge| nameNode
    dnOne --- blkA
    dnOne --- blkB
    dnTwo --- blkB
    dnTwo --- blkC
    dnThree --- blkC
    dnThree --- blkD
    dnFour --- blkD
    dnFour --- blkE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** `secNode` is intentionally a renamed node (not `secNN` alias) to avoid the Mermaid subgraph name collision. The dotted arrows represent **control-plane** signals (heartbeats, checkpoints), while solid arrows represent **data-plane** requests. Each block is replicated across multiple DataNodes to ensure **fault tolerance** — if any single DataNode fails, the block is still available from replicas.

---

## 4.2 Complete Hadoop Ecosystem Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INGEST[Ingestion Layer]
        sqoopNode[Sqoop - RDBMS Bulk Import]
        flumeNode[Flume - Stream Log Ingest]
        kafkaNode[Kafka - Message Queue]
    end
    
    subgraph STORE[Storage Layer]
        hdfsNode[HDFS - Distributed File System]
        hbaseNode[HBase - NoSQL Columnar DB]
    end
    
    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Layer]
        mrNode[MapReduce - Batch Processing]
        sparkNode[Spark - In-Memory Processing]
        tezNode[Tez - DAG Execution]
    end
    
    subgraph QUERY[Query and Analytics Layer]
        hiveNode[Hive - SQL on HDFS]
        pigNode[Pig - ETL Scripting]
        impalaNode[Impala - Low Latency SQL]
    end
    
    subgraph MGMT[Management and Coordination Layer]
        yarnNode[YARN - Resource Manager]
        oozieNode[Oozie - Workflow Scheduler]
        zkNode[ZooKeeper - Coordination]
    end
    
    subgraph ML[Machine Learning Layer]
        mahoutNode[Mahout - Scalable ML]
        sparkMLNode[Spark MLlib - ML Algorithms]
    end
    
    sqoopNode --> hdfsNode
    flumeNode --> hdfsNode
    flumeNode --> hbaseNode
    kafkaNode --> hdfsNode
    kafkaNode --> sparkNode
    hdfsNode --> mrNode
    hdfsNode --> sparkNode
    hdfsNode --> tezNode
    yarnNode --> mrNode
    yarnNode --> sparkNode
    yarnNode --> tezNode
    hdfsNode --> hiveNode
    hdfsNode --> pigNode
    hdfsNode --> impalaNode
    hbaseNode --> hiveNode
    hdfsNode --> sparkMLNode
    hdfsNode --> mahoutNode
    zkNode --> hbaseNode
    zkNode --> yarnNode
    oozieNode --> mrNode
    oozieNode --> hiveNode
    oozieNode --> sparkNode
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Ingestion tools** (Sqoop, Flume, Kafka) **feed data into** HDFS/HBase.
> - **YARN sits underneath every processing engine** — it is the resource manager for *all* computation.
> - **Query tools** (Hive, Pig, Impala) read *from* HDFS but *do not own* data.
> - **ML libraries** (Mahout, MLlib) read from HDFS and run inside the Processing Layer.
> - **ZooKeeper** is the "nervous system" providing distributed locks and leader election for HBase and YARN.

---

## 4.3 YARN Master-Slave Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    clientApp[Client Application]
    rmNode[ResourceManager RM - Global]
    asNode[ApplicationMaster Scheduler]
    amNode[ApplicationMaster AM - Per Job]
    nmOne[NodeManager 1]
    nmTwo[NodeManager 2]
    nmThree[NodeManager 3]
    conOne[Container 1 - CPU plus RAM]
    conTwo[Container 2 - CPU plus RAM]
    conThree[Container 3 - CPU plus RAM]
    conFour[Container 4 - CPU plus RAM]
    
    clientApp -->|1. Submit Job| rmNode
    rmNode -->|2. Allocate Container| nmOne
    rmNode -->|2. Allocate Container| nmTwo
    nmOne -->|3. Launch| amNode
    amNode -->|4. Request Resources| asNode
    asNode -->|5. Grant Containers| nmTwo
    asNode -->|5. Grant Containers| nmThree
    nmTwo --> conOne
    nmTwo --> conTwo
    nmThree --> conThree
    nmThree --> conFour
    nmOne -.->|Heartbeat| rmNode
    nmTwo -.->|Heartbeat| rmNode
    nmThree -.->|Heartbeat| rmNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key YARN Insight:** The **ApplicationMaster (AM)** negotiates with the **ResourceManager (RM)** for resources, then works directly with **NodeManagers (NM)** to launch containers. This decouples resource management from application logic — a major improvement over Hadoop 1.x's monolithic JobTracker.

---

## 4.4 MapReduce Data Flow Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputFile[Input File on HDFS]
    splitA[Input Split 1]
    splitB[Input Split 2]
    mapOne[Mapper 1]
    mapTwo[Mapper 2]
    partOne[Partitioner 1]
    partTwo[Partitioner 2]
    shuffle[Shuffle and Sort - Network Transfer]
    redOne[Reducer 1]
    redTwo[Reducer 2]
    outputFile[Final Output on HDFS]
    
    inputFile --> splitA
    inputFile --> splitB
    splitA --> mapOne
    splitB --> mapTwo
    mapOne --> partOne
    mapTwo --> partTwo
    partOne --> shuffle
    partTwo --> shuffle
    shuffle --> redOne
    shuffle --> redTwo
    redOne --> outputFile
    redTwo --> outputFile
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pipeline Insight:** The **Shuffle & Sort** phase is the **most network-expensive** stage. It involves cross-rack data transfer and is the prime target for performance optimization (via combiners, partitioners, and compression).

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HDFS | HBase |
