## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]****

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **HDFS architecture** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode**. What happens when the **NameNode fails**?

> **(b) [7 Marks]** A file of size **820 MB** is uploaded to HDFS with a **block size of 64 MB** and a **replication factor of 3**. Calculate:
> 1. The number of blocks.
> 2. The total physical storage occupied.
> 3. The replication overhead in MB and as a percentage.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**HDFS Architecture:**

HDFS follows a **Master-Slave architecture**.

1. **NameNode (Master):**
   - Stores **metadata** in RAM (file names, permissions, block → DataNode mapping).
   - Persists metadata to disk as `fsimage` (snapshot) + `EditLog` (transaction log).
   - Receives **heartbeats from every DataNode** every 3 seconds.
   - Manages client access and metadata operations.

2. **DataNode (Slave/Worker):**
   - Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disk.
   - Sends heartbeats and **BlockReports** to the NameNode periodically.
   - Performs read/write operations as instructed by the client.

3. **Secondary NameNode:**
   - **NOT a backup NameNode** — it is a checkpoint helper.
   - Periodically (default 1 hour or when EditLog ≥ 64 MB) merges `fsimage` + `EditLog` → new `fsimage`.
   - Prevents the EditLog from growing unbounded and exhausting NameNode memory.

4. **NameNode Failure Scenario:**
   - The cluster **cannot serve read/write requests** because metadata is unavailable.
   - In Hadoop 1.x: **manual recovery** required by copying `fsimage` from Secondary NameNode to a new NameNode.
   - In Hadoop 2.x+: **High Availability (HA)** setup with a **Standby NameNode** provides automatic failover using **ZooKeeper** and **ZooKeeper Failover Controller (ZKFC)**.

**Diagram** (refer Section 4.1).

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Number of blocks:**

$$B = \left\lceil \frac{820 \text{ MB}}{64 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = \lceil 12.81 \rceil = 13 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 2 — Total physical storage:**

$$S_{total} = B \times \text{Block Size} \times R = 13 \times 64 \times 3 = 2496 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3 — Replication overhead:**

Effective storage:
$$S_{eff} = 13 \times 64 = 832 \text{ MB}$$

Wasted storage:
$$S_{waste} = 2496 - 832 = 1664 \text{ MB}$$

Overhead percentage:
$$O\% = \frac{R - 1}{R} \times 100 = \frac{2}{3} \times 100 = 66.67\%$$

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Number of blocks | **13** |
| Total physical storage | **2496 MB** |
| Effective storage | **832 MB** |
| Overhead | **1664 MB (66.67 %)** |

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **MapReduce programming model** with the **WordCount example**. Clearly identify the **Map, Shuffle & Sort, and Reduce** phases with intermediate outputs.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe **YARN architecture** in Hadoop 2.x. List and explain the responsibilities of **ResourceManager, NodeManager, and ApplicationMaster**. How does YARN improve scalability over Hadoop 1.x?

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

MapReduce is a **parallel, distributed data processing model** that processes massive data in two main user-defined phases plus one framework-managed phase.

**1. Map Phase (User-defined):**
- Input is split into key-value pairs: $k_1, v_1$.
- For WordCount: $k_1$ = byte offset, $v_1$ = line of text.
- Mapper tokenizes the line, lowercases each word, and emits intermediate $k_2, v_2$ pairs where $k_2$ = word, $v_2$ = 1.

**2. Shuffle & Sort Phase (Framework-managed):**
- Framework groups all values for the same key.
- Result: $k_2, [v_2, v_2, \ldots]$
- Example output: $(hadoop, [1,1]), (is, [1,1]), (data, [1])$.

**3. Reduce Phase (User-defined):**
- Reducer iterates through the list of 1's and sums them.
- Final output: $k_3, v_3$ pairs.
- Example: $(hadoop, 2), (is, 2), (data, 1)$.

**Final Output written back to HDFS** as `part-r-00000` file.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** is the cluster resource management layer introduced in Hadoop 2.x.

| Component | Responsibility |
| :--- | :--- |
| **ResourceManager (RM)** | Global arbiter of cluster resources. Runs the **Scheduler** (capacity/fair) and the **ApplicationsManager (AsM)**. |
| **NodeManager (NM)** | Per-node agent. Manages **containers**, monitors resource usage (CPU, RAM, disk, network), and sends heartbeats to RM. |
| **ApplicationMaster (AM)** | Per-application negotiator. Requests containers from RM and works with NMs to run Map/Reduce tasks. |
| **Container** | Abstract bundle of resources (RAM + vCores) on a node where a task runs. |

**How YARN Improves Scalability over Hadoop 1.x:**

| Aspect | Hadoop 1.x | Hadoop 2.x+ YARN |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Job Scheduling | JobTracker (single point of failure) | ResourceManager (decoupled from apps) |
| Max Cluster Size | ~4,000 nodes | ~10,000+ nodes |
| Application Support | Only MapReduce | MapReduce, Spark, Tez, Storm, etc. |
| Resource Model | Fixed slots (map/reduce) | Fine-grained containers |

> **Summary:** YARN transforms Hadoop from a **batch-only system** into a **multi-tenant data operating system** that can run diverse workloads in parallel.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where you LOSE marks:**
>
> 1. **Block Count Calculation** — Forgetting to apply the **ceiling function**. If 820 MB / 64 MB = 12.81, writing "12 blocks" instead of **13** is a 1-mark loss.
>
> 2. **Total vs Effective Storage Confusion** — Many students confuse $S_{eff}$ with $S_{total}$. Remember: $S_{total}$ **includes** the replicas, $S_{eff}$ does NOT. Read the question carefully: "physical storage" = with replicas.
>
> 3. **Confusing HDFS Read/Write Direction** — HDFS write uses a **pipeline** (3-stage ACK), but HDFS read uses a **direct single-connection** per block. Mixing these is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Calling Secondary NameNode a "Backup"** — It is a **CHECKPOINT HELPER**, not a hot standby. This is a favorite KTU trick question.
>
> 5. **YARN vs MapReduce Confusion** — YARN is the **resource manager**, NOT a processing engine. MapReduce runs *on top of* YARN. Conflating the two costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 6. **Omitting Units** — Always write **MB, %, sec, blocks** explicitly. Bare numbers without units = 0.5 mark penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hadoop Ecosystem**

- **Hadoop** = **HDFS (Storage) + MapReduce (Processing) + YARN (Resource Management)**.
- **HDFS** is **Write-Once, Read-Many (WORM)** with a **Master-Slave** model: **NameNode (metadata) + DataNodes (blocks)**.
- **Default block size**: **64 MB (v1) / 128 MB (v2+)**. **Default replication**: **3**.
- **Secondary NameNode = Checkpoint node, NOT a backup NameNode.**
- **HDFS read pattern**: Client → NameNode (metadata) → then directly to DataNodes. NameNode **never** sends data.
- **MapReduce phases**: **Map → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce**. Shuffle is the **most network-intensive** step.
- **Number of Mappers = Number of Input Splits = Number of HDFS blocks**.
- **YARN = Yet Another Resource Negotiator**. Key components: **ResourceManager, NodeManager, ApplicationMaster, Container**.
- **YARN is decoupled from MapReduce** — supports Spark, Tez, Storm, etc.
- **Hive** = SQL-like warehouse on HDFS (HQL).
- **Pig** = ETL scripting (Pig Latin).
- **HBase** = real-time NoSQL on HDFS (columnar, sparse).
- **Sqoop** = RDBMS ↔ HDFS. **Flume** = logs/streaming → HDFS.
- **Spark** = in-memory processing (100× faster than MapReduce for iterative ML).
- **Oozie** = workflow scheduler for Hadoop jobs. **ZooKeeper** = distributed coordination.
- **Heartbeat interval = 3 seconds** between DataNode and NameNode.
- **Block re-replication** is triggered automatically when a DataNode fails.
- **Replicas placed on different racks** for fault tolerance (rack awareness).
- **High Availability (HA)** uses a **Standby NameNode + ZooKeeper + ZKFC** for auto-failover.
- **Formulas to memorize**: $B = \lceil F / B_s \rceil$, $S_{total} = B \times B_s \times R$, $O\% = (R-1)/R \times 100$.
- **Hadoop 1.x limit = 4,000 nodes**; **Hadoop 2.x+ YARN limit = 10,000+ nodes**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MapReduce - Algorithms
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MapReduce - Algorithms

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MapReduce - Algorithms

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MapReduce Algorithm** is a programming model designed for processing and generating large-scale datasets in a **distributed, parallel** fashion across a cluster of commodity machines. It operates on two primary user-defined functions — `Map()` and `Reduce()` — coordinated by an implicit **distributed shuffle-and-sort** phase, enabling scalable batch analytics on petabyte-scale data.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCIT503)**, MapReduce algorithms refer to the *algorithmic patterns* used to express classical data-mining, linear-algebra, and relational operations in a parallelizable, fault-tolerant manner suitable for **distributed file systems** like **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)**.

The framework was originally introduced by **Jeffrey Dean and Sanjay Ghemawat** at Google in their seminal 2004 paper for simplifying large-scale data processing on clusters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Library Census Analogy:**
> Imagine a librarian who wants to count the total number of books written by each author across **100 library branches** (each containing millions of books).
>
> - **MAP phase:** Send 100 junior librarians (one per branch). Each one independently reads books in their branch and writes down a sticky note: *(Author Name → 1)* for every book.
> - **SHUFFLE & SORT phase:** A central sorting desk collects all sticky notes and groups them by Author Name, so all notes for the same author are bundled together.
> - **REDUCE phase:** Send the bundled sticky notes to 10 senior librarians. Each senior librarian sums up the counts for their assigned authors and writes the final tally.
>
> This is exactly how MapReduce works: **Map** = local processing, **Shuffle/Sort** = grouping by key, **Reduce** = aggregation per group. The system is **horizontally scalable** — add more branches (workers), and the job finishes faster.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Highlight

- **Mapper:** A worker node that processes a (key, value) input pair and emits intermediate (key, value) pairs.
- **Reducer:** A worker node that aggregates all intermediate values associated with the same intermediate key.
- **Combiner:** An optional local reducer run on the mapper's output *before* the shuffle, reducing network traffic.
- **Partitioner:** Decides which reducer receives which intermediate key (default: `hash(key) mod R`).
- **Shuffle:** The implicit transfer of intermediate data from mappers to reducers.
- **Sort:** The implicit sorting of intermediate data by key before it reaches the reducer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics in MapReduce:**
> - **Data Locality:** Computation is moved to the data, not vice versa.
> - **Fault Tolerance Threshold:** Re-execution on worker failure (default: 3 retries).
> - **Block Size (HDFS):** **128 MB** (default, as of Hadoop 2.x and 3.x).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a MapReduce Algorithm

A MapReduce algorithm is fully specified by the following user-defined and framework-supplied components:

### 2.1.1 User-Defined Functions
1. **`Map(k1, v1) → list(k2, v2)`** — Processes one input record at a time.
2. **`Reduce(k2, list(v2)) → list(k3, v3)`** — Aggregates all values associated with a key.

### 2.1.2 Implicit Framework Functions
3. **`Partition(k2)`** — Decides reducer routing.
4. **`Combine(k2, list(v2)) → list(k2, v2)`** — Local pre-aggregation.
5. **`Shuffle & Sort`** — Framework-managed transport + ordering.

## 2.2 Key Algorithmic Patterns in MapReduce

### Pattern 1: Matrix–Vector Multiplication
Given a sparse matrix **M** of size $m \times n$ and a vector **v** of size $n$, the product **Mv** is computed as:

$$
(\mathbf{Mv})_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij} \cdot v_j
$$

- **Map Input:** Each non-zero entry $M_{ij}$ represented as `(i, j, M_ij)`.
- **Map Output:** Emit `(i, M_ij * v_j)` (the value $v_j$ is fetched from the vector, typically loaded into distributed cache).
- **Reduce:** Sum all values per row index `i`.

### Pattern 2: Matrix–Matrix Multiplication
For two matrices $\mathbf{A}$ ($m \times n$) and $\mathbf{B}$ ($n \times p$), the product $\mathbf{C = AB}$ has entries:

$$
C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{ik} \cdot B_{kj}
$$

- **Map Input:** Each entry of A as `(A, i, k, A_ik)` and each entry of B as `(B, k, j, B_kj)`.
- **Map Output:** Emit `(i, j, (A, k, A_ik))` from A's mapper and `(i, j, (B, k, B_kj))` from B's mapper — both keyed by the same `(i, j)` pair.
- **Reduce:** For each `(i, j)`, perform inner product of A's row-slice and B's column-slice.

### Pattern 3: Relational Join — Reduce-Side Join
Used when two relations **R** and **S** cannot be co-partitioned (joined attribute is not the partitioning key).

- **Map Phase:** Tag each tuple with its source relation name.
- **Reduce Phase:** For each key value, perform **cross-product** between the tuples of R and S that share the key.

### Pattern 4: Relational Join — Map-Side Join
Used when both relations are **already sorted and partitioned** on the join key (e.g., output of a previous MapReduce job stored as **SequentialFiles**). No reducer is needed; joins happen in the mapper.

### Pattern 5: Grouping and Aggregation
This is the canonical pattern used for computing histograms, word counts, and feature statistics.

- **Map:** Emit `(grouping_attribute, 1)` per record.
- **Reduce:** Sum all `1`s per grouping key.

### Pattern 6: K-Means Clustering with MapReduce
A standard **iterative** ML algorithm. Each iteration is a full MapReduce job.

- **Map:** Assign each data point to the nearest centroid.
- **Reduce:** Compute new centroid as the mean of all points in the cluster.
- **Loop:** Until convergence or max iterations.

### Pattern 7: PageRank (Graph Algorithm)
Used in **graph processing** with iterative MapReduce:

$$
PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

Where:
- $d$ = damping factor (typically **0.85**)
- $N$ = total number of pages
- $L(q)$ = number of outbound links from page $q$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MapReduce for ML?**
> MapReduce enables **embarrassingly parallel** decomposition of linear-algebraic and statistical operations, allowing ML algorithms like k-means, Naive Bayes, and linear regression to scale to **web-scale datasets** where data exceeds the memory of any single machine.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Components | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| MapReduce Job Output Size | $O(R \cdot k)$ | $R$ = number of reducers, $k$ = avg output/key | Linear in reducers |
| Matrix–Vector Multiply | $(\mathbf{Mv})_i = \sum_{j} M_{ij} \cdot v_j$ | $M$ = matrix, $v$ = vector | Sparse representation preferred |
| Matrix–Matrix Multiply | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{ik} \cdot B_{kj}$ | A, B, C are matrices | Inner-product approach |
| PageRank Iteration | $PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | $d$ ≈ 0.85 | Iterative MapReduce |
| Hadoop Default Block Size | **128 MB** (Hadoop ≥ 2.x) | HDFS | Tunable via `dfs.blocksize` |
| Replication Factor | **3** (default) | HDFS | Trade-off: reliability vs. storage |
| K-Means Objective (J) | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \min_{\mu_j} \vert\vert x_i - \mu_j \vert\vert^2$ | $\mu_j$ = centroid | Minimized iteratively |
| Combiners Benefit | Reduces shuffle data by factor $\alpha$ | $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ | Use when associative + commutative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for MapReduce Algorithms:** The Reduce function must be **associative** and **commutative** for correctness when using a Combiner. Example: SUM is valid, AVERAGE requires custom combiner logic (sum + count, then divide in reducer).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines:** PageRank, inverted index construction.
- **Social Networks:** Friend recommendation, community detection.
- **E-Commerce:** Collaborative filtering, click-stream analytics.
- **Bioinformatics:** K-mer counting, sequence alignment (initial stages).
- **Finance:** Risk aggregation, fraud detection across transactional shards.
- **IoT Analytics:** Aggregation of sensor streams.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Matrix–Vector Multiplication in MapReduce

### Problem Setup
Compute $\mathbf{y = Mv}$ where:
- $\mathbf{M}$ is a sparse $m \times n$ matrix with non-zero entries stored as `(i, j, M_ij)`.
- $\mathbf{v}$ is a dense vector of length $n$.

### Step 1: Represent the Vector in Distributed Cache
The vector $\mathbf{v}$ is small enough to be loaded into the **DistributedCache**, which broadcasts it to all mappers.

$$
\mathbf{v} = [v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots, v_n]
$$

### Step 2: Map Function Design
For each non-zero entry $M_{ij}$ of the matrix:

**Input record:** `(i, j, M_ij)`

**Map logic:** Look up $v_j$ from the in-memory vector and emit:
**Output:** `(i, M_ij * v_j)`

This produces a partial product term $M_{ij} \cdot v_j$ keyed by row index $i$.

### Step 3: Framework Shuffle
The framework groups all emitted pairs by their key `i`. So for row $i$, the reducer receives:
$$
[(M_{i1} \cdot v_1), (M_{i2} \cdot v_2), \ldots, (M_{ik} \cdot v_k)]
$$
where $k$ is the number of non-zero entries in row $i$.

### Step 4: Reduce Function Design
For each key `i`, the reducer sums all associated values:

$$
y_i = \sum_{j: M_{ij} \neq 0} M_{ij} \cdot v_j
$$

### Step 5: Formal Algorithm

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Map}(i, j, M_{ij}): &\quad \text{emit}(i,\ M_{ij} \cdot v_j) \\
\text{Reduce}(i,\ \text{list}(v'_1, v'_2, \ldots, v'_k)): &\quad y_i = \sum_{t=1}^{k} v'_t \\
&\quad \text{emit}(i,\ y_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Complexity Analysis
- **Time complexity:** $O(\text{nnz}(M) + m)$ where $\text{nnz}(M)$ is the number of non-zero entries.
- **Space (shuffle):** $O(\text{nnz}(M))$ intermediate key-value pairs.
- **Number of reducers:** Optimal is $m$ (one per output row), but often fewer due to overhead.

### Step 7: Numerical Example
Let $\mathbf{M} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 4 & 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{v} = [1, 2, 3]^T$.

**Map outputs:**
- $(1, 2 \cdot 1) = (1, 2)$
- $(1, 1 \cdot 3) = (1, 3)$
- $(2, 3 \cdot 2) = (2, 6)$
- $(3, 4 \cdot 1) = (3, 4)$
- $(3, 5 \cdot 3) = (3, 15)$

**Reduce aggregation:**
- $y_1 = 2 + 3 = 5$
- $y_2 = 6$
- $y_3 = 4 + 15 = 19$

**Result:** $\mathbf{y} = [5, 6, 19]^T$ ✓ (verifiable by manual matrix–vector product).

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: PageRank in MapReduce

### Step 1: Initialize PageRank
Assign each node an initial rank $PR_0(p) = 1/N$.

### Step 2: Map Function (per node $p$)
For each outgoing link from $p$ to neighbor $q$, emit the rank contribution:

$$
\text{Map}(p, \text{AdjList}(p)):\ \ \text{emit}\left(q,\ \frac{PR(p)}{L(p)}\right) \quad \text{for each } q \in \text{Adj}(p)
$$

Also emit the **graph structure** (AdjList) as a special tagged value so the reducer can propagate it to the next iteration.

### Step 3: Reduce Function (per node $p$)
Sum all contributions received from neighbors, apply damping, and re-emit the new rank:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduce}(p,\ \text{list}(v_1, v_2, \ldots)): \\
\quad \text{sumRank} = \sum_{q \to p} v_q \\
\quad PR_{\text{new}}(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \cdot \text{sumRank} \\
\quad \text{emit}(p,\ (PR_{\text{new}}(p),\ \text{AdjList}(p)))
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Convergence Check
Compute $\sum_p \vert PR_{\text{new}}(p) - PR_{\text{old}}(p) \vert$. If below threshold $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-4}$), stop.

### Step 5: Number of MapReduce Jobs
- For **N iterations**: exactly **N** MapReduce jobs are launched.
- A common optimization: **"Schedule in advance"** — Google ran 26 MapReduce iterations over multiple days for the entire web graph.

> [!NOTE]
> **PageRank with MapReduce is inefficient** for graph algorithms because each iteration serializes the entire graph state. This motivated the development of specialized systems like **Pregel** (BSP model) and **Apache Giraph** (Hadoop-based BSP).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MapReduce Execution Flow (End-to-End Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Input Split 1] --> map1[Mapper 1]
    input2[Input Split 2] --> map2[Mapper 2]
    input3[Input Split 3] --> map3[Mapper 3]
    input4[Input Split 4] --> map4[Mapper 4]
    map1 --> comb1[Combiner]
    map2 --> comb2[Combiner]
    map3 --> comb3[Combiner]
    map4 --> comb4[Combiner]
    comb1 --> part1[Partition]
    comb2 --> part2[Partition]
    comb3 --> part3[Partition]
    comb4 --> part4[Partition]
    part1 --> shuffle[Shuffle and Sort]
    part2 --> shuffle
    part3 --> shuffle
    part4 --> shuffle
    shuffle --> red1[Reducer 1]
    shuffle --> red2[Reducer 2]
    shuffle --> red3[Reducer 3]
    red1 --> out1[Output Part 1]
    red2 --> out2[Output Part 2]
    red3 --> out3[Output Part 3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the canonical 3-stage data flow: **Map → Shuffle/Sort → Reduce**, with optional **Combiner** and **Partitioner** stages. Arrows represent data movement; partitions are routed to specific reducers via `hash(key) mod R`.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture for a K-Means MapReduce Job

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data Points on HDFS] --> B[Load Initial Centroids from HDFS]
    B --> C[Map Task: Assign Each Point to Nearest Centroid]
    C --> D[Emit Key: Centroid ID, Value: Point Coordinates]
    D --> E[Shuffle and Sort by Centroid ID]
    E --> F[Reduce Task: Compute Mean of Points per Cluster]
    F --> G[Emit New Centroid Coordinates]
    G --> H{Convergence Check}
    H -- Not Converged --> C
    H -- Converged --> I[Output Final Centroids to HDFS]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Reduce-Side Join

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Relation R on HDFS] --> MR[Map R]
    S[Relation S on HDFS] --> MS[Map S]
    MR --> TR[Tagger: Add Source R to Tuple]
    MS --> TS[Tagger: Add Source S to Tuple]
    TR --> SH[Shuffle by Join Key K]
    TS --> SH
    SH --> RD[Reducer for Key K]
    RD --> CP[Cross Product of R Tuples and S Tuples]
    CP --> OUT[Joined Output to HDFS]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reduce-side join** is the **default** join strategy in MapReduce when the datasets are large and not pre-partitioned. Its main drawback is the cost of materializing all tuples with the same join key on a single reducer, which can cause **data skew** and **out-of-memory errors** for highly skewed keys (e.g., NULL values, "Unknown" labels).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the Map function and the Reduce function in a MapReduce algorithm. Give one example of a real-world problem where each is used.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Map function** is a user-defined function that processes one input (key, value) pair and emits a set of intermediate (key, value) pairs. Formally, $\text{Map}(k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$. **Example:** In word count, the Map function takes a line of text, tokenizes it, and emits `(word, 1)` for each token.
- **Reduce function** aggregates all intermediate values that share the same key. Formally, $\text{Reduce}(k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(v_3)$. **Example:** In word count, the Reduce function sums up the counts for each unique word to produce a final tally like `(the, 1523)`.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**What is a Combiner in MapReduce? When is it beneficial to use one?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Combiner** is an optional, locally-executed Reduce function that runs on the **output of a Mapper** within the same JVM, *before* the data is shuffled across the network to the reducers. It performs **local pre-aggregation**, which significantly reduces the volume of intermediate data transferred over the network.

A Combiner is beneficial when the Reduce operation is **commutative and associative** (e.g., SUM, MAX, MIN, COUNT). For example, in word count, a Combiner can locally aggregate `(the, 1) (the, 1) (the, 1)` into `(the, 3)` on each mapper, reducing network traffic by a factor of 3.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Many students incorrectly state that Combiners are *always* safe to use. **They are not.** A Combiner that is not commutative/associative (e.g., computing AVERAGE without also tracking the COUNT) will produce **incorrect results** because the same key may be partially aggregated in the combiner and then further processed in the reducer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the working of the **PageRank algorithm** in the MapReduce framework. Show the Map and Reduce functions clearly. Discuss why PageRank is computed **iteratively** as a sequence of MapReduce jobs. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design a MapReduce algorithm for **Matrix–Vector Multiplication**. Show the data representation, Map function, Reduce function, and trace through a $3 \times 3$ numerical example. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a): PageRank in MapReduce

**PageRank formula:**

$$
PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

Where $d = 0.85$ (damping factor), $N$ = total nodes, $L(q)$ = number of outbound links from $q$.

**Step 1: Initialization**
- Each node $p$ is initialized with $PR_0(p) = 1/N$.
- Each node stores its adjacency list $\text{Adj}(p)$.

**Step 2: Map Function**
For each node $p$ in the current iteration's input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Map}(\text{nodeID} = p,\ \text{value} = (PR_{\text{old}}(p),\ \text{AdjList}(p))): \\
\text{// Emit rank contribution to each neighbor} \\
\text{for each } q \in \text{Adj}(p): \\
\quad \text{emit}\left(q,\ \left(\text{`rank'},\ \frac{PR_{\text{old}}(p)}{L(p)}\right)\right) \\
\text{// Propagate graph structure to next iteration} \\
\text{emit}(p,\ (\text{`struct'},\ \text{AdjList}(p)))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Reduce Function**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduce}(\text{nodeID} = p,\ \text{list}(v_1, v_2, \ldots)): \\
\quad \text{rankSum} = \sum v_i\ \text{where}\ v_i.\text{tag} = \text{`rank'} \\
\quad PR_{\text{new}}(p) = \frac{1 - d}{N} + d \cdot \text{rankSum} \\
\quad \text{struct} = v_j\ \text{where}\ v_j.\text{tag} = \text{`struct'} \\
\quad \text{emit}(p,\ (PR_{\text{new}}(p),\ \text{struct}))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Why Iterative MapReduce?**
PageRank is computed **iteratively** because the rank of each node depends on the ranks of all its in-neighbors, which in turn depend on *their* in-neighbors. A single MapReduce pass cannot capture this **transitive dependency** across the entire graph. The result of iteration $t$ is the input to iteration $t+1$. Termination is decided by checking:

$$
\sum_{p} \vert PR_{\text{new}}(p) - PR_{\text{old}}(p) \vert < \epsilon
$$

A driver program (e.g., a `for` loop) launches successive MapReduce jobs until convergence.

#### Model Solution to (b): Matrix–Vector MapReduce

**Setup:** Compute $\mathbf{y = Mv}$ where $\mathbf{M}$ is a $3 \times 3$ sparse matrix and $\mathbf{v}$ is a $3 \times 1$ vector.

**Example:** $\mathbf{M} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 4 & 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v} = [1, 2, 3]^T$.

**Data Representation:**
- Matrix stored as 5 non-zero entries: `(1,1,2), (1,3,1), (2,2,3), (3,1,4), (3,3,5)`.
- Vector $\mathbf{v}$ loaded into DistributedCache as a HashMap: `{1→1, 2→2, 3→3}`.

**Map Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Map}(i, j, M_{ij}): \\
\quad v_j = \text{DistributedCache.get}(j) \\
\quad \text{emit}(i,\ M_{ij} \cdot v_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduce Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduce}(i, \text{list}(v'_1, v'_2, \ldots)): \\
\quad y_i = \sum v'_t \\
\quad \text{emit}(i, y_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace through the example:**

| Mapper Input | Map Output |
|---|---|
| (1, 1, 2) | emit(1, 2·1) = (1, 2) |
| (1, 3, 1) | emit(1, 1·3) = (1, 3) |
| (2, 2, 3) | emit(2, 3·2) = (2, 6) |
| (3, 1, 4) | emit(3, 4·1) = (3, 4) |
| (3, 3, 5) | emit(3, 5·3) = (3, 15) |

**Shuffle and Sort groups by key:**
- Key 1 → [2, 3]
- Key 2 → [6]
- Key 3 → [4, 15]

**Reduce aggregation:**
- $y_1 = 2 + 3 = 5$
- $y_2 = 6$
- $y_3 = 4 + 15 = 19$

**Final Result:** $\mathbf{y} = [5, 6, 19]^T$ ✓

**Verification by hand:**
- $y_1 = (2)(1) + (0)(2) + (1)(3) = 5$ ✓
- $y_2 = (0)(1) + (3)(2) + (0)(3) = 6$ ✓
- $y_3 = (4)(1) + (0)(2) + (5)(3) = 19$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **MapReduce implementation of K-Means clustering**. Clearly state the role of the Map and Reduce functions, and describe how the algorithm handles **iteration and convergence**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss **Reduce-Side Join** and **Map-Side Join** in MapReduce. Compare them in terms of **performance, assumptions, and use cases**. Illustrate with a small example of joining two relations on attribute `K`. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution Outline to (a): K-Means in MapReduce
- **Initialization:** Load initial centroids $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ into DistributedCache.
- **Map function:** For each data point $x$, compute distance to all $k$ centroids and assign $x$ to the nearest cluster $j^* = \arg\min_j \vert\vert x - C_j \vert\vert^2$. Emit `(j*, (x, 1))`.
- **Reduce function:** For each cluster $j$, compute the new centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it: $C_j^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum x \cdot 1}{\sum 1}$. Emit `(j, C_j^{\text{new}})`.
- **Iteration:** A driver program reads the new centroids, recomputes $J = \sum_i \min_j \vert\vert x_i - C_j \vert\vert^2$, and launches a new MapReduce job if $J$ has not converged.
- **Convergence:** Stop when $\vert J_{t+1} - J_t \vert < \epsilon$ or after $T$ iterations.

#### Model Solution Outline to (b): Reduce-Side vs Map-Side Join
- **Reduce-Side Join:** No assumption about input partitioning. Map emits tagged tuples; reduce cross-products them by key. High shuffle cost, robust.
- **Map-Side Join:** Assumes both inputs are **already sorted and partitioned** on the join key (e.g., output of a prior MapReduce job). No reducer; join done in mapper via sequential file reads. Very fast, but rigid assumption.
- **Comparison Table:** (Performance, Assumptions, Use Cases).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> For PageRank questions, students often forget to:
> 1. **Tag the graph structure** in the mapper output so the reducer can propagate adjacency lists to the next iteration.
> 2. **Apply the damping factor** $d$ in the reducer (not the mapper).
> 3. **Mention the convergence criterion** explicitly — without it, the answer is incomplete and loses 2–3 marks.
>
> For Matrix–Vector questions, a common mistake is **emitting the wrong key** (e.g., emitting by `j` instead of `i`), which would give a transposed/wrong result. Always double-check that the **output key is the row index**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MapReduce** is a programming model with two user-defined functions: **Map** and **Reduce**, plus framework-supplied **Shuffle, Sort, Partition, Combine**.
- The **Combiner** reduces network traffic; it is **only safe** for commutative, associative operations.
- **Matrix–Vector Multiplication** uses DistributedCache for the vector; mappers emit row-indexed partial products; reducers sum them.
- **Matrix–Matrix Multiplication** is naturally expressed by emitting all A-entries and B-entries with the composite key `(i, j)`, then computing the inner product in the reducer.
- **Reduce-Side Join** is the default, most general, but most expensive join strategy.
- **Map-Side Join** requires inputs to be **pre-sorted and pre-partitioned** on the join key; it has no reducer.
- **PageRank** is computed as a sequence of **iterative MapReduce jobs** using the formula $PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$.
- **K-Means in MapReduce** is an iterative algorithm where the Map step assigns points and the Reduce step recomputes centroids.
- The default **HDFS block size is 128 MB** and the default **replication factor is 3**.
- MapReduce algorithms must be designed to **minimize data skew** — hot keys can overwhelm a single reducer.
- A MapReduce algorithm's correctness depends on the **associativity and commutativity** of its Reduce function.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frameworks - Applications on Big Data Using Pig and Hive - Data Processing Operators in Pig, Hive Services, HiveQL, Querying Data in Hive, Fundamentals of HBase and ZooKeeper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frameworks - Applications on Big Data Using Pig and Hive - Data Processing Operators in Pig, Hive Services, HiveQL, Querying Data in Hive, Fundamentals of HBase and ZooKeeper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Big Data Application Frameworks – The High-Level Picture

When datasets grow from **gigabytes (GB)** to **petabytes (PB)** and beyond, traditional RDBMS engines (Oracle, MySQL, SQL Server) collapse under the weight of read/write, schema, and transactional overhead. To solve this, the Apache ecosystem provides **higher-level abstraction frameworks** that sit *on top* of **Hadoop** (HDFS + YARN + MapReduce) and allow developers, analysts, and data scientists to write queries **without writing low-level Java MapReduce code**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** The three pillars you must master are **(1) Apache Pig**, **(2) Apache Hive**, and **(3) Apache HBase + ZooKeeper**. Pig is a *data-flow* language, Hive is a *SQL-like warehouse*, and HBase is a *NoSQL real-time store*. ZooKeeper is the silent **coordination service** that keeps the whole distributed cluster alive and consistent.

The four frameworks can be visualised as a layered pyramid on top of HDFS:

| Layer | Framework | Style | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top (Analyst) | **Hive** | SQL-like (HiveQL) | Ad-hoc BI, reporting, warehousing |
| Middle (ETL) | **Pig** | Data-flow scripting (Pig Latin) | ETL pipelines, transformation jobs |
| Real-time | **HBase** | NoSQL columnar store | Random read/write on Big Data |
| Coordination | **ZooKeeper** | Distributed coordination | Leader election, configuration, locks |

---

## 1.2 Apache Pig – Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Apache Pig is a platform for analysing large data sets that consists of a high-level language called **Pig Latin** to express data analysis programs, coupled with infrastructure for evaluating these programs. The salient property of Pig programs is that their structure is amenable to substantial parallelisation, which in turn enables them to handle very large data sets.*

In simple words: **Pig Latin is a scripting language for MapReduce**. Instead of writing 50+ lines of Java MapReduce, you write 5–6 lines of Pig Latin and Pig converts it to MapReduce under the hood.

### Intuitive Analogy – The "Factory Conveyor Belt"
Imagine a car factory. Raw steel sheets enter one end of the conveyor belt. Workers (operators) at various stations **LOAD** the steel, **FILTER** out the rusted ones, **JOIN** two parts together, **GROUP** bolts, and **STORE** the assembled chassis at the end. 
- The conveyor belt = **Pig data flow**.
- Each worker = a **Pig operator** (`LOAD`, `FILTER`, `JOIN`, `GROUP`, `STORE`).
- The blueprint = **Pig Latin script**.

---

## 1.3 Apache Hive – Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Apache Hive is a data warehouse software facilitating reading, writing, and managing large datasets residing in distributed storage using SQL. Structure can be projected onto data already in storage. A command-line tool and JDBC driver are provided to connect users to Hive.*

In simple words: **Hive = SQL for Hadoop**. If you already know SQL, you can query Big Data on HDFS with very little learning curve.

### Intuitive Analogy – The "Librarian"
Think of a massive library with **billions of books** stored chaotically. A **librarian (Hive Metastore)** keeps a **card-catalogue** describing *where* every book is, *what* it contains, and *how* it is organised. When you ask *"Find all books by Author X published after 2020"*, the librarian translates your question into a precise search plan and returns the books. Hive does exactly this — it translates **HiveQL** into **MapReduce / Tez / Spark** jobs and queries HDFS.

---

## 1.4 Apache HBase – Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *HBase is a distributed, scalable, NoSQL database built on top of HDFS that provides real-time read/write access to large datasets. It is modelled after Google's **BigTable** and uses a sparse, distributed, persistent multi-dimensional sorted map.*

### Intuitive Analogy – The "Infinite Spreadsheet"
Imagine a **Google Sheet** that is **infinitely wide and infinitely tall**, where:
- The **row key** is a unique timestamp/ID.
- **Columns are grouped into "Column Families"** (e.g., `personal`, `professional`).
- Cells can be **sparse** — empty cells cost nothing.
You can `GET` a single cell in **milliseconds** (real-time), unlike Hive which is batch-oriented.

---

## 1.5 Apache ZooKeeper – Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *ZooKeeper is a centralized service for maintaining configuration information, naming, providing distributed synchronization, and providing group services. All of these kinds of services are used in some form or another by distributed applications.*

### Intuitive Analogy – The "Referee"
In a cricket match with 22 players running around, a **referee** keeps score, ensures only one captain appeals at a time, blows the whistle on fouls, and synchronises play. **ZooKeeper** is the referee of the Hadoop cluster — it ensures that only one **NameNode** is the leader at a time (leader election), that all nodes agree on cluster configuration, and that distributed locks are honoured.

---

## 1.6 Why These Four? (Engineering Justification)

| Problem | Solved By | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Writing MapReduce is hard | **Pig** | Pig Latin is a high-level data-flow language; ~10× fewer lines |
| SQL users cannot access HDFS | **Hive** | HiveQL is ANSI-SQL-like; familiar to analysts |
| HDFS is write-once, batch | **HBase** | Provides low-latency random read/write on HDFS |
| Distributed systems need consensus | **ZooKeeper** | Provides coordination primitives (locks, queues, leader election) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Apache Pig – Architecture & Data Types

Pig has a two-tier execution architecture:

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Pig Latin** (Language) | High-level scripting language with operators |
| **Pig Engine** (Parser + Optimiser + Compiler) | Parses script → optimises → compiles to **MapReduce / Tez / Spark** |
| **Execution Engine** | Runs the actual job on YARN (local mode for small data) |

### Pig Latin Data Model

- **Atom (Scalar):** `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `chararray` (string), `bytearray`.
- **Tuple:** Ordered set of fields. `('Ramu', 22)` — written as `()`.
- **Bag:** *Unordered* collection of tuples. Written as `{}`. Can have duplicates.
- **Map:** Key-value pairs. Written as `[]`. Keys must be `chararray`.

> A **bag of tuples** is the most common Pig data structure. A relation = a bag.

---

## 2.2 Data Processing Operators in Pig (KTU High-Yield)

Operators in Pig Latin are classified into six families. **You must remember at least 6–8 of these for the exam.**

### 2.2.1 Loading & Storing Operators

| Operator | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `LOAD` | `LOAD 'path' [USING PigStorage(',')] AS (schema);` | Read data from HDFS/local into a relation |
| `STORE` | `STORE R INTO 'path' USING PigStorage(',');` | Write relation to HDFS |
| `DUMP` | `DUMP R;` | Print to console (debug only) |

Example:
```pig
students = LOAD '/data/students.csv' 
           USING PigStorage(',') 
           AS (id:int, name:chararray, marks:int);
```

### 2.2.2 Diagnostic Operators

| Operator | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `DESCRIBE R` | Print schema of relation R |
| `EXPLAIN R` | Print logical + physical plans |
| `ILLUSTRATE R` | Show sample execution with small data |

### 2.2.3 Filtering & Projection Operators

| Operator | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `FILTER` | `FILTER R BY condition;` | Remove unwanted rows (WHERE equivalent) |
| `DISTINCT` | `S = DISTINCT R;` | Remove duplicate tuples |
| `FOREACH…GENERATE` | `F = FOREACH R GENERATE f1, f2*2;` | Project/transform columns (SELECT equivalent) |
| `LIMIT` | `S = LIMIT R 10;` | Top-N rows |
| `SAMPLE` | `S = SAMPLE R 0.1;` | Random 10% sample |

### 2.2.4 Grouping & Joining Operators

| Operator | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `GROUP` | Group tuples by a key → creates a nested bag |
| `COGROUP` | Group multiple relations simultaneously (full outer join semantics) |
| `JOIN` (inner/outer) | Equi-join on common key |
| `CROSS` | Cartesian product |

**Inner vs Outer JOIN variants in Pig:**

| Pig Keyword | SQL Equivalent |
|---|---|
| `JOIN A BY x, B BY x;` | `INNER JOIN` |
| `JOIN A BY x LEFT, B BY x;` | `LEFT OUTER JOIN` |
| `JOIN A BY x RIGHT, B BY x;` | `RIGHT OUTER JOIN` |
| `JOIN A BY x FULL, B BY x;` | `FULL OUTER JOIN` |

### 2.2.5 Combining & Splitting Operators

| Operator | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `UNION` | Merge two relations with identical schema (set union, with duplicates) |
| `SPLIT` | Split one relation into many based on conditions |

### 2.2.6 Built-in & User-Defined Functions

| Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Eval Functions** | `AVG`, `SUM`, `COUNT`, `MAX`, `MIN`, `TOKENIZE`, `SIZE` |
| **String Functions** | `UPPER`, `LOWER`, `SUBSTRING`, `INDEXOF`, `TRIM`, `CONCAT` |
| **Math Functions** | `ABS`, `SQRT`, `RANDOM`, `ROUND` |
| **Date Functions** | `CurrentTime()`, `ToDate()`, `GetDay()` |
| **UDFs** | Java functions registered via `REGISTER` and `DEFINE` |

### Complete Pig Latin Script Example (Word Count)

```pig
-- 1. Load the input lines
lines = LOAD '/data/text.txt' AS (line:chararray);

-- 2. Tokenise each line into words (creates a bag)
words = FOREACH lines GENERATE FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(line)) AS word;

-- 3. Group identical words together
grouped = GROUP words BY word;

-- 4. Count words in each group
counts = FOREACH grouped GENERATE group AS word, COUNT(words) AS freq;

-- 5. Store the result
STORE counts INTO '/output/wordcount' USING PigStorage('\t');
```

---

## 2.3 Apache Hive – Architecture

Hive uses a **service-oriented architecture** (often called the *Hive Service Architecture* or *Hive 2/3 Architecture*).

### 2.3.1 Hive Services (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember these services — they appear almost every year in KTU papers.**

| Service | Role |
|---|---|
| **HiveServer2 (HS2)** | Thrift-based server; the entry-point for JDBC/ODBC clients, Beeline, and the new CLI |
| **Hive CLI (legacy)** | Old command-line shell (deprecated in Hive 2+; replaced by **Beeline**) |
| **Beeline** | New CLI client that talks to HiveServer2 via JDBC |
| **Compiler (Driver)** | Parses HiveQL → converts to **Query Plan (DAG)** |
| **Metastore Service** | Stores **table schemas, partitions, column types** in an RDBMS (MySQL/Derby) |
| **Execution Engine** | Runs the DAG on **MapReduce, Tez, or Spark** |
| **WebHCat / Hive LLAP** | Optional services: web API for metadata, and *Live Long And Process* daemon for low-latency queries |

### 2.3.2 Hive Architecture Diagram (Conceptual)

```
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │  Client (Beeline │   CLI / JDBC / ODBC / Thrift
   │  / JDBC / ODBC)  │
   └────────┬─────────┘
            │ HiveQL
            ▼
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │   Driver +        │   Parser → Semantic Analyser → Planner
   │   Compiler        │   → Physical Plan (DAG of MR/Tez/Spark)
   └────────┬─────────┘
            │
            ▼
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │   Execution       │   Submits the DAG to YARN
   │   Engine          │   (Hive on MR / Tez / Spark)
   └────────┬─────────┘
            │
            ▼
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │   MetaStore       │   Talks to MySQL/Derby/Postgres
   │   Service         │   Stores schema, partitions, serde info
   └──────────────────┘
            │
            ▼
       HDFS (raw data files)
```

### 2.3.3 Metastore Modes

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Metastore** | Default; Derby DB; only one session — not for production |
| **Local Metastore** | Metastore service in same JVM as HS2, separate RDBMS (e.g., MySQL) |
| **Remote Metastore** | Standalone Metastore server; multiple HS2 instances connect to it — production grade |

---

## 2.4 HiveQL – The Query Language

HiveQL is **declarative**, looks like ANSI SQL, but has extensions for **complex types**, **user-defined functions**, and **windowing**.

### 2.4.1 Data Definition Language (DDL)

```sql
-- Create internal table
CREATE TABLE employees (
    id        INT,
    name      STRING,
    salary    DOUBLE,
    dept      STRING
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;

-- External table (Hive does NOT delete data on DROP)
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE sales_external (
    txn_id STRING,
    amt    DOUBLE,
    dt     STRING
)
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE
LOCATION '/data/sales/';

-- Partitioned table
CREATE TABLE logs (ts STRING, msg STRING)
PARTITIONED BY (log_date STRING)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY '\t';
```

### 2.4.2 Data Types – Primitive & Complex

| Category | Types |
|---|---|
| **Primitive** | `TINYINT`, `SMALLINT`, `INT`, `BIGINT`, `FLOAT`, `DOUBLE`, `DECIMAL`, `STRING`, `VARCHAR`, `CHAR`, `BOOLEAN`, `BINARY`, `TIMESTAMP`, `DATE` |
| **Complex** | `ARRAY<data_type>`, `MAP<primitive, data_type>`, `STRUCT<field:type, ...>`, `UNIONTYPE<...>` |

### 2.4.3 Data Manipulation Language (DML)

```sql
-- Load data from HDFS
LOAD DATA INPATH '/raw/employees.csv' INTO TABLE employees;

-- Insert from query
INSERT INTO TABLE senior_emp
SELECT id, name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000;

-- Export results
INSERT OVERWRITE DIRECTORY '/output/emp'
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
SELECT * FROM employees;
```

### 2.4.4 Partitioning & Bucketing (Performance Boosters)

- **Partitioning:** Splits table by column value (e.g., `year=2024/month=01/`). Reduces scan.
- **Bucketing:** Hash-partitions files inside a partition into N buckets. Speeds up joins and sampling.

```sql
CREATE TABLE txn (id STRING, amt DOUBLE)
PARTITIONED BY (country STRING)
CLUSTERED BY (id) INTO 8 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC;
```

### 2.4.5 SerDe (Serializer/Deserializer)

Hive uses a **SerDe** to read and write each row.
- `LazySimpleSerDe` — CSV / TSV / delimited text (default).
- `RegexSerDe` — regex-based parsing of log lines.
- `ORC SerDe`, `Parquet SerDe`, `Avro SerDe` — binary columnar formats.

### 2.4.6 Querying Data in Hive (Important Examples)

```sql
-- Q1. Department-wise total salary (GROUP BY)
SELECT dept, SUM(salary) AS total_sal
FROM employees
GROUP BY dept;

-- Q2. Top-5 highest-paid employees (ORDER BY + LIMIT)
SELECT id, name, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- Q3. Join employees with departments (JOIN)
SELECT e.name, d.dname, e.salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.dept = d.dept_id;

-- Q4. Window function: rank employees by salary within each dept
SELECT id, name, dept, salary,
       RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rk
FROM employees;
```

### 2.4.7 Hive Execution Engines (Comparison)

| Engine | Speed | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **MapReduce** | Slowest | Legacy, high fault-tolerance |
| **Apache Tez** | Fast (DAG) | Default in Hive 2/3; recommended |
| **Apache Spark** | Fastest (in-memory) | Newer integrations, ML pipelines |

`SET hive.execution.engine=tez;` — switch engine.

### 2.4.8 High-Yield Formula / Syntax Sheet (KTU)

| Concept | Hive Construct |
|---|---|
| Filter | `WHERE` |
| Group | `GROUP BY` |
| Sort | `ORDER BY` (global), `SORT BY` (per reducer) |
| Top N | `LIMIT n` |
| Join | `JOIN ... ON` / `JOIN ... USING` |
| Distinct | `DISTINCT` |
| Set ops | `UNION ALL` (no `UNION` dedupe) |
| View | `CREATE VIEW v AS SELECT ...` |
| Index (legacy) | `CREATE INDEX ... AS 'COMPACT'` |
| Materialised view (3.x) | `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW ...` |

---

## 2.5 Apache HBase – Fundamentals

### 2.5.1 Data Model

> HBase organises data as a **sparse, distributed, persistent multi-dimensional sorted map** indexed by **row key, column family, column qualifier, and timestamp.**

**Conceptual hierarchy:**

```
Table
 └── Row (identified by RowKey)        ← sorted lexicographically
      └── Column Family (CF)            ← physical grouping, stored together
           └── Column Qualifier (CQ)    ← dynamic; new CQs can be added freely
                └── Cell = {RowKey, CF, CQ, Timestamp} → Value (byte[])
```

### 2.5.2 Table Example

| RowKey | personal:name | personal:age | professional:role | professional:salary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| r001 | Ravi | 28 | Engineer | 75000 |
| r002 | Meena | 31 | Manager | 95000 |

Note that the **column qualifier `salary` does not exist for `r001`** (sparse).

### 2.5.3 HBase Architecture

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **HMaster** | Manages metadata, region assignment, DDL operations |
| **RegionServer** | Serves reads/writes for a set of Regions |
| **Region** | A contiguous range of rows [startKey, endKey) |
| **Store** | Per Column Family within a Region |
| **MemStore** | In-memory write buffer; flushed to HDFS |
| **HFile** | Immutable, sorted on-disk file stored in HDFS |
| **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)** | Durability — write here first, then to MemStore |
| **ZooKeeper** | Coordinates HMaster ↔ RegionServers, tracks `-ROOT-` and `.META.` (replaced by `hbase:meta` in 2.x) |

### 2.5.4 HBase CRUD (HBase Shell)

```bash
# Create table with two column families
create 'students', 'personal', 'professional'

# Insert (Put)
put 'students', 'r001', 'personal:name', 'Ravi'
put 'students', 'r001', 'personal:age',  '28'
put 'students', 'r001', 'professional:role', 'Engineer'

# Read one row
get 'students', 'r001'

# Scan all rows
scan 'students'

# Delete
delete 'students', 'r001', 'personal:age'

# Drop
disable 'students'
drop   'students'
```

### 2.5.5 HBase Read/Write Path Summary

- **Write path:** Client → WAL → MemStore → (when full) → HFile flushed to HDFS.
- **Read path:** Client → BlockCache + MemStore + HFiles (merged) → Result returned.
- **Compaction:** Minor (merge small HFiles) + Major (rewrite into one HFile per CF).

---

## 2.6 Apache ZooKeeper – Fundamentals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ZooKeeper is the silent backbone** of HBase, Kafka, Yarn HA, Solr, and many other distributed systems. It provides **consensus** in the face of failures.

### 2.6.1 Core Data Model – ZNode

ZooKeeper exposes a **hierarchical namespace** similar to a Unix file system. Each node is called a **ZNode** and can hold:
- Up to **1 MB** of data.
- **Stat** metadata: version, ACL, timestamp, etc.
- **Children** (Ephemeral ZNodes auto-deleted when session ends).

### 2.6.2 ZNode Types

| Type | Description | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Persistent** | Stays until explicitly deleted | Configuration data |
| **Ephemeral** | Deleted when client session ends | Cluster membership, leader election |
| **Sequential** | Persistent or ephemeral with monotonically increasing counter | Distributed queues, barriers |

### 2.6.3 ZooKeeper Services (ZAB Protocol)

- **ZAB (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast):** The protocol that guarantees **atomicity, total order, and consistency** across the ensemble.
- **Leader Election:** On startup, one of the servers is elected *Leader*; the rest are *Followers*.
- **zxid (ZooKeeper Transaction ID):** 64-bit number combining *epoch* and *counter*; used for ordering.
- **Watcher Notifications:** Clients can register a *watch* on a ZNode; get notified on change.

### 2.6.4 ZooKeeper Ensemble Rules

- Ensemble must have an **odd number of servers** (3, 5, 7…) → majority quorum.
- For N servers, the cluster tolerates floor((N−1)/2) failures.
  - 3 servers → 1 failure
  - 5 servers → 2 failures

### 2.6.5 Formula Sheet (ZooKeeper Quorum)

$$
Q = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

where $Q$ = quorum required and $N$ = total ensemble size. This is the minimum number of servers that must be alive and connected to serve writes.

$$
\text{Fault Tolerance} = N - Q = N - \left( \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 \right) = \left\lfloor \frac{N - 1}{2} \right\rfloor
$$

For $N = 5$:

$$
\text{Fault Tolerance} = \left\lfloor \frac{5 - 1}{2} \right\rfloor = 2
$$

For $N = 3$:

$$
\text{Fault Tolerance} = \left\lfloor \frac{3 - 1}{2} \right\rfloor = 1
$$

### 2.6.6 Typical Use Cases

| Use Case | Implementation in ZK |
|---|---|
| **Leader Election** | Create an **ephemeral sequential** ZNode; smallest sequence = leader |
| **Configuration** | Store config in a **persistent** ZNode; watches notify all |
| **Distributed Lock** | Create ephemeral ZNode on `locknode`; if creation succeeds, lock held |
| **Group Membership** | Each member creates ephemeral ZNode under `/members` |
| **Barrier / Queue** | Sequential ZNodes enforce ordering |

---

## 2.7 Master Comparison – Pig vs Hive

| Feature | Pig | Hive |
|---|---|---|
| Language | Pig Latin (procedural data flow) | HiveQL (declarative SQL-like) |
| Skill Set | Programmer / ETL developer | SQL analyst / BI user |
| Schema | Schema-on-read, late binding | Schema-on-read, metastore-driven |
| Best For | ETL, complex data pipelines | Reporting, BI, ad-hoc queries |
| Engines | MapReduce, Tez, Spark | MapReduce, Tez, Spark, LLAP |
| Lines of Code (word count) | ~5 | ~3 (similar to SQL) |
| Procedural logic | Yes (nested FOREACH, UDFs) | Limited (use Python/Spark UDFs) |
| Custom functions | Easy UDF, UDAF, UDTF | UDF, UDAF, UDTF in Java/Python |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Script Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

This section goes **step-by-step** through critical exam-relevant scripts and derivations, showing the complete logical chain.

---

## 3.1 Pig Latin — End-to-End Walkthrough (Word Count on Logs)

> **Problem:** Given a log file `/data/app.log` where each line is a log entry, compute the **frequency of every distinct word** appearing in the file.

### Step 1: Understand the input
Assume `/data/app.log` contains:
```
ERROR user login failed
WARN retry attempt
ERROR timeout occurred
```

### Step 2: LOAD — Read raw lines into a relation
```pig
lines = LOAD '/data/app.log' AS (line:chararray);
```
Logical interpretation: every line of the file becomes one tuple with one field named `line` of type `chararray`. The output relation `lines` is a bag of tuples.

### Step 3: TOKENIZE — Split each line into a bag of words
```pig
tokenised = FOREACH lines GENERATE FLATTEN(TOKENIZE(line)) AS word;
```
- `TOKENIZE(line)` returns a **bag** of words: `{'ERROR','user','login','failed'}`.
- `FLATTEN(...)` un-nests the bag → one tuple per word.
- Result: a relation where each tuple is a single word.

### Step 4: GROUP — Group identical words
```pig
grouped = GROUP tokenised BY word;
```
The resulting schema of `grouped`:
| group (chararray) | tokenised (bag{word}) |
|---|---|
| ERROR | {('ERROR'), ('ERROR')} |
| WARN | {('WARN')} |
| user | {('user')} |
| login | {('login')} |
| ... | ... |

### Step 5: GENERATE — Compute the count
```pig
counts = FOREACH grouped GENERATE 
            group    AS word, 
            COUNT(tokenised) AS freq;
```
`COUNT(tokenised)` is an **aggregate eval function** that returns the number of tuples in the bag.

### Step 6: STORE — Persist to HDFS
```pig
STORE counts INTO '/output/wc' USING PigStorage('\t');
```
- Output directory: `/output/wc/`.
- File format: tab-separated `word\tfreq`.

### Step 7: Verify
```pig
DUMP counts;
```
Console output:
```
(ERROR,2)
(WARN,1)
(user,1)
(login,1)
(failed,1)
(retry,1)
(attempt,1)
(timeout,1)
(occurred,1)
```

**Logical Derivations** for counts:

$$
\text{freq}(w) = \sum_{t \in \text{group}(w)} 1 = \vert \text{group}(w) \vert
$$

In SQL-equivalent terms:

```sql
SELECT word, COUNT(*) AS freq
FROM   (SELECT FLATTEN(SPLIT(line, ' ')) AS word FROM logs) t
GROUP  BY word
ORDER  BY freq DESC;
```

---

## 3.2 Pig Latin — INNER JOIN Walkthrough

> **Problem:** Given two relations `customers` `(id, name, city)` and `orders` `(cust_id, product, amount)`, list **all orders with customer name and amount** where amount > 1000.

### Step 1: Load both relations
```pig
customers = LOAD '/data/customers.txt'
            USING PigStorage(',')
            AS (id:int, name:chararray, city:chararray);

orders = LOAD '/data/orders.txt'
         USING PigStorage(',')
         AS (cust_id:int, product:chararray, amount:double);
```

### Step 2: Inner Join
```pig
joined = JOIN customers BY id, orders BY cust_id;
```
- This is a **replicated join** if one side is small; else a **default reduce-side join**.
- Output tuple: `(id, name, city, cust_id, product, amount)`.

### Step 3: Filter
```pig
high_value = FILTER joined BY amount > 1000.0;
```

### Step 4: Project required columns
```pig
result = FOREACH high_value GENERATE 
              name AS customer, 
              product, 
              amount;
```

### Step 5: Order & Limit
```pig
sorted_top = ORDER result BY amount DESC;
top10      = LIMIT sorted_top 10;
```

### Step 6: Store
```pig
STORE top10 INTO '/output/top_customers' USING PigStorage('\t');
```

**Algebraic interpretation of the JOIN:**
$$
\text{joined} = \sigma_{c.id = o.cust\_id} \, (\text{customers} \times \text{orders})
$$

$$
\text{result} = \pi_{name, product, amount} \, \sigma_{amount > 1000} (\text{joined})
$$

$$
\text{top10} = \tau_{amount \, \downarrow} \, (\text{result}) \;\;\text{[first 10 tuples]}
$$

---

## 3.3 HiveQL — Step-by-Step Aggregation

> **Problem:** Compute the **average salary per department**, filter departments with `AVG(salary) > 60000`, and order descending.

### Step 1: DDL
```sql
CREATE TABLE employees (
    id     INT,
    name   STRING,
    dept   STRING,
    salary DOUBLE
) ROW FORMAT DELIMITED FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
  STORED AS TEXTFILE;
```

### Step 2: Load data
```sql
LOAD DATA INPATH '/raw/emp.csv' INTO TABLE employees;
```

### Step 3: HiveQL Query
```sql
SELECT dept, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
FROM   employees
GROUP  BY dept
HAVING AVG(salary) > 60000
ORDER  BY avg_sal DESC;
```

### Step 4: Translation to execution plan
The Hive compiler converts this HiveQL into the following logical plan:
1. **Scan** `employees` table (HDFS read).
2. **Filter** (if any `WHERE` clause — none here).
3. **Group By** `dept` → one reducer per dept (or auto-reducer combiner).
4. **Aggregate** `AVG(salary)` per group.
5. **Filter** groups by `HAVING`.
6. **Order By** globally (single reducer; uses total ordering).
7. **Output** to client / file.

### Step 5: Algebraic representation
$$
\text{out} = \tau_{avg\_sal \,\downarrow} \, \sigma_{avg(sal) > 60000} \, ( \gamma_{dept, \, avg(salary) \to avg\_sal}(\text{employees}) )
$$

Where:
- $\gamma$ = GROUP BY aggregation.
- $\sigma$ = selection (WHERE / HAVING).
- $\tau$ = ORDER BY (sort).

---

## 3.4 HiveQL — Partitioning & Bucketing

### Step 1: Create partitioned + bucketed ORC table
```sql
CREATE TABLE txn (
    txn_id STRING,
    amt    DOUBLE,
    cat    STRING
)
PARTITIONED BY (country STRING)
CLUSTERED BY (txn_id) INTO 16 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('transactional'='true');
```

### Step 2: Add partitions
```sql
ALTER TABLE txn ADD PARTITION (country='IN');
ALTER TABLE txn ADD PARTITION (country='US');
```

### Step 3: Insert with dynamic partitioning
```sql
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode = nonstrict;
INSERT INTO TABLE txn PARTITION (country)
SELECT txn_id, amt, cat, country FROM raw_txn;
```

### Step 4: Read with partition pruning
```sql
SELECT * FROM txn WHERE country='IN';
```
Hive's planner **prunes** all other partitions → reads only `/txn/country=IN/`.

### Step 5: Sampling via bucket
```sql
SELECT * FROM txn TABLESAMPLE (BUCKET 4 OUT OF 16);
```
Reads only the 4th of every 16 buckets (random scan inside a single bucket).

---

## 3.5 HBase — Step-by-Step Region-Splitting Logic

> **Problem:** A table `users` is created with **one column family `info`** and **200 regions** initially. Over time, a region grows beyond the configured split size; HBase auto-splits it.

### Step 1: Initial state
- Total Regions = 200.
- Each region covers a contiguous range `[startKey, endKey)`.
- The middle key of a region: 
$$
m_k = \frac{startKey + endKey}{2} \quad \text{(lexicographically)}
$$

### Step 2: A region grows beyond `hbase.hregion.max.filesize` (default 10 GB)
HBase writes trigger a **flush** of MemStore to HFile. When HFiles cross the threshold, HBase initiates a **split request**.

### Step 3: Split point selection
HBase uses a **mid-key** algorithm (default `RegionSplitPolicy = IncreasingToUpperBoundRegionSplitPolicy`):

$$
\text{splitSize}_i = \min\!\left(\, \text{maxFileSize},\;\; \text{initialSize} \times \text{regionCount}^{growthFactor} \,\right)
$$

The split is performed at the **first row key in the middle of the block** (or the user-defined `KeyPrefixSplitPolicy`).

### Step 4: After split
- Original region R: `[a, z)` is split into R1: `[a, m)` and R2: `[m, z)`.
- Total regions = 201.
- HMaster assigns the two daughters to RegionServers (possibly the same or different).

### Step 5: Number of regions after $k$ splits
$$
R_k = R_0 + k \quad \text{where } R_0 = \text{initial regions}
$$

For example, starting with $R_0 = 1$ and after 4 splits:

$$
R_4 = 1 + 4 = 5
$$

---

## 3.6 ZooKeeper — Quorum Derivation

> **Problem:** Determine the minimum number of ZooKeeper servers required to tolerate $f$ simultaneous failures.

### Derivation

Let $N$ = total servers, $f$ = tolerable failures, $Q$ = majority quorum.

To continue serving writes, the surviving servers must still form a majority:

$$
N - f \;\geq\; Q \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

Solving for the minimum $N$ given $f$:

$$
N \;\geq\; 2f + 1
$$

### Worked examples

| Tolerate $f$ | Min $N = 2f+1$ | Quorum $Q$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 2 |
| 2 | 5 | 3 |
| 3 | 7 | 4 |
| 4 | 9 | 5 |

### Why odd?
For any **even** $N$ (say $N=4$), the quorum is $\left\lfloor 4/2 \right\rfloor + 1 = 3$, but the cluster can still tolerate only 1 failure. Adding a 5th server gives the same fault tolerance with the same quorum, **but uses more disk/CPU for no gain** — hence odd is preferred.

---

## 3.7 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Big-Data Operator Cheat Sheet)

| Framework | Operator / Construct | Equivalent SQL | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pig | `LOAD` | `FROM table` (read) | Ingest |
| Pig | `STORE` | `INSERT INTO` (write) | Persist |
| Pig | `FILTER R BY p` | `WHERE p` | Filter |
| Pig | `FOREACH R GENERATE f` | `SELECT f` | Project/Transform |
| Pig | `GROUP R BY k` | `GROUP BY k` | Aggregate |
| Pig | `JOIN A BY x, B BY y` | `INNER JOIN` | Combine |
| Pig | `COGROUP` | `FULL OUTER JOIN` + group | Multi-way group |
| Pig | `ORDER R BY k` | `ORDER BY k` | Sort |
| Pig | `DISTINCT R` | `SELECT DISTINCT` | Dedup |
| Pig | `UNION A, B` | `UNION ALL` | Merge |
| Pig | `SPLIT R INTO A IF … B IF …` | `CASE WHEN` / multi-WHERE | Branch |
| Hive | `CREATE TABLE` (DDL) | `CREATE TABLE` | Schema |
| Hive | `LOAD DATA` | Bulk insert | Ingest |
| Hive | `INSERT … SELECT` | `INSERT … SELECT` | Move data |
| Hive | `PARTITIONED BY` | `PARTITION BY` (Oracle) | Pruning |
| Hive | `CLUSTERED BY … BUCKETS` | N/A | Sampling/Joins |
| Hive | `RANK() OVER (...)` | Window function | Analytics |
| HBase | `put 't','r','cf:q','val'` | `INSERT/UPDATE` | Cell write |
| HBase | `get 't','r'` | `SELECT WHERE rowkey=?` | Cell read |
| HBase | `scan 't'` | `SELECT *` | Range read |
| HBase | `delete 't','r','cf:q'` | `DELETE` | Cell delete |
| ZooKeeper | `create -e /node` | N/A | Ephemeral member |
| ZooKeeper | `create -s /lock/seq-` | N/A | Sequential + lock |
| ZooKeeper | `get /cfg watch:true` | N/A | Watch + read |
