The controller **ingests** the radar blip, **correlates** it with flight-plan data, **predicts** collision trajectories in real time using a model, and **acts** (issues a climb/descend instruction) all within seconds.

| ATC Tower Element | RTAP Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Radar sweep (2.5 s refresh) | Event stream ingestion (Kafka/Kinesis) |
| Flight plan database | In-memory state store (Redis/Ignite) |
| Controller's mental model | Online ML inference engine |
| Climb/descend instruction | Action trigger / alert / API call |
| Black-box recorder | Cold-path data lake (S3/HDFS) |

**Key Performance Metrics of an RTAP** (must be memorized for KTU):

- **Latency ($L$):** Time between event generation and insight delivery, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Throughput ($\lambda$):** Events processed per second (**eps**), typically ranging from **$10^{3}$ to $10^{6}$ eps**.
- **Jitter ($\sigma_{L}$):** Standard deviation of latency — a measure of *predictability*.
- **Data-in-motion vs Data-at-rest:** RTAP operates on the former; traditional BI operates on the latter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Streaming vs Real-time — A Subtle Distinction:** *Streaming* refers to the data flow pattern (continuous, unbounded). *Real-time* refers to a **latency SLA (Service Level Agreement)**. A system can stream data but still be *batch* (e.g., micro-batches every 10 minutes are streaming but **not** real-time).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of RTAP Architecture

An RTAP is decomposed into **three logical tiers**, each serving a distinct purpose in the streaming pipeline:

**Pillar 1 — Ingestion Tier (The "Veins")**
- **Role:** Decouple producers from consumers; provide durable, replayable event logs.
- **Canonical Technologies:** **Apache Kafka**, Amazon Kinesis, Google Pub/Sub, Azure Event Hubs.
- **Key Property:** **Partitioning** for horizontal scalability. Each partition is an *ordered, append-only* log.
- **Throughput bound:** $\lambda_{\text{max}} = N_{\text{partitions}} \times \mu_{\text{consumer}}$ where $\mu_{\text{consumer}}$ is the per-partition consumer rate.

**Pillar 2 — Stream Processing Tier (The "Brain")**
- **Role:** Stateful, distributed computation over unbounded event streams.
- **Canonical Technologies:** **Apache Flink**, Apache Spark Structured Streaming, Apache Storm, Apache Beam.
- **Two Semantic Guarantees (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
  - **At-most-once:** No retries; possible data loss. $P(\text{loss}) > 0$.
  - **At-least-once:** Retries; possible duplicates. $P(\text{dup}) > 0$.
  - **Exactly-once:** Achieved via idempotent sinks + checkpointing (Flink's hallmark). $P(\text{loss}) = 0 \land P(\text{dup}) = 0$.

**Pillar 3 — Serving & Action Tier (The "Hands")**
- **Role:** Persist results, trigger alerts, push to dashboards, or invoke downstream APIs.
- **Latency tiers:**
  - **Hard real-time:** $L \le 10$ ms (algorithmic trading, pacemakers)
  - **Soft real-time:** $10 \text{ ms} < L \le 5$ s (fraud detection, recommendation)
  - **Near real-time:** $5 \text{ s} < L \le 60$ s (operational dashboards)

### 2.2 Architectural Paradigms — Lambda vs Kappa

The **Lambda Architecture** runs two parallel paths:
1. **Speed layer (real-time):** Processes stream with low latency, may have approximate results.
2. **Batch layer (correctness):** Reprocesses all historical data to produce *ground truth*.
3. **Serving layer:** Merges both outputs at query time.

The **Kappa Architecture** (Jay Kreps, 2014) eliminates the batch layer entirely. All data flows through a single stream processor, and reprocessing is achieved by **replaying the log** from a stored offset.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *compare* the two, always emphasize that **Kappa simplifies operations** (one codebase) but assumes **log retention is infinite and cheap**, which Lambda avoids by keeping batch storage finite.

### 2.3 Windowing — The Heart of Stream Analytics

Because streams are **unbounded**, we apply windows to slice them into finite groups:

| Window Type | Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Tumbling** | Fixed-size, non-overlapping intervals $[t, t+W)$ | Hourly sales aggregation |
| **Sliding (Hopping)** | Fixed-size, overlapping by a hop $H < W$ | 5-min moving average over 1-min hops |
| **Session** | Dynamic, gap-based, per-key | User click-stream analysis |
| **Global** | One window per key, spans entire stream | Running totals per customer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Event Time vs Processing Time** — A *classic* KTU Module 4 trap. If a network delay causes events to arrive out-of-order, **processing time** windows produce wrong results. **Event time** windows use the timestamp *embedded* in the data and require **watermarks** to bound out-of-orderness.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{End-to-End Latency} \quad L_{e2e} = L_{\text{ingest}} + L_{\text{process}} + L_{\text{serve}} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Throughput (Little's Law)} \quad \lambda = \dfrac{N_{\text{in-flight}}}{L_{e2e}} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Stream Capacity} \quad C = N_{\text{partitions}} \times \mu_{\text{consumer}} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Window Completeness} \quad W_{\text{complete}} = t_{\text{watermark}} \ge t_{\text{end}} + \epsilon \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Out-of-Orderness} \quad \Delta_{\text{ooo}} = t_{\text{arrival}} - t_{\text{event}} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{99th Percentile SLA} \quad P(L_{e2e} \le L_{\text{SLA}}) \ge 0.99 \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Backpressure} \quad \rho = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{arrival}}}{\mu_{\text{service}}}, \quad \rho \to 1 \Rightarrow \text{stall} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{Exactly-Once Cost} \quad \text{Checkpoint Interval} = T_{cp} \propto L_{\text{recovery}}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Little's Law ($\lambda = N / L$) is the single most powerful sizing tool in RTAP design. If your SLA demands $L \le 500$ ms and the platform must support $\lambda = 100{,}000$ eps, then the system can have at most $N = \lambda \times L = 50{,}000$ events in-flight at any moment. This directly dictates memory and parallelism.

### 2.5 RTAP Application Domains — A High-Yield Mapping

| Domain | RTAP Application | Latency Target | Key Analytics |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BFSI (Banking, Financial Services, Insurance)** | Fraud detection, algorithmic trading, credit scoring | Hard ($\le 10$ ms) | Anomaly detection, time-series forecasting |
| **Healthcare** | ICU patient monitoring, sepsis early-warning | Hard ($\le 1$ s) | Vital-sign CEP, threshold-based alerts |
| **Telecom** | CDR analytics, churn prediction, network fault mgmt | Soft ($\le 5$ s) | CDR stream mining, sliding-window aggregations |
| **E-commerce / Retail** | Personalized recommendations, dynamic pricing | Soft ($\le 1$ s) | Online learning (multi-armed bandits), sessionization |
| **IoT / Smart Cities** | Traffic control, energy grid balancing, predictive maintenance | Soft ($\le 5$ s) | Sensor fusion, online clustering |
| **Social Media / AdTech** | Trending topic detection, real-time bidding (RTB) | Hard ($\le 100$ ms) | Sliding-window count, CTR prediction |
| **Cybersecurity** | Intrusion detection (IDS/IPS), SIEM correlation | Hard ($\le 1$ s) | Pattern matching, graph analytics on event streams |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Fleet tracking, cold-chain monitoring | Soft ($\le 5$ s) | Geofencing, ETA prediction |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sizing an RTAP Using Little's Law

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A Kerala-based UPI payment gateway must process transactions in real time. The peak load is $\lambda = 50{,}000$ events per second, and the business SLA mandates that 99% of transactions receive a fraud-decision in under $L_{\text{SLA}} = 200$ ms. The platform is Apache Flink, deployed on a Kafka cluster with partitions sized at $\mu_{\text{consumer}} = 2{,}000$ eps each. Compute: (a) the number of in-flight events, (b) the minimum number of consumer partitions, and (c) the additional processing latency budget if the ingestion tier consumes $60\%$ of the SLA.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing relationship.**
We use Little's Law applied to a steady-state queuing system:
$$
\lambda = \frac{N_{\text{in-flight}}}{L_{e2e}}
$$
where $N_{\text{in-flight}}$ is the average number of events being processed at any instant.

**Step 2 — Solve for $N_{\text{in-flight}}$.**
Rearrange to isolate the unknown:
$$
N_{\text{in-flight}} = \lambda \times L_{e2e}
$$
Substitute the SLA figures (using the worst-case SLA budget $L_{e2e} = L_{\text{SLA}} = 200 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ s}$):
$$
N_{\text{in-flight}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.2
$$
$$
N_{\text{in-flight}} = 10{,}000 \text{ events}
$$
**Interpretation:** At peak, **10,000 transactions are simultaneously in-flight** through the platform. This directly translates to memory pressure and must be sized into the Flink TaskManager heap.

**Step 3 — Compute minimum partition count.**
The stream capacity is the per-partition rate times the number of partitions:
$$
C = N_{\text{partitions}} \times \mu_{\text{consumer}}
$$
For the system to *not* fall behind, we need $C \ge \lambda$:
$$
N_{\text{partitions}} \ge \frac{\lambda}{\mu_{\text{consumer}}}
$$
Substitute:
$$
N_{\text{partitions}} \ge \frac{50{,}000}{2{,}000}
$$
$$
N_{\text{partitions}} \ge 25
$$
**Round up** to a Kafka-recommended power of 2: $N_{\text{partitions}} = 32$ to maintain headroom (the standard practice is to keep $\rho < 0.8$ to avoid jitter spikes).

**Step 4 — Allocate the latency budget.**
Given that the ingestion tier consumes $L_{\text{ingest}} = 0.6 \times 200 \text{ ms} = 120$ ms, the remaining budget is:
$$
L_{\text{process}} + L_{\text{serve}} = L_{\text{SLA}} - L_{\text{ingest}}
$$
$$
L_{\text{process}} + L_{\text{serve}} = 200 - 120 = 80 \text{ ms}
$$
A common split is 70% processing / 30% serving:
$$
L_{\text{process}} = 0.7 \times 80 = 56 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
L_{\text{serve}} = 0.3 \times 80 = 24 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify via the stability condition.**
Check that the system is *not* saturated ($\rho < 1$):
$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{N_{\text{partitions}} \times \mu_{\text{consumer}}} = \frac{50{,}000}{32 \times 2{,}000} = \frac{50{,}000}{64{,}000} = 0.78125
$$
Since $\rho \approx 0.78 < 1$, the system has $\approx 22\%$ headroom. ✔ Valid design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Many students forget the unit conversion (ms → s) when applying Little's Law. A common error is writing $N = 50{,}000 \times 200 = 10{,}000{,}000$ — this is **wrong by 1000×**. Always keep SI units.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Sliding-Window Count for Trending Topics

**Setup:** Twitter-like platform wants to count mentions of "#KeralaElections" using a **sliding window** of size $W = 60$ seconds with a hop $H = 10$ seconds. The raw arrival rate is $\lambda_{\text{topic}} = 1{,}200$ tweets/min.

**Step 1 — Window cardinality.**
The number of overlapping windows active at any instant is:
$$
N_{\text{windows}} = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{60}{10} = 6
$$

**Step 2 — Effective count per window.**
On average, a window of $60$ s captures:
$$
N_{\text{window}} = \lambda_{\text{topic}} \times W_{\text{min}} = 1{,}200 \times 1 = 1{,}200 \text{ tweets}
$$

**Step 3 — Total state to maintain (for the trending detector).**
Since all 6 windows are *active simultaneously* during a hop transition:
$$
N_{\text{state}} = N_{\text{windows}} \times N_{\text{window}} = 6 \times 1{,}200 = 7{,}200 \text{ counters}
$$
This is the **memory footprint** the Flink job must hold per key in its RocksDB state backend.

**Step 4 — Watermark lag tolerance.**
If we set allowed lateness $\epsilon = 5$ s, a late-arriving tweet is accepted iff:
$$
t_{\text{arrival}} \le t_{\text{window\_end}} + \epsilon
$$
Tweets arriving later are *dropped* (or routed to a side-output for offline reconciliation).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Backpressure Threshold

If arrival rate exceeds service rate, the queue grows unboundedly and latency explodes (the *knee-of-death* curve). The condition for *bounded* queue length is:
$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda_{\text{arrival}}}{\mu_{\text{service}}} < 1
$$
For our UPI example, the system tolerates up to:
$$
\lambda_{\text{max}} = 0.8 \times 32 \times 2{,}000 = 51{,}200 \text{ eps}
$$
Beyond this, **backpressure** propagates upstream to Kafka, and producers block on `acks=all` writes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End RTAP Functional Architecture

The following block diagram captures the canonical end-to-end flow of a Real-time Analytics Platform, from event generation on the producer side to action delivery on the consumer side.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[IoT Sensors] --> IG
    P2[Web Clickstream] --> IG
    P3[Mobile App Events] --> IG
    P4[Payment Gateway] --> IG

    IG[Ingestion Tier - Apache Kafka] --> SP

    SP[Stream Processing Tier - Apache Flink] --> ST1
    SP --> ST2
    SP --> ST3

    ST1[CEP Engine] --> AC
    ST2[Online ML Inference] --> AC
    ST3[Stream SQL Aggregations] --> AC

    AC[Action Trigger Layer] --> A1[Dashboard]
    AC --> A2[Alerting PagerDuty]
    AC --> A3[Downstream Microservice API]
    AC --> A4[Long-term Data Lake S3 HDFS]

    subgraph ColdPath[Historical Replay Path]
        A4 --> R1[Batch Retraining]
        R1 --> SP
    end

    style IG fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style SP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style AC fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style ColdPath fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

### 4.2 Stream Processing Internal Pipeline (State + Time)

The internal anatomy of a single Flink-style operator shows how **state**, **time**, and **windows** interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E[Event Arrives] --> T{Assign Timestamp}
    T --> W[Watermark Generator]
    W --> K{Key By}
    K --> S[State Backend RocksDB]
    S --> WV[Window Evaluator]
    WV --> WN{Window Complete Question}
    WN --> Y[Trigger Window Result]
    WN --> N[Buffer State Increment]
    N --> S
    Y --> O[Emit to Downstream Operator]
    O --> CK[Checkpoint Barrier]
    CK --> S

    style T fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style W fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style S fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00
    style WV fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style CK fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

### 4.3 Lambda vs Kappa — Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LA[Lambda Architecture]
        L1[Source] --> L2[Speed Layer Stream]
        L1 --> L3[Batch Layer Hadoop Spark]
        L2 --> L4[Serving Layer Merge]
        L3 --> L4
    end

    subgraph KA[Kappa Architecture]
        K1[Source] --> K2[Stream Processor Only]
        K2 --> K3[Serving Layer]
        K2 --> K4[Log Replay for Reprocessing]
        K4 --> K2
    end

    style LA fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style KA fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Which Architecture When?

| Criterion | Choose **Lambda** | Choose **Kappa** |
|---|---|---|
| Distinct batch-only algorithms required | ✔ Yes | ✘ Awkward |
| Stream is naturally replayable | Not required | ✔ Mandatory |
| Operational simplicity valued | Lower | ✔ Higher |
| Late-arriving corrections common | Easier via batch merge | Repaint whole log |
| Historical data volume | Terabytes+ | Petabytes (log must be retained) |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define a Real-time Analytics Platform (RTAP). List any two distinguishing features that differentiate it from traditional batch analytics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An RTAP is a distributed computational system designed to ingest, process, and act upon high-velocity data streams within strictly bounded latency (sub-second to a few seconds), delivering insights on **data-in-motion** rather than stored data.

*Distinguishing features (any two, 1.5 marks each):*

1. **Low and bounded latency** — RTAPs guarantee a latency SLA (e.g., $L_{99} \le 1$ s), whereas batch systems have unbounded latency measured in hours.
2. **Stateful stream processing** — RTAPs maintain continuous, queryable state over unbounded streams (e.g., running aggregates, session windows), while batch systems process static, finite datasets.
3. **Continuous, incremental computation** — Results are updated incrementally as events arrive, not via full re-computation.
4. **Event-time semantics with watermarking** — RTAPs reason about *when* events happened, not just *when* they were received.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
*Explain the difference between **event time** and **processing time** in a stream processing engine. Why is event time preferred for windowed aggregations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Event time** is the timestamp *embedded* in the data by the producer (e.g., the instant a sensor recorded the reading). **Processing time** is the timestamp *at which the stream processor observes* the event (e.g., when Kafka delivered it to Flink).

The two diverge whenever there is **network delay, buffering, partition skew, or replay** (1 mark).

Event time is preferred for windowed aggregations because it produces **deterministic, replayable, and correct results** even when events arrive out-of-order. Processing time windows would yield different results on each run if events are re-ordered (1 mark). Event time also makes the computation robust to backfills and late-arriving data, which is critical for analytics like billing, fraud, and SLA reporting (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **three-tier architecture of a Real-time Analytics Platform**. Identify the role of each tier and give one canonical open-source technology used in each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare the **Lambda and Kappa** architectural paradigms. State one scenario where Lambda is preferable and one where Kappa is preferable. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three-Tier Architecture (7 marks):**

| Tier | Role | Canonical Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Ingestion Tier** | Durable, ordered, partitioned log of events. Decouples producers and consumers. Supports backpressure and replay. | **Apache Kafka** (1.5 marks) |
| **2. Stream Processing Tier** | Performs stateful, distributed computation: filtering, enrichment, CEP, online ML inference, windowing. | **Apache Flink** (1.5 marks) |
| **3. Serving & Action Tier** | Persists results, fires alerts, updates dashboards, invokes downstream APIs. May use fast OLAP stores. | **Apache Pinot / Druid / Redis** (1 mark) |

*Block diagram (2 marks):*
```
[Producers] → [Ingestion - Kafka] → [Processing - Flink] → [Serving - Druid] → [Consumers/Dashboards]
              ↑ Log retention         ↑ State backend        ↑ In-memory cache
              ↑ Exactly-once via       ↑ Checkpointing        ↑ Sub-second
                idempotent sinks         barrier alignment       OLAP queries
```

*Additional reasoning (1 mark):* The tiers communicate via well-defined APIs. Failures in any tier are isolated; the ingestion log acts as the *source of truth* and the *replayable buffer* for recovery.

**(b) Lambda vs Kappa (7 marks):**

| Dimension | **Lambda Architecture** | **Kappa Architecture** |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | Two: speed (stream) + batch (3 marks) | One: stream only (3 marks) |
| Reprocessing | Recompute from raw batch store (1.5 marks) | Replay the log from a stored offset (1.5 marks) |
| Codebase | Two (must keep in sync) | One |
| Latency | Two-tier (real-time + eventual correct) | Single, consistent |
| Storage cost | Higher (raw + batch views) | Lower if log is shared |
| Complexity | Higher operational overhead | Lower |
| Suitable when… | Heavy ML retraining, ad-hoc historical queries | Pure event-driven, log is durable |

*Scenario — Lambda preferable (1 mark):* A **banking fraud analytics platform** must reconcile real-time alerts against end-of-day batch re-computation for regulatory reporting. The two views are independently auditable.
*Scenario — Kappa preferable (1 mark):* A **real-time bidding (RTB) ad platform** that processes billions of bid requests per day, where every event is naturally a stream and the log is the single source of truth.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **windowing** in stream processing. With examples, distinguish between **tumbling**, **sliding**, and **session** windows. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A retail analytics platform must detect a sudden spike in online orders. The current event rate is $\lambda = 8{,}000$ orders/min. The system uses tumbling windows of $W = 5$ minutes and triggers an alert if the order count in any window exceeds $\theta = 45{,}000$ orders. **[7 marks]**
  1. Compute the expected number of orders per window under normal load.
  2. Determine the *multiplier* of normal load that triggers the alert.
  3. Briefly explain how **backpressure** propagates if $\lambda$ doubles suddenly.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Windowing — Concept and Types (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A *window* is a finite slice of an otherwise unbounded event stream, defined by a time boundary (or count boundary). It allows the system to apply finite aggregations — counts, sums, averages, top-K — over logically grouped events.

**Three types (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Tumbling Window:** Fixed-size, non-overlapping intervals of width $W$. Every event belongs to *exactly one* window. **Example:** Count transactions per 5-minute interval. $\text{Windows: } [0,5), [5,10), [10,15), \ldots$

2. **Sliding (Hopping) Window:** Fixed-size windows of width $W$ that *overlap*, advancing by a smaller hop $H$. **Example:** A 1-hour moving average computed every 10 minutes ($W = 60$ min, $H = 10$ min). An event belongs to $W/H$ windows.

3. **Session Window:** Dynamic, per-key, gap-based windows. A new window starts when a key is seen after an inactivity gap $G$ (typically 30 min). **Example:** A user's click session — idle for 30 minutes ends the session; the next click starts a new one.

**Bonus (0.5 mark):** Session windows are *data-driven* (not time-driven), making them harder to parallelize; they require state merging across keys when shuffle occurs.

**(b) Numerical / Conceptual (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Expected orders per window (2 marks):**
$$
N_{\text{normal}} = \lambda \times W = 8{,}000 \text{ orders/min} \times 5 \text{ min} = 40{,}000 \text{ orders}
$$

**Step 2 — Alert multiplier (2.5 marks):**
$$
M_{\text{alert}} = \frac{\theta}{N_{\text{normal}}} = \frac{45{,}000}{40{,}000} = 1.125
$$
A 12.5% increase over normal load is enough to trigger the alert. (The student should comment on the sensitivity of this threshold — 1 mark for the interpretation.)

**Step 3 — Backpressure on sudden doubling (2.5 marks):**
If $\lambda$ suddenly jumps to $16{,}000$ orders/min:
- The new load per window becomes $16{,}000 \times 5 = 80{,}000$ orders, far exceeding $\theta$.
- The arrival rate now exceeds the consumer's service rate, so the **in-memory queue** of the stream processor grows.
- The engine signals backpressure upstream to the ingestion tier (Kafka). Producers writing with `acks=all` and bounded `buffer.memory` will **block**, throttling the source.
- Persistent backpressure leads to **increased end-to-end latency** (the SLA $L_{99}$ is violated) and may eventually cause **out-of-memory errors** if the buffer is unbounded.
- Mitigation: **auto-scaling** the consumer group, **load shedding** (dropping low-priority events), and **rate-limiting** at the producer side.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Module:**
> 1. **Confusing *streaming* with *real-time*.** Streaming is a *data pattern*; real-time is a *latency guarantee*. A 10-minute micro-batch is streaming but NOT real-time. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Ignoring units in Little's Law.** Latency must be in *seconds* (not ms) when throughput is in events/sec. Mixing units gives 1000× errors. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting to state the difference between event time and processing time.** Most KTU papers test this directly. If you only mention one, you lose at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Drawing a Lambda diagram without the merge step.** The serving layer's *merging* of speed + batch views is the *defining* feature — do not omit it.
> 5. **Using "real-time" loosely.** Always state the **latency SLA** (e.g., "100 ms p99") instead of the vague phrase "fast". Examiners mark precisely.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✔ An **RTAP** is a *latency-bounded*, *stateful*, *streaming-first* architecture for data-in-motion analytics.
- ✔ Three tiers: **Ingestion (Kafka)** → **Processing (Flink)** → **Serving/Action (Druid/Redis/Alerting)**.
- ✔ **Latency** is the headline metric; **throughput** must be sized to never saturate ($\rho < 1$).
- ✔ **Little's Law** is the master sizing equation: $\lambda = N / L$. Memorize the unit conversion.
- ✔ **Event time** + **watermarks** is the correct way to handle out-of-order streams; **processing time** is simpler but unsafe.
- ✔ **Exactly-once** is achievable (Flink's checkpointing + idempotent sinks) but costs latency; **at-least-once** is the pragmatic default.
- ✔ **Windows** turn infinite streams into finite aggregations: tumbling, sliding, session, global.
- ✔ **Lambda** = speed + batch (two codebases); **Kappa** = single stream + log replay. Choose based on whether reprocessing via replay is sufficient.
- ✔ **Backpressure** is a *feature* of well-designed systems; it prevents data loss at the cost of latency, and must be managed with auto-scaling or load shedding.
- ✔ High-yield application domains: **BFSI (fraud)**, **Healthcare (monitoring)**, **AdTech (RTB)**, **IoT (smart cities)**, **Telecom (CDR)**, **Cybersecurity (IDS)**.
- ✔ Always quote a **specific latency SLA** (e.g., "100 ms p99"), never the vague "fast" or "real-time".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case Studies (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> In the context of *Machine Learning and Data Analytics*, a **Case Study** refers to a structured, end-to-end investigation of a real-world business, scientific, or engineering problem that is solved by applying the complete ML pipeline — from problem formulation, data acquisition, preprocessing, feature engineering, model selection, training, evaluation, to deployment and interpretation. KTU evaluates case studies as integrated problem-solving exercises that test a student's ability to **synthesize concepts from multiple modules** (supervised learning, unsupervised learning, dimensionality reduction, and evaluation metrics) into a single coherent solution.

The Module-4 case-study block typically covers the following standard industry-grade problems:

1. **Sentiment Analysis on Social Media / Product Reviews**
2. **Recommender Systems** (Movies, E-commerce)
3. **Customer Churn / Credit Card Fraud Detection**
4. **Medical Diagnosis / Disease Prediction**
5. **Image Classification (Digit / Object Recognition)**
6. **Market Basket Analysis / Association Rule Mining**
7. **Spam Email / SMS Detection**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Diagnostic Process"

Imagine a **General Physician (GP)** diagnosing a patient:

| Doctor's Step | ML Case-Study Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient walks in with a complaint | Business Problem Statement |
| Doctor asks questions, takes samples | Data Collection / Acquisition |
| Doctor orders blood tests, X-rays | Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering |
| Doctor recalls similar past cases | Model Training on Historical Data |
| Doctor applies diagnostic logic | Algorithm Selection & Inference |
| Doctor prescribes medicine and monitors | Deployment, Monitoring, Feedback Loop |
| Doctor explains diagnosis to patient | Result Interpretation & Visualization |

Just as a doctor does not use only **one tool** (e.g., only a stethoscope), a strong ML case study does not rely on **one algorithm**. It combines tools from **supervised learning, unsupervised learning, dimensionality reduction, and evaluation metrics**.

## 1.3 Why Case Studies are Critical in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Tests Case Studies**
> - Tests **higher-order Bloom's levels**: *Apply (L3)*, *Analyze (L4)*, *Evaluate (L5)*, and *Create (L6)*.
> - Encourages **cross-module integration**: A single case study can blend regression, classification, PCA, clustering, and metrics.
> - Mirrors **real engineering practice** where ML models are never deployed in isolation.

## 1.4 The Universal Case-Study Pipeline

Every KTU-grade case study follows this **canonical 7-stage pipeline**:

1. **Problem Definition** — Classification, Regression, Clustering, or Recommendation?
2. **Data Acquisition** — Source, Volume, Labelled/Unlabelled.
3. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** — Visualization, statistics.
4. **Preprocessing** — Cleaning, Normalization, Encoding, Imputation.
5. **Feature Engineering & Selection** — PCA, LDA, Correlation-based.
6. **Model Building & Evaluation** — Train/Test split, K-Fold CV, Metrics.
7. **Deployment & Insights** — Interpretation, business recommendations.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a case study question appears, the first thing the student must do is **identify the ML task type** (Supervised vs Unsupervised vs Reinforcement). This single decision drives the rest of the solution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Case Studies by ML Task Type

| Case Study | ML Task | Algorithm Family | Key Module |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spam Detection | Binary Classification | Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression | Module 1 / 2 |
| Movie Recommendation | Collaborative Filtering | User-User, Item-Item, Matrix Factorization | Module 4 |
| Customer Segmentation | Clustering | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical | Module 3 |
| Handwritten Digit Recognition | Multiclass Classification | CNN, k-NN, SVM, ANN | Module 2 / 5 |
| Sentiment Analysis | Text Classification | Naive Bayes, LSTM, BERT | Module 2 / 5 |
| Credit Card Fraud | Imbalanced Classification | Random Forest, XGBoost, Isolation Forest | Module 2 / 4 |
| Market Basket Analysis | Association Mining | Apriori, FP-Growth | Module 4 |
| Disease Prediction | Binary/Multiclass Classification | Logistic, Decision Tree, Ensemble | Module 1 / 2 |
| Image Compression | Dimensionality Reduction | PCA, Autoencoders | Module 3 |

## 2.2 Detailed Walkthrough — Canonical Case Study Skeleton

### 2.2.1 Case Study A: Movie Recommendation System (Netflix / Amazon Prime)

**Business Problem:** Predict how a user will rate a movie they have not yet watched.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Let $U = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_m\}$ be the set of $m$ users, and $M = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n\}$ be the set of $n$ movies. The user–item interaction matrix is:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & \cdots & r_{1n} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & \cdots & r_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_{m1} & r_{m2} & \cdots & r_{mn} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $r_{ij}$ is the rating user $i$ gives to movie $j$. Most entries are **missing (sparse matrix)**.

**Approach 1 — User-Based Collaborative Filtering (UBCF):**

The predicted rating of user $u$ for item $i$ is:

$$
\hat{r}_{ui} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{vi} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}
$$

where:
- $\bar{r}_u$ = average rating of user $u$.
- $N(u)$ = set of $K$ nearest neighbours of user $u$.
- $\text{sim}(u,v)$ = similarity between users $u$ and $v$ (Pearson / Cosine).

**Approach 2 — Matrix Factorization (SVD):**

$$
R \approx P \cdot Q^T
$$

where $P \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$ is the user latent-factor matrix and $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ is the item latent-factor matrix. The prediction is:

$$
\hat{r}_{ui} = p_u \cdot q_i^T = \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_{uk} \, q_{ik}
$$

**Loss Function (with Regularization):**

$$
L = \sum_{(u,i) \in \text{known}} (r_{ui} - p_u q_i^T)^2 + \lambda \left( \Vert p_u \Vert^2 + \Vert q_i \Vert^2 \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sparsity Challenge:** Real-world rating matrices have **> 99% sparsity**. Hence pure collaborative filtering fails on cold-start users/items. Hybrid systems (Content + Collaborative) are used as fixes.

---

### 2.2.2 Case Study B: Credit Card Fraud Detection (Anomaly Detection + Imbalanced Classification)

**Business Problem:** Identify fraudulent transactions out of millions of daily transactions.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Highly imbalanced** dataset: $\approx 0.1\%$ fraud vs $99.9\%$ legitimate.
- **Cost-sensitive**: Missing a fraud (FN) is far more expensive than false alarm (FP).

**Algorithms Used:**

| Algorithm | Why Used |
|---|---|
| Logistic Regression | Baseline, interpretable coefficients |
| Random Forest | Handles non-linearity, feature importance |
| XGBoost / LightGBM | State-of-the-art tabular performance |
| Isolation Forest | Unsupervised anomaly detection |
| Autoencoder (Module 5) | Reconstruction error > threshold ⇒ fraud |

**Critical Metrics (NOT plain Accuracy!):**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \qquad \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

$$
\text{AUC-ROC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(t) \, d(\text{FPR}(t))
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Reporting **Accuracy** on imbalanced datasets. A model predicting "No Fraud" always achieves 99.9% accuracy. KTU examiners expect **Precision, Recall, F1-score, AUC-ROC** and discussion of the **Precision-Recall trade-off**.

---

### 2.2.3 Case Study C: Customer Segmentation (Clustering)

**Business Problem:** Group retail customers into segments for targeted marketing.

**Pipeline:**
1. StandardScaler → Normalize Age, Income, Spending Score.
2. **Elbow Method** to find $K$:

$$
J(K) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2
$$

3. **Silhouette Score** to validate $K$:

$$
s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}
$$

where:
- $a(i)$ = average distance from point $i$ to other points in the **same** cluster.
- $b(i)$ = average distance from point $i$ to points in the **nearest other** cluster.

4. Visualize using **PCA (Module 3)** to project to 2-D for plotting.

---

### 2.2.4 Case Study D: Market Basket Analysis (Association Rule Mining)

**Business Problem:** Find products frequently bought together.

**Support, Confidence, Lift:**

$$
\text{Support}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{count}(X \cup Y)}{N}
$$

$$
\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X)}
$$

$$
\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y)}{\text{Support}(Y)} = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \cdot \text{Support}(Y)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> - **Lift > 1** → Positive correlation (Y is more likely with X).
> - **Lift = 1** → Independence.
> - **Lift < 1** → Negative correlation (anti-pattern).

**Algorithm:** **Apriori** uses the downward-closure property: if a set is infrequent, all its supersets are infrequent.

---

### 2.2.5 Case Study E: Sentiment Analysis (NLP + Classification)

**Pipeline:**
1. **Text Preprocessing**: Lowercasing, Tokenization, Stopword removal, Stemming/Lemmatization.
2. **Feature Extraction**:
   - **Bag-of-Words (BoW)**: Term Frequency matrix.
   - **TF-IDF**:

$$
\text{TF-IDF}(t,d) = \text{TF}(t,d) \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{\text{DF}(t)}\right)
$$

   - **Word Embeddings** (Module 5): Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT.
3. **Classifier**: Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, SVM, or LSTM.
4. **Evaluation**: Accuracy, F1 (macro-averaged for multi-class).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(A,B) = \dfrac{A \cdot B}{\Vert A \Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert}$ | Recommendation Systems |
| Pearson Correlation | $\rho_{uv} = \dfrac{\sum(r_{ui} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{vi} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sqrt{\sum(r_{ui}-\bar{r}_u)^2 \sum(r_{vi}-\bar{r}_v)^2}$ | UBCF |
| Precision | $TP / (TP + FP)$ | Imbalanced Classification |
| Recall (Sensitivity) | $TP / (TP + FN)$ | Fraud / Medical Diagnosis |
| F1-Score | $2PR/(P+R)$ | Harmonic balance |
| AUC-ROC | Area under TPR vs FPR | Threshold-independent metric |
| WCSS (Elbow) | $\sum \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | K-Means clustering |
| Silhouette Score | $(b(i)-a(i))/\max\{a(i),b(i)\}$ | Cluster validation |
| Support | $\text{count}(X \cup Y)/N$ | Apriori |
| Confidence | $\text{Supp}(X \cup Y)/\text{Supp}(X)$ | Apriori |
| Lift | $\text{Conf}(X \Rightarrow Y)/\text{Supp}(Y)$ | Association Rules |
| TF-IDF | $tf(t,d) \cdot \log(N/df(t))$ | NLP feature extraction |
| Bias–Variance Trade-off | $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Model selection |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Case Study | Industry Use |
|---|---|
| Recommender Systems | E-commerce, OTT platforms, music streaming |
| Fraud Detection | Banking, Insurance, Fintech |
| Sentiment Analysis | Brand monitoring, political analysis |
| Customer Segmentation | Retail, Telecom, Banking marketing |
| Disease Prediction | Clinical decision support, public health |
| Market Basket | Inventory planning, cross-selling |
| Spam Detection | Email gateways, SMS filtering |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **complete mathematical walkthrough** for two flagship KTU case-study questions: **(i) Recommender System using Matrix Factorization (SGD)** and **(ii) K-Means Customer Segmentation from scratch**.

## 3.1 Derivation — Matrix Factorization for Movie Recommendation

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

We are given the rating matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ where most entries are missing. We want to factor $R$ into two lower-rank matrices $P \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$ and $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ such that:

$$
R \approx P Q^T
$$

The predicted rating for user $u$ on item $i$ is:

$$
\hat{r}_{ui} = p_u \cdot q_i^T
$$

### 3.1.2 Loss Function Derivation

The standard **squared-error loss** over known ratings is:

$$
L = \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{K}} (r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})^2
$$

where $\mathcal{K}$ is the set of known (user, item) pairs.

To prevent **overfitting**, we add **L2 regularization** on both latent vectors:

$$
L = \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{K}} \left( r_{ui} - p_u q_i^T \right)^2 + \lambda \left( \Vert p_u \Vert^2 + \Vert q_i \Vert^2 \right)
$$

### 3.1.3 Gradient Descent Update Rules

Let the prediction error be:

$$
e_{ui} = r_{ui} - p_u q_i^T
$$

Taking the partial derivative of $L$ with respect to $p_u$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial p_u} = -2 e_{ui} \, q_i + 2\lambda p_u
$$

Similarly with respect to $q_i$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = -2 e_{ui} \, p_u + 2\lambda q_i
$$

### 3.1.4 Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) Updates

Applying learning rate $\eta$:

$$
p_u \leftarrow p_u + \eta \left( e_{ui} \, q_i - \lambda \, p_u \right)
$$

$$
q_i \leftarrow q_i + \eta \left( e_{ui} \, p_u - \lambda \, q_i \right)
$$

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $k = 2$, and initial values are:

$$
p_u = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad q_i = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad r_{ui} = 5, \quad \lambda = 0.02, \quad \eta = 0.1
$$

**Step 1** — Compute prediction:

$$
\hat{r}_{ui} = 0.1 \cdot 0.3 + 0.2 \cdot 0.4 = 0.03 + 0.08 = 0.11
$$

**Step 2** — Compute error:

$$
e_{ui} = 5 - 0.11 = 4.89
$$

**Step 3** — Update $p_u$:

$$
p_u^{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} + 0.1 \left( 4.89 \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} - 0.02 \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \right)
$$

$$
p_u^{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} + 0.1 \begin{bmatrix} 1.467 - 0.002 \\ 1.956 - 0.004 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
p_u^{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1465 \\ 0.1952 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2465 \\ 0.3952 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4** — Update $q_i$:

$$
q_i^{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} + 0.1 \left( 4.89 \begin{bmatrix} 0.2465 \\ 0.3952 \end{bmatrix} - 0.02 \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \right)
$$

$$
q_i^{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} + 0.1 \begin{bmatrix} 1.2054 - 0.006 \\ 1.9325 - 0.008 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
q_i^{new} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1199 \\ 0.1925 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4199 \\ 0.5925 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5** — Recompute prediction:

$$
\hat{r}_{ui}^{new} = 0.2465 \cdot 0.4199 + 0.3952 \cdot 0.5925 = 0.1035 + 0.2342 = 0.3377
$$

After many epochs, $\hat{r}_{ui}$ converges to $\approx 5$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Behavior:** The error decreases monotonically per epoch and plateaus once regularization balances the squared error. This iterative learning is what enables Netflix-style personalization.

### 3.1.6 Evaluation Metric — RMSE

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert} \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{K}} (r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})^2}
$$

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step — K-Means Customer Segmentation

### 3.2.1 Toy Dataset

Let us segment 4 customers based on **Annual Income (₹ lakhs)** and **Spending Score (1–100)**:

| Customer | Income ($x_1$) | Spending Score ($x_2$) |
|---|---|---|
| $c_1$ | 15 | 39 |
| $c_2$ | 16 | 81 |
| $c_3$ | 17 | 6 |
| $c_4$ | 18 | 77 |

### 3.2.2 Step 0 — Initial Centroid Selection (assume $K=2$)

Random initialization:
- $\mu_1^{(0)} = (15, 39)$
- $\mu_2^{(0)} = (17, 6)$

### 3.2.3 Step 1 — Compute Euclidean Distance (Iteration 1)

$$
d(c_i, \mu_j) = \sqrt{(x_1 - \mu_{1j})^2 + (x_2 - \mu_{2j})^2}
$$

For $c_1 = (15, 39)$:

$$
d(c_1, \mu_1^{(0)}) = \sqrt{(15-15)^2 + (39-39)^2} = 0.00
$$

$$
d(c_1, \mu_2^{(0)}) = \sqrt{(15-17)^2 + (39-6)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 1089} = 33.02
$$

→ Assign $c_1$ to Cluster 1.

For $c_2 = (16, 81)$:

$$
d(c_2, \mu_1^{(0)}) = \sqrt{(16-15)^2 + (81-39)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1764} = 42.01
$$

$$
d(c_2, \mu_2^{(0)}) = \sqrt{(16-17)^2 + (81-6)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 5625} = 75.01
$$

→ Assign $c_2$ to Cluster 1 (closer to $\mu_1$).

For $c_3 = (17, 6)$:

$$
d(c_3, \mu_1^{(0)}) = \sqrt{(17-15)^2 + (6-39)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 1089} = 33.02
$$

$$
d(c_3, \mu_2^{(0)}) = \sqrt{(17-17)^2 + (6-6)^2} = 0.00
$$

→ Assign $c_3$ to Cluster 2.

For $c_4 = (18, 77)$:

$$
d(c_4, \mu_1^{(0)}) = \sqrt{(18-15)^2 + (77-39)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 1444} = 38.07
$$

$$
d(c_4, \mu_2^{(0)}) = \sqrt{(18-17)^2 + (77-6)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 5041} = 71.01
$$

→ Assign $c_4$ to Cluster 1.

### 3.2.4 Step 2 — Update Centroids (Iteration 1)

Cluster 1 members: $\{c_1, c_2, c_4\}$

$$
\mu_1^{(1)} = \left( \frac{15+16+18}{3}, \frac{39+81+77}{3} \right) = (16.33, 65.67)
$$

Cluster 2 members: $\{c_3\}$

$$
\mu_2^{(1)} = (17, 6)
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 3 — Convergence Check

WCSS at iteration 0:

$$
J_0 = (c_1-\mu_1)^2 + (c_2-\mu_1)^2 + (c_4-\mu_1)^2 + (c_3-\mu_2)^2
$$

Since $c_1 = \mu_1^{(0)}$, the first term is 0.

After recomputation at iteration 1, the cluster assignments remain the same → **Convergence achieved**.

### 3.2.6 Step 4 — Interpretation

- **Cluster 1** (low–mid income, high spending) → **Impulsive Buyers** → Marketing: loyalty rewards.
- **Cluster 2** (mid income, very low spending) → **Frugal Customers** → Marketing: discount coupons.

This exact numerical derivation is what KTU expects in **14-mark case study questions**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal ML Case-Study Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Problem Definition"] --> B["Data Acquisition"]
```
