# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Explain the role of exploratory data analysis (EDA) in a machine learning case study. List any four EDA techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

EDA is the **pre-modelling investigation phase** where the data scientist understands the structure, distribution, and quality of the dataset before feeding it to any algorithm. It bridges raw data and the modelling phase.

Four key EDA techniques:

1. **Summary Statistics** — Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation, Quartiles.
2. **Univariate Visualization** — Histograms, Box plots, Density plots for individual features.
3. **Bivariate / Multivariate Visualization** — Scatter plots, Pair plots, Heatmaps to detect correlation.
4. **Missing-Value and Outlier Detection** — `isnull().sum()`, Z-score, IQR method.

> EDA prevents **garbage-in-garbage-out**, the single biggest reason real-world ML projects fail.

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between content-based and collaborative filtering in recommender systems. State one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Content-Based Filtering | Collaborative Filtering |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Item features / metadata | User–Item interaction history |
| Cold Start | Works on new items (if features exist) | Fails for new users/items |
| Diversity | Limited (similar to past liked items) | High (surprise recommendations) |
| Sparsity | Not affected | Heavily affected by data sparsity |

**Limitations:**
- **Content-Based:** Overspecialization — never recommends items outside user's past profile.
- **Collaborative:** Cold-start problem and data sparsity.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider a movie recommendation case study with 1000 users and 500 movies. The user–item rating matrix is highly sparse (> 95% missing entries). Explain how **Matrix Factorization (SVD-based)** solves this problem. Derive the SGD update rule for the latent factors $p_u$ and $q_i$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bank wants to detect fraudulent credit-card transactions. The dataset has 100,000 transactions with only 150 frauds. Discuss the **class imbalance problem** and propose a complete methodology: (i) appropriate resampling technique, (ii) suitable algorithms, (iii) correct evaluation metrics. Justify each choice.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

Matrix factorization addresses sparsity by learning **latent (hidden) features** that explain observed ratings. Instead of working with 1000×500 entries, we factor:

$$
R \approx P Q^T
$$

where $P \in \mathbb{R}^{1000 \times k}$ and $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{500 \times k}$, with $k \ll \min(1000, 500)$ (typically $k = 10$ to $100$).

**Why it works:** Even if user $u$ has rated only 5 movies, the model learns a latent vector $p_u$ that captures their taste, and this vector can predict ratings for all 500 movies.

**Loss Function:**

$$
L = \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{K}} (r_{ui} - p_u q_i^T)^2 + \lambda (\Vert p_u \Vert^2 + \Vert q_i \Vert^2)
$$

**Gradient computation:**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial p_u} = -2 e_{ui} q_i + 2 \lambda p_u \quad \text{where} \quad e_{ui} = r_{ui} - p_u q_i^T
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = -2 e_{ui} p_u + 2 \lambda q_i
$$

**SGD Update Rules:**

$$
p_u \leftarrow p_u + \eta (e_{ui} q_i - \lambda p_u)
$$

$$
q_i \leftarrow q_i + \eta (e_{ui} p_u - \lambda q_i)
$$

After convergence, predictions for unrated movies are $\hat{r}_{ui} = p_u q_i^T$, and the top-N highest-predicted items are recommended.

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Resampling:** Fraud ratio = 150/100000 = 0.15% — extremely imbalanced. Use **SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique)** to synthetically generate new fraud samples rather than naive duplication (which causes overfitting).

**(ii) Algorithms:**
- **Random Forest** — robust to imbalance, provides feature importance.
- **XGBoost with `scale_pos_weight`** — handles imbalance via sample weighting.
- **Isolation Forest** — unsupervised anomaly detection, no labels needed.

**(iii) Metrics:**
- **Accuracy is misleading** here (99.85% by always predicting "non-fraud").
- Use **Precision** (to limit false alarms) and **Recall** (to catch maximum frauds).
- **F1-Score** balances both.
- **AUC-ROC** and **AUC-PR** are threshold-independent.
- **MCC (Matthews Correlation Coefficient)** is the most balanced single metric for imbalanced data:

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$

**Final Recommendation:** Deploy XGBoost with SMOTE, optimize threshold on Precision-Recall curve to maximize Recall (fraud is costlier than false alarm).

---

### **Question B.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A retail company has collected 200 customer records with features *Age, Annual Income, and Spending Score*. Design a complete **K-Means clustering case study**: explain the algorithm steps, derive the WCSS objective, describe how you would choose $K$ using the **Elbow Method**, and propose a method to validate clusters using the **Silhouette Score**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A supermarket wants to perform **Market Basket Analysis** on 5000 transactions. Define Support, Confidence, and Lift. Given the rule {Bread, Butter} ⇒ {Milk}, with `support({Bread, Butter, Milk}) = 0.04`, `support({Bread}) = 0.20`, `support({Butter}) = 0.25`, `support({Milk}) = 0.30`, compute **Support, Confidence, and Lift** of the rule. Interpret the result.

#### **Model Solution (a):**

K-Means partitions $N$ customers into $K$ clusters by minimizing within-cluster variance:

$$
J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2
$$

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize** $K$ cluster centroids randomly.
2. **Assignment step:** For each customer $x_i$, assign it to the cluster with nearest centroid:

$$
C(x_i) = \arg\min_{k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2
$$

3. **Update step:** Recompute centroids as cluster means:

$$
\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i
$$

4. Repeat steps 2–3 until centroids stabilize.

**Choosing K — Elbow Method:**

Compute $J$ for $K = 1, 2, \dots, 10$. Plot $J$ vs $K$. The "elbow point" where the rate of decrease sharply changes is the optimal $K$ (typically $K = 5$ for retail segmentation).

**Validation — Silhouette Score:**

For each customer $i$:

$$
s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}
$$

where $a(i)$ = mean distance to other customers in same cluster, $b(i)$ = mean distance to customers in nearest other cluster.

Average $s(i)$ over all $i$; values near **+1** indicate well-clustered data; near **0** indicates overlapping clusters.

**Business Interpretation (typical 5 segments):**

| Segment | Age | Income | Spending | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Young | Low | High | Discount coupons |
| 2 | Young | High | High | Premium products |
| 3 | Middle | Mid | Mid | Loyalty programs |
| 4 | Old | High | Low | Personalized service |
| 5 | Old | Low | Low | Value packs |

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Definitions:**
- **Support:** Fraction of transactions containing all items in the rule.
- **Confidence:** Probability of seeing Y given X.
- **Lift:** Ratio of observed support to expected support under independence.

**Given:**

$$
\text{support}(B, Bu, M) = 0.04, \quad \text{support}(B) = 0.20, \quad \text{support}(Bu) = 0.25, \quad \text{support}(M) = 0.30
$$

**Support of the rule:**

$$
\text{Support}(\{Bread, Butter\} \Rightarrow \{Milk\}) = 0.04
$$

**Confidence:**

$$
\text{Confidence} = \frac{\text{Support}(B, Bu, M)}{\text{Support}(Bread, Butter)}
$$

We need $\text{Support}(B, Bu)$ — assuming the problem provides it via transaction count: with $\text{support}(B, Bu, M) = 0.04$, and using the directly given single-item supports, we approximate the antecedent support via the joint support. If the conditional support of {Bread, Butter} is not given separately, we use the conservative calculation:

Using the rule's natural formulation:

$$
\text{Confidence} = \frac{0.04}{\text{Support}(Bread, Butter)}
$$

If we approximate $\text{Support}(Bread, Butter) \approx \text{Support}(Bread) \cdot \text{Support}(Butter) = 0.20 \cdot 0.25 = 0.05$ (assuming near-independence of antecedents):

$$
\text{Confidence} = \frac{0.04}{0.05} = 0.80
$$

**Lift:**

$$
\text{Lift} = \frac{\text{Confidence}}{\text{Support}(Milk)} = \frac{0.80}{0.30} \approx 2.67
$$

**Interpretation:**

- **Support = 4%** → The combination {Bread, Butter, Milk} appears in 4% of all 5000 transactions (≈ 200 baskets).
- **Confidence = 80%** → When customers buy Bread and Butter, they buy Milk 80% of the time.
- **Lift = 2.67 > 1** → Customers buying Bread and Butter are **2.67 times more likely** to buy Milk than the average customer. This is a **strong positive association** — the supermarket should place Milk next to Bread and Butter, or run a combo offer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Case-Study Questions**
> 1. **Skipping Problem Formulation:** Students jump to algorithms without stating the ML task type (classification/regression/clustering). **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Reporting Plain Accuracy on Imbalanced Data:** Always discuss Precision/Recall/F1. **(-2 to -3 marks)**
> 3. **No Diagram:** Case-study answers without a pipeline diagram are marked down 1–2 marks.
> 4. **No Business Interpretation:** Final recommendations are mandatory. Models without actionable insights are penalized.
> 5. **Ignoring Cold Start & Sparsity:** Recommender system answers that do not address cold-start lose marks.
> 6. **Confusing Support and Confidence:** Be sure to use the correct denominator (Confidence uses antecedent support; Lift uses consequent support).
> 7. **No Mention of Cross-Validation:** Always use k-fold CV for evaluation; do not rely on a single train-test split.
> 8. **Mixing Up User-Based vs Item-Based CF:** User-based uses similarity across users for a target user; item-based uses similarity across items for a target item.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Case Studies Module 4**

- A **case study** is an end-to-end ML problem solving exercise, not just an algorithm question.
- The **7-stage pipeline**: Problem → Data → EDA → Preprocessing → Feature Engineering → Model → Deploy.
- **Recommender Systems:** Cosine Similarity, Pearson Correlation, Matrix Factorization (SGD), Cold-Start problem, Hybrid systems.
- **Fraud Detection:** Imbalanced data, SMOTE, Precision/Recall/F1, AUC-ROC, threshold tuning.
- **Customer Segmentation:** K-Means, Elbow Method (WCSS), Silhouette Score, business interpretation of clusters.
- **Market Basket Analysis:** Apriori algorithm, Support, Confidence, Lift; **Lift > 1** indicates positive correlation.
- **Sentiment Analysis:** Text preprocessing pipeline (Tokenize → Stopword removal → Stemming → TF-IDF → Classifier).
- **Evaluation Metrics Matrix:**

| Task | Best Metric |
|---|---|
| Balanced Classification | Accuracy |
| Imbalanced Classification | F1 / AUC-ROC / MCC |
| Regression | RMSE / MAE / R² |
| Clustering | Silhouette / Davies-Bouldin Index |
| Ranking | NDCG / MAP@K |
| Recommendation | RMSE / Precision@K / Recall@K |

- **Always justify algorithm choice** with reference to data characteristics (size, dimensionality, imbalance, linearity).
- **Always include a flowchart** of your proposed pipeline in long answers.
- **Cross-validate** using K-Fold (typically $K = 5$ or $10$).
- **Hyperparameter Tuning** via Grid Search or Random Search.
- **Avoid data leakage:** preprocessing steps must be fit only on training data and then applied to test data.
- **Feature Scaling is mandatory** for distance-based algorithms (KNN, K-Means, SVM, PCA) but not for tree-based models (Random Forest, XGBoost).
- **Curse of Dimensionality:** Use PCA (Module 3) when $d > 50$ or when features are highly correlated.
- **Bias–Variance Trade-off** is the guiding principle behind choosing simpler vs complex models.
- **Final answer must include business / domain insights**, not just numerical results.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real Time Sentiment Analysis - Stock Market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Real Time Sentiment Analysis - Stock Market ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) **Real-Time Sentiment Analysis (RTSA)** in the stock market domain is a specialized sub-field of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Text Mining** that involves the automated extraction, quantification, and classification of subjective opinions, emotions, and attitudes expressed in unstructured textual data streams (such as news headlines, social media posts, financial blogs, and earnings call transcripts) into polarity categories — **Positive**, **Negative**, or **Neutral** — in near-zero latency, with the explicit objective of forecasting short-term equity price movements, volatility, and trading volume. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Real-time sentiment analysis is the computational study of opinions, sentiments, evaluations, attitudes, appraisals, and emotions expressed by investors, traders, and the public in textual form, leveraged as a *leading indicator* (a predictive signal that precedes actual market movement) for algorithmic and high-frequency trading systems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Market Mood Thermometer" Imagine a stadium full of 50,000 cricket spectators. Before a match, you can sense whether the crowd is **euphoric**, **tense**, or **dejected** simply by listening to the noise. A seasoned bookmaker uses this "crowd mood" to adjust the odds **before** the actual game outcome is known. **Real-Time Sentiment Analysis works exactly the same way in the stock market:** - The **crowd** = Millions of tweets, Reddit posts, news articles, and analyst reports. - The **noise/mood** = Aggregate sentiment polarity (bullish vs. bearish). - The **bookmaker** = The ML/quantitative trading model that converts text-mood into a **buy/sell/hold** decision. - The **game** = The actual stock price movement (which usually **follows** the mood, not the other way around). > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** Stock prices are driven less by *fundamentals* and more by the *herd psychology* of market participants. Sentiment analysis attempts to **measure** this psychology at scale and speed that no human analyst could ever match. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Board Emphasis) The following metrics are the standard units of measurement in stock-market sentiment analysis: | Metric | Symbol | Unit / Range | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | **Polarity Score** | $p$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Negative to Positive sentiment intensity | | **Subjectivity Score** | $s$ | $0$ to $1$ | Objective (factual) vs. Subjective (opinionated) | | **Compound Score (VADER)** | $c$ | $-1$ to $+1$ | Normalized aggregate sentiment | | **Sentiment Velocity** | $v_s$ | $\Delta p / \Delta t$ | Rate of change of sentiment per unit time | | **News Volume / Mention Count** | $N$ | Integer count | Frequency of ticker symbol mentions | | **Fear & Greed Index** | $F\&G$ | $0$ to $100$ | Composite market mood indicator | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** A polarity score of **$p = 0$** does NOT mean neutral text — it could be perfectly balanced positive/negative phrases. Always pair polarity with **subjectivity** to disambiguate. ### 1.4 Why This Topic is "High-Yield" for KTU 2024 This topic sits at the **convergence of three KTU modules**: 1. **Module 3 (NLP fundamentals)** — tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming. 2. **Module 4 (Real-time analytics & advanced applications)** — streaming data, online learning. 3. **Module 5 (Case studies & deployment)** — production-grade ML pipelines. Expect 14-mark analytical questions on **pipeline design**, **feature engineering for finance**, and **sentiment-price correlation**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline — Six Logical Phases Real-time stock market sentiment analysis is implemented as a **six-stage streaming pipeline**. Each stage has a distinct responsibility and its own failure modes that KTU examiners love to test. #### Phase 1: **Data Ingestion (Streaming Layer)** - **Sources:** Twitter/X Streaming API, Reddit Pushshift API, Reuters/Bloomberg RSS feeds, Google News RSS, SEC EDGAR filings. - **Mechanism:** Apache Kafka topics, AWS Kinesis, or serverless WebSocket listeners. - **Why critical:** Stock sentiment has a **half-life of minutes** (news from 4 hours ago is largely priced-in). Latency must be **< 5 seconds** for HFT-grade systems. #### Phase 2: **Text Preprocessing & Normalization** Raw financial text is exceptionally noisy. Required cleaning steps: - **Ticker symbol extraction** using regex: `\$[A-Z]{1,5}` → e.g., `$TSLA`, `$AAPL`. - **Cashtag normalization:** `$AAPL` and `Apple` mapped to the same entity via a ticker dictionary. - **Emoji decoding:** 🚀📈 = bullish, 💀📉 = bearish (these are HIGH-signal in retail trading text). - **Stop-word removal** (custom financial stop-list: "stock", "share", "market" — words that carry no polarity). - **Lemmatization** (not stemming — preserves "earnings" vs. "earning" distinction). - **Handling sarcasm:** "Yeah, $TSLA is *really* going to $1000 🙄" — requires contextual models (BERT), not bag-of-words. #### Phase 3: **Feature Engineering & Representation** Convert cleaned text into numerical vectors: - **Lexicon-based (VADER, TextBlob):** Rule-based, fast, interpretable. - **Classical ML (TF-IDF + SVM/LR):** Sparse vectors, $d = 50{,}000+$ dimensions. - **Deep Learning (Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText):** Dense 300-d embeddings capturing semantics. - **Transformer-based (FinBERT, BERT, RoBERTa):** 768-d contextual embeddings, pre-trained on financial corpora. #### Phase 4: **Sentiment Classification Model** - **Output:** Polarity class $y \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ or continuous polarity score $p \in [-1, 1]$. - **Model choice depends on latency budget:** - Latency $< 1$ ms → VADER lexicon lookup. - Latency $< 50$ ms → Distilled FinBERT (TinyBERT). - Latency $< 500$ ms → Full FinBERT inference. #### Phase 5: **Aggregation & Time-Series Construction** - Aggregate per-second sentiment into **rolling windows** (1-min, 5-min, 15-min). - Compute **Sentiment Velocity** $v_s$ and **Sentiment Momentum** (rate of acceleration). #### Phase 6: **Prediction & Trading Signal Generation** - Feed aggregated sentiment + historical price into a downstream model: - **Regression:** Predict next-period return $r_{t+1} = f(S_t, P_t, V_t)$. - **Classification:** Predict direction $D_{t+1} \in \{\text{up, down}\}$. - **Reinforcement Learning:** Directly output buy/sell/hold actions maximizing Sharpe ratio. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Memorize the VADER compound score formula and the Pearson correlation formula. They appear in nearly every past paper involving this topic. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Range / Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Term importance in document corpus | | 2 | **VADER Compound Score** | $c = \dfrac{\sum_{i} s_i}{\sqrt{\left(\sum_{i} s_i\right)^2 + \alpha}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Normalized aggregate polarity | | 3 | **Pearson Correlation (Sentiment vs. Return)** | $\rho = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(p_i - \bar{p})(r_i - \bar{r})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i}(p_i - \bar{p})^2 \cdot \sum_{i}(r_i - \bar{r})^2}}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Linear dependence strength | | 4 | **Sentiment Velocity** | $v_s(t) = \dfrac{p(t) - p(t - \Delta t)}{\Delta t}$ | $\text{score} / \text{min}$ | Rate of mood change | | 5 | **Sentiment Momentum** | $m_s(t) = \dfrac{d v_s}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 p}{dt^2}$ | $\text{score} / \text{min}^2$ | Acceleration of mood | | 6 | **Sharpe Ratio (Trading Performance)** | $S = \dfrac{\mathbb{E}[R_p - R_f]}{\sigma_p}$ | Dimensionless | Risk-adjusted return of strategy | | 7 | **Bag-of-Words Polarity (Lexicon)** | $p = \dfrac{P_{\text{pos}} - P_{\text{neg}}}{P_{\text{pos}} + P_{\text{neg}} + 2\alpha}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Smoothing-adjusted polarity | | 8 | **Information Coefficient (IC)** | $IC = \text{corr}(f_{\text{pred}}, r_{\text{actual}})$ | $[-1, +1]$ | Quant-strategy performance metric | > **Notation Convention:** In all formulas, $\alpha$ is a **smoothing constant** (typically $\alpha = 15$ for VADER), $N$ = number of documents, $tf$ = term frequency, $df$ = document frequency, $R_f$ = risk-free rate, $\sigma_p$ = portfolio standard deviation. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility This system is the **backbone of:** - **Quantitative Hedge Funds** (Renaissance Technologies, Two Sigma) — accounts for an estimated **30–40%** of alpha in some strategies. - **Robo-Advisors** (Wealthfront, Betterment) — for portfolio rebalancing triggers. - **Regulatory Surveillance** (SEC, FINRA) — to detect pump-and-dump schemes. - **Central Bank Policy Analysis** — measuring public reaction to interest-rate decisions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: VADER Compound Score Normalization The **Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner (VADER)** is the most commonly tested lexicon model. The compound score is derived as follows: **Step 1 — Lexicon Lookup:** Each word $w_i$ in the cleaned text is matched against the VADER lexicon $\mathcal{L}$, retrieving a valence score $s_i \in [-4, +4]$. $$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i$$ **Step 2 — Smoothing Normalization:** The raw sum $S$ is normalized into $[-1, +1]$ using the $\alpha$-smoothing function: $$c = \frac{S}{\sqrt{S^2 + \alpha}}$$ **Step 3 — Solve the limits to confirm range:** When $S \to +\infty$: $$c = \lim_{S \to +\infty} \frac{S}{\sqrt{S^2 + \alpha}} = \lim_{S \to +\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \alpha/S^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0}} = +1$$ When $S \to -\infty$: $$c = \lim_{S \to -\infty} \frac{S}{\sqrt{S^2 + \alpha}} = \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1 + 0}} = -1$$ When $S = 0$: $$c = \frac{0}{\sqrt{0 + \alpha}} = 0$$ **Conclusion:** $c$ is monotonically increasing in $S$, bounded in $(-1, +1)$, and the constant $\alpha = 15$ controls the steepness of the sigmoid-like response. Higher $\alpha$ → less extreme scores; lower $\alpha$ → more polarized scores. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: TF-IDF Vector Construction **Given:** A corpus $\mathcal{D} = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N\}$ of $N$ financial news articles. **Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF):** The frequency of term $t$ in document $d$: $$tf(t, d) = \frac{\text{count of } t \text{ in } d}{\text{total word count of } d}$$ **Step 2 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** How unique is term $t$ across the corpus? $$idf(t) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t) + 1}\right)$$ The $+1$ in the denominator is **Laplace smoothing** to prevent division by zero when $df(t) = 0$ (term not in any training document). **Step 3 — Final TF-IDF Weight:** The product measures both local importance ($tf$) and global rarity ($idf$): $$w(t, d) = tf(t, d) \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t) + 1}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:** Let $N = 1000$ news articles, and the word "earnings" appears in $50$ of them, occurring $4$ times in a specific article $d^*$ of length $200$ words. $$tf(\text{``earnings''}, d^*) = \frac{4}{200} = 0.02$$ $$idf(\text{``earnings''}) = \log\left(\frac{1000}{50 + 1}\right) = \log(19.608) \approx 2.976$$ $$w(\text{``earnings''}, d^*) = 0.02 \times 2.976 \approx 0.0595$$ Compare with a common word like "the" ($df = 950$): $idf = \log(1000/951) \approx 0.050$, so the weight is **drastically reduced** — exactly the desired behavior. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pearson Correlation Between Sentiment and Stock Return To prove that sentiment *actually* predicts price movement, the industry-standard is the **Pearson correlation coefficient**. **Given:** Two time series of length $N$: - $p_i$ = aggregated sentiment polarity at time $i$. - $r_i$ = log-return of the stock at time $i$, defined as $r_i = \ln(P_i / P_{i-1})$. **Step 1 — Compute the means:** $$\bar{p} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \quad,\quad \bar{r} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i$$ **Step 2 — Compute deviations from the mean:** $$\Delta p_i = p_i - \bar{p} \quad,\quad \Delta r_i = r_i - \bar{r}$$ **Step 3 — Assemble the covariance numerator:** $$\text{Cov}(p, r) = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta p_i \cdot \Delta r_i$$ **Step 4 — Assemble the denominator (geometric mean of variances):** $$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\Delta p_i)^2} \quad,\quad \sigma_r = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\Delta r_i)^2}$$ **Step 5 — Final correlation coefficient:** $$\rho_{p,r} = \frac{\text{Cov}(p, r)}{\sigma_p \cdot \sigma_r} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta p_i \cdot \Delta r_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\Delta p_i)^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\Delta r_i)^2}}$$ **Step 6 — Statistical Significance Test (KTU favorite):** The $t$-statistic for testing $H_0: \rho = 0$: $$t = \rho \sqrt{\frac{N - 2}{1 - \rho^2}}$$ This follows a Student's $t$-distribution with $N-2$ degrees of freedom. For $\vert t \vert > 1.96$, the correlation is significant at the **95% confidence level** ($p < 0.05$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pitfall:** Correlation $\rho \neq 0$ does **NOT** imply causation. Sentiment may rise *because* prices are rising (reverse causality), or both may be driven by a third factor like an earnings announcement. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Real-Time Sentiment Analysis Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[dataSource1] -->|Twitter/X API| B[streamIngest] A2[dataSource2] -->|Reuters/Bloomberg RSS| B A3[dataSource3] -->|Reddit Pushshift| B A4[dataSource4] -->|SEC EDGAR Filings| B B -->|Apache Kafka Topic| C[preprocess] C -->|Regex: Tickers, Emoji Decode| D[tokenizeLemmatize] D -->|Stop-word Removal| E[featureEngineer] E -->|TF-IDF| F1[modelLexicon] E -->|Word2Vec / FinBERT| F2[modelDeep] F1 --> G[aggregateWindow] F2 --> G G -->|Rolling 5-min Window| H[computeMetrics] H -->|Polarity, Velocity, Momentum| I[downstreamPredictor] I -->|Buy/Sell/Hold Signal| J[tradingEngine] I -->|IC, Sharpe, Drawdown| K[performanceMonitor] K -->|Drift Detected| F1 K -->|Drift Detected| F2 J -->|Order Execution| L[brokerAPI] classDef io fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef model fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef act fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,A2,A3,A4,B io class C,D,E,G,H proc class F1,F2,I model class J,K,L act ``` **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Students:** | Block Label | Function | KTU Keyword | |---|---|---| | `streamIngest` | Kafka consumer pulling from social/news APIs | Real-time streaming | | `preprocess` | Regex, emoji decode, ticker extraction | NLP preprocessing | | `featureEngineer` | TF-IDF or embedding generation | Feature extraction | | `modelLexicon` | VADER / TextBlob rule-based scoring | Lexicon approach | | `modelDeep` | FinBERT transformer-based classification | Deep learning approach | | `aggregateWindow` | Rolling 5-min polarity mean | Time-series aggregation | | `computeMetrics` | Polarity, velocity, momentum | Signal generation | | `downstreamPredictor` | LSTM / XGBoost on (sentiment + price) | Hybrid model | | `tradingEngine` | Order routing, position sizing | Algorithmic trading | | `performanceMonitor` | Tracks Sharpe, IC, drift | MLOps monitoring | ### 4.2 Data Flow Sequence — Streaming Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Tweet participant K as Kafka Broker participant P as Preprocessor participant M as FinBERT Model participant DB as Time-Series DB participant T as Trading Engine participant X as Exchange API U->>K: Publish "TSLA to the moon! 🚀" Note over K: Topic: finance.tweets
Latency budget: 500ms K->>P: Consume raw JSON P->>P: Extract $TSLA, decode 🚀 P->>M: Tokenized input vector M->>M: Forward pass (12-layer transformer) M->>DB: p = +0.87, s = 0.92 DB->>T: Query: 5-min rolling mean T->>T: v_s = 0.12 / min (bullish momentum) T->>X: BUY 100 shares $TSLA MKT X-->>T: Filled @ $245.30 T->>DB: Log: PnL, slippage, fill time ``` ### 4.3 Concept Map — Sentiment → Price Causal Pathway ```mermaid graph LR A[News Event] --> B[Media Coverage] B --> C[Public Discussion] C --> D[Aggregated Sentiment Score] D --> E[Trading Signal] E --> F[Order Flow Imbalance] F --> G[Price Movement] G --> H[Reinforcement to Sentiment] H --> C classDef ev fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 classDef sig fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 class A,B ev class D,E,F sig class G,H fb ``` > [!NOTE] > **The feedback loop** (G → H → C) is what makes stock markets **non-stationary** — the statistical properties of sentiment-price relationships drift over time, requiring **online model retraining**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Define Real-Time Sentiment Analysis. List any FOUR data sources commonly used for stock-market sentiment extraction.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Real-Time Sentiment Analysis is a sub-field of NLP that involves the automated extraction, classification, and quantification of subjective opinions from streaming textual data sources into polarity categories (positive, negative, neutral) with minimal latency, in order to forecast near-term stock price movements, volatility, and trading volume. **Data Sources (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Twitter / X Streaming API** — high-velocity retail trader sentiment. 2. **Reddit (r/wallstreetbets, r/stocks)** — coordinated retail-driven narratives. 3. **Reuters / Bloomberg News Feeds** — institutional-grade, low-noise. 4. **SEC EDGAR Filings** — 10-K, 10-Q, 8-K filings with management sentiment. 5. *(Bonus)* Earnings call transcripts, Google News RSS, StockTwits. --- #### **Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Explain the VADER compound score formula. Why is $\alpha = 15$ chosen as the smoothing constant?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Formula (2 marks):** The VADER compound score is computed as: $$c = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i}{\sqrt{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i\right)^2 + \alpha}}$$ where $s_i$ is the valence score of the $i$-th lexicon-matched word and $\alpha$ is a smoothing constant. The output $c \in [-1, +1]$ represents the normalized aggregate polarity of the input text. **Choice of $\alpha = 15$ (1 mark):** The constant $\alpha = 15$ was empirically tuned on a human-annotated social-media corpus. It provides a balance: large enough to **avoid extreme polarization** on short texts (a single "+4" word doesn't immediately saturate the score to +1), yet small enough to **saturate near extremes** for clearly positive/negative longer texts. It approximates a soft sigmoid response centered near 0. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **complete end-to-end pipeline** of a real-time stock market sentiment analysis system, clearly identifying each stage, the technique(s) used at each stage, and the typical latency budget. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **Pearson correlation coefficient** between the following 5-day aggregated sentiment scores and the corresponding stock log-returns. Test the **statistical significance at the 95% confidence level** using $t = \rho\sqrt{(N-2)/(1-\rho^2)}$. | Day | Sentiment Polarity $p_i$ | Stock Price $P_i$ (₹) | |---|---|---| | 1 | $+0.40$ | $100$ | | 2 | $+0.25$ | $102$ | | 3 | $-0.10$ | $101$ | | 4 | $-0.35$ | $98$ | | 5 | $-0.60$ | $94$ | **Model Solution for (a) — [7 marks]:** | Stage | Technique | Latency Budget | |---|---|---| | 1. **Data Ingestion** | Twitter Streaming API, Kafka topics | $< 100$ ms | | 2. **Preprocessing** | Regex ticker extraction, emoji decode, lemmatization | $< 50$ ms | | 3. **Feature Extraction** | FinBERT embeddings (768-d) | $< 200$ ms | | 4. **Sentiment Classification** | FinBERT inference (distilled) | $< 100$ ms | | 5. **Aggregation** | Rolling 5-min window mean | $< 10$ ms | | 6. **Signal Generation** | Polarity + velocity + momentum | $< 50$ ms | | 7. **Order Execution** | Broker API call | $< 200$ ms | **Total latency:** $\approx 710$ ms — well within HFT acceptable range. *(1 mark for naming each stage with 0.5 marks for technique + 0.5 marks for justification.)* **Model Solution for (b) — [7 marks]:** **Step 1: Compute log-returns** $r_i = \ln(P_i / P_{i-1})$: $$r_2 = \ln(102/100) = 0.0198, \quad r_3 = \ln(101/102) = -0.0099$$ $$r_4 = \ln(98/101) = -0.0300, \quad r_5 = \ln(94/98) = -0.0416$$ **Step 2: Drop day 1 from correlation (no return available)**, so $N = 4$ paired observations. **Step 3: Compute means** of $p_i$ (days 2–5) and $r_i$: $$\bar{p} = \frac{0.25 - 0.10 - 0.35 - 0.60}{4} = \frac{-0.80}{4} = -0.20$$ $$\bar{r} = \frac{0.0198 - 0.0099 - 0.0300 - 0.0416}{4} = \frac{-0.0617}{4} = -0.01543$$ **Step 4: Build the deviation table:** | Day | $p_i$ | $r_i$ | $\Delta p_i$ | $\Delta r_i$ | $\Delta p_i \cdot \Delta r_i$ | $(\Delta p_i)^2$ | $(\Delta r_i)^2$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 2 | $+0.25$ | $+0.0198$ | $+0.45$ | $+0.0352$ | $+0.01584$ | $0.2025$ | $0.001239$ | | 3 | $-0.10$ | $-0.0099$ | $+0.10$ | $+0.0055$ | $+0.00055$ | $0.0100$ | $0.000030$ | | 4 | $-0.35$ | $-0.0300$ | $-0.15$ | $-0.0146$ | $+0.00219$ | $0.0225$ | $0.000213$ | | 5 | $-0.60$ | $-0.0416$ | $-0.40$ | $-0.0262$ | $+0.01048$ | $0.1600$ | $0.000687$ | | **Σ** | | | | | **$+0.02906$** | **$0.3950$** | **$0.002169$** | **Step 5: Apply the Pearson formula:** $$\rho = \frac{+0.02906}{\sqrt{0.3950 \times 0.002169}} = \frac{+0.02906}{\sqrt{0.000857}} = \frac{+0.02906}{0.02927} \approx +0.993$$ **Step 6: Test significance** with $N = 4$, degrees of freedom $= 2$: $$t = 0.993 \times \sqrt{\frac{4-2}{1 - 0.993^2}} = 0.993 \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{0.01395}} = 0.993 \times \sqrt{143.4} \approx 0.993 \times 11.97 \approx 11.89$$ **Step 7: Critical value:** For $df = 2$, $t_{0.025} = 4.303$ (two-tailed, 95%). **Conclusion:** $t = 11.89 > 4.303 \Rightarrow$ **Reject $H_0$**. There is strong statistical evidence of a **positive correlation** between sentiment polarity and stock returns in this dataset. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly **forget to drop day 1** (which has no prior price to compute return) and end up with a length-mismatch error. Always verify $N$ matches in BOTH series before plugging into the formula. Also, do NOT confuse the **stock price $P_i$** with the **log-return $r_i$** — they are entirely different quantities. --- #### **Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **VADER (lexicon-based)**, **LSTM-based**, and **FinBERT (transformer-based)** sentiment classifiers across FIVE dimensions: speed, accuracy, interpretability, training data requirement, and handling of sarcasm/negation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A financial news corpus contains $N = 10{,}000$ articles. The word "crash" appears in $40$ articles, with an average article length of $250$ words. In a specific article of length $300$ words, "crash" appears $3$ times. **Calculate the TF-IDF weight** of the word "crash" in that article and comment on why this weight is high. **Model Solution for (a) — [7 marks]:** | Dimension | VADER | LSTM | FinBERT | |---|---|---|---| | **Speed** | $< 1$ ms (lookup) ⚡ | $\sim 20$ ms | $\sim 200$ ms | | **Accuracy** | $\sim 75\%$ on social text | $\sim 85\%$ | $\sim 92\%$ on financial text | | **Interpretability** | Full (rule-based) 🟢 | Low (black box) | Very low | | **Training Data** | None (rule-based) | $50{,}000$+ labeled | Pre-trained; $10{,}000$+ for fine-tuning | | **Sarcasm/Negation** | Partial (handles negation: "not good" → negative) | Good (sequence context) | Excellent (deep bidirectional context) | **Score allocation:** 1.4 marks per dimension. *(1 mark for filling each row correctly with one-sentence justification.)* **Concluding remark (0 marks, but adds value):** The choice depends on the **latency-accuracy tradeoff**. HFT systems use VADER; institutional research uses FinBERT; mid-frequency trading uses LSTM. **Model Solution for (b) — [7 marks]:** **Given:** - $N = 10{,}000$ (total documents in corpus) - $df(\text{``crash''}) = 40$ (documents containing "crash") - Article $d^*$ has length $300$ words - "crash" appears $3$ times in $d^*$ **Step 1: Compute Term Frequency** (raw count form acceptable; normalization form shown): $$tf(\text{``crash''}, d^*) = \frac{3}{300} = 0.010$$ **Step 2: Compute Inverse Document Frequency** (with Laplace smoothing $+1$): $$idf(\text{``crash''}) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df + 1}\right) = \log\left(\frac{10000}{40 + 1}\right) = \log(243.90) \approx 5.497$$ **Step 3: Compute TF-IDF:** $$w(\text{``crash''}, d^*) = tf \times idf = 0.010 \times 5.497 = 0.05497 \approx 0.0550$$ **Step 4: Interpretation (3 marks):** - The weight $\approx 0.055$ is **relatively high** compared to common words like "the" (which would yield $idf \approx 0.005$ and weight $\approx 0.0001$). - This is because "crash" is **rare globally** (only $40/10000 = 0.4\%$ of documents) but **frequent locally** ($3$ times in this article). - Such a high TF-IDF score is a strong indicator that the article is **specifically about a market downturn** — exactly the kind of signal a sentiment-classification pipeline would flag as **bearish content**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting the $+1$ in $idf$** — this is a $1$-mark deduction in the KTU scheme. Always mention "Laplace smoothing." > 2. **Using natural log vs. log base 10** — both are accepted, but **state your convention explicitly** in the exam. VADER and most NLP toolkits use $\log_e$ (natural log). > 3. **Forgetting to interpret** the result. Calculation alone gets 4/7 marks; the 3-mark interpretation is mandatory for full credit. > 4. **Misreading "10,000 articles"** as the number of times "crash" appears — it is the **corpus size** $N$, not $df$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Cheat-Sheet — Memorize These:** - [x] **Pipeline order:** Ingest → Preprocess → Tokenize → Feature → Classify → Aggregate → Signal → Execute. - [x] **VADER formula:** $c = S / \sqrt{S^2 + \alpha}$, with $\alpha = 15$. - [x] **TF-IDF formula:** $w = tf \cdot \log(N / (df + 1))$ — **always include the $+1$ for Laplace smoothing**. - [x] **Pearson $\rho$ is bounded in $[-1, +1]$**; values closer to $\pm 1$ indicate strong linear correlation. - [x] **Significance test:** $t = \rho\sqrt{(N-2)/(1-\rho^2)}$ with $df = N-2$; critical $t = 1.96$ for large $N$, but use the $t$-table for small $N$. - [x] **Lexicon methods** = fast + interpretable + poor on sarcasm. - [x] **FinBERT** = slow + accurate + handles context. - [x] **Latency budget for HFT:** end-to-end $< 1$ second. - [x] **Emoji are HIGH-signal in retail trading text** 🚀📈 vs. 💀📉. - [x] **Cashtag normalization:** `$TSLA`, `Tesla`, `TSLA stock` → same entity. - [x] **Polarity ≠ Causality:** Correlation $\rho \neq 0$ does NOT prove sentiment *causes* price movement. - [x] **Feedback loop:** Sentiment → Price → Sentiment makes markets **non-stationary**; models must be retrained periodically (concept drift). - [x] **Sharpe Ratio** = risk-adjusted return: $S = \mathbb{E}[R_p - R_f] / \sigma_p$. - [x] **Information Coefficient (IC)** is the quant-industry's gold-standard metric for sentiment model performance. - [x] **VADER** works on **unlabeled text** (no training needed); LSTM/FinBERT require **labeled corpora**. - [x] **Stop-words in finance are domain-specific:** remove "stock", "share", "market" — they carry zero polarity. - [x] **Lemmatization > Stemming** for financial text (preserves "earnings" vs. "earning" distinction).

topics.title: Visualizations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT503 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Visualizations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Visualization** is the graphical representation of information and data. By using visual elements like charts, graphs, and maps, data visualization tools provide an accessible way to see and understand trends, outliers, and patterns in data. In the context of **Machine Learning (ML)** and **Data Analytics**, visualization serves as a critical bridge between raw data and actionable insight, enabling both *Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)* and *Model Interpretation*.

Mathematically, a visualization is a mapping:

$$V: \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \mathcal{G}$$

where $\mathcal{D}$ is the original data space (often high-dimensional, $\mathbb{R}^n$) and $\mathcal{G}$ is the graphical representation space (typically $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^3$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Visualization in ML is **NOT just plotting** — it encompasses EDA, feature engineering validation, model diagnostics (confusion matrix, ROC, learning curves), and explainable AI (feature importance, partial dependence plots).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Stethoscope" Intuition

Imagine a doctor examining a patient. Before any sophisticated test, the doctor first **listens** (auscultation), **looks** (visual inspection), and **feels** (palpation). These quick, intuitive observations give the first hints of what's wrong — even before an MRI or blood test.

**Visualization is the "stethoscope" of a data scientist.**

- A **bar chart** is like checking temperature — quick, simple, single-variable.
- A **scatter plot** is like an X-ray — reveals relationships between two factors.
- A **heatmap** is like a full-body scan — shows patterns across many variables simultaneously.
- A **confusion matrix** is like a pathology report — tells you exactly *where* a model is failing.

Without visualization, a data scientist is like a doctor trying to diagnose blindfolded — relying purely on summary statistics, missing the *story* in the data.

## 1.3 Core Categories of Visualization (KTU Taxonomy)

| Category | Purpose | Example Plots |
|---|---|---|
| **Univariate** | Analyze ONE variable at a time | Histogram, Box plot, Density plot |
| **Bivariate** | Analyze relationship between TWO variables | Scatter plot, Line plot, Bar chart |
| **Multivariate** | Analyze 3+ variables together | Heatmap, Parallel coordinates, Bubble chart |
| **High-Dimensional** | Reduce dimensions for display | PCA scatter, t-SNE, UMAP |
| **ML-Specific** | Diagnose and interpret models | Confusion matrix, ROC curve, Learning curve |

> [!NOTE]
> The **human visual cortex** processes images **60,000 times faster** than text. This is why "a picture is worth a thousand numbers" — and why KTU examiners love questions on choosing the *right* plot for the *right* data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Visualization Pipeline (Theory of Operation)

Every visualization in the KTU ML workflow follows a structured pipeline. Understanding each stage is critical for exam answers.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow:

1. **Data Ingestion** → Load dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_i, y_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$
2. **Data Profiling** → Identify data types: *categorical*, *numerical*, *ordinal*, *temporal*
3. **Goal Definition** → Ask: *"What question am I answering?"* (distribution? relationship? composition? comparison?)
4. **Encoding Selection** → Map data dimensions to visual channels (position, color, size, shape)
5. **Plot Generation** → Render the chosen chart type
6. **Interpretation** → Extract patterns, outliers, and insights
7. **Action** → Feed insights back into preprocessing or model building

## 2.2 The Grammar of Graphics — Theoretical Foundation

The modern theory of visualization rests on **Leland Wilkinson's Grammar of Graphics**. Every plot can be decomposed into:

| Component | Mathematical Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | The input set $\mathcal{D}$ | DataFrame |
| **Aesthetics (aes)** | Mapping variables → visual channels | `aes(x=age, y=salary)` |
| **Geometries (geom)** | The shape used to represent data | Bar, Point, Line |
| **Scales** | Transformation of data values → pixel values | Linear, Log |
| **Facets** | Sub-plots for subgroups | `facet_wrap(~gender)` |
| **Themes** | Non-data ink (background, fonts) | Minimal, Classic |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE paper related to visualization and analytics.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean (Central Tendency) | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Annotated on plots |
| 2 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Box plot whiskers |
| 3 | Interquartile Range | $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ | Outlier detection |
| 4 | Outlier Boundaries | $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR},\ Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}]$ | Box plot whiskers |
| 5 | Pearson Correlation | $r = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Heatmap correlation |
| 6 | Min-Max Normalization | $x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | Pre-plot scaling |
| 7 | Z-Score Standardization | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardized plots |
| 8 | Confusion Matrix Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Classification plots |
| 9 | True Positive Rate | $\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | ROC curve Y-axis |
| 10 | False Positive Rate | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | ROC curve X-axis |
| 11 | AUC (Area Under Curve) | $\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR})\, d(\text{FPR})$ | Model comparison |
| 12 | Explained Variance Ratio (PCA) | $\text{EVR}_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j}$ | Scree plot |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** When using absolute value in your exam scripts, ALWAYS write `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert`. Using vertical bars `|x|` may render incorrectly.

## 2.4 Why "Why" — Engineering Utility of Visualization

| Domain | Visualization Application |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | Tumor heatmaps, ECG waveforms |
| **Finance** | Stock candlesticks, risk dashboards |
| **Manufacturing** | SPC control charts, defect heatmaps |
| **NLP** | Word clouds, t-SNE of embeddings |
| **Computer Vision** | Confusion matrices, bounding box overlays |
| **Recommender Systems** | User-item interaction heatmaps |

A well-designed visualization reduces **cognitive load**, accelerates **decision-making**, and exposes **hidden biases** in ML models — making it indispensable in the modern AI pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pearson Correlation Coefficient (Used in Heatmaps)

The Pearson correlation coefficient is the foundation of correlation heatmaps — one of the most frequently asked KTU visualization questions.

### Starting Point:

Given paired data $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$, we want to measure **linear association strength**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Express deviations from mean.**
Define $a_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ and $b_i = y_i - \bar{y}$.

These represent how far each observation is from its respective mean.

**Step 2: Define the covariance numerator.**
The covariance between $X$ and $Y$ is:

$$\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$$

This measures **joint variability**.

**Step 3: Normalize by standard deviations.**
To remove the effect of units and scale, divide by $\sigma_x \cdot \sigma_y$:

$$r = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_x \cdot \sigma_y}$$

**Step 4: Substitute expanded forms.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}} \\
&= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\frac{1}{N}$ cancels out from numerator and denominator — this is why the formula is often written without it.

**Step 5: Interpret the result.**
- $r = +1$ → Perfect positive correlation (used in heatmaps as deep red)
- $r = -1$ → Perfect negative correlation (deep blue)
- $r = 0$ → No linear correlation (white/neutral color)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Confusion Matrix Metrics (For Classification Visualization)

A **confusion matrix** is a 2×2 (binary) or N×N (multiclass) table that visualizes classifier performance.

### Binary Case Setup:

For a binary classifier with classes $\{0, 1\}$:

| | Predicted = 0 | Predicted = 1 |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual = 0** | TN | FP |
| **Actual = 1** | FN | TP |

### Derived Metrics — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1: Accuracy** — Fraction of all correct predictions:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Step 2: Precision** — Of those predicted positive, how many are actually positive:

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Step 3: Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** — Of actual positives, how many were caught:

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 4: F1-Score** — Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Specificity (TNR)** — Of actual negatives, how many were correctly rejected:

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

**Step 6: False Positive Rate (FPR)** — Used as the X-axis in ROC curves:

$$\text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Explained Variance Ratio in PCA Scree Plots

When visualizing high-dimensional data using PCA, a **scree plot** shows how much variance each principal component captures.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Compute the covariance matrix $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$.

**Step 2:** Perform eigen-decomposition:

$$\Sigma v_k = \lambda_k v_k, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

where $\lambda_k$ is the $k$-th eigenvalue and $v_k$ is the corresponding eigenvector.

**Step 3:** Total variance is the sum of eigenvalues:

$$\text{Total Variance} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j$$

**Step 4:** Explained Variance Ratio for component $k$:

$$\text{EVR}_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j}$$

**Step 5:** Cumulative Explained Variance (for choosing number of components):

$$\text{Cumulative EVR}(k) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{\lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i}$$

A common KTU rule: select $k$ such that $\text{Cumulative EVR}(k) \geq 0.95$ (95% rule).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Plot to Use?"

This is the **most exam-relevant table** for KTU 2024 Scheme. Examiners frequently ask: *"Given data X, which visualization is appropriate?"*

| Data Type | Goal | Recommended Plot | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 Numerical var. | Distribution | Histogram / Density plot | High |
| 1 Numerical var. | Outliers + Spread | Box plot / Violin plot | High |
| 1 Categorical var. | Frequency | Bar chart | Medium |
| 1 Categorical var. | Proportion (≤7 cats) | Pie chart | Medium |
| 2 Numerical vars. | Relationship | Scatter plot | Very High |
| 2 Numerical vars. | Time series | Line plot | Very High |
| 2 vars. (1 cat + 1 num) | Compare groups | Grouped Box plot | High |
| Many Numerical vars. | Correlation structure | Heatmap | Very High |
| 3+ Numerical vars. | Multi-dim relationships | Bubble chart / Pair plot | High |
| High-dimensional | Cluster visualization | PCA / t-SNE scatter | Very High |
| Classification results | Performance | Confusion matrix | Very High |
| Classification threshold | Trade-off | ROC curve | Very High |
| Model training | Overfitting diagnosis | Learning curve | High |
| Geographic data | Spatial patterns | Choropleth map | Medium |
| Hierarchical data | Tree structure | Dendrogram / Treemap | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to recommend a plot, ALWAYS justify your choice by referring to (a) data type, (b) the analytical goal, and (c) the dimensionality. A bare recommendation without reasoning scores **zero marks**.
